| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Commenting what syntax already says    | Noise obscures real contracts.                                         | Comment ownership, lifetime, error behavior, and non-obvious invariants.                            |
| Omitting failure behavior              | C APIs often fail through return codes or sentinels.                   | Every fallible public function should define its failure convention.                                |
| Omitting pointer validity requirements | Pointer parameters do not encode non-nullness or bounds by themselves. | State whether pointers may be `NULL`, must point to arrays, or must remain valid after return.      |
| Treating documentation as enforcement  | Comments do not make invalid calls impossible.                         | Pair documentation with assertions, tests, static analysis, and defensive checks where appropriate. |

### Identifiers and Naming — keywords, namespaces, reserved identifiers, professional conventions

C identifiers name objects, functions, labels, tags, members, typedef names, macros, and parameters. C does not have package namespaces, so naming conventions and reserved-identifier rules matter more than in languages with robust module systems.

| Category            | Meaning                                  | Example                         | Scope or namespace behavior               | Practical consequence                                    |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Keyword             | Reserved language word                   | `int`, `return`, `if`, `static` | Cannot be used as ordinary identifier     | Keywords define syntax categories.                       |
| Ordinary identifier | Object, function, typedef, enum constant | `count`, `buffer_init`          | Ordinary identifier namespace             | Conflicts possible across scopes and linkage boundaries. |
| Tag name            | `struct`, `union`, or `enum` tag         | `struct buffer`                 | Separate tag namespace                    | `struct buffer` and `buffer` are different names.        |
| Member name         | Field inside a `struct` or `union`       | `buf->len`                      | Separate per-aggregate context            | Same member name may appear in different structs.        |
| Label               | Target for `goto`                        | `cleanup:`                      | Function-local label namespace            | Used in cleanup patterns but should be disciplined.      |
| Macro name          | Preprocessor identifier                  | `BUFFER_MAX`                    | Preprocessor namespace before compilation | Can collide with ordinary names textually.               |

Examples:

```c
struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

typedef struct buffer Buffer;

int buffer_init(Buffer *buf, size_t capacity);
void buffer_destroy(Buffer *buf);
```

Here, `struct buffer` is a tag name, `Buffer` is a typedef name in the ordinary identifier namespace, and `buffer_init`/`buffer_destroy` are ordinary identifiers with external linkage if defined that way.

Reserved identifier caution:

| Identifier form                              | Risk                                 | Reason                                            |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Begins with `_` followed by uppercase letter | Reserved                             | Reserved for implementation use.                  |
| Contains double underscore `__`              | Reserved                             | Reserved for implementation use.                  |
| Begins with `_` at file scope                | Reserved in many contexts            | May collide with implementation identifiers.      |
| Standard library names                       | Reserved in relevant headers/context | Defining conflicting names can break portability. |

Professional naming patterns:

| Naming pattern                     | Common use              | Example                                      | Why it helps                                 |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Prefix public symbols              | Library API names       | `buf_init`, `buf_destroy`                    | Avoids global symbol collisions.             |
| Use `snake_case`                   | Functions and variables | `read_count`                                 | Common C convention.                         |
| Use uppercase for macros/constants | Preprocessor constants  | `BUFFER_DEFAULT_CAPACITY`                    | Distinguishes macro substitution risk.       |
| Use `_t` carefully                 | Type names              | Avoid defining `foo_t` in POSIX-exposed code | POSIX reserves many `_t` names.              |
| Use verb-object for functions      | Operations              | `buffer_reserve`                             | Makes side effects clearer.                  |
| Use noun for structs               | Data concepts           | `struct buffer`                              | Separates data representation from behavior. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                    | Why it matters                                                   | Better rule                                                    |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using implementation-reserved identifiers                  | Can collide with compiler or standard library internals.         | Avoid leading underscores and double underscores in user code. |
| Defining generic global names                              | C has no package namespace for external symbols.                 | Prefix exported names with library/module prefix.              |
| Overusing typedefs to hide pointer-ness                    | `typedef struct foo *Foo;` can obscure ownership and mutability. | Prefer visible pointer syntax for APIs where lifetime matters. |
| Using macro names that collide with variables or functions | Macro substitution happens before type checking.                 | Give macros clear uppercase or project-prefixed names.         |

### Lexical Literals — integers, floating constants, character constants, string literals

C literals are source-level notations for values or arrays. Their types and representations are more subtle than their appearance suggests.

| Literal category   | Syntax                | Type behavior                                                                | Canonical example           | Common pitfall                                              |
| ------------------ | --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Decimal integer    | `123`                 | First fitting type among standard candidates                                 | `int n = 123;`              | Assuming all integer literals are `int`.                    |
| Octal integer      | Leading `0`           | Base 8                                                                       | `int mode = 0644;`          | Accidentally writing `010` when intending decimal ten.      |
| Hex integer        | `0x...`               | Base 16                                                                      | `unsigned mask = 0xffu;`    | Forgetting suffixes for unsigned/width-sensitive constants. |
| Binary integer     | `0b...` in C23        | Base 2 where supported                                                       | `unsigned flags = 0b1010u;` | Portability issue with older standards/toolchains.          |
| Floating constant  | `1.0`, `1e-3`, `1.0f` | Usually `double` unless suffixed                                             | `double x = 1.0;`           | Assuming decimal floating is exact.                         |
| Character constant | `'a'`, `'\n'`         | In C, ordinary character constants have type `int`                           | `int c = '\n';`             | Assuming same type behavior as C++.                         |
| String literal     | `"abc"`               | Array of `char` with null terminator; often used as pointer after conversion | `const char *s = "abc";`    | Attempting to modify a string literal.                      |
| Escape sequence    | Backslash notation    | Represents special character/code value                                      | `"\n\t"`                    | Confusing byte values, characters, and encoding.            |

Integer literal examples:

```c
int a = 42;
unsigned b = 42u;
long c = 42L;
unsigned long long d = 42ULL;

int octal = 010;      /* decimal 8 */
int hex = 0x10;       /* decimal 16 */
unsigned mask = 0xffu;
```

String literal examples:

```c
const char *name = "Ada";
char local[] = "Ada";
```

These two declarations are not equivalent.

| Declaration                 | Meaning                                        | Mutability of characters      | Storage implication                                                                      |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `const char *name = "Ada";` | Pointer to a string literal                    | Do not modify through pointer | Literal has static storage duration; pointer object is local if declared inside a block. |
| `char local[] = "Ada";`     | Local array initialized with copied characters | Array elements are modifiable | Array storage belongs to `local`; includes terminating `'\0'`.                           |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A string literal is just a writable `char *`.”                                                                                  |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `char *s = "abc"; s[0] = 'x';` may crash or exhibit undefined behavior.                                                          |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Attempting to modify a string literal has undefined behavior.                                                                    |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use `const char *` for string literals; use `char array[]` or allocated storage for mutable strings.                             |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some old compilers allowed writable string literals as an extension or legacy mode, but portable modern C must not rely on that. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                                                                               | Better rule                                                                         |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Leading zero in integer literals          | `010` is octal, not decimal ten.                                                             | Avoid leading zero except when intentionally using octal.                           |
| Assuming `sizeof("abc") == strlen("abc")` | `sizeof("abc")` includes the null terminator and is compile-time array size in this context. | Distinguish storage size from string length.                                        |
| Modifying string literals                 | Undefined behavior.                                                                          | Use `const char *` for literals.                                                    |
| Assuming character constants are `char`   | In C, ordinary character constants have type `int`.                                          | Be explicit when storing into `char` or comparing with `int` values from `getchar`. |
| Ignoring suffixes                         | Literal type affects conversions and overflow.                                               | Use suffixes such as `u`, `L`, `ULL` deliberately in width-sensitive code.          |

### Primitive Types Overview — scalar vocabulary before data modeling

C’s primitive types are the vocabulary for arithmetic, memory layout, and APIs. Their exact sizes are not uniformly fixed by ISO C, although relationships and minimum ranges are specified. Professional code often uses standard typedefs such as `size_t`, `ptrdiff_t`, and fixed-width integer types when appropriate.

| Type family            | Examples                                                        | Primary use                                         | Design meaning                                                    | Common pitfall                                                            |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Character types        | `char`, `signed char`, `unsigned char`                          | Bytes, character data, object representation access | `unsigned char` can inspect object representation                 | Assuming plain `char` is signed or unsigned portably.                     |
| Signed integers        | `short`, `int`, `long`, `long long`                             | Arithmetic with negative values                     | Natural machine-oriented arithmetic types                         | Signed overflow is undefined behavior.                                    |
| Unsigned integers      | `unsigned`, `size_t`, `uint32_t`                                | Sizes, bit operations, modular arithmetic           | Defined wraparound modulo arithmetic                              | Accidental underflow and signed/unsigned comparison bugs.                 |
| Floating types         | `float`, `double`, `long double`                                | Approximate real-number computation                 | Usually IEEE-like on modern platforms, but ISO C is more abstract | Expecting decimal exactness or identical behavior across all platforms.   |
| Boolean type           | `bool` from `<stdbool.h>` historically; C23 changes header need | Truth values                                        | Zero is false; nonzero is true                                    | Treating arbitrary integers and logical booleans interchangeably in APIs. |
| Void                   | `void`, `void *`                                                | No value; generic object pointer                    | `void *` supports generic storage/API patterns                    | Treating `void *` as preserving type information.                         |
| Null pointer constants | `NULL`, `0`, sometimes `nullptr` in C23                         | No valid object/function pointer                    | Represents absence of pointer target                              | Confusing null pointer with integer zero at representation level.         |

Examples:

```c
#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

size_t n = 10;          /* object size or count */
ptrdiff_t delta = -1;   /* pointer difference */
uint32_t flags = 0;     /* exactly 32-bit unsigned integer if available */
bool ok = true;
```

C integer types must be handled with discipline because their conversions and promotions can silently change meaning. A full treatment belongs in PART 3 and PART 7, but a source reader should already know this rule: **the spelling of an integer type is not enough; signedness, rank, width, and conversion context matter**.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                                                      | Better rule                                                             |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using `int` for sizes                         | Large objects may exceed `int`; negative values may not make sense. | Use `size_t` for object sizes and counts.                               |
| Comparing signed and unsigned values casually | Conversions can produce surprising results.                         | Align signedness deliberately before comparison.                        |
| Assuming `long` is 64-bit everywhere          | Data models differ across platforms.                                | Use `<stdint.h>` types when exact width matters.                        |
| Assuming unsigned means “safe integer”        | Unsigned wraparound is defined but often still a bug.               | Use unsigned for bit patterns and sizes, not as a generic overflow fix. |
| Treating `void *` as dynamic typing           | C does not attach runtime type tags to `void *`.                    | Maintain explicit contracts about the pointed-to type.                  |

### Declarations as a Reading Skill — declaration specifiers, declarators, and initialization

C declarations are one of the language’s most important source-reading challenges. A declaration generally combines **declaration specifiers** with one or more **declarators**.

A simple declaration:

```c
const unsigned long count = 10;
```

Here, `const unsigned long` supplies specifiers, and `count = 10` is the declarator plus initializer.

A more complex declaration:

```c
int *p, a[10], f(void);
```

This declares:

| Declarator | Meaning                                                     |
| ---------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| `*p`       | `p` is a pointer to `int`.                                  |
| `a[10]`    | `a` is an array of 10 `int`.                                |
| `f(void)`  | `f` is a function taking no parameters and returning `int`. |

The declaration specifier `int` applies to all declarators, but each declarator has its own pointer/array/function structure. This is a common source of errors.

| Construct            | Meaning                          | Syntax               | Canonical example | Design meaning                                                                | Practical consequence                                   | Common pitfall                                       |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Object declaration   | Introduces an object             | `type name;`         | `int count;`      | Name is associated with typed storage.                                        | May be uninitialized for automatic storage.             | Reading an uninitialized local object.               |
| Initialization       | Gives initial value              | `type name = value;` | `int count = 0;`  | Initialization is distinct from later assignment.                             | Initialization rules vary by storage duration and type. | Assuming local variables default to zero.            |
| Multiple declarators | Several names in one declaration | `int a, *p;`         | `int x, y;`       | Specifiers shared; declarator syntax differs per name.                        | Compact but easy to misread.                            | Thinking `int* a, b;` makes both pointers.           |
| Pointer declarator   | Declares pointer object          | `type *name;`        | `int *p;`         | Pointer is an object whose value points to another object or is null/invalid. | Pointer validity is separate from pointer type.         | Confusing pointer declaration style with ownership.  |
| Array declarator     | Declares array object            | `type name[n];`      | `int a[10];`      | Array contains contiguous elements.                                           | Often converts to pointer in expressions.               | Saying arrays “are” pointers.                        |
| Function declarator  | Declares function                | `ret name(params);`  | `int f(void);`    | Function type participates in calls and linkage.                              | Declarations can appear before definitions.             | Omitting prototypes or using old-style declarations. |

Declaration reading examples:

```c
int *p;          /* p is pointer to int */
int a[10];       /* a is array of 10 int */
int (*pa)[10];   /* pa is pointer to array of 10 int */
int *ap[10];     /* ap is array of 10 pointers to int */
int (*fp)(int);  /* fp is pointer to function taking int and returning int */
```

A practical reading rule is: **start at the identifier and read outward according to C declarator binding**. Parentheses override the default binding.

| Declaration     | Read as                                        | Practical use                                                      |
| --------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `int *p`        | `p` is pointer to `int`                        | Mutable access to one or more `int` objects depending on contract. |
| `int a[10]`     | `a` is array of 10 `int`                       | Fixed-size contiguous storage.                                     |
| `int *a[10]`    | `a` is array of 10 pointers to `int`           | Table of object pointers.                                          |
| `int (*p)[10]`  | `p` is pointer to array of 10 `int`            | Passing fixed-width arrays.                                        |
| `int f(int)`    | `f` is function from `int` to `int`            | Function declaration/prototype.                                    |
| `int (*f)(int)` | `f` is pointer to function from `int` to `int` | Callback or dispatch slot.                                         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters                                                  | Better rule                                                 |
| --------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Writing `int* a, b;` and thinking both are pointers | Only `a` is a pointer; `b` is an `int`.                         | Prefer one declaration per line when pointers are involved. |
| Treating declaration syntax as ownership syntax     | `int *p` says type shape, not who owns the pointed-to object.   | Document ownership separately.                              |
| Omitting initialization for automatic objects       | Local automatic objects are indeterminate unless initialized.   | Initialize variables when possible.                         |
| Confusing pointer-to-array with array-of-pointers   | Parentheses change meaning.                                     | Read from the identifier outward.                           |
| Using old-style function declarations               | They weaken type checking and are obsolete for modern practice. | Use prototypes such as `int f(void);`.                      |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “The `*` belongs to the type specifier, so `int* a, b;` declares two pointers.”               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `b` is an `int`, not an `int *`, causing type errors or incorrect assumptions.                |
| Correct semantic explanation    | In C, pointer syntax is part of each declarator, not solely the shared declaration specifier. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Declare one pointer per declaration or place `*` near the identifier to reflect the grammar.  |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Style conventions vary, but the grammar does not.                                             |

### Storage-Class and Type Qualifier Syntax — `static`, `extern`, `auto`, `register`, `const`, `volatile`, `restrict`, `_Atomic`

Some declaration specifiers affect type; others affect storage duration, linkage, or optimization assumptions. Their full meaning belongs in PART 5 and PART 7, but their syntax must be recognizable early.

| Specifier or qualifier  | Basic meaning                                                                     | Example                       | Primary semantic area                     | Common pitfall                                                                         |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `static` at block scope | Static storage duration local object                                              | `static int count;`           | Lifetime and initialization               | Confusing persistent local state with thread safety.                                   |
| `static` at file scope  | Internal linkage                                                                  | `static void helper(void);`   | Visibility/linkage                        | Forgetting it and exporting private symbols.                                           |
| `extern`                | Declaration referring to external definition                                      | `extern int global_count;`    | Linkage                                   | Defining globals in headers accidentally.                                              |
| `auto`                  | Automatic storage duration; mostly obsolete as explicit spelling in older C usage | `auto int x;`                 | Storage duration                          | Rarely useful in traditional C; C23 changes around `auto` require toolchain awareness. |
| `register`              | Historical optimization hint; address-taking restricted in traditional semantics  | `register int i;`             | Storage hint/legacy                       | Assuming modern compilers need it.                                                     |
| `const`                 | Object not modifiable through this access path                                    | `const int n = 3;`            | Type qualification/API contract           | Assuming deep or absolute immutability.                                                |
| `volatile`              | Access has externally observable behavior                                         | `volatile sig_atomic_t flag;` | Optimization boundary for special objects | Misusing it for thread synchronization.                                                |
| `restrict`              | Pointer is intended as sole initial access path for an object in a scope          | `void f(int *restrict p);`    | Aliasing contract                         | Violating the promise causes undefined behavior.                                       |
| `_Atomic`               | Atomic object/type qualifier                                                      | `_Atomic int x;`              | Concurrency memory model                  | Assuming atomic alone creates a correct concurrent design.                             |

Examples:

```c
static int internal_counter;          /* file-scope internal linkage */

extern int shared_counter;            /* declaration, definition elsewhere */

void scale(size_t n,
           double *restrict out,
           const double *restrict in,
           double factor);
```

In the `scale` example, `restrict` communicates a no-aliasing promise that may enable optimization. It is not merely documentation. If the caller violates the promise, behavior can become undefined.

`const` examples:

```c
const int *p;        /* pointer to const int: *p not modifiable through p */
int *const q = NULL; /* const pointer to int: q itself not assignable */
const int *const r = NULL; /* const pointer to const int */
```

| Declaration          | Meaning                                               |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `const int *p`       | `p` may change; `*p` may not be modified through `p`. |
| `int *const p`       | `p` may not change; `*p` may be modified.             |
| `const int *const p` | Neither `p` nor `*p` may be modified through `p`.     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                                                                  | Better rule                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Thinking `const` means globally immutable        | Another non-const access path may modify the same object.                       | Treat `const` as an access-path and API promise.                         |
| Using `volatile` for thread communication        | `volatile` does not provide atomicity or inter-thread synchronization in ISO C. | Use `_Atomic` or proper synchronization primitives.                      |
| Applying `static` without checking context       | `static` has different meanings at file scope and block scope.                  | Interpret `static` based on declaration location.                        |
| Violating `restrict` assumptions                 | The compiler may optimize based on non-aliasing.                                | Use `restrict` only when the aliasing contract is true and documented.   |
| Defining `extern` objects in headers incorrectly | Multiple definitions or ODR-like build failures in C terms.                     | Put declarations in headers and exactly one definition in a source file. |

### Expressions and Operators — value computation, side effects, precedence, and conversions

C expressions compute values and may produce side effects. The surface form of an expression does not fully determine evaluation order, type conversions, or definedness. This is one of the most important source-reading cautions in C.

| Operator category      | Examples                         | Meaning                                      | Practical consequence                                                | Common pitfall                                       |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | - | -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Arithmetic             | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`          | Numeric operations after conversions         | Integer promotions and signedness matter.                            | Signed overflow UB; integer division truncation.     |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Assignment             | `=`, `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, etc.      | Store value in modifiable lvalue             | Assignment is an expression with side effect.                        | Confusing assignment `=` with equality `==`.         |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Comparison             | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | Produces `int` result `0` or `1`             | Common in conditions.                                                | Signed/unsigned comparison surprises.                |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Logical                | `&&`, `                          |                                              | `, `!`                                                               | Boolean-style logic with short-circuit for `&&`/`    |                                                                     | ` | Controls conditional evaluation. | Assuming bitwise and logical operators are interchangeable. |
| Bitwise                | `&`, `                           | `, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`                      | Bit-level operations                                                 | Useful for flags and masks.                          | Shifting by invalid amounts or shifting negative values carelessly. |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Increment/decrement    | `++`, `--`                       | Modify object by one                         | Compact loop idiom.                                                  | Multiple unsequenced modifications.                  |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Member access          | `.`, `->`                        | Access struct/union member                   | `p->x` means `(*p).x`.                                               | Using `.` on pointer or `->` on object.              |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Address/dereference    | `&`, `*`                         | Take address; access pointed-to object       | Foundation of pointer programming.                                   | Dereferencing null, invalid, or misaligned pointer.  |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Array subscript        | `a[i]`                           | Defined as `*(a + i)` after conversions      | Depends on valid pointer arithmetic.                                 | Out-of-bounds access.                                |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Function call          | `f(args)`                        | Call function designator with arguments      | Arguments passed by value.                                           | Assuming argument evaluation order is left-to-right. |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Conditional            | `cond ? a : b`                   | Select expression by condition               | Expression-level branching.                                          | Type conversions between branches.                   |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Comma                  | `e1, e2`                         | Evaluate first then second; result is second | Rare but useful in controlled contexts.                              | Confusing comma operator with comma separators.      |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| Cast                   | `(type)expr`                     | Explicit conversion                          | Boundary or low-level operation.                                     | Silencing warnings and creating UB.                  |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| `sizeof`               | `sizeof expr` or `sizeof(type)`  | Size in bytes as `size_t`                    | Usually compile-time except variable length arrays in relevant modes | Applying to pointer when array size was intended.    |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |
| `_Alignof` / `alignof` | Alignment requirement            | `alignof(int)` in modern spelling contexts   | Alignment-sensitive programming                                      | Confusing alignment with size.                       |                                                                     |   |                                  |                                                             |

Precedence matters, but professional C programmers should not rely on readers memorizing every precedence level. Parentheses are cheap documentation.

| Expression          | Parses as                                                  | Better when unclear                                       |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `*p++`              | `*(p++)`                                                   | `*(p++)`                                                  |
| `*p[i]`             | `*(p[i])`                                                  | Use explicit grouping if type is non-obvious.             |
| `a & b == 0`        | `a & (b == 0)` because `==` binds tighter than bitwise `&` | `(a & b) == 0`                                            |
| `x = y == z`        | `x = (y == z)`                                             | Use separate statements if assignment is not intentional. |
| `flags & MASK != 0` | `flags & (MASK != 0)`                                      | `(flags & MASK) != 0`                                     |

Side effects and sequencing:

```c
i = i + 1;     /* clear */
++i;           /* clear */
a[i++] = i;    /* dangerous: evaluation-order issues depending on expression form */
```

The exact rules are more technical and belong in PART 7, but the early rule is: **avoid expressions that both modify and read the same scalar object in complex ways unless the sequencing is obvious and guaranteed**.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                  |   |                                                                              |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | - | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C evaluates expressions left to right because that is how I read them.”                                                     |   |                                                                              |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Function arguments or subexpressions appear to execute in different orders under different compilers or optimization levels. |   |                                                                              |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C specifies some sequencing rules but leaves many evaluation orders unspecified.                                             |   |                                                                              |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Do not put correctness-critical side effects in evaluation-order-sensitive positions.                                        |   |                                                                              |
| Boundary where the rule changes | `&&`, `                                                                                                                      |   | `, comma operator, and `?:` impose specific sequencing for certain operands. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                                                 | Better rule                                                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Confusing bitwise and logical operators                 | `&` and `&&` have different semantics and evaluation behavior. | Use bitwise only for bit patterns; logical for truth conditions. |
| Assuming function arguments are evaluated left-to-right | C does not generally guarantee that.                           | Split side effects into separate statements.                     |
| Ignoring integer promotions                             | Small integer types often promote to `int`.                    | Analyze expression type, not only variable type.                 |
| Using assignment inside conditions accidentally         | `if (x = y)` assigns.                                          | Enable warnings; write `if (x == y)` for comparison.             |
| Trusting precedence in non-obvious expressions          | Readers and maintainers may misread it.                        | Parenthesize intent.                                             |
| Applying `sizeof` to a pointer instead of an array      | Gives pointer size, not pointed-to allocation size.            | Track lengths explicitly.                                        |

### Lvalues, Modifiable Lvalues, and Object Identity — names, storage, and assignability

C expressions are not merely “values.” Some expressions designate objects. Historically, the term `lvalue` refers to an expression that identifies an object or function. A **modifiable lvalue** is an lvalue that can be assigned to.

| Concept                | Meaning in C                                        | Example                 | Practical consequence                                             |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Object                 | Region of data storage representing a value         | `int x;`                | Has type, storage duration, alignment, and lifetime.              |
| Lvalue                 | Expression designating an object or function        | `x`, `a[i]`, `p->field` | Can often be read; may or may not be assignable.                  |
| Modifiable lvalue      | Lvalue that may appear on left side of assignment   | `x = 3;`                | Excludes arrays, `const` objects, and non-modifiable expressions. |
| Rvalue-like expression | Expression value not designating assignable storage | `x + 1`, `42`           | Cannot assign to it.                                              |
| Identity               | Object identity through storage location/lifetime   | `&x`                    | Two expressions may access the same object via aliases.           |

Examples:

```c
int x = 1;
int y = 2;

x = 3;        /* x is a modifiable lvalue */
x + y = 5;    /* invalid: x + y is not assignable */
```

Arrays are lvalues but not modifiable lvalues as whole objects:

```c
int a[3] = {1, 2, 3};
int b[3] = {4, 5, 6};

/* a = b; */  /* invalid: arrays are not assignable */
a[0] = b[0];  /* valid: array element is modifiable */
```

`const` affects modifiability through the expression:

```c
const int c = 10;
/* c = 11; */     /* invalid */

int x = 10;
const int *p = &x;
/* *p = 11; */    /* invalid through p */
x = 11;           /* valid through non-const object name */
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                   | Why it matters                                                          | Better rule                                                             |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Thinking every expression has assignable storage          | Many expressions compute values without designating assignable objects. | Distinguish value computation from object designation.                  |
| Trying to assign arrays                                   | Arrays are not assignable as whole objects in C.                        | Use element-wise copy or `memcpy` when appropriate and valid.           |
| Assuming `const` object and pointer-to-const are the same | They express different constraints.                                     | Read the declarator precisely.                                          |
| Ignoring aliasing                                         | Multiple lvalues may designate the same object.                         | Consider whether mutation through one name affects another access path. |

### Assignment and Initialization — different operations with different rules

Initialization gives an object its initial value at the point its lifetime begins. Assignment stores a new value into an existing modifiable object. These are not the same operation.

| Operation           | Meaning                                                                          | Syntax                 | Example                                                           | Practical consequence                                                        |
| ------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Initialization      | Establish initial value as object is created                                     | `type name = init;`    | `int x = 0;`                                                      | May use initializer rules specific to aggregate/scalar type.                 |
| Assignment          | Modify existing object                                                           | `lhs = rhs;`           | `x = 1;`                                                          | Requires modifiable lvalue.                                                  |
| Compound assignment | Read-modify-write with conversion rules                                          | `lhs += rhs;`          | `x += 2;`                                                         | Not always identical to `lhs = lhs + rhs` because of conversions/evaluation. |
| Zero initialization | Static/thread objects initialized to zero-like values if no explicit initializer | `static int x;`        | `x` starts as `0`                                                 | Does not apply to uninitialized automatic local objects.                     |
| Indeterminate value | Uninitialized automatic object value                                             | `int x;` then read `x` | Reading may be undefined or unspecified depending on type/context | Initialize before reading.                                                   |

Examples:

```c
int x = 0;  /* initialization */
x = 5;      /* assignment */

const int c = 10; /* initialization allowed */
/* c = 20; */     /* assignment not allowed */
```

Automatic vs static initialization:

```c
static int global_count; /* initialized to 0 */

void f(void)
{
    int local_count;     /* indeterminate value */
    int safe_count = 0;  /* initialized */
}
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                           |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C probably initializes local variables to zero like some higher-level environments.”                 |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A local variable has a seemingly random value, or optimization assumes impossible paths.              |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Automatic storage objects without initialization have indeterminate values; reading them is not safe. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Initialize local variables before use, preferably at declaration when meaningful.                     |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Static and thread storage duration objects are zero-initialized if not explicitly initialized.        |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                                             | Better rule                                                            |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Reading uninitialized locals                           | Can cause undefined behavior or unstable program behavior. | Initialize before first read.                                          |
| Confusing initialization with assignment for `const`   | `const` objects may be initialized but not assigned later. | Treat initialization as object creation, assignment as later mutation. |
| Assuming compound assignment has obvious type behavior | Conversions still apply.                                   | Be careful with small integer types and signedness.                    |
| Assuming all storage durations initialize the same way | Automatic local objects differ from static objects.        | Always identify storage duration.                                      |
### Blocks, Statements, and Sequencing Surface — expression statements, compound statements, null statements

C code is organized into **statements**. A statement may perform computation, control flow, declaration-like work inside a block, or no operation at all. A **compound statement** creates a block using `{ ... }`, and blocks are central to scope, lifetime, and readable control flow.

| Statement form           | Meaning                                              | Syntax             | Canonical example        | Design meaning                              | Practical consequence                                         | Common pitfall                                                  |
| ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Expression statement     | Evaluate expression for side effect or value discard | `expr;`            | `x++;`                   | C permits expressions as statements.        | Function calls and assignments commonly appear here.          | Writing an expression whose value is discarded unintentionally. |
| Compound statement       | Sequence of declarations/statements in a block       | `{ ... }`          | `{ int x = 0; use(x); }` | Block creates local scope.                  | Automatic objects may begin/end lifetime at block entry/exit. | Omitting braces in control-flow bodies.                         |
| Null statement           | Statement that does nothing                          | `;`                | `while (*p++) ;`         | Useful but easy to create accidentally.     | Sometimes used in empty loop bodies.                          | Accidental semicolon after `if` or `while`.                     |
| Declaration inside block | Introduces local name                                | `type name;`       | `int i = 0;`             | C allows local declarations.                | Scope starts at declaration point.                            | Assuming name is visible before declaration.                    |
| Labeled statement        | Statement with label                                 | `label: statement` | `cleanup: free(p);`      | Enables `goto` targets and `switch` labels. | Useful for cleanup patterns.                                  | Spaghetti control flow.                                         |

Example:

```c id="6tkj4e"
int parse_record(const char *s)
{
    int status = -1;

    if (s == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    {
        int local_state = 0;
        local_state += s[0] != '\0';
        status = local_state;
    }

    return status;
}
```

The inner block creates a narrower scope for `local_state`. In C, narrowing scope is not merely style; it can reduce accidental reuse, shadowing, and lifetime confusion.

Null statement trap:

```c id="yb4v80"
if (ready);
{
    run_task();
}
```

This always executes `run_task()`. The semicolon after `if (ready)` is a complete null statement; the following block is not the body of the `if`.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                                   | Better rule                                                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Accidental null statement after `if`, `while`, or `for` | Control flow no longer guards the intended body. | Enable warnings such as `-Wempty-body`; use braces consistently. |
| Omitting braces around single-statement bodies          | Later edits can silently change behavior.        | Prefer braces for all nontrivial control-flow bodies.            |
| Declaring variables too early                           | Increases accidental reuse and weakens locality. | Declare near first meaningful initialization.                    |
| Shadowing names casually                                | Makes code review and debugging harder.          | Use narrow scopes but avoid misleading duplicate names.          |

### Scope, Name Visibility, and Lifetime Basics — block scope, file scope, function prototype scope

C uses several scopes. Scope determines **where a name can be used**. Scope is not the same as storage duration, linkage, or lifetime, although they interact.

| Scope category           | Applies to                                  | Example              | Name visible where                          | Common pitfall                                                    |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Block scope              | Local variables, parameters, local typedefs | `int x;` inside `{}` | From declaration to end of block            | Assuming visibility before declaration.                           |
| File scope               | File-level declarations                     | `static int count;`  | From declaration to end of translation unit | Assuming file scope means globally visible; linkage also matters. |
| Function prototype scope | Parameter names in prototypes               | `int f(int n);`      | Only within prototype declaration           | Thinking prototype parameter names define variables.              |
| Function scope           | Labels                                      | `cleanup:`           | Entire function body for `goto` labels      | Jumping across initialization/lifetime boundaries carelessly.     |

Example:

```c id="2lipfw"
int file_scope_object = 1;

void f(int parameter)
{
    int x = parameter;

    if (x > 0) {
        int y = x + 1;
        x = y;
    }

    /* y is not visible here */
}
```

A name’s **scope** answers “where can this name be written?” Its **lifetime** answers “when does the object exist?” Its **linkage** answers “whether the same name in another scope or translation unit denotes the same entity.”

| Concept          | Question answered                                                                | Example                                                       |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Scope            | Where is the name visible?                                                       | `y` visible only inside the `if` block.                       |
| Lifetime         | When does the object exist?                                                      | Automatic `y` exists while its block is executing.            |
| Linkage          | Can declarations in different scopes/translation units refer to the same entity? | `extern int errno_like;` may refer to an external definition. |
| Storage duration | How long storage persists?                                                       | `static int count;` persists for program duration.            |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                       |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “If a variable name is not visible, the object must no longer exist; if it is visible, the object must still be valid.”           |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A pointer to a local object escapes its block and is later dereferenced.                                                          |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Scope is about name visibility; lifetime is about object existence. Pointers can outlive the scope of a name and become dangling. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Always reason separately about name scope, object lifetime, linkage, and ownership.                                               |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Static storage duration objects may outlive their local name scope when declared as block-scope `static`.                         |

Example of block-scope static:

```c id="udq9pk"
int next_id(void)
{
    static int id = 0;
    id += 1;
    return id;
}
```

The name `id` has block scope, but the object has static storage duration. It persists across calls. This is useful but creates hidden state and possible thread-safety concerns.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                        | Why it matters                                                                                | Better rule                                                                            |
| ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Confusing block scope with automatic lifetime  | `static` local objects have block scope but static storage duration.                          | Treat scope and storage duration separately.                                           |
| Returning addresses of automatic local objects | The name disappears and the object lifetime ends.                                             | Return by value, write into caller-provided storage, or allocate with clear ownership. |
| Assuming file-scope names are private          | File-scope declarations may have external linkage unless declared `static` where appropriate. | Use file-scope `static` for private functions/objects.                                 |
| Using global names before declarations         | C requires declarations before use in modern practice.                                        | Put declarations in headers or earlier file-scope prototypes.                          |

### Selection Statements — `if`, `else`, `switch`, labels, fallthrough

Selection statements choose among control paths. In C, the condition expression is interpreted by comparing to zero: zero is false, nonzero is true.

| Construct   | Meaning                                     | Syntax                          | Canonical example                    | Design meaning                                    | Practical consequence                            | Common pitfall                                     |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `if`        | Conditional branch                          | `if (expr) stmt`                | `if (p == NULL) return -1;`          | Any scalar expression can act as condition.       | C does not require a Boolean type in conditions. | Accidental assignment in condition.                |
| `if`/`else` | Two-way branch                              | `if (expr) stmt else stmt`      | `if (ok) use(); else fail();`        | `else` binds to nearest unmatched `if`.           | Braces improve readability.                      | Dangling-`else` ambiguity in visual layout.        |
| `switch`    | Multi-way branch by integer-like expression | `switch (expr) { case C: ... }` | `switch (state) { case READY: ... }` | Case labels are jump labels, not scoped branches. | `break` usually needed.                          | Accidental fallthrough.                            |
| `case`      | Label in switch                             | `case constant:`                | `case 0:`                            | Must be integer constant expression.              | Cases do not create blocks automatically.        | Declaring variables across labels incorrectly.     |
| `default`   | Fallback label                              | `default:`                      | `default: return -1;`                | Handles unmatched values.                         | Important for defensive handling.                | Omitting default when unknown values are possible. |

`if` example:

```c id="38wd1o"
if (count == 0) {
    return 0;
} else {
    return process(count);
}
```

`switch` example:

```c id="i69xp9"
enum parser_state {
    STATE_START,
    STATE_BODY,
    STATE_DONE,
    STATE_ERROR
};

int handle_state(enum parser_state st)
{
    switch (st) {
    case STATE_START:
        return 1;

    case STATE_BODY:
        return 2;

    case STATE_DONE:
        return 0;

    case STATE_ERROR:
    default:
        return -1;
    }
}
```

Fallthrough can be intentional, but it must be explicit in style and review.

```c id="y2wsm2"
switch (ch) {
case ' ':
case '\t':
case '\n':
    return TOKEN_SPACE;

default:
    return TOKEN_OTHER;
}
```

This fallthrough is idiomatic: several cases share one body.

Risky fallthrough:

```c id="ag0p95"
switch (mode) {
case MODE_READ:
    open_for_read();
/* missing break? */
case MODE_WRITE:
    open_for_write();
    break;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                                                           |
| --------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `if (x = y)` instead of `if (x == y)`         | Assignment occurs; condition tests assigned value.       | Enable warnings; use clear comparison expressions.                    |
| Dangling `else`                               | `else` binds to nearest unmatched `if`, not indentation. | Use braces to show intended structure.                                |
| Missing `break` in `switch`                   | Control falls through to next case.                      | Mark intentional fallthrough clearly; enable fallthrough warnings.    |
| Assuming `case` creates scope                 | It does not.                                             | Add braces around case bodies when declaring variables.               |
| Omitting handling of invalid enum-like values | C enums do not prevent invalid integer values.           | Include defensive `default` where external or unchecked values enter. |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                  |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A `switch` case behaves like an isolated branch in a high-level pattern match.”             |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Execution continues into the next case after a matched case.                                 |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `case` labels are labels inside one switch body; they do not automatically break.            |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use `break`, `return`, or clearly documented intentional fallthrough in every nonempty case. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Stacked empty cases are a common idiom for grouping multiple values.                         |

### Iteration Statements — `while`, `do`, `for`, loop control

C has three loop forms. The language does not provide high-level iterators, generators, or `foreach` in ISO C; iteration is expressed through counters, pointers, conditions, and explicit control flow.

| Construct    | Meaning                                 | Syntax                          | Canonical use                      | Design meaning                          | Common pitfall                                     |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `while`      | Pre-test loop                           | `while (cond) stmt`             | Read/process until condition fails | Condition checked before body.          | Infinite loop due to missing state update.         |
| `do`/`while` | Post-test loop                          | `do stmt while (cond);`         | Body must run at least once        | Condition checked after body.           | Forgetting trailing semicolon.                     |
| `for`        | Compact loop with init/condition/update | `for (init; cond; update) stmt` | Counted loops, pointer walks       | All three clauses optional.             | Modifying loop variable unpredictably inside body. |
| `break`      | Exit nearest loop or switch             | `break;`                        | Stop early on found item           | Local escape.                           | Breaking only inner loop when outer was intended.  |
| `continue`   | Skip to next iteration                  | `continue;`                     | Skip invalid item                  | In `for`, update expression still runs. | Misreading effect in `while` vs `for`.             |

Counted loop:

```c id="3rc2m3"
for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    total += a[i];
}
```

Pointer loop:

```c id="gr4m07"
for (const unsigned char *p = buf; p != end; p++) {
    checksum += *p;
}
```

Search loop:

```c id="sq390x"
size_t find_byte(const unsigned char *buf, size_t n, unsigned char target)
{
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (buf[i] == target) {
            return i;
        }
    }

    return n; /* sentinel: not found */
}
```

A `for` statement does not require all clauses:

```c id="y2rv0u"
for (;;) {
    if (should_stop()) {
        break;
    }
    run_once();
}
```

This is an intentional infinite loop with explicit exit.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                 | Why it matters                                                              | Better rule                                                   |
| --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Off-by-one errors                       | Array bounds are not checked by the language.                               | Prefer loop forms that visibly use `i < n`, not `i <= n - 1`. |
| Unsigned countdown bugs                 | `size_t i >= 0` is always true.                                             | Use alternative countdown forms for unsigned values.          |
| Pointer loop beyond valid range         | Pointer arithmetic is only defined within array-object bounds and one-past. | Maintain explicit begin/end contracts.                        |
| Forgetting `continue` behavior in `for` | `continue` jumps to update expression before condition check.               | Keep update expression simple.                                |
| Mutating loop bound unexpectedly        | Can cause skipped elements or out-of-bounds access.                         | Separate traversal from mutation when possible.               |

Unsigned countdown example:

```c id="i43zyi"
/* Risky: i is unsigned; i >= 0 is always true */
for (size_t i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    use(i);
}
```

Safer idiom:

```c id="q8sx07"
for (size_t i = n; i > 0; i--) {
    use(i - 1);
}
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Unsigned integers are just nonnegative integers, so `i >= 0` is a good loop guard.”                       |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | The loop never terminates after `i--` wraps from `0` to a large value.                                     |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Unsigned arithmetic is modulo arithmetic; `size_t` can never be negative.                                  |
| Professional rule of thumb      | For reverse loops over `size_t`, write `for (size_t i = n; i > 0; i--) use(i - 1);`.                       |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Signed indices can express negative sentinel values but introduce their own overflow and conversion risks. |

### Jump Statements — `return`, `goto`, `break`, `continue`

C has explicit jump statements. Used carefully, they make error handling and cleanup clearer. Used casually, they make control flow difficult to reason about.

| Statement  | Meaning                              | Syntax          | Canonical use                | Professional rule                                                          |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------ | --------------- | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `return`   | Exit function, optionally with value | `return expr;`  | Return result or error code  | Use early returns for simple guard failures; ensure resources are handled. |
| `break`    | Exit nearest loop or switch          | `break;`        | End loop or switch case      | Keep nesting shallow enough that target is obvious.                        |
| `continue` | Continue nearest loop                | `continue;`     | Skip current iteration       | Avoid when it hides essential cleanup or state update.                     |
| `goto`     | Jump to label in same function       | `goto cleanup;` | Centralized cleanup on error | Restrict mostly to forward jumps to cleanup labels.                        |

Return examples:

```c id="2azyas"
int is_empty(const struct buffer *buf)
{
    if (buf == NULL) {
        return 1;
    }

    return buf->len == 0;
}
```

Cleanup with `goto`:

```c id="1vpc2j"
int build_resource(void)
{
    char *a = NULL;
    char *b = NULL;
    int rc = -1;

    a = malloc(128);
    if (a == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    b = malloc(256);
    if (b == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    rc = 0;

cleanup:
    free(b);
    free(a);
    return rc;
}
```

This pattern is idiomatic C when multiple resources require cleanup and there is no native RAII. The `goto` is not being used for arbitrary control flow; it is used to preserve a single cleanup path.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                    | Why it matters                                                | Better rule                                                                   |
| ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Early return after acquiring resources     | Can leak resources.                                           | Use cleanup labels or structured cleanup before return.                       |
| Arbitrary `goto` jumps                     | Destroy local reasoning and can skip required initialization. | Restrict `goto` to disciplined cleanup/error paths.                           |
| `break` in nested loops                    | Exits only the innermost loop or switch.                      | Use helper functions, flags, or labeled cleanup when logic becomes nested.    |
| `continue` skipping required state updates | Can create infinite loops or inconsistent state.              | Keep update logic before `continue` or use `for` update clause intentionally. |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “`goto` is always an anti-pattern.”                                                                |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Code without `goto` duplicates cleanup logic and forgets one error path.                           |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C lacks native RAII and exceptions; a forward `goto cleanup` can centralize resource release.      |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Avoid arbitrary jumps, but allow disciplined cleanup labels in resource-owning functions.          |
| Boundary where the rule changes | In trivial functions with no acquired resources, early returns may be clearer than cleanup labels. |

### Function Syntax Basics — prototypes, definitions, parameters, return types

Functions are C’s primary behavior abstraction. A **function declaration** tells the compiler a function’s type. A **function definition** supplies the body. Modern C practice uses prototypes.

| Function form         | Meaning                                    | Syntax                      | Canonical example           | Practical consequence                          | Common pitfall                                                   |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Prototype declaration | Declares function interface                | `ret name(params);`         | `int parse(const char *s);` | Enables type checking before definition.       | Omitting prototypes or using obsolete declarations.              |
| Definition            | Provides function body                     | `ret name(params) { ... }`  | `int f(void) { return 0; }` | Emits function definition in translation unit. | Defining non-`static` helper functions unintentionally exported. |
| No-parameter function | Function taking no arguments               | `int f(void);`              | `int main(void)`            | `void` explicitly means no parameters.         | Writing `int f();` in old-style meaning.                         |
| Return type           | Type of returned value                     | `int`, `void`, pointer type | `void cleanup(void);`       | Caller depends on contract.                    | Returning pointer to invalid storage.                            |
| Parameter             | Local object initialized by argument value | `int n`, `char *p`          | `void g(int n)`             | Arguments are passed by value.                 | Thinking parameter reassignment changes caller variable.         |

Function declaration and definition:

```c id="n99bd9"
int add(int a, int b);

int add(int a, int b)
{
    return a + b;
}
```

No-argument function:

```c id="wgyadp"
int get_status(void);
```

This is the correct prototype for a function that takes no arguments. In older C syntax, `int get_status();` did not provide the same parameter checking meaning and should be avoided in modern code.

`void` return:

```c id="hf9vpr"
void buffer_clear(struct buffer *buf)
{
    buf->len = 0;
}
```

Function parameters are passed by value:

```c id="u2roeh"
void set_to_zero(int x)
{
    x = 0;
}

int n = 5;
set_to_zero(n);
/* n is still 5 */
```

To mutate caller-owned storage, pass a pointer:

```c id="pqz7yo"
void set_to_zero(int *p)
{
    if (p != NULL) {
        *p = 0;
    }
}
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
