| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Passing a variable to a function lets the function modify that variable.”                                                                                                                 |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A function assigns to its parameter, but the caller’s variable is unchanged.                                                                                                               |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C passes arguments by value; the parameter is a separate object initialized from the argument.                                                                                             |
| Professional rule of thumb      | To let a function modify caller-owned data, pass a pointer and document the pointer contract.                                                                                              |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Passing a pointer by value still copies the pointer; the function can mutate the pointed-to object but cannot change the caller’s pointer variable unless given a pointer to that pointer. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                         | Why it matters                                                            | Better rule                                                                               |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using `int f();` instead of `int f(void);`      | Weakens or changes parameter checking depending on standard mode/context. | Use explicit prototypes.                                                                  |
| Ignoring return values                          | Many C functions signal failure by return value.                          | Mark/check fallible calls; use compiler warnings.                                         |
| Returning addresses of local variables          | Dangling pointer.                                                         | Return by value, allocate with ownership contract, or use caller-provided output storage. |
| Forgetting `static` on private helper functions | Accidental external linkage.                                              | Use file-scope `static` for internal helpers.                                             |
| Assuming pointer parameter cannot be null       | C type does not express non-null by default.                              | Document and/or check pointer validity.                                                   |

### `main` Function Forms — hosted program entry point

In hosted C environments, `main` has special status as the program entry point. ISO C permits specific forms.

| Form                        | Meaning                                    | Example                            | Use                            |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| No command-line parameters  | Program does not inspect arguments         | `int main(void)`                   | Preferred for simple programs. |
| Command-line parameters     | Program receives argument count and vector | `int main(int argc, char *argv[])` | Command-line tools.            |
| Equivalent pointer spelling | `argv` as pointer to pointer               | `int main(int argc, char **argv)`  | Same adjusted parameter type.  |

Examples:

```c id="hjg4mp"
int main(void)
{
    return 0;
}
```

```c id="tg27k6"
int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
    for (int i = 0; i < argc; i++) {
        puts(argv[i]);
    }

    return 0;
}
```

`argv` strings are conventionally null-terminated character arrays supplied by the host environment. Their exact encoding and command-line rules are platform concerns, not purely ISO C semantics.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                                                | Better rule                                                   |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming `argv[1]` exists                    | Program may be invoked with no extra arguments.               | Check `argc` before indexing `argv`.                          |
| Assuming command-line encoding is portable   | Encoding and shell parsing are platform/environment-specific. | Treat command-line text as external input.                    |
| Returning arbitrary unportable status values | Host environment interprets status codes.                     | Use `0`, `EXIT_SUCCESS`, or `EXIT_FAILURE` where appropriate. |
| Using nonstandard `void main()`              | Not a valid hosted ISO C form.                                | Use `int main(void)` or `int main(int argc, char *argv[])`.   |

### Basic Pointer Syntax — address, dereference, null, pointer parameters

Pointers are central enough to appear in Part 2, though their full semantics belong in PART 3 and PART 7. At the syntax level, a pointer object stores a value that may point to an object or function of an appropriate type, may be null, or may be invalid/indeterminate depending on how it was produced.

| Construct             | Meaning                                         | Syntax                                     | Canonical example  | Practical consequence          | Common pitfall                                 |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------ | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Address-of            | Produce pointer to object/function              | `&expr`                                    | `int *p = &x;`     | Requires suitable operand.     | Taking address then using after lifetime ends. |
| Dereference           | Access pointed-to object/function               | `*p`                                       | `*p = 3;`          | Requires valid pointer.        | Dereferencing null or invalid pointer.         |
| Null pointer          | Pointer value that points to no object/function | `NULL`, `0`, C23 `nullptr` where available | `int *p = NULL;`   | Used as absence sentinel.      | Treating null as a valid empty object.         |
| Pointer member access | Dereference then select member                  | `p->field`                                 | `node->next`       | Equivalent to `(*p).field`.    | Using on null pointer.                         |
| Pointer parameter     | Function receives pointer value                 | `void f(int *p)`                           | `set_to_zero(&x);` | Enables mutation or borrowing. | Assuming pointer ownership from syntax alone.  |

Example:

```c id="knqst7"
int x = 10;
int *p = &x;

*p = 20;
/* x is now 20 */
```

Null check example:

```c id="tbq2qv"
int read_value(const int *p, int *out)
{
    if (p == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = *p;
    return 0;
}
```

Pointer-to-pointer example:

```c id="1noo8y"
void reset_pointer(int **pp)
{
    if (pp != NULL) {
        *pp = NULL;
    }
}
```

This changes the caller’s pointer object because the function receives a pointer to that pointer object.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                                                       | Better rule                                                      |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Dereferencing before null check           | Immediate undefined behavior if pointer is null.                     | Validate before dereference unless contract guarantees non-null. |
| Assuming pointer type proves validity     | Type says intended referent type, not current validity.              | Track lifetime, alignment, bounds, and ownership.                |
| Returning pointer to local object         | Pointer becomes dangling after function returns.                     | Do not expose addresses of automatic objects beyond lifetime.    |
| Confusing `p` and `*p`                    | One is the pointer object value; the other is the pointed-to object. | Read pointer expressions explicitly.                             |
| Confusing `int *p[10]` and `int (*p)[10]` | Array-of-pointers vs pointer-to-array.                               | Read declarators from identifier outward.                        |

### Basic Array Syntax — fixed storage, indexing, initialization, decay

Arrays are contiguous sequences of elements. In many expressions, an array expression is converted to a pointer to its first element, but arrays and pointers are not the same thing.

| Construct                  | Meaning                                        | Syntax            | Canonical example                           | Practical consequence                                                | Common pitfall                                   |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------- | ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Array declaration          | Object containing fixed number of elements     | `T a[N];`         | `int a[4];`                                 | Storage for all elements is part of array object.                    | Assuming array length is stored at runtime.      |
| Indexing                   | Access element                                 | `a[i]`            | `a[0] = 1;`                                 | Equivalent to pointer arithmetic after conversion.                   | Out-of-bounds access.                            |
| Initializer list           | Initialize elements                            | `{ ... }`         | `int a[3] = {1, 2, 3};`                     | Missing elements often zero-initialized in aggregate initialization. | Assuming assignment later can use same syntax.   |
| Size inference             | Infer element count from initializer           | `T a[] = {...};`  | `int a[] = {1, 2, 3};`                      | Useful for fixed tables.                                             | Losing size after passing to function.           |
| Array parameter adjustment | Parameter declared as array adjusts to pointer | `void f(int a[])` | Same as `void f(int *a)` for parameter type | Length is not passed automatically.                                  | Thinking function receives whole array by value. |

Example:

```c id="zcsigv"
int a[3] = {10, 20, 30};

for (size_t i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    printf("%d\n", a[i]);
}
```

Array size with `sizeof`:

```c id="5ncvj7"
int a[] = {10, 20, 30};
size_t count = sizeof a / sizeof a[0];
```

This works only where `a` is still an actual array expression, not after it has adjusted to a pointer parameter.

```c id="5j1g66"
void bad_count(int a[])
{
    size_t wrong = sizeof a / sizeof a[0]; /* sizeof pointer / sizeof int */
}
```

Better function signature:

```c id="ocbcxr"
void process_ints(const int *a, size_t n)
{
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        use(a[i]);
    }
}
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Arrays are pointers, so `sizeof` should work the same in a function.”                             |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `sizeof a` inside a function parameter gives pointer size, not array size.                         |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Array parameters are adjusted to pointer types; the array length is not carried.                   |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Pass arrays as pointer plus explicit length unless using a fixed-size array pointer intentionally. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | In the declaring scope of an actual array object, `sizeof array` gives total array storage size.   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                                  | Better rule                                                 |
| -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Out-of-bounds indexing                       | Undefined behavior.                             | Track and check bounds explicitly.                          |
| Assuming arrays are assignable               | Whole-array assignment is invalid in C.         | Copy elements or use appropriate memory functions.          |
| Losing length at function boundary           | Array parameters become pointers.               | Pass length separately.                                     |
| Confusing array object with pointer variable | `sizeof`, assignment, and storage differ.       | Distinguish array declaration from pointer declaration.     |
| Using sentinel termination without guarantee | Byte arrays may not be null-terminated strings. | Track length separately unless string contract is explicit. |

### Basic `struct`, `union`, and `enum` Syntax — aggregate forms before modeling

C’s main user-defined data forms are `struct`, `union`, and `enum`. Their full modeling role belongs in PART 3, but their syntax must be readable here.

| Construct     | Meaning                                                    | Syntax                       | Canonical example                   | Design meaning                          | Common pitfall                                           |
| ------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| `struct`      | Aggregate containing named members                         | `struct name { ... };`       | `struct point { int x; int y; };`   | Product-like data structure.            | Assuming no padding or stable serialized layout.         |
| `union`       | Object storing one member at a time in overlapping storage | `union name { ... };`        | `union value { int i; double d; };` | Representation-sharing construct.       | Reading inactive member incorrectly.                     |
| `enum`        | Named integer constants and enum type                      | `enum color { RED, GREEN };` | `enum state st = RED;`              | Symbolic names for integer-like states. | Assuming enum variable cannot hold other integer values. |
| Member access | Access aggregate field                                     | `obj.field`, `ptr->field`    | `p.x`, `node->next`                 | Direct representation access.           | Exposing representation in public APIs prematurely.      |

`struct` example:

```c id="3aj4id"
struct point {
    int x;
    int y;
};

struct point p = { .x = 10, .y = 20 };
p.x = 30;
```

`union` example:

```c id="jzzmns"
union number {
    int i;
    double d;
};

union number n;
n.i = 42;
```

`enum` example:

```c id="b3i8qh"
enum result_code {
    RESULT_OK = 0,
    RESULT_IO_ERROR = -1,
    RESULT_PARSE_ERROR = -2
};
```

Typedef pattern:

```c id="nv8q3u"
typedef struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
} Buffer;
```

This creates both the tag name `struct buffer` and typedef name `Buffer`. Whether to use typedefs for structs is style- and project-dependent. In public APIs, explicit `struct name` can make incomplete/opaque types clearer.

Designated initializers:

```c id="25wbvp"
struct point p = {
    .x = 10,
    .y = 20
};
```

Designated initializers improve clarity and reduce positional mistakes, especially when structs grow.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                     | Why it matters                                                           | Better rule                                               |
| ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming struct memory has no padding       | Padding may exist for alignment.                                         | Do not serialize raw structs as portable formats.         |
| Treating enums as closed safe variants      | C enum objects may hold values outside named constants.                  | Validate external integer values before converting/using. |
| Reading wrong union member                  | May violate effective type/object representation rules depending on use. | Track active member explicitly.                           |
| Exposing public struct fields unnecessarily | Freezes representation and ABI expectations.                             | Use opaque structs for stable libraries.                  |
| Positional initializer mistakes             | Field order changes can break initialization.                            | Prefer designated initializers for nontrivial aggregates. |

### Basic `typedef` Syntax — aliases, readability, and opacity

`typedef` introduces an alias for a type. It does not create a new distinct type in the way some languages create nominal wrappers. This is crucial.

| Use of `typedef`       | Example                                                          | Good use                                    | Risk                                                   |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Shorten verbose type   | `typedef unsigned long ulong;`                                   | Rarely valuable in modern professional code | Can hide important width/signedness assumptions.       |
| Name struct type       | `typedef struct buffer Buffer;`                                  | Common API style                            | May obscure incomplete type if overused.               |
| Function pointer alias | `typedef int (*compare_fn)(const void *, const void *);`         | Improves readability                        | Can hide pointer/function distinction if named poorly. |
| Platform abstraction   | `typedef uint32_t reg32_t;`                                      | Useful in controlled low-level code         | Alias does not enforce domain-specific constraints.    |
| Opaque handle style    | `typedef struct buffer Buffer;` with incomplete struct in header | Strong encapsulation pattern                | Requires clear create/destroy API.                     |

Example:

```c id="uo4z2v"
typedef struct buffer Buffer;

struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Function pointer typedef:

```c id="7wccrd"
typedef int (*compare_fn)(const void *a, const void *b);

void sort_items(void *base, size_t n, size_t size, compare_fn cmp);
```

`typedef` does not create a distinct type:

```c id="4b4l5l"
typedef int user_id;
typedef int order_id;

user_id u = 1;
order_id o = 2;

u = o; /* allowed: both are aliases for int */
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                       |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A `typedef` creates a new domain-specific type.”                                                                 |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A `user_id` is assigned from an `order_id` without compiler error.                                                |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `typedef` creates an alias, not a new nominal type.                                                               |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use structs or stronger API boundaries when domain separation must be enforced.                                   |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some static analyzers, naming conventions, or compiler extensions may help, but ISO C treats typedefs as aliases. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                                    | Better rule                                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Hiding pointer types with typedefs               | Ownership and nullability become less visible.    | Avoid typedefs like `typedef struct foo *FooHandle` unless project convention is strict. |
| Believing typedef enforces domain safety         | It is only an alias.                              | Use wrapper structs or validation APIs for stronger distinction.                         |
| Creating aliases for basic types without purpose | Reduces clarity.                                  | Use standard names unless abstraction is meaningful.                                     |
| Confusing tag name and typedef name              | `struct foo` and `Foo` are separate naming forms. | Be explicit in public APIs.                                                              |

### Basic Preprocessor Syntax — includes, macros, conditional compilation

The preprocessor is a separate textual transformation system. It is part of C translation, but it is not type-aware ordinary C evaluation.

| Directive               | Meaning                             | Syntax                                                 | Canonical example                  | Professional use                                 | Common pitfall                   |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| `#include`              | Textually include header            | `#include <...>` or `#include "..."`                   | `#include <stddef.h>`              | Bring declarations/macros into translation unit. | Treating it as semantic import.  |
| Object-like macro       | Replace name with tokens            | `#define NAME value`                                   | `#define BUFFER_CAP 1024`          | Constants, feature switches                      | No type or scope like variables. |
| Function-like macro     | Token substitution with parameters  | `#define MAX(a,b) ...`                                 | Dangerous unless carefully written | Generic-like or compile-time utility             | Multiple evaluation.             |
| Conditional compilation | Include/exclude code                | `#if`, `#ifdef`, `#ifndef`, `#elif`, `#else`, `#endif` | Header guards, platform branches   | Portability and configuration                    | Untested branches rot.           |
| Macro undefinition      | Remove macro                        | `#undef NAME`                                          | Rare controlled use                | Avoid collision or local macro scope emulation   | Hard-to-follow macro state.      |
| Error directive         | Force preprocessing error           | `#error message`                                       | `#error unsupported platform`      | Build-time guard                                 | Overly broad platform rejection. |
| Pragma                  | Implementation-specific instruction | `#pragma ...`                                          | compiler-specific                  | Toolchain control                                | Nonportable unless guarded.      |

Header guard:

```c id="dqssgt"
#ifndef BUFFER_H
#define BUFFER_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct buffer Buffer;

int buffer_init(Buffer *buf, size_t capacity);
void buffer_destroy(Buffer *buf);

#endif
```

Macro side-effect trap:

```c id="1h98l8"
#define SQUARE(x) ((x) * (x))

int i = 3;
int y = SQUARE(i++); /* i++ may be evaluated twice */
```

Prefer function when possible:

```c id="4l9jaw"
static inline int square_int(int x)
{
    return x * x;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                               | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                                               |
| ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Function-like macro double evaluation | Side effects may occur multiple times.                   | Avoid side-effect arguments or use functions.             |
| Missing parentheses in macros         | Operator precedence bugs.                                | Parenthesize parameters and whole expansion.              |
| Header without include guard          | Multiple inclusion can cause redefinition errors.        | Use include guards or carefully supported `#pragma once`. |
| Platform conditionals everywhere      | Code becomes fragmented and under-tested.                | Isolate platform-specific code behind narrow interfaces.  |
| Macro names colliding with functions  | Macro substitution can change later source unexpectedly. | Use project prefixes and avoid common names.              |

### Basic Error-Signaling Syntax — return codes, null, sentinels, `errno` surface

C has no special error syntax like `try`/`catch`, `throw`, `?`, or `Result<T, E>`. Error handling is expressed through ordinary values, control flow, and library conventions.

| Error form                   | Syntax shape                                               | Canonical example                     | Meaning                                                        | Common pitfall                                         |
| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Integer status               | `int rc`                                                   | `return -1;`                          | Usually `0` success, nonzero or negative failure by convention | Inconsistent conventions across APIs.                  |
| Null pointer return          | `T *p`                                                     | `if (p == NULL)`                      | Allocation/lookup/open failure by convention                   | Dereferencing before check.                            |
| Sentinel value               | Special valid-type value                                   | `return n;` where `n` means not found | Compact failure signal                                         | Sentinel collides with valid domain.                   |
| Output parameter plus status | `int f(T *out)`                                            | `return 0;` and set `*out`            | Separates result from failure                                  | Forgetting to define output state on failure.          |
| `errno`                      | Global/thread-local-like error indicator per library rules | `perror("open")`                      | Additional diagnostic for specified functions                  | Checking `errno` when function did not signal failure. |
| `assert`                     | Debug-time contract check                                  | `assert(p != NULL);`                  | Detects programmer errors in debug builds                      | Using assert for recoverable runtime input errors.     |

Example:

```c id="sxqsli"
int parse_u32(const char *s, uint32_t *out)
{
    if (s == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    /* parsing omitted */
    *out = 0;
    return 0;
}
```

A clear C error convention answers:

| Contract question           | Example answer                                                                    |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| What means success?         | `0` return value.                                                                 |
| What means failure?         | Negative return value.                                                            |
| Is output valid on failure? | No, unless explicitly stated.                                                     |
| Who owns returned storage?  | Caller owns and must free, or borrowed pointer remains valid until next mutation. |
| Is `errno` set?             | Only if documented.                                                               |
| Can inputs be null?         | State explicitly.                                                                 |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                                                        | Better rule                                                                |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Ignoring return values                                 | Failure is often only visible through return value.                   | Check every fallible function.                                             |
| Using `errno` without checking failure first           | `errno` may contain stale value.                                      | Consult `errno` only after an API indicates failure and documents `errno`. |
| Leaving output parameters partially written on failure | Caller may use invalid state.                                         | Define output state on failure or leave it untouched.                      |
| Using `assert` for external input validation           | Assertions may be disabled with `NDEBUG`.                             | Use runtime checks for untrusted input.                                    |
| Mixing success conventions                             | Some APIs use `0` success; others return positive counts or pointers. | Document and normalize conventions in wrapper APIs.                        |

### Equality, Identity, and Comparison Surface — values, addresses, strings, structs

C distinguishes value comparison, pointer comparison, and object identity. There is no universal equality operator for all object types.

| Comparison target | Syntax                         | Meaning                                  | Common pitfall                                                  | Better rule                                                          |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Arithmetic values | `a == b`                       | Numeric comparison after conversions     | Signed/unsigned surprises                                       | Align types deliberately.                                            |
| Pointers          | `p == q`                       | Compare pointer values                   | Assuming equal representation rules beyond defined cases        | Use for same-object/null checks under valid pointer rules.           |
| Strings           | `strcmp(a, b) == 0`            | Compare characters up to null terminator | Using `a == b` for string content                               | Use string library functions with valid strings.                     |
| Arrays            | No direct equality             | Must compare elements                    | Writing `a == b` after decay compares pointers in many contexts | Compare element-wise or with valid memory comparison.                |
| Structs           | No direct equality in ISO C    | Compare members manually                 | Expecting `s1 == s2` to work                                    | Define comparison function.                                          |
| Floating values   | `x == y` possible but delicate | Exact floating comparison                | Expecting decimal mathematical equality                         | Use domain-appropriate tolerance only when mathematically justified. |

String comparison trap:

```c id="52yjd7"
const char *a = "abc";
const char *b = "abc";

if (a == b) {
    /* compares pointer values, not string content */
}
```

Correct content comparison:

```c id="9q1oyt"
if (strcmp(a, b) == 0) {
    /* same string content */
}
```

Struct comparison:

```c id="uib3t1"
struct point {
    int x;
    int y;
};

int point_equal(struct point a, struct point b)
{
    return a.x == b.x && a.y == b.y;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                                     | Better rule                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Using `==` for string content                        | Compares addresses, not characters.                | Use `strcmp` or length-aware comparison.                                 |
| Using `memcmp` for struct equality blindly           | Padding bytes may be indeterminate or irrelevant.  | Compare semantic fields.                                                 |
| Comparing floating values casually                   | Rounding and representation affect exact equality. | Use exact comparison only when exactness is semantically valid.          |
| Comparing pointers outside valid relational contexts | Relational pointer comparisons are limited.        | Compare pointers relationally only within the same array object context. |
| Assuming enum comparison validates enum domain       | An enum object may contain unnamed values.         | Validate values from external sources.                                   |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “`==` compares what two things mean.”                                                                                                  |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Two strings with identical text compare unequal because their pointers differ.                                                         |
| Correct semantic explanation    | For pointers, `==` compares pointer values, not pointed-to contents.                                                                   |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Choose comparison based on semantic domain: numeric, pointer identity, string content, array contents, or struct fields.               |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Interned or identical string literals may share storage in some implementations, but relying on pointer equality for content is wrong. |

### Basic Cast Syntax — explicit conversions and escape hatches

A cast explicitly converts an expression to a specified type. In C, casts are powerful and often dangerous because they can suppress diagnostics or assert facts the compiler cannot verify.

| Cast use              | Example      | Appropriate when                                  | Risk                                                  |
| --------------------- | ------------ | ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Numeric conversion    | `(double)n`  | Intentional arithmetic conversion                 | Narrowing, overflow, precision loss.                  |
| Pointer conversion    | `(void *)p`  | Generic API boundary                              | Losing specific type information.                     |
| Removing `const`      | `(char *)s`  | Rare, only if original object is actually mutable | UB if modifying truly const object or string literal. |
| Reinterpretation      | `(T *)p`     | Low-level code with strict contract               | Alignment, effective type, aliasing violations.       |
| Function pointer cast | `(fn_t)f`    | Rare ABI-level interop                            | Calling through incompatible type can be undefined.   |
| Silencing warning     | `(type)expr` | Almost never by itself                            | Hides real bugs.                                      |

Example:

```c id="c4x7mu"
int n = 3;
double x = (double)n / 2.0;
```

Risky cast:

```c id="jainkg"
const char *s = "abc";
char *p = (char *)s;
/* p[0] = 'x'; */ /* undefined behavior if attempted */
```

Casts do not validate runtime data. Casting a `void *` back to the wrong type does not create a valid object of that type.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                                                                                                    | Better rule                                              |
| --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Casting to silence compiler warnings          | The warning may indicate a real type or lifetime bug.                                                             | Fix the declaration or API mismatch first.               |
| Casting away `const` and modifying            | Undefined behavior if original object is not modifiable.                                                          | Preserve const-correctness.                              |
| Assuming pointer cast changes the object      | It changes the access expression, not the object’s actual lifetime/effective type.                                | Use valid object creation and access rules.              |
| Overusing casts in ordinary code              | Reduces type-system value.                                                                                        | Treat casts as boundary markers requiring justification. |
| Casting allocation results in C unnecessarily | In C, `void *` converts to object pointer types automatically; casting may hide missing declarations in old code. | Include proper headers and avoid needless casts.         |

### Minimal Standard Header Awareness — syntax-facing declarations

Part 6 will cover the standard library by task. At the syntax level, a reader should know that many names require including the correct header. C does not automatically know `printf`, `size_t`, `bool`, `uint32_t`, or `malloc` without declarations.

| Header        | Common names                                | Why it appears early                           | Common pitfall                                                      |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `<stddef.h>`  | `size_t`, `ptrdiff_t`, `NULL`, `offsetof`   | Core type vocabulary                           | Defining custom size types unnecessarily.                           |
| `<stdint.h>`  | `int32_t`, `uint64_t`, `uintptr_t`          | Fixed-width integer vocabulary where available | Assuming fixed-width types exist for every width on every platform. |
| `<stdbool.h>` | `bool`, `true`, `false` in pre-C23 practice | Boolean readability                            | Treating Boolean as enforcing richer invariants.                    |
| `<stdio.h>`   | `printf`, `puts`, `FILE`                    | Basic I/O examples                             | Calling undeclared functions or ignoring return values.             |
| `<stdlib.h>`  | `malloc`, `free`, `EXIT_SUCCESS`            | Allocation and program status                  | Forgetting header and relying on non-modern implicit declarations.  |
| `<string.h>`  | `memcpy`, `strlen`, `strcmp`                | Memory/string operations                       | Using string functions on non-terminated buffers.                   |
| `<assert.h>`  | `assert`                                    | Debug contracts                                | Using assertions for recoverable external errors.                   |

Example:

```c id="hp6b6x"
#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void)
{
    uint32_t flags = 0;
    size_t count = 3;

    printf("%zu %u\n", count, flags);
    return 0;
}
```

Format strings are especially important: the format specifier must match the argument type after default argument promotions. This is syntax-adjacent but also ABI-relevant for variadic functions.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                                             | Better rule                                                  |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Missing required header                          | Modern C requires declarations for proper checking.        | Include the header that declares each library function/type. |
| Wrong `printf` format                            | Variadic calls are not type-safe in the ordinary way.      | Use correct format specifiers and compiler format warnings.  |
| Assuming standard-library names are built in     | They are declared by headers.                              | Treat headers as interface declarations.                     |
| Mixing ISO C and POSIX names without distinction | POSIX APIs require POSIX headers and platform assumptions. | Label APIs by standard layer.                                |

### Syntax Reference Index for Part 2 — compact source-reading table

| Syntax item         | Meaning                                        | Example                           | First question to ask                                                  |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `#include`          | Textual header inclusion                       | `#include <stdio.h>`              | Is this ISO C, POSIX, compiler-specific, or project-local?             |
| `#define`           | Macro substitution                             | `#define N 10`                    | Is this safe textual substitution or should it be a function/constant? |
| `int x;`            | Object declaration                             | `int x = 0;`                      | Is it initialized before read?                                         |
| `int *p;`           | Pointer declaration                            | `int *p = &x;`                    | What object may it point to, and for how long?                         |
| `int a[10];`        | Array declaration                              | `int a[10] = {0};`                | Where is the length tracked?                                           |
| `struct S { ... };` | Aggregate type definition                      | `struct point { int x; int y; };` | Is layout part of the API or private?                                  |
| `typedef T U;`      | Type alias                                     | `typedef struct buffer Buffer;`   | Does this clarify or hide important information?                       |
| `if (expr)`         | Conditional branch                             | `if (p == NULL)`                  | Is the condition explicit and side-effect-safe?                        |
| `switch`            | Integer multi-way branch                       | `switch (state)`                  | Are fallthrough and default handling correct?                          |
| `for`               | Iteration                                      | `for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++)`  | Are bounds and updates correct?                                        |
| `while`             | Condition-controlled loop                      | `while (*s != '\0')`              | Is termination guaranteed?                                             |
| `return`            | Exit function                                  | `return -1;`                      | Are resources released and error convention clear?                     |
| `goto cleanup`      | Jump to cleanup label                          | `goto cleanup;`                   | Is this disciplined cleanup or arbitrary control flow?                 |
| `&x`                | Address of object                              | `int *p = &x;`                    | Does the pointed-to object outlive the pointer use?                    |
| `*p`                | Dereference                                    | `*p = 1;`                         | Is `p` valid, non-null, aligned, and within lifetime?                  |
| `p->m`              | Member through pointer                         | `node->next`                      | Is `p` valid?                                                          |
| `a[i]`              | Element access                                 | `buf[i]`                          | Is `i` within bounds?                                                  |
| `(T)x`              | Cast                                           | `(double)n`                       | Is this a justified conversion or warning suppression?                 |
| `sizeof x`          | Size of object/type                            | `sizeof a`                        | Is `x` an array object or already a pointer?                           |
| `const`             | Non-modifiable through access path             | `const int *p`                    | What exactly is const: pointer, pointed-to object, or both?            |
| `static`            | Storage duration or linkage depending on scope | `static int count;`               | Is this block-scope persistence or file-scope internal linkage?        |
| `extern`            | External declaration                           | `extern int x;`                   | Where is the single definition?                                        |
| `volatile`          | Special observable access                      | `volatile sig_atomic_t flag;`     | Is this truly volatile, or should it be atomic/synchronized?           |
| `restrict`          | No-aliasing pointer contract                   | `int *restrict p`                 | Can callers actually satisfy this contract?                            |

### Part 2 Review Artifacts — what to memorize and what to defer

| Memorize now                                             | Reason                                                    |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Declaration reading from identifier outward              | Necessary for pointers, arrays, functions, and callbacks. |
| Difference between initialization and assignment         | Prevents uninitialized reads and const confusion.         |
| Difference between arrays and pointers                   | Prevents size, bounds, and parameter bugs.                |
| `int f(void)` vs `int f()`                               | Modern prototype correctness.                             |
| `.` vs `->`                                              | Basic aggregate access.                                   |
| `==` for pointer identity vs `strcmp` for string content | Prevents common comparison bugs.                          |
| `static` has context-dependent meanings                  | Prevents linkage/lifetime confusion.                      |
| `const int *p` vs `int *const p`                         | Required for API reading.                                 |
| `return`, `goto cleanup`, and resource paths             | Foundation for C error handling.                          |
| Preprocessor before compiler                             | Explains macro behavior and header inclusion.             |

| Defer deeper treatment to later parts                      | Primary later location |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- |
| Integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions        | PART 3 and PART 7      |
| Effective type, strict aliasing, and object representation | PART 3 and PART 7      |
| Dynamic allocation and ownership                           | PART 5, PART 6, PART 7 |
| Header design and linkage                                  | PART 5 and PART 7      |
| ABI and calling conventions                                | PART 7 and PART 9      |
| Atomics and data races                                     | PART 7                 |
| Standard library APIs                                      | PART 6                 |
| Build systems and toolchains                               | PART 9                 |
| Undefined behavior taxonomy                                | PART 7 and PART 10     |

### Part 2 Common Pitfall Index — syntax that causes semantic bugs

| Pitfall                       | Category                | Why it is dangerous                               | Review cue                                       |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| `int* a, b;`                  | Declaration syntax      | Only `a` is pointer.                              | One pointer declaration per line.                |
| `if (x = y)`                  | Expression/selection    | Assignment used as condition.                     | Compiler warnings; explicit comparisons.         |
| Missing `break`               | `switch`                | Accidental fallthrough.                           | Every case exits or documents fallthrough.       |
| `sizeof` on array parameter   | Array/pointer confusion | Gives pointer size.                               | Pass explicit length.                            |
| Modifying string literal      | Literal/pointer bug     | Undefined behavior.                               | Use `const char *` for literals.                 |
| Returning local address       | Lifetime bug            | Dangling pointer.                                 | Check every returned pointer’s storage duration. |
| `size_t i >= 0`               | Unsigned loop bug       | Always true.                                      | Use `for (i = n; i > 0; i--)`.                   |
| Macro double evaluation       | Preprocessor bug        | Side effects repeated.                            | Avoid side-effect macro arguments.               |
| `strcmp` forgotten            | Equality bug            | Pointer comparison instead of content comparison. | Ask whether equality means identity or contents. |
| Missing header                | Declaration/API bug     | Wrong or absent type checking.                    | Include the declaring header.                    |
| Wrong `printf` format         | Variadic/ABI bug        | Argument interpretation mismatch.                 | Enable format warnings.                          |
| Cast hiding warning           | Type-boundary bug       | Suppresses useful diagnostics.                    | Justify every cast.                              |
| Uninitialized local read      | Initialization bug      | Undefined or unstable behavior.                   | Initialize before read.                          |
| `volatile` as synchronization | Concurrency bug         | Does not provide atomicity or ordering.           | Use atomics or locks.                            |

### Part 2 Closing Mental Model — syntax is a contract surface, not a safety net

C syntax tells the reader the shape of names, objects, expressions, and control flow. It does not, by itself, prove that memory is valid, arrays are in bounds, pointers are live, objects are initialized, resources are released, strings are terminated, or external inputs are trusted.

The core Part 2 reading discipline is:

| Source feature     | Immediate reading question                                                                      |
| ------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Declaration        | What entity is introduced, with what type shape, storage implication, and initialization state? |
| Pointer expression | What object is intended, and what proves validity, lifetime, alignment, bounds, and ownership?  |
| Array expression   | Is this still an array object, or has it converted to a pointer? Where is the length?           |
| Function call      | What is passed by value, what may be mutated through pointers, and how is failure reported?     |
| Control flow       | Which paths initialize, mutate, release, or return?                                             |
| Macro              | What source does this become after expansion?                                                   |
| Cast               | What check is being bypassed or what conversion is being made explicit?                         |
| Header inclusion   | What declarations and macros enter this translation unit?                                       |

This completes the core syntax and semantic-primitives layer. Subsequent parts treat C less as syntax and more as a design environment for data representation, abstraction, resource boundaries, standard-library use, runtime semantics, and professional engineering judgment.
## PART 3 — Data, Types, and Modeling Reference by Task Pattern

### Scope of Part 3 — modeling values, objects, state, absence, and representation

Part 3 treats C’s type and data system as a **modeling toolkit**, not as a list of type spellings. In C, data modeling is inseparable from representation: choosing `int`, `size_t`, `struct`, `union`, `enum`, pointer, array, or byte buffer affects memory layout, ABI compatibility, lifetime, aliasing, copying cost, validation strategy, and undefined-behavior risk.

C does not offer algebraic data types, classes, traits, generics, ownership types, nullable annotations, dependent types, or standard runtime reflection. It instead gives a compact set of low-level mechanisms. Professional modeling in C means knowing what each mechanism can express, what it cannot express, and what must be enforced by convention, API design, tests, or tools.

| Modeling task                | Primary C mechanisms                                         | What C can express directly                          | What remains programmer discipline                 |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Represent numeric quantities | Integer and floating types                                   | Width category, signedness, approximate numeric type | Units, ranges, overflow policy, domain constraints |
| Represent byte-level data    | `unsigned char`, `char`, arrays, pointers, `memcpy`          | Object representation and raw buffers                | Bounds, encoding, ownership, termination           |
| Represent records            | `struct`                                                     | Named fields and aggregate layout                    | Invariants across fields, privacy unless opaque    |
| Represent alternatives       | `enum`, `union`, tagged struct                               | Tags and overlapping storage                         | Active variant tracking and validation             |
| Represent sequences          | Arrays, pointer plus length, allocated buffers               | Contiguous storage and indexing                      | Bounds checking, capacity discipline, lifetime     |
| Represent absence            | Null pointer, sentinel, status code, optional-output pattern | Some absence conventions                             | Non-null guarantees and invalid-state prevention   |
| Represent resources          | Pointer handles, integer handles, opaque structs             | Resource identity and access path                    | Ownership, cleanup, error paths                    |
| Represent abstraction        | Opaque structs, function pointers, callbacks                 | Interface boundaries                                 | Contracts, lifecycle, thread-safety                |
| Represent genericity         | `void *`, macros, `_Generic`                                 | Some type-erased or compile-time-dispatch patterns   | Type correctness across erased boundaries          |

The most important Part 3 rule is: **a C type often describes representation more directly than domain meaning**. If domain meaning matters, the API must encode or document it.

### Type-System Properties — static typing, weak spots, conversions, and unsound edges

C is statically typed: declarations assign types to objects, functions, expressions, and pointers. However, “statically typed” does not mean memory-safe, bounds-safe, lifetime-safe, alias-safe, or semantically sound in the strong type-theoretic sense.

| Type-system property   | C’s behavior                                                                     | Practical consequence                                                  | Common misunderstanding                                     |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Static typing          | Types are checked during translation.                                            | Function calls, assignments, and many conversions receive diagnostics. | “Static typing means safe.”                                 |
| Explicit declarations  | Most objects and functions require declared types.                               | Interfaces are visible in source and headers.                          | “Verbose declarations express all important constraints.”   |
| Arithmetic conversions | Integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions occur automatically.         | Expression type may differ from operand spelling.                      | “The variable’s declared type controls all arithmetic.”     |
| Pointer typing         | Pointer types indicate intended pointed-to type.                                 | Enables typed dereference and diagnostics.                             | “A typed pointer is necessarily valid.”                     |
| `void *` conversion    | Object pointers can convert to/from `void *`.                                    | Supports generic APIs such as allocation and containers.               | “`void *` is dynamic typing.”                               |
| Casts                  | Explicit conversions can bypass checks.                                          | Useful at boundaries but dangerous in ordinary logic.                  | “A cast fixes the value.”                                   |
| Qualifiers             | `const`, `volatile`, `restrict`, `_Atomic` modify access semantics or contracts. | Express important API and optimization information.                    | “Qualifiers are just documentation.”                        |
| Incomplete types       | Type can be declared without full definition.                                    | Enables opaque APIs and separate compilation.                          | “Incomplete means unusable.”                                |
| Type aliases           | `typedef` aliases existing types.                                                | Improves naming and API clarity.                                       | “`typedef` creates a distinct domain type.”                 |
| Undefined behavior     | Some typed programs have no defined semantics.                                   | Optimizers may assume invalid situations do not occur.                 | “If it compiles, it has some predictable runtime behavior.” |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C has static types, so once the compiler accepts a pointer expression, the pointed-to object must be valid.”                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A pointer of type `struct node *` is dereferenced after its storage was freed. The compiler accepts it; the program later corrupts memory.  |
| Correct semantic explanation    | The pointer type describes the expected referent type, not the referent’s current lifetime, ownership, bounds, or allocation status.        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Treat every pointer value as needing a separate validity proof: non-nullness, lifetime, alignment, effective type, and bounds.              |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Static analyzers, sanitizers, ownership annotations, or restricted coding standards can add checks, but ISO C itself does not enforce them. |

### Core Data Vocabulary — scalar, aggregate, derived, and incomplete types

C’s type system is often easier to reason about if divided into scalar, aggregate, derived, and incomplete categories.

| Category                | Examples                                                     | Main purpose                                             | Key risks                                            |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Arithmetic scalar types | `int`, `unsigned`, `double`, `char`                          | Numeric and character-like values                        | Overflow, conversions, signedness, precision         |
| Pointer types           | `T *`, `void *`, function pointers                           | Access to objects/functions indirectly                   | Invalid lifetime, null, bounds, aliasing             |
| Aggregate types         | `struct`, array                                              | Grouping values or elements                              | Padding, copying cost, exposed layout                |
| Union types             | `union`                                                      | Overlapping representation alternatives                  | Active-member discipline, effective type             |
| Enumerated types        | `enum`                                                       | Named integer constants and state labels                 | Not closed against invalid integer values            |
| Function types          | `int (int)`, pointer forms                                   | Behavior interface                                       | Signature mismatch, ABI issues                       |
| Incomplete types        | `struct buffer;`                                             | Opaque boundaries and recursive references               | Cannot take `sizeof` or access fields until complete |
| Qualified types         | `const T`, `volatile T`, `_Atomic T`, `restrict` pointer use | Access constraints or concurrency/optimization semantics | Misinterpreting qualifier guarantees                 |
| Aliased types           | `typedef` names                                              | Readability and abstraction                              | Hiding important representation or pointer-ness      |

Example of incomplete type for opacity:

```c
/* buffer.h */
#ifndef BUFFER_H
#define BUFFER_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct buffer Buffer;

int buffer_create(Buffer **out, size_t capacity);
void buffer_destroy(Buffer *buf);
size_t buffer_size(const Buffer *buf);

#endif
```

```c
/* buffer.c */
#include "buffer.h"
#include <stdlib.h>

struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

The header exposes `Buffer` as a type name but not its fields. Client code can hold `Buffer *`, pass it to functions, and destroy it through the API, but cannot depend on the representation.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                            | Why it matters                                                                                 | Better rule                                                                           |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Treating `typedef` opacity and incomplete-type opacity as the same | `typedef struct buffer Buffer;` alone does not hide fields if the struct definition is public. | Use incomplete struct declarations in public headers and define fields in `.c` files. |
| Assuming `enum` prevents invalid values                            | C enum variables can hold values not listed by enumerators.                                    | Validate external or cast integer values before switch logic.                         |
| Treating `void *` as self-describing                               | It carries no runtime type metadata.                                                           | Pair `void *` with size, tag, callback, or documented type contract.                  |
| Assuming aggregate copy enforces invariants                        | Assignment copies bits/fields, not ownership semantics.                                        | Define copy/clone functions for owning aggregates.                                    |

### Numeric Modeling — integer types, sizes, signedness, and domain meaning

Numeric modeling in C is not just choosing “an integer.” The choice among `int`, `long`, `size_t`, `ptrdiff_t`, `uint32_t`, `int64_t`, `double`, and related types expresses portability assumptions, arithmetic rules, API contracts, and error behavior.

| Task                                   | Good C choice                                                                | When to use                                             | Design meaning                   | Common pitfall                             |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Count objects in memory                | `size_t`                                                                     | Sizes, lengths, capacities, array counts                | Nonnegative object-size domain   | Unsigned underflow in reverse loops        |
| Difference between pointers or indices | `ptrdiff_t`                                                                  | Subtracting pointers or signed offsets                  | Signed difference domain         | Storing pointer differences in `int`       |
| General local arithmetic               | `int`                                                                        | Small bounded values with no width-specific requirement | Natural integer type             | Assuming overflow wraps                    |
| Bit masks and flags                    | Unsigned integer type, often fixed width                                     | Flags, protocol bits, registers                         | Bit pattern, not signed quantity | Mixing flags with signed arithmetic        |
| Fixed binary format field              | `uint8_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint32_t`, `uint64_t` where available                | File/network/protocol formats                           | Explicit width                   | Forgetting endian conversion               |
| Potentially negative count/error mix   | Signed type such as `ssize_t` in POSIX, or explicit status/result separation | APIs returning count or error                           | Sentinel-capable domain          | Using ISO C where `ssize_t` is unavailable |
| Approximate real computation           | `double`                                                                     | Most floating calculations                              | Approximate real-number model    | Expecting decimal exactness                |
| Storage-constrained numeric field      | Narrow integer type                                                          | Packed data with explicit constraints                   | Space/layout pressure            | Arithmetic promotes before calculation     |

Integer type decision table:

| Requirement                                   | Prefer                                                                            | Avoid                                 | Reason                                                       |
| --------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Object size, allocation size, string length   | `size_t`                                                                          | `int`                                 | `size_t` is the standard type for object sizes.              |
| Array index where reverse iteration is needed | `size_t` with careful idiom, or signed index if domain requires negative sentinel | `for (size_t i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--)` | Unsigned wraparound can create infinite loops.               |
| External binary format                        | Fixed-width types from `<stdint.h>`                                               | Plain `int`, `long`                   | Plain type widths vary by implementation.                    |
| Arithmetic where overflow is a bug            | Signed type with explicit bounds checks, or checked arithmetic helper             | Blind unsigned arithmetic             | Unsigned wrap is defined but may hide bugs.                  |
| Bit operations                                | Unsigned types                                                                    | Signed types                          | Shifts and bit interpretation are cleaner and more portable. |
| Public ABI field                              | Carefully documented fixed or ABI-specific type                                   | Casual `long`                         | ABI data models differ.                                      |

Example: length-safe buffer append interface:

```c
#include <stddef.h>

struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

int buffer_append(struct buffer *buf, const unsigned char *src, size_t n);
```

Here, `size_t` is appropriate because `len`, `cap`, and `n` describe object sizes. But this does not automatically prevent overflow:

```c
if (n > buf->cap - buf->len) {
    return -1;
}
```

This check is only safe if `buf->len <= buf->cap` is already an invariant. A more defensive form is:

```c
if (buf->len > buf->cap) {
    return -1;
}

if (n > buf->cap - buf->len) {
    return -1;
}
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                               |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Use unsigned integers for quantities that cannot be negative; then negative bugs disappear.”                             |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `size_t remaining = len - used;` becomes a huge number when `used > len`.                                                 |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Unsigned arithmetic is modulo arithmetic. It prevents negative representation, not invalid arithmetic.                    |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use unsigned types for sizes and bit patterns, but still check ordering before subtraction.                               |
| Boundary where the rule changes | In tightly controlled bit-level code, unsigned wraparound may be the intended operation and should be documented as such. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                          | Category                               | Better rule                                                                                        |
| -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Signed integer overflow          | Undefined behavior                     | Check bounds before arithmetic or use compiler/library checked arithmetic support where available. |
| Unsigned underflow               | Defined modulo wrap, often still a bug | Check before subtracting.                                                                          |
| Signed/unsigned comparison       | Surprising conversions                 | Convert deliberately or redesign API types.                                                        |
| Assuming exact type widths       | Portability bug                        | Use `<stdint.h>` for fixed-width external representations.                                         |
| Using `char` for byte arithmetic | Plain `char` signedness varies         | Use `unsigned char` for raw bytes.                                                                 |
| Using floating equality casually | Precision bug                          | Compare according to numerical method and domain requirements.                                     |

### Character and Byte Modeling — `char`, `signed char`, `unsigned char`, strings, and object representation

C uses character types both for text and for byte-level object representation. This dual role causes many mistakes.

| Type              | Primary interpretation                            | Special property                                    | Common pitfall                                     |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `char`            | Character data and C strings                      | Distinct type; signedness implementation-defined    | Assuming it is signed or unsigned                  |
| `signed char`     | Small signed integer                              | Explicit signedness                                 | Using it for raw bytes and getting negative values |
| `unsigned char`   | Raw byte/object representation                    | Can inspect object representation byte by byte      | Confusing bytes with encoded characters            |
| `char *`          | Mutable character buffer or legacy string pointer | May point to modifiable storage if contract says so | Pointing to string literal and modifying           |
| `const char *`    | Read-only access to character data/string         | Common for string literals                          | Assuming null-termination without contract         |
| `unsigned char *` | Raw binary buffer                                 | Best for memory bytes                               | Passing to string functions accidentally           |

Byte buffer example:

```c
#include <stddef.h>

unsigned checksum_bytes(const unsigned char *buf, size_t n)
{
    unsigned sum = 0;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        sum += buf[i];
    }

    return sum;
}
```

C string example:

```c
#include <string.h>

size_t name_length(const char *name)
{
    if (name == NULL) {
        return 0;
    }

    return strlen(name);
}
```

These two APIs have different contracts.

| API shape                            | Contract                                               | Failure mode if confused                                     |
| ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `const unsigned char *buf, size_t n` | Buffer of exactly `n` bytes; may contain zero bytes    | Treating as string stops early or reads beyond intended data |
| `const char *s`                      | Null-terminated string; must contain `'\0'` eventually | `strlen` reads out of bounds if not terminated               |
| `char *dst, size_t cap`              | Mutable destination buffer with capacity               | Overflow or truncation if capacity is wrong                  |
| `const char *s, size_t n`            | Length-bounded text or byte-span-like string view      | Not necessarily null-terminated                              |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A `char *` is a string.”                                                                                                  |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A buffer passed to `strlen` contains no null terminator, so `strlen` reads beyond the buffer.                              |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A C string is not merely `char *`; it is a sequence of non-null characters followed by `'\0'` in valid accessible storage. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | State whether a pointer is a null-terminated string or a pointer to a buffer with explicit length.                         |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some APIs intentionally accept either length-bounded text or null-terminated text, but the distinction must be explicit.   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters                                         | Better rule                                                           |
