| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using string functions on binary buffers | Binary data may contain zero bytes or lack terminator. | Use `mem*` functions with explicit lengths for binary data.           |
| Using `char` for raw bytes               | Signedness varies.                                     | Use `unsigned char` for object representation and raw memory.         |
| Forgetting space for `'\0'`              | String storage must include terminator.                | Allocate length plus one for C strings.                               |
| Assuming encoding                        | ISO C does not make UTF-8 universal.                   | Treat encoding as an external contract.                               |
| Modifying string literals                | Undefined behavior.                                    | Store literals as `const char *`; copy into mutable arrays if needed. |

### Structured Data — `struct` as product type, layout contract, and invariant container

A `struct` groups named members into one object. Conceptually, it is C’s main **product type**: a value containing several fields simultaneously. Practically, it is also a layout-bearing object with padding, alignment, ABI consequences, and copying behavior.

| Task                         | C construct                            | When to use                                   | Design meaning                | Practical consequence                              |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Group related fields         | `struct`                               | Fields form one conceptual object             | Product type                  | Field invariants must be maintained manually.      |
| Expose simple data record    | Public `struct` definition             | Stable, transparent data layout is acceptable | Representation is part of API | Future layout changes may break clients/ABI.       |
| Hide representation          | Incomplete `struct` plus API functions | Library or module needs flexibility           | Opaque type                   | Clients must use functions, not fields.            |
| Create stack/local aggregate | Automatic `struct` object              | Small bounded-lifetime object                 | Value-like usage              | Copying may duplicate internal pointers shallowly. |
| Store resource owner         | `struct` with pointer/handle fields    | Encapsulate resource state                    | Ownership by convention       | Requires explicit init/destroy/copy policy.        |

Simple value-like struct:

```c
struct point {
    int x;
    int y;
};

struct point translate(struct point p, int dx, int dy)
{
    p.x += dx;
    p.y += dy;
    return p;
}
```

Resource-owning struct:

```c
struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

The second struct is not safely copyable by ordinary assignment if ownership is attached to `data`. This is a central C modeling issue.

```c
struct buffer a;
struct buffer b;

/* If a owns a.data, this shallow copy is dangerous unless explicitly intended. */
b = a;
```

If both `a` and `b` later call `free` on the same `data`, the result may be double free. If one mutates the buffer, both observe the same underlying allocation.

Struct initialization patterns:

```c
struct point p1 = { 10, 20 };
struct point p2 = { .x = 10, .y = 20 };
struct point p3 = { 0 };
```

Designated initializers are preferable for nontrivial structs because they survive field reordering better at source-reading level and clarify intent.

Struct layout caveat:

```c
struct packet_header {
    unsigned short type;
    unsigned int length;
};
```

This struct may contain padding between fields or after the last field. Its layout may vary by ABI, compiler options, and target. It should not be blindly written to a file or network socket as a portable binary format.

Interdisciplinary Lens: computer architecture and ABI
What it clarifies: why structs have alignment and padding, and why public struct layout becomes an ABI commitment.
Language feature involved: `struct` member layout, `sizeof`, alignment, public headers, binary libraries.
Practical consequence: use opaque structs for stable APIs; use explicit serialization for portable formats.
Limit of the lens: architecture explains why padding exists, but the exact language guarantees still come from ISO C and the platform ABI.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                            | Why it matters                                       | Better rule                                                                    |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Serializing raw structs                            | Padding, endian, and layout differ.                  | Serialize fields explicitly.                                                   |
| Shallow-copying owning structs                     | Duplicates pointers, not owned resources.            | Define copy, move-like transfer convention, or prohibit copying by API design. |
| Exposing struct fields in public headers too early | Freezes representation and may freeze ABI.           | Use opaque structs when evolution matters.                                     |
| Assuming field order can be changed freely         | Field order affects layout and source compatibility. | Treat public structs as API.                                                   |
| Not preserving invariants across fields            | C does not enforce relationships like `len <= cap`.  | Centralize mutation through functions.                                         |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Struct assignment copies the object, so it creates an independent duplicate.”                                   |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Two buffers after assignment point to the same allocation and are both destroyed, causing double free.           |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Struct assignment copies member values. Pointer members are copied as pointer values, not deep-copied referents. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Treat structs containing owning pointers or handles as needing explicit copy/destroy policy.                     |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Plain value structs containing only scalar fields can often be copied safely by assignment.                      |

### Choosing Collections — arrays, pointer-length buffers, dynamic arrays, linked lists, and tables

C has arrays as a language feature, but no standard generic vector, map, set, slice, or list. Collection modeling is therefore a design decision, not a standard-library default.

| Task                       | C representation                                     | When to use                                                              | Strength                             | Cost or risk                                                      |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Fixed-size local sequence  | `T a[N]`                                             | Size known at compile time or block entry where VLA is deliberately used | Simple contiguous storage            | Length not carried across function boundary                       |
| Read-only view of sequence | `const T *p, size_t n`                               | Function reads caller-owned array                                        | Efficient and idiomatic              | Caller must ensure lifetime and bounds                            |
| Mutable view of sequence   | `T *p, size_t n`                                     | Function mutates elements but not capacity                               | Efficient mutation                   | Bounds and aliasing discipline required                           |
| Growable buffer            | `struct { T *data; size_t len, cap; }`               | Dynamic accumulation                                                     | Vector-like behavior                 | Manual allocation, overflow checks, cleanup                       |
| Linked list                | `struct node { T value; struct node *next; }`        | Frequent insertion/removal with stable node addresses                    | Simple node-based structure          | Poor cache locality, allocation overhead                          |
| Hash table/tree            | Custom or library-provided structure                 | Lookup by key                                                            | Efficient lookup if implemented well | Complex ownership and genericity                                  |
| Null-terminated sequence   | `char *` string or sentinel-terminated pointer array | Legacy APIs, simple strings                                              | Compact interface                    | Requires terminator and cannot represent embedded sentinel easily |

Fixed array:

```c
int scores[16] = {0};
```

Pointer plus length:

```c
int sum_ints(const int *values, size_t n)
{
    int total = 0;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        total += values[i];
    }

    return total;
}
```

Dynamic array pattern:

```c
struct int_vec {
    int *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

int int_vec_push(struct int_vec *v, int value)
{
    if (v->len == v->cap) {
        size_t new_cap = v->cap == 0 ? 8 : v->cap * 2;

        if (new_cap < v->cap) {
            return -1; /* overflow */
        }

        int *new_data = realloc(v->data, new_cap * sizeof new_data[0]);
        if (new_data == NULL) {
            return -1;
        }

        v->data = new_data;
        v->cap = new_cap;
    }

    v->data[v->len++] = value;
    return 0;
}
```

This example is intentionally realistic enough to show the modeling burden: capacity growth, overflow check, allocation failure, update ordering, and ownership all matter.

However, the multiplication `new_cap * sizeof new_data[0]` also needs overflow discipline in fully defensive code. A robust production helper often uses a checked multiplication function.

Collection decision table:

| If the main requirement is...          | Prefer                                         | Avoid                                                   |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Fast sequential traversal              | Contiguous array/buffer                        | Linked list unless insertion pattern demands it         |
| Stable element addresses during growth | Node allocation or reserved capacity strategy  | Reallocating vector without pointer invalidation policy |
| Minimal allocation                     | Fixed array or caller-provided buffer          | Per-element heap allocation                             |
| Unknown growth size                    | Dynamic array with `len`/`cap`                 | Repeated `realloc` by one element                       |
| Binary data                            | `unsigned char *` plus length                  | Null-terminated string representation                   |
| Public API sequence input              | `const T *items, size_t count`                 | Hidden global length or sentinel unless natural         |
| Ownership transfer                     | Explicit create/destroy or take/release naming | Ambiguous pointer parameter                             |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                           |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A pointer to the first element is enough to represent an array.”                                                                     |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A function walks past the end because it has no length information.                                                                   |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Pointer values do not carry array length in C.                                                                                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Pass pointer and length together unless a sentinel-terminated contract is explicit and valid.                                         |
| Boundary where the rule changes | A fixed-size array pointer such as `int (*p)[10]` carries size in the type shape, but is less flexible and must be used deliberately. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                                     | Better rule                                                                 |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Losing length at API boundary             | Arrays decay to pointers in parameters.            | Always pass length or use fixed-size array pointer intentionally.           |
| Using linked lists by default             | Often poor cache locality and allocation overhead. | Prefer contiguous storage unless insertion/removal pattern justifies nodes. |
| Holding element pointers across `realloc` | Reallocation may move storage.                     | Invalidate or recompute interior pointers after growth.                     |
| Forgetting capacity vs length distinction | Writes beyond initialized or allocated range.      | Maintain invariant `len <= cap`.                                            |
| Using string sentinel for binary data     | Binary data may contain zero bytes.                | Use explicit length.                                                        |

### Optional and Missing Values — null pointers, sentinels, status/result separation

C has no standard `Option<T>` or nullable type annotation. Absence is represented by convention. The right representation depends on whether the missing thing is a pointer, a numeric value, a resource, a lookup result, or a produced output.

| Absence pattern              | Example                                | Best use                                       | Strength                      | Failure mode                                               |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| Null pointer                 | `T *p = NULL`                          | Optional object reference or failed allocation | Simple and conventional       | Dereference before check                                   |
| Sentinel integer             | `return -1;`                           | Small domains with spare invalid value         | Compact                       | Sentinel collides with valid value                         |
| Count sentinel               | `return n;` for not found              | Search in array of length `n`                  | Avoids signed/unsigned issue  | Caller forgets sentinel rule                               |
| Status plus output parameter | `int parse(T *out)`                    | Producing a value may fail                     | Separates success from result | Output state ambiguous on failure                          |
| Tagged struct                | `struct maybe_T { bool ok; T value; }` | Need explicit optional value object            | Clear local representation    | Boilerplate; copying/resource issues if `T` owns resources |
| Pointer to optional output   | `T *out` may be `NULL`                 | Optional output request                        | Flexible API                  | Ambiguous nullability if undocumented                      |
| Error object plus result     | Library-specific                       | Detailed diagnostics                           | Rich error reporting          | More complex lifecycle                                     |

Null pointer lookup:

```c
struct node *map_find(struct map *m, const char *key);

struct node *node = map_find(m, key);
if (node == NULL) {
    return -1;
}

use_node(node);
```

Status plus output:

```c
#include <stdint.h>

int parse_u32(const char *s, uint32_t *out)
{
    if (s == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    /* parsing omitted */
    *out = 0;
    return 0;
}
```

Search sentinel using length:

```c
#include <stddef.h>

size_t find_int(const int *a, size_t n, int target)
{
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (a[i] == target) {
            return i;
        }
    }

    return n;
}
```

Caller:

```c
size_t pos = find_int(a, n, target);
if (pos == n) {
    /* not found */
} else {
    /* found at pos */
}
```

This avoids returning `-1` from a `size_t` function. The sentinel is outside the valid index range but inside the type domain.

Optional struct:

```c
#include <stdbool.h>
#include <stdint.h>

struct maybe_u32 {
    bool ok;
    uint32_t value;
};

struct maybe_u32 parse_u32_value(const char *s)
{
    struct maybe_u32 result = { .ok = false, .value = 0 };

    if (s == NULL) {
        return result;
    }

    /* parsing omitted */
    result.ok = true;
    result.value = 0;
    return result;
}
```

This is clear for small values. For large or resource-owning values, returning structs requires more careful copy and cleanup design.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                             | Better rule                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Using `NULL` for every kind of absence            | Only pointer types naturally support null. | Match absence representation to domain.                            |
| Returning `-1` from unsigned functions            | Converts to a large unsigned value.        | Use `n` sentinel, status code, or signed result type deliberately. |
| Leaving output parameter uninitialized on failure | Caller may read garbage.                   | Define whether output is untouched, zeroed, or invalid on failure. |
| Treating null pointer as empty object             | Null means no object.                      | Do not dereference; provide explicit empty object if needed.       |
| Using sentinel that can become valid later        | API breaks when domain expands.            | Reserve sentinel formally or use status/result separation.         |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Return `-1` for not found; everyone understands that.”                                         |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A function returning `size_t` returns `-1`, which becomes a huge value and is used as an index. |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `size_t` is unsigned; conversion from `-1` wraps modulo the type range.                         |
| Professional rule of thumb      | For `size_t` searches, return `n` as the not-found sentinel or return status plus output index. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | POSIX uses signed count types such as `ssize_t`, but that is POSIX, not ISO C.                  |

### Finite State Modeling — `enum`, state machines, tags, and validation

C’s `enum` gives named integer constants and an enum type. It is useful for finite states, result codes, modes, and tags. But C enums are not closed algebraic variants: an enum object can hold values outside the listed enumerators through casts, external input, memory corruption, or ABI boundaries.

| Task                     | C mechanism                                          | When to use                            | Design meaning                    | Common pitfall                                            |
| ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Name finite states       | `enum state`                                         | Parser states, lifecycle states, modes | Symbolic integer domain           | Assuming invalid values impossible                        |
| Result code set          | `enum result`                                        | Small fixed error/status family        | Named status values               | Mixing with unrelated integer codes                       |
| Tag for union            | `enum kind` plus `union`                             | Variant data                           | Manual tagged union               | Forgetting to update tag with payload                     |
| Bit flags                | Unsigned integer constants, sometimes enum constants | Combinable options                     | Set of bits, not exclusive states | Using enum as if flags are mutually exclusive             |
| External protocol values | Fixed-width integer plus validation to enum          | Binary/API boundary                    | Explicit validation layer         | Casting external integer directly to enum and trusting it |

Basic enum:

```c
enum parser_state {
    PARSER_START,
    PARSER_KEY,
    PARSER_VALUE,
    PARSER_DONE,
    PARSER_ERROR
};
```

Switching on enum:

```c
int parser_state_is_terminal(enum parser_state st)
{
    switch (st) {
    case PARSER_DONE:
    case PARSER_ERROR:
        return 1;

    case PARSER_START:
    case PARSER_KEY:
    case PARSER_VALUE:
        return 0;

    default:
        return 1; /* defensive treatment for invalid value */
    }
}
```

A `default` is not always desirable in internal switches: omitting it can let compiler warnings detect unhandled enumerators. But at trust boundaries, defensive default handling is valuable. The decision depends on whether the value is internally controlled or externally contaminated.

Enum decision rule:

| Situation                                                             | Switch strategy                                                                          |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Internal enum value created only by trusted code                      | Consider omitting `default` and enabling warnings for unhandled enumerators.             |
| Enum value may come from file, network, cast, ABI, or untrusted input | Validate first or include defensive `default`.                                           |
| Public API accepts enum parameter                                     | Define behavior for invalid values or state that invalid values are contract violations. |
| Security-sensitive boundary                                           | Do not assume enum value is valid; validate explicitly.                                  |

Validation example:

```c
#include <stdbool.h>

enum color {
    COLOR_RED = 1,
    COLOR_GREEN = 2,
    COLOR_BLUE = 3
};

bool color_from_int(int value, enum color *out)
{
    if (out == NULL) {
        return false;
    }

    switch (value) {
    case COLOR_RED:
    case COLOR_GREEN:
    case COLOR_BLUE:
        *out = (enum color)value;
        return true;

    default:
        return false;
    }
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                                                                     | Better rule                                           |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming enum is closed                              | Invalid numeric values can exist.                                                  | Validate at boundaries.                               |
| Using enum for bit flags without clarity             | Enumerators suggest alternatives, while flags combine.                             | Use unsigned masks and clear names for flags.         |
| Forgetting default or validation for external values | Unexpected state can drive unsafe behavior.                                        | Validate before switching on external input.          |
| Treating enum size as fixed                          | Underlying representation is implementation-dependent within standard constraints. | Do not expose enum layout in portable binary formats. |
| Mixing unrelated enum families                       | C may allow integer conversions.                                                   | Keep APIs specific and avoid casts.                   |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                          |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “An `enum color` variable can only be `COLOR_RED`, `COLOR_GREEN`, or `COLOR_BLUE`.”                                  |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A value read from disk is cast to `enum color` and reaches a switch with no matching case.                           |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C enumerations provide named integer constants and a type, but do not enforce closed runtime variants.               |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Treat enum values crossing trust boundaries as unvalidated integers until checked.                                   |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Internally created enum values in tightly controlled code can often be treated as valid if invariants are preserved. |

### Tagged Unions — modeling variants manually

C does not have built-in sum types such as Rust `enum`, Haskell ADTs, Swift associated-value enums, or TypeScript discriminated unions. The idiomatic C representation is a **tagged union**: a `struct` containing a tag and a `union` payload.

| Component          | Role                                           | Example                                      |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Tag                | Records which variant is active                | `enum value_kind kind;`                      |
| Union              | Stores one payload at a time                   | `union { int i; double d; char *s; } as;`    |
| Access functions   | Maintain invariant between tag and payload     | `value_set_int`, `value_destroy`             |
| Destructor/cleanup | Releases active payload if needed              | free string only when active variant owns it |
| Copy policy        | Defines whether payload is shallow/deep copied | deep-copy string variant if owning           |

Example:

```c
enum value_kind {
    VALUE_INT,
    VALUE_DOUBLE,
    VALUE_STRING
};

struct value {
    enum value_kind kind;
    union {
        int i;
        double d;
        char *s;
    } as;
};
```

Access:

```c
int value_get_int(const struct value *v, int *out)
{
    if (v == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (v->kind != VALUE_INT) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = v->as.i;
    return 0;
}
```

The tag/payload invariant must be preserved manually. If `kind == VALUE_STRING`, then `as.s` must be the active meaningful field and its ownership must be defined. If a string variant owns memory, the struct needs a destructor-like cleanup function:

```c
#include <stdlib.h>

void value_destroy(struct value *v)
{
    if (v == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    if (v->kind == VALUE_STRING) {
        free(v->as.s);
        v->as.s = NULL;
    }

    v->kind = VALUE_INT;
    v->as.i = 0;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                                                         | Better rule                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Reading union member not indicated by tag         | Can violate semantic invariant and may run into effective-type issues. | Always check tag before reading payload.                 |
| Updating payload without updating tag             | Object becomes internally inconsistent.                                | Centralize construction/mutation in functions.           |
| Owning pointer inside union without destructor    | Leaks or double frees.                                                 | Define cleanup and copy policy.                          |
| Exposing tagged union publicly without invariants | Clients can create invalid combinations.                               | Use constructors/accessors or document strict contracts. |
| Assuming C union is a safe variant type           | It is overlapping storage, not a checked sum type.                     | Treat it as a low-level representation mechanism.        |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A union is C’s version of a safe variant type.”                                                                                                           |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Code reads `v.as.d` while `v.kind == VALUE_STRING`, producing meaningless data or worse.                                                                   |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A union overlays storage; it does not track which member is active. The tag is a separate programmer-maintained invariant.                                 |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Never expose raw union mutation widely; use tag-aware constructors, accessors, and cleanup functions.                                                      |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Very low-level code may inspect representation intentionally, but must obey the C object representation and effective-type rules or documented extensions. |

### Domain Concepts — aliases, wrapper structs, opaque handles, and invariant-preserving APIs

C programmers often want to model domain concepts such as user IDs, file offsets, buffer capacities, parser states, resource handles, and validated strings. C provides several strategies, each with different enforcement strength.

| Modeling option  | Example                                       | Enforcement strength            | Best use                    | Pitfall                           |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | --------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Plain primitive  | `int user_id`                                 | Low                             | Local simple code           | Domain mixups                     |
| `typedef` alias  | `typedef int user_id;`                        | Low                             | API readability             | No distinct type checking         |
| Wrapper struct   | `struct user_id { int value; };`              | Medium                          | Prevent accidental mixing   | More verbose; ABI/layout concerns |
| Opaque handle    | `typedef struct user User;`                   | High at representation boundary | Library-managed objects     | Requires lifecycle functions      |
| Enum             | `enum mode`                                   | Medium for named constants      | Finite labels               | Invalid values still possible     |
| Tagged union     | `struct value` with tag                       | Medium if disciplined           | Variants with payloads      | Invariant manually maintained     |
| Validated object | `struct email { char *s; };` with constructor | High if fields hidden           | Values requiring validation | Allocation and cleanup overhead   |

Typedef alias does not enforce domain distinction:

```c
typedef int user_id;
typedef int order_id;

void load_user(user_id id);

order_id order = 42;
load_user(order); /* allowed */
```

Wrapper struct creates a distinct type:

```c
struct user_id {
    int value;
};

struct order_id {
    int value;
};

void load_user(struct user_id id);

/* struct order_id order = { 42 }; */
/* load_user(order); */ /* incompatible type */
```

Opaque validated type:

```c
/* email.h */
typedef struct email Email;

int email_create(Email **out, const char *s);
void email_destroy(Email *email);
const char *email_cstr(const Email *email);
```

This design prevents clients from constructing invalid `Email` values directly, assuming the implementation does not expose the struct fields.

Domain modeling decision table:

| Need                                           | Prefer                              | Reason                                       |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Improve readability only                       | `typedef` alias                     | Lightweight naming.                          |
| Prevent accidental mixing of primitive domains | Wrapper `struct`                    | Creates distinct type at compile time.       |
| Hide representation and enforce invariants     | Opaque struct                       | Clients cannot mutate fields directly.       |
| Represent finite internal state                | `enum`                              | Readable state labels.                       |
| Represent external protocol field              | Fixed-width integer plus validation | Avoids trusting raw input as internal state. |
| Represent resource lifecycle                   | Opaque handle plus create/destroy   | Centralizes ownership and cleanup.           |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                         | Why it matters                            | Better rule                                                       |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using `typedef` for type safety                                 | Alias does not enforce domain separation. | Use wrapper structs when accidental mixing matters.               |
| Exposing fields of validated domain objects                     | Clients can violate invariants.           | Hide fields behind opaque types.                                  |
| Overengineering tiny local domains                              | Excess wrappers can obscure simple code.  | Use stronger modeling where incorrect mixing is likely or costly. |
| Hiding allocation behind opaque handles without clear ownership | API becomes hard to use safely.           | Pair opaque types with explicit lifecycle functions.              |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                         |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “`typedef int user_id;` makes `user_id` a separate type from `int`.”                                |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A plain `int` or another typedef alias is passed where `user_id` is expected.                       |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `typedef` creates an alias, not a new nominal type.                                                 |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use typedef for readability; use wrapper structs or opaque types for enforceable domain boundaries. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Tooling or coding standards may flag domain-mixing typedefs, but ISO C itself will not.             |

### Arrays, Pointers, and Object Bounds — modeling sequences without lying to the compiler

Arrays and pointers are central to C, but their relationship is often misstated. An array object contains elements. A pointer object contains a pointer value. In many expression contexts, an array expression is converted to a pointer to its first element. This conversion does not mean arrays and pointers are the same type or carry the same information.

| Representation                    | Meaning                                          | Carries length?                            | Assignable?               | Typical use                         |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `T a[N]`                          | Array object with `N` elements                   | Size known in declaring scope through type | No whole-array assignment | Fixed storage                       |
| `T *p`                            | Pointer to `T`                                   | No                                         | Pointer object assignable | View, dynamic storage, traversal    |
| `T (*p)[N]`                       | Pointer to array of `N` `T`                      | Size in pointed-to type                    | Pointer assignable        | Fixed-width multidimensional arrays |
| `T a[][N]` parameter              | Adjusted parameter for array rows of fixed width | Inner dimension retained                   | Parameter is pointer form | Matrix-like APIs                    |
| `struct { T *data; size_t len; }` | Explicit slice-like view                         | Yes, by convention                         | Struct assignable         | Safer sequence passing              |

Pointer plus length struct:

```c
#include <stddef.h>

struct byte_span {
    const unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
};
```

This makes the pair harder to separate accidentally:

```c
unsigned checksum_span(struct byte_span span)
{
    unsigned sum = 0;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < span.len; i++) {
        sum += span.data[i];
    }

    return sum;
}
```

This still does not prove `span.data` is valid for `span.len` bytes. It improves API shape but does not create memory safety.

Fixed-size array pointer:

```c
void clear_block(int (*block)[16])
{
    for (size_t i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
        (*block)[i] = 0;
    }
}
```

Call:

```c
int values[16];
clear_block(&values);
```

This is less common than pointer plus length but useful when the size is part of the type contract.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                                            |
| -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Saying “arrays are pointers”                 | Leads to `sizeof`, assignment, and bounds mistakes.      | Say “arrays often convert to pointers in expressions.” |
| Passing pointer without length               | Callee cannot know bounds.                               | Pass length, sentinel, or fixed-size array pointer.    |
| Assuming one-past pointer is dereferenceable | One-past is valid for comparison/arithmetic, not access. | Never dereference one-past.                            |
| Pointer arithmetic across unrelated objects  | Undefined behavior.                                      | Keep pointer arithmetic within a single array object.  |
| Treating span structs as memory-safe         | They carry length but do not prove validity.             | Maintain lifetime and bounds contracts.                |

### Object Representation, Alignment, and Padding — data modeling under the machine lens

Every C object has a representation in storage, but not every representation detail is portable or semantically meaningful. `unsigned char` access to object representation is special, but representation-level programming must be done with care.

| Concept               | Meaning                                                             | Why it matters                                           |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Object representation | Bytes that represent an object value                                | Needed for serialization, hashing, copying, debugging    |
| Value representation  | Bits participating in value                                         | Padding bits may not affect value                        |
| Padding               | Extra bytes/bits inserted for alignment or representation           | Raw comparison/serialization may include irrelevant data |
| Alignment             | Address constraint for object access                                | Misaligned access may be invalid or slow                 |
| Trap representation   | Representation that does not represent a valid value for some types | Reading may be undefined for certain types               |
| Endianness            | Byte order for multi-byte integer representation                    | File/network formats must define byte order              |
| Effective type        | Type through which object may be accessed                           | Affects aliasing and optimization                        |

Inspecting object representation:

```c
#include <stddef.h>

void dump_bytes(const void *ptr, size_t n)
{
    const unsigned char *p = ptr;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        print_byte(p[i]);
    }
}
```

Using `unsigned char *` to inspect bytes is a standard-sanctioned pattern. But writing arbitrary bytes into an object and reading it as a typed value may produce invalid values depending on the type and representation.

Struct padding example:

```c
struct example {
    char c;
    int i;
};
```

This struct often has padding between `c` and `i` so that `i` is properly aligned. `sizeof(struct example)` may be larger than `sizeof(char) + sizeof(int)`.

Dangerous raw equality:

```c
#include <string.h>

int equal_raw(struct example a, struct example b)
{
    return memcmp(&a, &b, sizeof a) == 0; /* generally poor semantic equality */
}
```

Padding bytes may differ even when fields are equal. Prefer field-wise comparison.

Interdisciplinary Lens: computer architecture and safety engineering
What it clarifies: alignment and padding exist to satisfy hardware and ABI constraints, while raw memory operations can accidentally expose uninitialized padding or create nonportable behavior.
Language feature involved: `struct`, `sizeof`, `memcpy`, `memcmp`, serialization, alignment.
Practical consequence: compare semantic fields; serialize explicit fields; avoid leaking padding bytes across trust boundaries.
Limit of the lens: hardware explains many layout pressures, but the legality of access is still governed by C’s object and aliasing rules.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters                                        | Better rule                                                        |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Using `memcmp` for struct equality                    | Padding bytes may differ.                             | Compare fields semantically.                                       |
| Writing raw structs to disk/network                   | Layout, padding, endian, and width may differ.        | Define explicit serialization format.                              |
| Assuming alignment never matters on tolerant hardware | Other architectures may fault or slow down.           | Respect alignment requirements.                                    |
| Reading uninitialized padding                         | Can leak data or affect nondeterministic comparisons. | Initialize structures deliberately and avoid raw padding exposure. |
| Treating object bytes as portable value encoding      | Representations vary.                                 | Use fixed formats for persistence/interchange.                     |

### Type Qualifiers in Modeling — `const`, `volatile`, `restrict`, and `_Atomic`

Qualifiers affect how objects may be accessed or what assumptions the compiler and programmer may make. They are part of data modeling because they express mutability, observation, aliasing, and concurrency contracts.

| Qualifier  | Modeling role                                         | Correct use                                      | Common misuse                                     |
| ---------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| `const`    | Read-only access path or immutable object declaration | Function promises not to modify through pointer  | Assuming deep/global immutability                 |
| `volatile` | Accesses must be performed as observable side effects | Memory-mapped I/O, signal-related narrow cases   | Thread synchronization                            |
| `restrict` | No-aliasing contract for pointer-based access         | Performance-sensitive array operations           | Adding without proving no aliasing                |
| `_Atomic`  | Atomic access and memory-order participation          | Shared data between threads under C memory model | Assuming it replaces whole synchronization design |

`const` API:

```c
size_t buffer_size(const struct buffer *buf);
```

This says `buffer_size` should not modify the buffer through `buf`. It does not say no one else can modify that buffer concurrently, nor does it say the object was originally declared `const`.

`restrict` API:

```c
void add_arrays(size_t n,
                int *restrict out,
                const int *restrict a,
                const int *restrict b)
{
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        out[i] = a[i] + b[i];
    }
}
```

The intended contract is that the accessed regions do not overlap in a way that violates `restrict` rules. If callers pass overlapping arrays incorrectly, behavior can be undefined.

`volatile` caution:

```c
volatile int flag;
```

This does not make `flag` a correct inter-thread synchronization primitive. For threads, use `_Atomic` or platform synchronization APIs.

Atomic example:

```c
#include <stdatomic.h>

atomic_int ready = 0;
```

Full concurrency semantics require memory-order reasoning and are covered in PART 7.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                                                 |
| ------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Casting away `const`                              | May lead to UB if object is not actually modifiable.     | Preserve const-correctness through APIs.                    |
| Treating `volatile` as lock-free synchronization  | Does not provide atomicity or happens-before guarantees. | Use `_Atomic`, mutexes, or platform primitives.             |
| Adding `restrict` for speed without contract      | Violations can produce UB.                               | Use only when aliasing assumptions are true and documented. |
| Assuming `_Atomic` makes compound invariants safe | Multiple atomics may still require protocol or lock.     | Design synchronization at invariant level.                  |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “`volatile` means the value is safely shared because the compiler must reload it.”                                          |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Two threads communicate through a volatile flag and still observe inconsistent data.                                        |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `volatile` controls certain optimizations around accesses; it does not establish inter-thread synchronization in ISO C.     |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use `_Atomic` or proper locks for shared data between threads.                                                              |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some embedded or memory-mapped I/O contexts require `volatile`, but that is not the same problem as thread synchronization. |

### Modeling Invariants — what the type system cannot say

Many important C invariants are not encoded in types. A professional C design identifies these invariants explicitly and chooses where they are enforced.

| Invariant           | Example                            | Can ISO C type system enforce it? | Typical enforcement                                      |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Non-null pointer    | `buf != NULL`                      | No                                | API contract, runtime check, static analyzer annotations |
| Valid length        | `len <= cap`                       | No                                | Centralized mutation functions, assertions, tests        |
| Buffer bounds       | `p` valid for `n` elements         | No                                | Pointer-length pairs, validation, sanitizers             |
| Ownership           | Caller must `free` result          | No                                | Naming, documentation, create/destroy conventions        |
| Active union member | `kind` matches payload             | No                                | Tagged union discipline                                  |
| Valid enum domain   | State is one of known values       | Not fully                         | Validation at boundaries                                 |
| Initialization      | Object was initialized before read | Partially, not generally          | Initialization discipline, warnings, sanitizers          |
| Thread-safety       | Object accessed under lock         | No                                | API design, annotations, code review                     |
| Encoding            | String is UTF-8                    | No                                | Validation and explicit metadata                         |
| Units               | Milliseconds vs seconds            | No                                | Wrapper structs or naming                                |

Example invariant-bearing struct:

```c
struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Essential invariant:

```c
/* Always true for a valid buffer:
 * - data is NULL only when cap == 0
 * - len <= cap
 * - data points to cap bytes when cap > 0
 */
```

Better mutation boundary:

```c
int buffer_resize(struct buffer *buf, size_t new_len);
int buffer_append(struct buffer *buf, const void *src, size_t n);
void buffer_clear(struct buffer *buf);
```

Worse pattern:

```c
buf.len = new_len; /* Direct mutation may violate cap invariant. */
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                               | Better rule                                                     |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Exposing invariant-bearing fields broadly | Any caller can violate internal consistency. | Hide representation or restrict mutation.                       |
| Relying only on comments                  | Comments do not enforce invariants.          | Add checks at boundaries and centralize operations.             |
| Encoding too little in types              | All `int` values look alike.                 | Use enums, wrappers, or opaque types where mistakes are costly. |
| Encoding too much with complex macros     | Can obscure semantics.                       | Prefer simple structs and functions when possible.              |

### Part 3 Interim Decision Table — data modeling choices covered so far

| Task                             | Prefer                                        | Use when                                   | Avoid when                                        |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Count bytes/elements             | `size_t`                                      | Describing object sizes and capacities     | Need negative sentinel                            |
| Represent raw bytes              | `unsigned char *` plus length                 | Binary data, object representation         | Text semantics or null-terminated string required |
| Represent C string               | `const char *` with null-terminated contract  | Legacy/string APIs                         | Data may contain zero bytes or no terminator      |
| Group fields                     | `struct`                                      | Fields form one conceptual object          | Need hidden invariants without API control        |
| Hide representation              | Opaque `struct`                               | Library API, resource owner, ABI evolution | Tiny transparent local data                       |
| Represent finite labels          | `enum`                                        | Internal modes/states                      | External unvalidated values without validation    |
| Represent variant payloads       | Tagged union                                  | Need alternative shapes in one object      | Cannot maintain tag/payload invariant             |
| Represent optional pointer       | `NULL`                                        | Absence of object reference                | Absence of scalar value                           |
| Represent optional scalar result | Status plus output or small optional struct   | Parsing, lookup, validation                | Sentinel collides with valid domain               |
| Represent sequence input         | `const T *p, size_t n`                        | Read-only view                             | Callee needs ownership or resizing                |
| Represent mutable sequence       | `T *p, size_t n`                              | Mutate existing elements                   | Callee needs to grow storage                      |
| Represent growable sequence      | `struct { T *data; size_t len; size_t cap; }` | Dynamic accumulation                       | Ownership and cleanup cannot be handled           |
| Prevent primitive-domain mixups  | Wrapper `struct`                              | IDs, units, validated quantities           | Local trivial arithmetic                          |
| Express read-only access         | `const T *`                                   | Function should not mutate through pointer | Need deep immutability or thread safety           |
| Express no-aliasing              | `restrict`                                    | Proven non-overlapping pointer regions     | Callers may pass overlapping objects              |
| Express shared atomic state      | `_Atomic`                                     | Simple atomic variables                    | Compound invariants require locks/protocols       |
### Input Constraints and Validation — range checks, shape checks, and trust boundaries

C types rarely express full input constraints. A variable of type `int` does not say whether the value must be positive, within a protocol-defined range, represent a file descriptor, or identify a valid enum state. A pointer type does not say whether the pointer is non-null, points to one object, points to an array of `n` objects, or remains valid after the call.

Professional C therefore treats validation as a **boundary task**.

| Boundary kind       | Example input                 | Primary risk                                         | Validation strategy                                                  |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Pointer boundary    | `char *s`, `void *buf`        | Null, invalid lifetime, insufficient size            | Null checks where contract permits; explicit length; caller contract |
| Numeric boundary    | `int n`, `size_t len`         | Overflow, negative values, wraparound                | Range checks before conversion/arithmetic                            |
| Enum boundary       | integer from file/network/API | Invalid state value                                  | Convert through validation function                                  |
| String boundary     | external `char *`             | Missing terminator, encoding issue, excessive length | Length-bounded APIs, maximum length, encoding validation if needed   |
| Binary boundary     | packet/file data              | Malformed layout, endian, truncated data             | Parse field by field; avoid raw struct casts                         |
| Allocation boundary | requested size/capacity       | multiplication overflow, allocation failure          | Checked arithmetic; handle `NULL`                                    |
| Ownership boundary  | pointer passed to API         | leak, double free, use-after-free                    | Document take/borrow/give semantics                                  |
| Thread boundary     | shared object                 | data race, torn invariant                            | Locks, atomics, thread-safety documentation                          |

Example: validating an integer before converting to enum.

```c id="lfbrrh"
#include <stdbool.h>

enum compression_mode {
    COMPRESS_NONE = 0,
    COMPRESS_FAST = 1,
    COMPRESS_BEST = 2
};

bool compression_mode_from_int(int raw, enum compression_mode *out)
{
    if (out == NULL) {
        return false;
    }

    switch (raw) {
    case COMPRESS_NONE:
    case COMPRESS_FAST:
    case COMPRESS_BEST:
        *out = (enum compression_mode)raw;
        return true;

    default:
        return false;
    }
}
```

Example: validating size arithmetic before allocation.

```c id="uq8891"
#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int allocate_ints(size_t count, int **out)
{
    if (out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = NULL;

    if (count > SIZE_MAX / sizeof(int)) {
        return -1;
    }

    int *p = malloc(count * sizeof p[0]);
    if (p == NULL && count != 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = p;
    return 0;
}
```

The multiplication check is part of data modeling. Without it, a huge `count` can wrap to a small allocation size, followed by out-of-bounds writes.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “The type `size_t` already means the value is a valid allocation size.”                                                     |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `count * sizeof(T)` wraps around, allocation succeeds with too little memory, and later writes corrupt memory.              |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `size_t` arithmetic is unsigned modulo arithmetic. It does not guarantee that a product fits.                               |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Check multiplication and addition before allocation-size computation.                                                       |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some platforms or libraries provide checked allocation helpers, but ordinary ISO C arithmetic still requires explicit care. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                                   |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Validating after conversion                          | Dangerous conversions may already have lost information. | Validate before narrowing or casting.         |
| Trusting enum-typed parameters from public APIs      | Callers can pass invalid converted values.               | Validate where values cross trust boundaries. |
| Trusting null-terminated strings from external input | Missing terminator causes unbounded reads.               | Prefer length-bounded input APIs.             |
| Treating allocation failure as impossible            | Embedded, constrained, or adversarial contexts may fail. | Define allocation-failure behavior.           |
| Checking `count * size` after multiplication         | Overflow may already have occurred.                      | Check before multiplication.                  |

### Conversions, Promotions, Narrowing, and Casts — modeling movement between type domains

C performs many implicit conversions. These conversions are useful for machine-oriented arithmetic but are also a major source of bugs. Conversion modeling asks: **is this conversion preserving meaning, changing representation, narrowing a range, or bypassing a type boundary?**

| Conversion mechanism         | Example                       | Safety level                                | Failure mode                               | Professional use                                 |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Integer promotion            | `char` to `int` in expression | Usually safe but surprising                 | Sign extension, changed expression type    | Understand expression evaluation                 |
| Usual arithmetic conversions | `int + unsigned`              | Context-dependent                           | Signed value converted to unsigned         | Align operand types deliberately                 |
| Assignment conversion        | `short s = x;`                | Context-dependent                           | Narrowing or implementation-defined result | Range-check before assignment                    |
| Explicit numeric cast        | `(uint32_t)x`                 | Depends on range and intent                 | Truncation, wrap, sign change              | Mark deliberate boundary                         |
| Pointer to `void *`          | `void *p = obj;`              | Common for object pointers                  | Type information erased                    | Generic APIs                                     |
| `void *` to `T *`            | `T *p = vp;`                  | Safe only if original object is compatible  | Wrong type access, alignment issues        | Allocation and generic containers with contracts |
| Cast away `const`            | `(char *)s`                   | Dangerous                                   | UB if modifying nonmodifiable object       | Rare compatibility boundary                      |
| Pointer reinterpret cast     | `(struct h *)buf`             | Dangerous                                   | Alignment/effective type/aliasing UB       | Avoid unless platform contract is explicit       |
| Integer-pointer conversion   | `(uintptr_t)p`                | Implementation-defined availability/meaning | Nonportable assumptions                    | Low-level diagnostics or hashing with care       |

Integer promotion example:

```c id="mtf2rl"
unsigned char a = 250;
unsigned char b = 10;

/* a and b are promoted before addition */
int sum = a + b;
```

Signed/unsigned comparison trap:

```c id="hhdfvh"
int i = -1;
size_t n = 10;

if (i < n) {
    /* May be false because i is converted to size_t */
}
```

Better:

```c id="bqf6vb"
if (i >= 0 && (size_t)i < n) {
    /* safe index use */
}
```

Narrowing check:

```c id="1pt0s3"
#include <limits.h>

int int_from_size(size_t n, int *out)
{
    if (out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (n > (size_t)INT_MAX) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = (int)n;
    return 0;
}
```

Cast design table:

| Cast question                                         | If answer is no                            | If answer is yes                                          |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Is the value in range for the target type?            | Do not cast yet; validate or reject.       | Numeric cast may be justified.                            |
| Is the pointed-to object actually of compatible type? | Do not dereference through target pointer. | Pointer conversion may be justified.                      |
| Is alignment sufficient for the target type?          | Use byte copying/parsing instead.          | Low-level access may still need effective-type reasoning. |
| Is `const` removal followed by mutation?              | Usually unsafe.                            | Only valid if original object is genuinely modifiable.    |
| Is the cast only silencing a warning?                 | Fix the type mismatch.                     | Keep a comment for non-obvious boundary casts.            |
| Is this crossing ABI/FFI boundary?                    | Avoid if not needed.                       | Check ABI documentation, not only C syntax.               |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A cast tells C to reinterpret the value safely as the target type.”                                                                             |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A byte buffer is cast to `struct header *` and works on x86 but faults or misreads on another architecture.                                      |
| Correct semantic explanation    | The cast changes the expression type. It does not guarantee alignment, effective type, endian correctness, or valid object representation.       |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Parse external bytes explicitly; reserve pointer reinterpretation for narrow implementation-defined boundaries.                                  |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some platform ABIs, compiler extensions, or packed-struct protocols may permit specific patterns, but they are not general ISO C modeling rules. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                                                    | Better rule                               |
| --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Comparing signed and unsigned values directly | Negative signed values may become huge unsigned values.           | Check nonnegativity before conversion.    |
| Narrowing without range check                 | Value may change.                                                 | Validate before assigning or casting.     |
| Casting `malloc` result unnecessarily         | Can hide missing declaration issues in old code; not needed in C. | Include `<stdlib.h>` and assign directly. |
| Reinterpreting byte buffers as structs        | Alignment, endian, padding, and effective type issues.            | Decode field by field.                    |
| Casting away `const` casually                 | May cause UB or violate API contract.                             | Fix const-correctness instead.            |

### Unknown and Untrusted Data — external bytes, parsing, and representation discipline

Unknown data is data whose shape, length, range, encoding, or trustworthiness is not guaranteed by internal invariants. In C, treating unknown data as already-typed objects is a common security failure.

| Data source           | Tempting but unsafe model              | Better model                                                  |
| --------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Network packet        | Cast buffer to `struct packet *`       | Check length, parse fields explicitly, convert endian         |
| File format           | Read raw bytes into public struct      | Define serialized representation independent of struct layout |
| Command-line argument | Trust `argv[i]` as valid number/string | Parse with bounds and error checks                            |
| Environment variable  | Treat as trusted string                | Check presence, length, encoding, allowed values              |
| IPC/shared memory     | Treat as local object                  | Validate version, size, alignment, synchronization            |
| FFI callback data     | Trust `void *userdata`                 | Establish ownership/type/lifetime contract                    |
| User-supplied length  | Use directly for allocation            | Check maximum and multiplication overflow                     |

Explicit binary parsing example:

```c id="q1696r"
#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

static uint16_t read_be16(const unsigned char p[2])
{
    return (uint16_t)((uint16_t)p[0] << 8 | (uint16_t)p[1]);
}

bool parse_header(const unsigned char *buf, size_t len,
                  uint16_t *type, uint16_t *payload_len)
{
    if (buf == NULL || type == NULL || payload_len == NULL) {
        return false;
    }

    if (len < 4) {
        return false;
    }

    *type = read_be16(&buf[0]);
    *payload_len = read_be16(&buf[2]);
    return true;
}
```

This avoids assuming host endian, struct padding, or alignment.

Unsafe style:

```c id="w2umj7"
struct header {
    uint16_t type;
    uint16_t len;
};

/* Dangerous for portable parsing */
const struct header *h = (const struct header *)buf;
```

This may fail because `buf` may be misaligned, shorter than `sizeof *h`, encoded in different endian order, or not an object of type `struct header`.

Security-oriented validation map:

| Invariant to establish          | Typical check                                      |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Enough bytes exist              | `if (len < needed) return false;`                  |
| Addition does not overflow      | `if (offset > SIZE_MAX - field_len) return false;` |
| Field value is allowed          | `switch` or range check                            |
| Length is within maximum        | `if (payload_len > MAX_PAYLOAD) return false;`     |
| String is terminated or bounded | Use bounded scanning; avoid unbounded `strlen`     |
| Encoding is acceptable          | Validate if text encoding matters                  |
| Resource allocation is bounded  | Apply configured maximum before `malloc`           |
| Object remains valid            | Define ownership/lifetime of parsed result         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters                                        | Better rule                                                                               |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Casting external bytes to structs                   | Nonportable and may be UB.                            | Parse byte by byte or with `memcpy` into properly aligned objects plus endian conversion. |
| Trusting length fields                              | Malformed input can cause overflow or OOB access.     | Check every length before arithmetic and access.                                          |
| Using unbounded string functions on external data   | Missing terminator causes OOB reads.                  | Use length-bounded parsing.                                                               |
| Confusing validation and sanitization               | A checked length does not validate content semantics. | Validate each invariant separately.                                                       |
| Parsing into owning structures without cleanup plan | Partial parse failure leaks memory.                   | Design rollback/cleanup paths.                                                            |

### Reusable Generic Helpers — `void *`, macros, `_Generic`, and code generation

C lacks full parametric generics. Generic programming is therefore achieved through several imperfect techniques. Each technique trades type safety, readability, performance, and maintainability differently.

| Generic technique     | Example                             | Strength                                       | Cost                                | Best use                                      |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| `void *` plus size    | `qsort`, generic buffers            | Runtime type-erased data                       | Manual casts, weak type checking    | Generic containers, memory operations         |
| Function pointers     | Comparator, visitor, callback       | Behavior parameterization                      | Indirect call, signature discipline | Sorting, iteration, event hooks               |
| Macros                | Type-specialized generated code     | Zero runtime overhead, type-specific expansion | Textual hazards, poor debugging     | Small generic utilities, intrusive containers |
| `_Generic`            | Compile-time type selection         | Some type-based dispatch                       | Limited expressiveness              | Overload-like wrappers                        |
| Code generation       | Script/template generates `.c`/`.h` | Type safety in generated code                  | Build complexity                    | Large repeated type-specialized APIs          |
| Manual specialization | `int_vec`, `str_vec`, etc.          | Simple and explicit                            | Duplication                         | Small number of important types               |

`void *` generic callback pattern:

```c id="41dzfi"
typedef int (*compare_fn)(const void *a, const void *b);

int compare_ints(const void *a, const void *b)
{
    const int *ia = a;
    const int *ib = b;

    return (*ia > *ib) - (*ia < *ib);
}
```

The function assumes that `a` and `b` point to valid `int` objects. The type system does not verify this at the call site once type erasure occurs.

Macro generic pattern:

```c id="u9oaq2"
#define ARRAY_COUNT(a) (sizeof(a) / sizeof((a)[0]))
```

This works only for actual arrays in scope, not pointers. A professional macro should be named and documented to avoid misuse.

`_Generic` example:

```c id="tqhr3o"
#define abs_value(x) _Generic((x), \
    int: abs_int,                 \
    long: abs_long,               \
    double: abs_double            \
)(x)
```

This can offer limited overload-like behavior, but it is not equivalent to templates or traits.

Generic technique decision table:

| Need                            | Prefer                                        | Reason                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Generic memory movement         | `memcpy`, `memmove` with explicit sizes       | C already models object representation operations this way. |
| Generic sorting                 | `qsort`-style `void *` plus comparator        | Standard idiom, but comparator contract must be exact.      |
| Type-safe vector for many types | Code generation or macros with careful design | Avoids repeated `void *` casts at usage sites.              |
| Small overload-like utility     | `_Generic`                                    | Safer than some macro tricks.                               |
| Public API clarity              | Type-specific functions                       | C users understand explicit APIs better.                    |
| Performance-critical inner loop | Type-specific or macro-generated code         | Avoids indirect calls and casts.                            |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
