| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Returning `NULL` is enough error handling for any function that returns a pointer.”                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Caller sees `NULL` but cannot tell whether the file was missing, permission was denied, allocation failed, or input was malformed. |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A null pointer represents absence or failure but carries little diagnostic information.                                            |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use null returns for simple absence; use status codes or error outputs when callers need to react differently.                     |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Internal helper functions may intentionally collapse failure detail if the caller cannot use it.                                   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters                      | Better rule                                                     |
| ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Ambiguous pointer ownership              | Leaks, double frees, use-after-free | Use naming and comments: borrow, take, create, destroy, release |
| Output parameter undefined on failure    | Caller may read garbage             | Set outputs to neutral values or document untouched behavior    |
| Returning internal mutable pointer       | Breaks invariants                   | Prefer `const` view or controlled mutator functions             |
| Overusing global state for output/errors | Non-reentrant and hard to test      | Prefer explicit outputs                                         |
| Ignoring `const` in input signatures     | Reduces usability and hides intent  | Use `const` for read-only borrowed data                         |

### Pass-by-Value and Pointer Parameters — mutation, copying, and aliasing

C function arguments are passed by value. This phrase is often misunderstood. Passing a pointer by value copies the pointer value; the callee can use that copied pointer to mutate the pointed-to object, but reassigning the parameter does not change the caller’s pointer variable.

| Parameter form    | What is copied              | Can callee mutate caller’s object? | Can callee change caller’s variable? |
| ----------------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `int x`           | Integer value               | No                                 | No                                   |
| `struct point p`  | Struct value                | No, only local copy                | No                                   |
| `int *p`          | Pointer value               | Yes, through `*p` if valid         | No                                   |
| `int **pp`        | Pointer to caller’s pointer | Yes, object and pointer variable   | Yes, through `*pp`                   |
| `const T *p`      | Pointer value               | Not through `p`                    | No                                   |
| `T a[]` parameter | Adjusted pointer value      | Yes, elements if valid             | No length carried                    |

Example: reassignment does not affect caller.

```c id="p4_pass_pointer_copy"
void reset_local_pointer(int *p)
{
    p = NULL;
}

int x = 1;
int *q = &x;

reset_local_pointer(q);
/* q is still &x */
```

Example: pointer-to-pointer changes caller’s pointer.

```c id="p4_pass_pointer_pointer"
void reset_caller_pointer(int **pp)
{
    if (pp != NULL) {
        *pp = NULL;
    }
}

int x = 1;
int *q = &x;

reset_caller_pointer(&q);
/* q is now NULL */
```

Example: struct by value.

```c id="p4_pass_struct_value"
struct range {
    int lo;
    int hi;
};

int range_contains(struct range r, int x)
{
    return r.lo <= x && x <= r.hi;
}
```

Passing small structs by value is often clearer and efficient enough. Passing large structs or owning-resource structs by value may be expensive or semantically wrong.

Aliasing issue:

```c id="p4_pass_alias"
void swap_ints(int *a, int *b)
{
    int tmp;

    if (a == NULL || b == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    tmp = *a;
    *a = *b;
    *b = tmp;
}
```

If `a == b`, this still works. But many functions do not behave correctly under aliasing unless designed for it. If a function requires non-overlapping regions, it must document that, and may use `restrict` only when that promise is real.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C has pass-by-reference when I pass a pointer.”                                                                                            |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A function assigns `p = malloc(...)`, but the caller’s pointer remains unchanged.                                                           |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C always passes the pointer value by value. To modify the caller’s pointer variable, pass a pointer to that pointer.                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use `T *` to mutate a `T`; use `T **` to create, replace, or clear a caller-owned pointer.                                                  |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some APIs return the new pointer instead of using `T **`; this is often clearer for `realloc`-like operations if error handling is precise. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                        | Why it matters                         | Better rule                                  |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Saying “pass by reference” loosely             | Hides the actual copy of pointer value | Say “pass pointer by value”                  |
| Passing large structs by value unintentionally | Copy cost or shallow pointer semantics | Pass `const T *` for large read-only objects |
| Passing owning structs by value                | Double-free or aliasing ownership      | Define explicit clone/transfer policy        |
| Forgetting aliasing cases                      | Same object may be passed twice        | Document and test aliasing behavior          |
| Using `restrict` without proof                 | Violating it can cause UB              | Use only for real no-alias contracts         |

### Writing Reusable Helper Functions — decomposition, locality, and invariant preservation

C helper functions should reduce duplication, isolate invariants, and make error handling auditable. They should not hide essential ownership or side effects behind vague names.

| Helper type                    | Purpose                   | Good example           | Poor example                             |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------- | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Predicate helper               | Encapsulate condition     | `buffer_is_empty`      | `check`                                  |
| Validation helper              | Establish input invariant | `parse_mode_from_int`  | `fix_mode`                               |
| Mutation helper                | Preserve struct invariant | `buffer_reserve`       | Direct field writes everywhere           |
| Cleanup helper                 | Release resource safely   | `buffer_destroy`       | Repeated `free(buf->data)` at call sites |
| Conversion helper              | Range-checked conversion  | `size_to_int_checked`  | Raw cast at every use                    |
| Internal implementation helper | Split complex function    | `scan_identifier`      | Over-fragmented one-line wrappers        |
| Error helper                   | Normalize error reporting | `set_error_from_errno` | Hidden global mutation without naming    |

Example: preserving an invariant.

```c id="p4_helper_reserve"
int buffer_reserve(struct buffer *buf, size_t min_cap)
{
    unsigned char *new_data;
    size_t new_cap;

    if (buf == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (buf->cap >= min_cap) {
        return 0;
    }

    new_cap = buf->cap == 0 ? 8 : buf->cap;

    while (new_cap < min_cap) {
        if (new_cap > SIZE_MAX / 2) {
            return -1;
        }
        new_cap *= 2;
    }

    new_data = realloc(buf->data, new_cap);
    if (new_data == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    buf->data = new_data;
    buf->cap = new_cap;
    return 0;
}
```

This helper centralizes capacity growth. Other code should not duplicate growth logic because duplicated logic often forgets overflow checks or `realloc` invalidation behavior.

Helper granularity decision table:

| Situation           | Helper is justified when                      | Helper is probably over-abstraction when           |
| ------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Repeated condition  | It encodes a named domain rule                | It is a one-off expression with obvious meaning    |
| Repeated cleanup    | It owns resource-release policy               | It only calls `free` once without adding semantics |
| Repeated conversion | It checks range or parses errors consistently | It merely wraps a cast without constraints         |
| Complex loop        | It gives a clear subtask name                 | It forces reader to jump for trivial code          |
| Struct invariant    | It prevents invalid field combinations        | Struct is plain transparent value data             |
| Public behavior     | It stabilizes API contract                    | It exposes implementation too early                |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Helpers are just for reducing line count.”                                                            |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Three call sites implement capacity growth; one forgets overflow checking and creates a heap overflow. |
| Correct semantic explanation    | In C, helpers are often invariant-preserving boundaries, not merely convenience wrappers.              |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Create helpers around invariants, ownership, conversion, validation, and cleanup.                      |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Very small local expressions can remain inline when abstraction would obscure rather than clarify.     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters                  | Better rule                                        |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Vague helper names                       | Hide side effects and ownership | Use verb-object names                              |
| Helpers that silently allocate           | Callers may ignore failure      | Return status and name allocation behavior clearly |
| Helpers that mutate through global state | Hard to test and reason about   | Prefer explicit parameters                         |
| Too many tiny wrappers                   | Obscures control flow           | Abstract concepts, not every expression            |
| Copy-pasted invariant logic              | Diverges under maintenance      | Centralize invariant-preserving operations         |

### Function Pointers and Callbacks — behavior as data

Function pointers allow C programs to parameterize behavior at runtime. They are the core mechanism behind callbacks, dispatch tables, comparators, visitors, plugin interfaces, and C-style dynamic behavior.

| Task               | Function pointer pattern                 | Example use                   | Main risk                            |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Compare elements   | `int (*cmp)(const void *, const void *)` | Sorting/searching             | Wrong cast or comparator contract    |
| Visit items        | `void (*visit)(T *, void *)`             | Traversal                     | Callback mutates collection unsafely |
| Handle events      | `void (*on_event)(event, void *)`        | Event loop                    | `userdata` lifetime                  |
| Dispatch operation | Table of function pointers               | Interpreter, driver, protocol | Missing or invalid function slot     |
| Abstract interface | Struct of function pointers              | Plugin, backend, allocator    | ABI and lifecycle complexity         |
| Cleanup callback   | `void (*destroy)(void *)`                | Generic containers            | Double free or wrong destructor      |

Comparator example:

```c id="p4_callback_cmp"
typedef int (*int_compare_fn)(const int *a, const int *b);

int int_compare_ascending(const int *a, const int *b)
{
    return (*a > *b) - (*a < *b);
}
```

Type-erased comparator shape:

```c id="p4_callback_void_cmp"
typedef int (*compare_fn)(const void *a, const void *b);

int compare_int_objects(const void *a, const void *b)
{
    const int *ia = a;
    const int *ib = b;

    return (*ia > *ib) - (*ia < *ib);
}
```

The second form is more generic but less type-safe.

Visitor with `userdata`:

```c id="p4_callback_userdata"
typedef void (*int_visit_fn)(int value, void *userdata);

void int_array_visit(const int *items, size_t n,
                     int_visit_fn visit,
                     void *userdata)
{
    if (items == NULL || visit == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        visit(items[i], userdata);
    }
}
```

Caller:

```c id="p4_callback_userdata_use"
struct sum_ctx {
    int total;
};

void add_to_sum(int value, void *userdata)
{
    struct sum_ctx *ctx = userdata;
    ctx->total += value;
}
```

This pattern emulates a closure: the function pointer supplies behavior, and `userdata` supplies captured state. But C does not type-check the relationship between callback and `userdata`.

Dispatch table:

```c id="p4_dispatch_table"
struct command {
    const char *name;
    int (*run)(int argc, char **argv);
};

static int run_help(int argc, char **argv);
static int run_version(int argc, char **argv);

static const struct command commands[] = {
    { "help", run_help },
    { "version", run_version }
};
```

This is often clearer than a long chain of string comparisons and `if` statements when commands are table-like.

Tradeoff table:

| Feature             | Problem solved                 | Capability gained                 | Cost introduced                          | Misuse encouraged              |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------ | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Function pointer    | Behavior can vary at runtime   | Callback and dispatch systems     | Indirect call; signature must match      | Casting function pointers      |
| `void *userdata`    | Emulates captured context      | Closure-like patterns             | No type checking of context              | Use-after-free of context      |
| Dispatch table      | Data-driven branch replacement | Extensible command/state handling | Table validity and initialization matter | Overengineering small switches |
| Destructor callback | Generic cleanup                | Container can own erased objects  | Wrong destructor corrupts memory         | Ambiguous ownership            |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                  |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A callback plus `void *userdata` is basically a safe closure.”                                                              |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | The callback casts `userdata` to the wrong struct type or uses it after the context object has gone out of scope.            |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C does not pair a function pointer with a checked environment. `userdata` is type-erased and lifetime-managed by convention. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Document callback signature, `userdata` type expectation, retention behavior, and reentrancy/thread-safety.                  |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some frameworks wrap callbacks in typed registration APIs, but the underlying C mechanism remains unchecked.                 |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                                     | Better rule                                                     |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Casting function pointers to incompatible types        | Calling through incompatible type can be undefined | Use exact typedefs                                              |
| Passing pointer to stack context retained after return | Later callback use becomes dangling                | State whether callback/userdata is used synchronously or stored |
| Callback mutates collection during traversal           | Iterator invalidation or inconsistent state        | Define mutation policy                                          |
| Missing null callback checks                           | Null function pointer call is invalid              | Check or document non-null precondition                         |
| Ambiguous ownership of `userdata`                      | Leaks or use-after-free                            | Document whether callee borrows, stores, or destroys context    |

### Composing Functions — pipelines, staged validation, and explicit error propagation

C composition is usually ordinary function composition through calls and return values. There is no built-in pipeline operator, monadic error propagation, exception unwinding, or `try` syntax. Composition must therefore make intermediate failure and cleanup explicit.

| Composition style     | Use when                              | Strength                    | Cost                                 |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Direct nested calls   | Expressions are simple and infallible | Compact                     | Poor for fallible operations         |
| Sequential statements | Each step may fail or mutate state    | Auditable                   | Verbose                              |
| Guarded stages        | Validation → conversion → operation   | Clear boundary flow         | More boilerplate                     |
| Cleanup block         | Several acquired resources            | Centralized release         | Requires disciplined labels          |
| Table-driven dispatch | Many similar operations               | Data-oriented extensibility | Less direct control flow             |
| Callback composition  | Caller supplies behavior              | Flexible                    | Harder lifetime/reentrancy reasoning |

Poor composition for fallible operations:

```c id="p4_comp_bad"
return write_output(transform(parse_input(path)));
```

This may be fine in a language with exceptions or result types. In C, it hides where allocation, parsing, transformation, and writing can fail unless each function has a clear sentinel convention. Usually, staged composition is better.

Staged composition:

```c id="p4_comp_staged"
int process_file(const char *path)
{
    struct input input = {0};
    struct model model = {0};
    int rc = -1;

    if (input_read(&input, path) != 0) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    if (model_parse(&model, &input) != 0) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    if (model_validate(&model) != 0) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    if (model_write_output(&model) != 0) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    rc = 0;

cleanup:
    model_destroy(&model);
    input_destroy(&input);
    return rc;
}
```

This is verbose but reviewable. It makes ownership and failure paths visible.

Composition by staging table:

| Stage                              | Purpose                               | Typical C pattern                          |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Validate raw input                 | Reject invalid shape early            | `if (!valid) return -1;`                   |
| Convert to internal representation | Establish typed invariants            | `parse_*`, `*_from_*`                      |
| Operate on validated data          | Main algorithm                        | Functions assuming internal invariants     |
| Produce output                     | Serialize, write, mutate caller state | Status-returning functions                 |
| Cleanup                            | Release all owned resources           | `goto cleanup` or structured destroy calls |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Good composition means compressing operations into one expression.”                                                       |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A nested call fails, but the caller cannot identify which step failed or release partially created resources.              |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C does not automatically propagate rich errors or unwind resources. Expression-level composition hides failure boundaries. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Compose fallible C operations as explicit stages with checked return values and clear cleanup.                             |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Small infallible value computations can be composed directly in expressions.                                               |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters                           | Better rule                                                  |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Deep nesting of fallible calls           | Error source and cleanup become unclear  | Use staged statements                                        |
| Ignoring intermediate return codes       | Silent data corruption or invalid state  | Check every fallible operation                               |
| Mixing validation and mutation too early | Partial mutation after invalid input     | Validate before mutating durable state                       |
| Cleanup order wrong                      | Later resource may depend on earlier one | Destroy in reverse acquisition order where dependency exists |
| Overusing global error state             | Composition becomes non-reentrant        | Prefer explicit status/error objects when needed             |

### Interim Part 4 Reference — behavior-task decision table

| Task                                | Preferred C pattern                                  | When to use                          | Common failure mode                 |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Reject invalid input                | Guard clause                                         | Before resource acquisition          | Missing cleanup after later guard   |
| Handle finite internal state        | `switch` over enum                                   | State machine, token kind            | Missing new enum case               |
| Handle external state               | Validate raw value, then switch                      | File/network/API values              | Trusting invalid enum value         |
| Iterate fixed sequence              | `for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++)`                     | Array or buffer traversal            | Off-by-one                          |
| Reverse iterate                     | `for (size_t i = n; i > 0; i--)`                     | Reverse array traversal              | Unsigned underflow                  |
| Mutate sequence                     | `T *items, size_t n`                                 | In-place transformation              | Wrong length or aliasing            |
| Delete while traversing linked list | Pointer-to-link technique                            | Singly linked list removal           | Use-after-free                      |
| Return fallible scalar              | Status plus output parameter                         | All scalar values may be valid       | Undefined output on failure         |
| Return owned object                 | `create(T **out)` or `T *` with documented ownership | Allocation-producing API             | Caller forgets destroy/free         |
| Mutate caller object                | `T *obj`                                             | Object state update                  | Null or invalid pointer             |
| Change caller pointer               | `T **out` or return new pointer                      | Allocation/replacement/reset         | Misunderstanding pass-by-value      |
| Parameterize behavior               | Function pointer callback                            | Sorting, visiting, events            | Wrong callback/userdata lifetime    |
| Compose fallible operations         | Staged statements plus cleanup                       | Parsing, loading, resource workflows | Hidden failure in nested expression |
| Preserve invariant                  | Helper function                                      | Capacity, validation, ownership      | Copy-pasted invariant logic         |
### Designing Public APIs — function sets, naming, contracts, and representation boundaries

C public APIs are usually expressed through **headers**, **function declarations**, **opaque or public structs**, **macros**, and **documented conventions**. Since C has no native package namespace, class system, trait system, visibility modifiers, or module-level access control in the modern sense, public API design depends heavily on disciplined naming and header boundaries. 

A good C API makes ownership, failure, mutability, lifetime, and representation stability visible.

| API design task            | Preferred C mechanism                         | Professional use                        | Failure mode                                |
| -------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Expose library identity    | Prefix exported names                         | `buf_init`, `buf_destroy`, `buf_append` | Global symbol collision                     |
| Hide representation        | Incomplete `struct` in header                 | `typedef struct buffer Buffer;`         | Clients depend on private fields if exposed |
| Express lifecycle          | `init/deinit` or `create/destroy` pairs       | Resource management                     | Leaks or double cleanup                     |
| Express fallibility        | Return status code or result enum             | Checked failure paths                   | Ignored return values                       |
| Express read-only access   | `const T *`                                   | Borrowed inspection                     | Missing const-correctness                   |
| Express mutable access     | `T *`                                         | Controlled mutation                     | Invariant breakage                          |
| Express array input        | `const T *items, size_t count`                | Explicit bounds contract                | Wrong count                                 |
| Express ownership transfer | Naming and documentation                      | `take`, `release`, `create`, `destroy`  | Ambiguous ownership                         |
| Express extensibility      | Options struct, callback table, version field | Future-compatible APIs                  | ABI freeze or flag explosion                |
| Express error detail       | Error enum/object/output                      | Recoverable diagnostics                 | Global or stale error state                 |

Example: opaque API header.

```c id="p4_api_header"
#ifndef BUFFER_H
#define BUFFER_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct buffer Buffer;

int buffer_create(Buffer **out, size_t capacity);
void buffer_destroy(Buffer *buf);

size_t buffer_size(const Buffer *buf);
int buffer_append(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
const unsigned char *buffer_data(const Buffer *buf);

#endif
```

This header exposes behavior but not fields. The implementation may change `struct buffer` layout without requiring clients to recompile source that does not depend on the layout. Binary compatibility still requires ABI discipline.

Example: implementation file.

```c id="p4_api_impl"
#include "buffer.h"

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

size_t buffer_size(const Buffer *buf)
{
    return buf == NULL ? 0 : buf->len;
}
```

This is object-like design in C: a hidden representation plus functions taking a pointer to the object. It is not native object orientation. There are no constructors, destructors, methods, inheritance rules, or automatic dispatch at the language level.

API naming table:

| Name pattern             | Meaning usually implied               | Example               | Caveat                                      |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `*_init`                 | Initialize caller-provided storage    | `buffer_init(&buf)`   | Must define valid state after failure       |
| `*_deinit` / `*_destroy` | Release resources held by object      | `buffer_destroy(buf)` | Must define whether pointer itself is freed |
| `*_create`               | Allocate and initialize object        | `buffer_create(&buf)` | Caller ownership must be explicit           |
| `*_free`                 | Release object allocated by API       | `buffer_free(p)`      | Should match allocation family              |
| `*_borrow`               | Return non-owning reference           | `map_borrow_value`    | Lifetime must be documented                 |
| `*_take`                 | Callee takes ownership                | `buffer_take_data`    | Caller must stop using transferred object   |
| `*_release`              | Caller receives ownership from object | `buffer_release_data` | Object state after release must be defined  |
| `*_view` / `*_data`      | Return internal view                  | `buffer_data`         | Invalidation rules must be documented       |
| `*_from_*`               | Conversion/parsing                    | `mode_from_int`       | Failure behavior must be explicit           |

Tradeoff table:

| API choice       | Problem solved                      | Capability gained                      | Cost introduced                    | Programs that benefit                      |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Opaque handle    | Hide fields and preserve invariants | Representation freedom                 | Requires function calls for access | Libraries and ABI-stable components        |
| Public struct    | Simple direct access                | Low ceremony and easy stack allocation | Layout and invariants exposed      | Small internal modules, plain data records |
| `init/deinit`    | Caller controls storage             | Avoids heap allocation                 | More state-management burden       | Embedded, stack-friendly APIs              |
| `create/destroy` | API controls allocation             | Simpler lifecycle for clients          | Heap allocation and failure paths  | Complex objects with private layout        |
| Options struct   | Extensible parameter set            | Easier future compatibility            | Initialization/versioning burden   | Public APIs likely to grow                 |
| Callback table   | Behavior extension                  | Pluggable backends                     | ABI and lifetime complexity        | Drivers, plugins, allocators               |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A public struct is convenient; clients can just set the fields they need.”                                            |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Later versions need to change field layout or preserve `len <= cap`, but clients already mutate fields directly.       |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Public fields are part of the source and often ABI contract; C does not enforce invariants around direct field writes. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Expose public structs only for plain data. Use opaque types for invariant-bearing or evolvable objects.                |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Internal project-local structs may be public within a small module if representation stability is not a concern.       |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters                                  | Better rule                                                     |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Exporting unprefixed symbols                          | C has no package namespace for external linkage | Prefix public API names                                         |
| Exposing invariant-bearing fields                     | Clients can break object consistency            | Use opaque structs or controlled setters                        |
| Inconsistent lifecycle pairs                          | Users cannot infer cleanup responsibility       | Pair `create/destroy`, `init/deinit`, `open/close` consistently |
| Hiding allocation in innocent names                   | Callers miss failure and cleanup                | Use names that signal allocation                                |
| Returning internal pointer without invalidation rules | Caller may use stale pointer                    | Document lifetime and mutation invalidation                     |

### Functions vs Object-Like Interfaces — plain functions, handles, method tables, and dispatch

C has functions and structs, but not classes. Object-like design is possible by combining a struct pointer with functions that operate on it. Dynamic behavior can be added with function-pointer tables. The decision should depend on state, invariants, substitutability, and lifetime needs.

| Design option                       | Shape                                          | Best use                                | Cost                                 |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Plain function                      | `int parse_u32(const char *s, uint32_t *out)`  | Stateless operation                     | No persistent configuration          |
| Function family with explicit state | `parser_init`, `parser_feed`, `parser_destroy` | Stateful process                        | Lifecycle management                 |
| Opaque handle                       | `Parser *p`                                    | Hidden state/invariants                 | Heap or caller-storage policy needed |
| Public struct plus functions        | `struct parser p; parser_feed(&p, ...)`        | Internal or stable transparent data     | Fields exposed                       |
| Function-pointer table              | `ops->read(ctx, ...)`                          | Pluggable backend                       | Indirect calls and ABI contracts     |
| Tagged manual dispatch              | `switch (kind)`                                | Small finite behavior set               | Switch maintenance                   |
| Macro specialization                | Generated type-specific behavior               | Performance-sensitive generic-like code | Debuggability and macro hazards      |

Plain function:

```c id="p4_plain_function"
int clamp_int(int x, int lo, int hi)
{
    if (x < lo) {
        return lo;
    }

    if (x > hi) {
        return hi;
    }

    return x;
}
```

Stateful object-like interface:

```c id="p4_object_like"
typedef struct parser Parser;

int parser_create(Parser **out);
void parser_destroy(Parser *parser);
int parser_feed(Parser *parser, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
int parser_finish(Parser *parser);
```

Method-table style:

```c id="p4_method_table"
struct stream_ops {
    int (*read)(void *ctx, unsigned char *dst, size_t cap, size_t *out_n);
    int (*close)(void *ctx);
};

struct stream {
    void *ctx;
    const struct stream_ops *ops;
};
```

Use:

```c id="p4_method_table_use"
int stream_read(struct stream *s, unsigned char *dst, size_t cap, size_t *out_n)
{
    if (s == NULL || s->ops == NULL || s->ops->read == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    return s->ops->read(s->ctx, dst, cap, out_n);
}
```

This is similar to a manually implemented interface or vtable. It is powerful, but every contract is manual: `ctx` type, lifetime, ownership, thread-safety, reentrancy, and ABI stability.

Decision table:

| Need                                  | Prefer                               | Reason                           |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| Stateless computation                 | Plain function                       | Simple, testable, no lifecycle   |
| Mutable state with invariants         | Opaque handle plus functions         | Centralizes state changes        |
| Stack allocation and transparent data | Public struct plus `init/deinit`     | Avoids heap, useful internally   |
| Multiple interchangeable backends     | Function-pointer table               | Manual dynamic dispatch          |
| Small finite behavior variants        | `enum` plus `switch`                 | Simpler than vtable              |
| Performance-critical fixed behavior   | Direct functions or static dispatch  | Avoids indirect call overhead    |
| Public ABI stability                  | Opaque handle or versioned ops table | Avoids exposing layout too early |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                                       |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C object-like APIs should imitate C++ classes.”                                                                                                                  |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | API hides ownership poorly, lacks cleanup symmetry, and uses function pointers where a simple `switch` would suffice.                                             |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C does not provide constructors, destructors, inheritance, method lookup, or access control. Object-like design is a convention built from structs and functions. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use object-like APIs to protect state and lifecycle, not to imitate class syntax.                                                                                 |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Large plugin or driver systems may need vtable-like designs because runtime substitutability is real.                                                             |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters              | Better rule                                                    |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Building vtables for two simple cases                   | Over-abstraction            | Use `switch` or plain functions until substitutability is real |
| Public object fields plus “setter” functions            | Invariants still bypassable | Hide fields if setters matter                                  |
| No clear ownership of `ctx` in function table           | Use-after-free or leak      | Define who owns context and when it is destroyed               |
| Calling function pointer without null check or contract | Null call is invalid        | Validate or document non-null table invariants                 |
| Assuming object-like C has RAII                         | Cleanup is not automatic    | Provide explicit destroy/close functions                       |

### Composition Without Inheritance — struct embedding, delegation, tables, and explicit interfaces

C has no inheritance. It does, however, support patterns that solve some problems inheritance is often used for in other languages: sharing data layout, delegating behavior, grouping operations, and substituting implementations.

| Goal often solved by inheritance elsewhere | C pattern                           | Use when                                   | Risk                                         |
| ------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| Reuse fields                               | Struct embedding as first member    | Intrusive structures, common header layout | Unsafe downcasts if assumptions undocumented |
| Reuse behavior                             | Helper functions over shared struct | Common operations                          | Weak encapsulation                           |
| Runtime polymorphism                       | Function-pointer table              | Backend/plugin/driver                      | ABI and lifetime complexity                  |
| Compile-time specialization                | Macros or generated code            | Type-specific containers                   | Macro complexity                             |
| Variant behavior                           | Tagged union plus switch            | Closed set of alternatives                 | Tag maintenance                              |
| Interface boundary                         | Opaque type plus function family    | Library abstraction                        | Boilerplate                                  |

Struct embedding example:

```c id="p4_embed_struct"
struct list_node {
    struct list_node *next;
};

struct task {
    struct list_node link;
    int priority;
    const char *name;
};
```

This allows intrusive data structures where the node is stored inside the containing object. Recovering the containing object usually requires an offset-based pattern. The standard macro `offsetof` can compute member offset, but common `container_of` patterns often rely on compiler extensions or careful nontrivial assumptions. This should be treated as low-level C, not ordinary application-style abstraction.

Delegation example:

```c id="p4_delegation"
struct file_writer {
    FILE *fp;
};

int file_writer_write(struct file_writer *w, const void *data, size_t n)
{
    if (w == NULL || w->fp == NULL || data == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    return fwrite(data, 1, n, w->fp) == n ? 0 : -1;
}
```

A more generic writer interface:

```c id="p4_writer_interface"
typedef int (*write_fn)(void *ctx, const void *data, size_t n);

struct writer {
    void *ctx;
    write_fn write;
};
```

This can represent file writers, memory writers, socket writers, or test writers. The cost is manual context typing and lifecycle management.

Comparison table:

| Pattern                          | Coupling                          | Maintainability impact             | Best use                                  |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Direct helper functions          | Low to medium                     | Clear if names are precise         | Shared algorithms                         |
| Struct embedding                 | Medium to high                    | Efficient but layout-coupled       | Intrusive low-level structures            |
| Tagged union dispatch            | Medium                            | Easy for closed alternatives       | Parsers, ASTs, protocol variants          |
| Function-pointer interface       | Medium                            | Good when substitutability matters | Backends, plugins, callbacks              |
| Macro-generated behavior         | High                              | Harder debugging                   | Performance-sensitive type specialization |
| Manual copy-paste specialization | Low abstraction, high duplication | Acceptable for few types           | Small codebases or critical clarity       |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Struct embedding is inheritance.”                                                                                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Code assumes every embedded `list_node *` can safely be converted to a `struct task *`, but the node actually belongs to another container type. |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C struct embedding creates storage layout, not a type hierarchy or runtime type identity.                                                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use embedding only with strict ownership/type conventions and narrow APIs.                                                                       |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Kernel-style intrusive data structures use these patterns effectively, but they depend on mature conventions and review discipline.              |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                    | Why it matters                                        | Better rule                                   |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Treating embedding as subtype polymorphism                 | C does not check dynamic type                         | Keep container recovery local and well-tested |
| Exposing embedded layout publicly                          | ABI and representation freeze                         | Hide intrusive details where possible         |
| Using function-pointer interfaces for closed tiny variants | Adds needless indirection                             | Prefer enum plus switch                       |
| Using tagged union for open plugin systems                 | Requires editing central switch for every new variant | Use function tables for open extension        |
| Mixing ownership of embedded nodes                         | Removal/free bugs                                     | Define container ownership clearly            |

### Macros and Compile-Time Abstraction — when textual abstraction is justified

Macros are C’s most powerful and most dangerous abstraction tool. They operate before type checking, which means they can express things ordinary functions cannot, but they can also break expectations about evaluation, scope, and diagnostics.

| Macro task               | Appropriate?     | Example                             | Better alternative when possible                                 |
| ------------------------ | ---------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Include guard            | Yes              | `#ifndef BUFFER_H`                  | None in portable ISO C; `#pragma once` is common but nonstandard |
| Conditional compilation  | Yes, if isolated | `#if defined(_WIN32)`               | Build-system feature detection where possible                    |
| Simple constant          | Sometimes        | `#define MAX_PATH_LEN 4096`         | `enum` constant or `static const` depending on use               |
| Function-like utility    | Sometimes        | `#define MIN(a,b)`                  | `static inline` function if type-specific                        |
| Generic expression       | Sometimes        | `ARRAY_COUNT(a)`                    | No direct function equivalent for actual array size              |
| Code generation          | Sometimes        | Declare repeated type-specific APIs | External generator or carefully tested macro                     |
| Control-flow abstraction | Rarely           | Macro that returns/gotos            | Usually a readability hazard                                     |
| Type selection           | Sometimes        | `_Generic` macro wrapper            | Type-specific named functions                                    |

Safe-ish macro style:

```c id="p4_macro_array_count"
#define ARRAY_COUNT(a) (sizeof(a) / sizeof((a)[0]))
```

This macro is useful because a function cannot receive an array and still know its compile-time element count after parameter adjustment. But it fails if passed a pointer.

Dangerous macro:

```c id="p4_macro_min_bad"
#define MIN_BAD(a, b) ((a) < (b) ? (a) : (b))

int x = MIN_BAD(i++, j++);
```

The chosen operand may be evaluated twice.

Type-specific inline alternative:

```c id="p4_inline_min"
static inline int min_int(int a, int b)
{
    return a < b ? a : b;
}
```

`_Generic` wrapper:

```c id="p4_generic_min"
static inline int min_i(int a, int b) { return a < b ? a : b; }
static inline long min_l(long a, long b) { return a < b ? a : b; }

#define min_value(a, b) _Generic((a), \
    int: min_i,                       \
    long: min_l                       \
)(a, b)
```

This is still limited. It selects based on the type of `(a)` and can behave unexpectedly if `a` and `b` have different types.

Advanced macro mechanisms:

| Mechanism         | Syntax                     | Use                                            | Caveat                                                |
| ----------------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Stringification   | `#x`                       | Turn macro argument tokens into string literal | Operates on tokens, not runtime values                |
| Token pasting     | `a ## b`                   | Build identifiers/tokens                       | Can create unreadable generated names                 |
| Variadic macro    | `__VA_ARGS__`              | Logging wrappers                               | Argument handling and trailing comma portability      |
| Conditional macro | `#if`, `#ifdef`            | Platform/features                              | Untested branches decay                               |
| `defined`         | `#if defined(NAME)`        | Feature checks                                 | Must distinguish feature macro from actual capability |
| `_Pragma`         | Macro-friendly pragma form | Diagnostic or compiler control                 | Implementation-specific use common                    |

Logging macro example:

```c id="p4_variadic_macro"
#define LOG_ERROR(fmt, ...) log_error_impl(__FILE__, __LINE__, fmt, __VA_ARGS__)
```

This is common but has portability and empty-argument details depending on standard/compiler support. In serious code, logging macros should be tested under the target standards and compilers.

Macro tradeoff table:

| Macro feature           | Problem solved            | Capability gained                | Cost introduced       | Misuse encouraged                      |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Textual substitution    | Compile-time code shaping | Zero runtime overhead            | Weak type checking    | Function-like macros with side effects |
| Conditional compilation | Platform variability      | One source supports many targets | Fragmented code paths | Feature spaghetti                      |
| Token generation        | Repeated declarations     | Boilerplate reduction            | Hard debugging        | Clever unreadable APIs                 |
| `_Generic` macro        | Type-based dispatch       | Limited overloading              | Complex edge cases    | Simulating richer type systems poorly  |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                          |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A macro that looks like a function behaves like a function.”                                                                        |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A macro evaluates `i++` twice, changing program state unexpectedly.                                                                  |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Function-like macros substitute tokens before type checking and do not guarantee single evaluation.                                  |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Prefer `static inline` functions when type-specific function semantics are enough.                                                   |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Macros are justified when the abstraction needs compile-time syntax, array size, conditional compilation, or generated declarations. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                              | Why it matters                           | Better rule                                       |
| ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Side-effect macro arguments          | Multiple evaluation                      | Avoid or document strictly                        |
| Missing parentheses                  | Precedence bugs                          | Parenthesize parameters and expansion             |
| Macro hides `return` or `goto`       | Control flow becomes invisible           | Avoid except in tightly controlled internal style |
| Platform macros scattered everywhere | Portability logic becomes unmaintainable | Isolate platform branches                         |
| Token pasting for clever APIs        | Hard to search and debug                 | Prefer explicit generated code if large           |

### `inline`, `static inline`, and Function-Like Abstraction — performance without macro hazards

C supports `inline`, but its exact linkage behavior has historically been a source of confusion, especially across compilers and standard modes. For professional code, `static inline` in headers is a common pattern for small type-specific helper functions because it avoids external multiple-definition problems while giving the compiler inlining opportunity.

| Form                               | Typical use                                   | Linkage implication                   | Caveat                                                         |
| ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `static inline` function in header | Small helper visible to each translation unit | Internal linkage per translation unit | Code may be emitted per translation unit if not optimized away |
| Plain `inline`                     | Specialized standard semantics                | More subtle external definition rules | Easy to misuse across C standards/compiler modes               |
| External function in `.c`          | Normal callable API                           | One external definition               | Call overhead may or may not be optimized with LTO             |
| Macro                              | Compile-time substitution                     | No function symbol                    | Macro hazards                                                  |

Example:

```c id="p4_static_inline"
static inline int is_ascii_digit(unsigned char c)
{
    return c >= '0' && c <= '9';
}
```

This is safer than:

```c id="p4_digit_macro"
#define IS_ASCII_DIGIT(c) ((c) >= '0' && (c) <= '9')
```

because the function evaluates `c` once and has typed parameters. The macro might still be acceptable in constrained headers, but it is less semantically clean.

Decision table:

| Need                                          | Prefer                                                                               |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Type checking and single evaluation           | `static inline`                                                                      |
| Addressable function with one external symbol | Normal function definition                                                           |
| Compile-time array-size expression            | Macro                                                                                |
| Type-generic selection among functions        | `_Generic` wrapper                                                                   |
| Platform conditional declaration              | Preprocessor                                                                         |
| Performance in hot path                       | First write clear function; rely on compiler, profile, then consider `static inline` |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                                 | Better rule                                     |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming `inline` guarantees inlining                | It is not a mandatory command to the optimizer | Treat it as linkage/optimization hint mechanism |
| Putting non-`static` function definitions in headers | Multiple definition or linkage confusion       | Use `static inline` or one `.c` definition      |
| Replacing all small functions with macros            | Loses type checking and single evaluation      | Prefer inline functions                         |
| Inlining before profiling                            | Code bloat or no benefit                       | Optimize after measurement                      |

### Abstraction with Opaque Types — preserving invariants through incomplete definitions

Opaque types are one of C’s most important abstraction mechanisms. They let a header expose a type name and function contracts while hiding fields in the implementation file.

| Task                                   | Opaque type benefit                         | Cost                                |
| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Preserve invariants                    | Clients cannot mutate fields directly       | Requires accessor/mutator functions |
| Hide allocation strategy               | Implementation can change layout            | Lifecycle API needed                |
| Preserve ABI flexibility               | Struct size/layout hidden                   | Clients usually use pointers        |
| Separate interface from implementation | Header remains stable                       | More boilerplate                    |
| Enable resource ownership              | Create/destroy functions centralize cleanup | Caller must obey lifecycle          |

Header:

```c id="p4_opaque_header"
#ifndef PARSER_H
#define PARSER_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct parser Parser;

int parser_create(Parser **out);
void parser_destroy(Parser *parser);

int parser_feed(Parser *parser, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
int parser_finish(Parser *parser);

#endif
```

Implementation:

```c id="p4_opaque_impl"
#include "parser.h"

struct parser {
    enum parser_state state;
    size_t bytes_seen;
    struct buffer scratch;
};
```

Clients cannot write `parser->state` because the definition is incomplete outside `parser.c`. This is one of the few ways C can enforce representation hiding at compile time.

Opaque type variants:

| Variant                                             | Shape                                 | Use                                           |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Heap-owned opaque                                   | `create/destroy`                      | Implementation controls allocation            |
| Caller-storage opaque unavailable                   | Only pointer handle                   | ABI-stable library                            |
| Exposed-size opaque                                 | API provides `parser_size()` or macro | Rare; used in allocation-constrained APIs     |
| Public struct with private convention               | Fields exposed but documented private | Weaker; sometimes used for performance or ABI |
| Incomplete type plus `init` impossible without size | Requires heap or factory              | Natural consequence of incomplete type        |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Opaque types are just for hiding code style preferences.”                                      |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Public struct fields are changed by client code, breaking a newly added invariant.              |
| Correct semantic explanation    | In C, hiding the struct definition is a compile-time barrier against field access.              |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use opaque types when invariants, resource ownership, or ABI evolution matter.                  |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Plain data transfer structs can remain public if their layout is intentionally part of the API. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                          | Better rule                                       |
| ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Opaque type without clear destroy function       | Ownership unclear                       | Pair creation with destruction                    |
| Too many trivial opaque types                    | Boilerplate without value               | Hide only meaningful invariants or representation |
| Returning mutable internal pointers              | Opaqueness defeated                     | Return const views or controlled handles          |
| Exposing size/layout indirectly without ABI plan | Clients depend on representation anyway | Treat any layout exposure as contract             |
| Mixing allocation families                       | Freeing with wrong allocator            | Provide matching destroy function                 |

### Designing Callback-Based APIs — synchronous, asynchronous, retained, and reentrant callbacks

Callback APIs require more than a function pointer. They require a contract about when the callback is called, what it may do, whether `userdata` is retained, and whether the callback can reenter the library.

| Callback contract dimension | Question                                                 | Why it matters                    |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Timing                      | Is callback called before registration returns or later? | Determines `userdata` lifetime    |
| Retention                   | Does callee store callback/userdata?                     | Stack context may become dangling |
| Thread                      | Which thread calls the callback?                         | Synchronization and thread safety |
| Reentrancy                  | May callback call back into the same API?                | Deadlock or state corruption      |
| Mutation                    | May callback mutate the collection being traversed?      | Iterator invalidation             |
| Error propagation           | How does callback signal failure?                        | Traversal may need to stop        |
| Ownership                   | Who owns callback context?                               | Cleanup responsibility            |
| Nullability                 | May callback be `NULL`?                                  | Optional behavior or invalid call |

Synchronous callback API:

```c id="p4_sync_callback"
typedef int (*line_fn)(const char *line, void *userdata);

int read_lines(FILE *fp, line_fn on_line, void *userdata)
{
    char buf[1024];

    if (fp == NULL || on_line == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    while (fgets(buf, sizeof buf, fp) != NULL) {
        if (on_line(buf, userdata) != 0) {
            return -1;
        }
    }

    return ferror(fp) ? -1 : 0;
}
```

Since `on_line` is called synchronously during `read_lines`, `userdata` may safely point to a stack object owned by the caller for the duration of the call.

Registration-style retained callback:

```c id="p4_retained_callback"
typedef void (*event_fn)(int event, void *userdata);

int event_loop_set_handler(struct event_loop *loop,
                           event_fn handler,
                           void *userdata);
```

This signature alone does not say whether `userdata` must outlive the event loop. The documentation must say so.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Passing `userdata` to a callback API is safe as long as the pointer type matches what the callback expects.”                                   |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A retained callback later uses a pointer to a stack object that no longer exists.                                                               |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C does not track callback context lifetime. Synchronous and retained callbacks have different lifetime requirements.                            |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Every callback API must state whether callback/userdata are called synchronously, retained, invoked from another thread, or allowed to reenter. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some APIs copy context data internally, but that must be explicit in the contract.                                                              |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters               | Better rule                                             |
| -------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Retaining stack `userdata`                   | Dangling pointer             | Use heap/static storage or unregister before scope ends |
| Callback reenters locked API                 | Deadlock or corrupted state  | Define reentrancy rules                                 |
| Callback mutates collection during traversal | Invalidates traversal        | Explicitly allow or forbid                              |
| Callback error ignored                       | Caller cannot stop operation | Define callback return convention                       |
| Calling callback under internal lock         | User code may deadlock       | Avoid or document lock behavior                         |

### Designing State Machines — explicit states, transition functions, and invariant control

C is commonly used for parsers, protocol handlers, embedded controllers, and interpreters. These often benefit from explicit state-machine design.

| State-machine component | C representation                            | Purpose                       |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| State label             | `enum state`                                | Names finite states           |
| Context                 | `struct machine`                            | Stores current state and data |
| Transition function     | `int machine_step(struct machine *, input)` | Centralizes state change      |
| Event/input type        | `enum event` or byte/token                  | Drives transition             |
| Error state             | Dedicated enum value or return code         | Handles invalid transitions   |
| Transition table        | Array of function pointers or structs       | Data-driven machine           |
| Validation              | `switch` with default/error                 | Prevents invalid state use    |

Switch-based state machine:

```c id="p4_state_machine"
enum parse_state {
    PS_START,
    PS_KEY,
    PS_VALUE,
    PS_DONE,
    PS_ERROR
};

struct parser {
    enum parse_state state;
    size_t pos;
};

int parser_step(struct parser *p, unsigned char ch)
{
    if (p == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    switch (p->state) {
    case PS_START:
        if (is_key_start(ch)) {
            p->state = PS_KEY;
            return 0;
        }
        p->state = PS_ERROR;
        return -1;

    case PS_KEY:
        if (ch == '=') {
            p->state = PS_VALUE;
            return 0;
        }
        if (is_key_char(ch)) {
            return 0;
        }
        p->state = PS_ERROR;
        return -1;

    case PS_VALUE:
        if (ch == '\n') {
            p->state = PS_DONE;
        }
        return 0;

    case PS_DONE:
        return 0;

    case PS_ERROR:
    default:
        return -1;
    }
}
```

Table-driven state transition can be useful when transitions are regular and data-like, but it can also obscure complex conditions.

| Design choice               | Best use                | Failure mode                |
| --------------------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Central transition function | Small/medium machines   | Large switch may grow       |
| One function per state      | Complex state behavior  | Scattered transition logic  |
| Transition table            | Regular finite automata | Hard to encode side effects |
| State plus flags            | Orthogonal conditions   | Invalid flag combinations   |
| Separate sub-machines       | Complex protocol layers | Coordination complexity     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                    | Why it matters                           | Better rule                                       |
| ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Mutating state in many unrelated functions | Hard to audit transitions                | Centralize transitions                            |
| No explicit error state                    | Invalid states leak into normal logic    | Use error state or error return                   |
| Treating enum as proof of valid state      | Invalid values can exist                 | Validate at external boundaries                   |
| Mixing parsing and side effects too early  | Partial side effects after invalid input | Validate/accumulate before committing             |
| Table-driven design for irregular logic    | Hard to understand                       | Use switch/functions when conditions are semantic |

### Designing Reusable Abstractions — macros, functions, structs, callbacks, or modules

Choosing the right abstraction mechanism is one of the main skills in C. Since C has few high-level constructs, overusing any one mechanism creates predictable failure modes.

| Abstraction need                       | Prefer                       | Why                                 | Avoid                                          |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Name repeated behavior                 | Function                     | Type checked, debuggable            | Macro unless necessary                         |
| Name repeated expression over one type | `static inline`              | Single evaluation and type checking | Function-like macro                            |
| Hide representation                    | Opaque struct                | Enforces field privacy              | Public struct with “do not touch” comments     |
| Parameterize behavior                  | Function pointer             | Runtime behavior selection          | Huge `switch` if open-ended                    |
| Represent closed variants              | Tagged union + switch        | Direct and efficient                | Function table if variants are fixed and small |
| Generate type-specific code            | Macro/codegen                | Avoids `void *` overhead            | Hand-copying many inconsistent versions        |
| Share private helpers                  | `.c` file `static` functions | Internal linkage                    | Public header pollution                        |
| Configure platform behavior            | Narrow preprocessor layer    | Isolate nonportable code            | Scattered `#ifdef`s                            |

Abstraction mechanism comparison:

| Mechanism            | Best use                      | Safety                  | Runtime cost           | Maintainability risk |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------------------- | -------------------- |
| Function             | Ordinary behavior abstraction | High by C standards     | Call may be optimized  | Low                  |
| `static inline`      | Small header helper           | High                    | Usually low            | Low-medium           |
| Macro                | Syntax/code generation        | Low-medium              | None directly          | High if clever       |
| Opaque struct        | Data abstraction              | High for representation | Function-call access   | Low-medium           |
| Function pointer     | Runtime polymorphism          | Medium                  | Indirect call          | Medium               |
| `void *` generic API | Type erasure                  | Low-medium              | Cast/callback overhead | Medium-high          |
| Tagged union         | Closed variant                | Medium                  | Low                    | Medium               |
| Code generation      | Repeated typed APIs           | High after generation   | Low                    | Build complexity     |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                         |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Since C lacks generics, use `void *` everywhere for reusable code.”                                                |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Callers cast returned values incorrectly, destructors mismatch element types, and debugging loses type information. |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `void *` erases type information without adding runtime checks.                                                     |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use type-specific functions unless genericity is real and the erased contract is carefully designed.                |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Standard-style APIs such as sort/search/container libraries may justify `void *` with size and callback contracts.  |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters              | Better rule                                                    |
| ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Macro abstraction for ordinary functions | Loses type checking         | Use functions or `static inline`                               |
| Opaque everything                        | Excessive boilerplate       | Hide representation only when it protects invariants/evolution |
