| Public everything                        | Invariant and ABI breakage  | Keep internal helpers `static`                                 |
| Premature function-pointer interfaces    | Harder code with no benefit | Use direct calls until substitutability matters                |
| Copy-paste specialization                | Divergent bugs              | Use macro/codegen only when repetition is real and tested      |

### API Compatibility and Evolution — stable contracts without native modules

C APIs evolve under source and binary compatibility constraints. The language does not provide a package-level compatibility system, so design choices become long-term commitments.

| Compatibility concern | Risk                                 | Design response                                      |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Public function name  | External symbol collision or removal | Prefix names; preserve old functions where needed    |
| Function signature    | Source and ABI break if changed      | Add new function instead of changing existing one    |
| Public struct field   | Layout and source dependency         | Use opaque structs or append-only policies when safe |
| Enum values           | Switch handling and ABI assumptions  | Add carefully; document default handling             |
| Macro behavior        | Source expansion dependency          | Avoid changing macro semantics silently              |
| Error codes           | Caller logic depends on values       | Version and document error families                  |
| Callback signature    | ABI and source break                 | Use versioned ops structs if likely to evolve        |
| Options               | Parameter explosion                  | Options struct with size/version field               |

Options struct pattern:

```c id="p4_options_struct"
struct parser_options {
    size_t struct_size;
    unsigned flags;
    size_t max_depth;
};

int parser_create_with_options(Parser **out,
                               const struct parser_options *options);
```

The `struct_size` field allows the callee to distinguish old callers from new callers if the struct grows.

Source-compatible extension:

```c id="p4_api_extension"
int parser_create(Parser **out);
int parser_create_with_options(Parser **out,
                               const struct parser_options *options);
```

Adding a new function preserves old call sites.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                        | Why it matters                      | Better rule                                           |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Changing public struct layout casually         | Breaks ABI and client assumptions   | Use opaque types or versioned structs                 |
| Reusing error code values for new meanings     | Breaks caller logic                 | Treat error values as contract                        |
| Changing macro expansion semantics             | Silent source behavior changes      | Prefer functions for stable behavior                  |
| Adding callback parameters directly            | Breaks all callback implementations | Use versioned ops tables or userdata structs          |
| Exposing compiler-specific types in public API | Reduces portability                 | Keep public API standard or clearly platform-specific |

### Avoiding Over-Abstraction — recognizing C-specific abstraction debt

Over-abstraction in C often looks like importing habits from object-oriented, generic, or framework-heavy languages without C’s runtime support. Because C abstraction is manual, unnecessary layers create lifetime and ownership complexity.

| Over-abstraction symptom                          | Why it hurts in C                   | Better alternative                       |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Vtable for two fixed cases                        | Indirect calls and lifecycle burden | `enum` plus `switch`                     |
| Generic container for one type                    | `void *` casts without benefit      | Type-specific container                  |
| Macro DSL for simple control flow                 | Debugging and readability suffer    | Ordinary functions/statements            |
| Opaque type for plain passive struct              | Boilerplate and allocation burden   | Public value struct                      |
| Deep callback chains                              | Error/cleanup propagation obscure   | Staged direct calls                      |
| Excessive configuration macros                    | Untested compile-time variants      | Runtime options or narrow platform layer |
| Hiding every field behind trivial getters/setters | No real invariant protection        | Direct fields for plain data             |

Example of unnecessary abstraction:

```c id="p4_overabstract"
struct int_ops {
    int (*add)(int, int);
};

int compute(const struct int_ops *ops, int a, int b)
{
    return ops->add(a, b);
}
```

If there is only one addition behavior, this is needless indirection.

Better:

```c id="p4_simple_compute"
int compute(int a, int b)
{
    return a + b;
}
```

Over-abstraction tradeoff:

| Problem solved                | Capability gained        | Cost introduced                                 | Programs that suffer                        |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Anticipating future variation | Possible extension point | More indirection, contracts, null checks, tests | Small stable programs                       |
| Hiding representation         | Encapsulation            | Boilerplate and lifecycle design                | Plain data records                          |
| Generic reuse                 | Less duplicated source   | `void *`, macros, callbacks                     | Type-specific critical code                 |
| Centralized framework         | Uniformity               | Control-flow opacity                            | Systems code needing explicit failure paths |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                 | Why it matters                                        | Better rule                                  |
| --------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Designing for imaginary extension       | Adds complexity now                                   | Add extension points when variation is real  |
| Hiding cost behind abstraction          | C programmers need allocation/copy/failure visibility | Make expensive operations visible            |
| Using callbacks where direct calls work | Harder debugging                                      | Prefer direct control flow                   |
| Building object systems casually        | Manual runtime with weak checks                       | Use established patterns only when justified |

### Avoiding Under-Abstraction — preventing duplication, invariant leakage, and unsafe repetition

Under-abstraction is equally dangerous. In C, repeated low-level operations often mean repeated bugs.

| Under-abstraction symptom           | Why it hurts                    | Better abstraction                  |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Repeated capacity-growth code       | One site forgets overflow check | `buffer_reserve`                    |
| Repeated raw `free` sequences       | Cleanup inconsistency           | `*_destroy`                         |
| Public mutation of invariant fields | Invariants break everywhere     | Mutator functions or opaque type    |
| Repeated parsing casts              | Validation differs by site      | Central parse function              |
| Repeated platform `#ifdef`s         | Portability logic fragments     | Platform adapter layer              |
| Repeated error-code mapping         | Inconsistent diagnostics        | Error helper                        |
| Repeated lock/unlock sequences      | Missing unlock on error         | Scoped cleanup convention or helper |

Example: under-abstracted growth logic.

```c id="p4_under_bad"
/* Site A */
buf->cap *= 2;
buf->data = realloc(buf->data, buf->cap);

/* Site B */
buf->cap += 128;
buf->data = realloc(buf->data, buf->cap);
```

Problems include missing overflow checks, failure handling, and inconsistent growth policy.

Better:

```c id="p4_under_good"
if (buffer_reserve(buf, needed) != 0) {
    return -1;
}
```

Under-abstraction decision table:

| Repetition type       | Abstract when                          | Do not abstract when                  |
| --------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Same invariant        | Always if nontrivial                   | Almost never                          |
| Same error cleanup    | Multiple resources or repeated pattern | One clear local cleanup               |
| Same arithmetic check | Overflow/range/security relevance      | Obvious local constant expression     |
| Same platform branch  | More than one call site                | Truly isolated one-off                |
| Same tiny expression  | It has domain meaning                  | It is syntactically obvious only once |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                         |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C should stay simple, so avoid helper layers.”                                                     |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A security check is fixed in one duplicated parser path but not another.                            |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Simplicity in C means local reasoning and invariant control, not copy-pasting low-level operations. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Abstract repeated invariants, validation, allocation, cleanup, and platform boundaries.             |
| Boundary where the rule changes | One-off simple operations may remain inline if abstraction would hide rather than reveal intent.    |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                         | Why it matters                       | Better rule                                 |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Manual cleanup repeated across functions        | Inconsistent release logic           | Provide destroy/close helper                |
| Direct field mutation everywhere                | Invariant not centralized            | Use functions for invariant-bearing objects |
| Repeated `#ifdef` blocks                        | Platform drift                       | Isolate platform-specific implementation    |
| Duplicated validation                           | Security holes                       | Centralize boundary validation              |
| Avoiding all helper functions for “performance” | Compilers can inline; bugs cost more | Profile before removing clarity             |

### Metaprogramming Boundaries — preprocessor, generated code, build system, and compiler extensions

C metaprogramming spans several layers: the standard preprocessor, `_Generic`, external code generation, build-system configuration, and compiler extensions. These are distinct and should not be confused.

| Metaprogramming layer         | Standard status                               | Use                                       | Caveat                        |
| ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Preprocessor macros           | ISO C                                         | Conditional compilation, token generation | Textual and weakly typed      |
| `_Generic`                    | ISO C11+                                      | Type-selected expressions                 | Limited and sometimes awkward |
| Include-file generation       | Build-system/tool-specific                    | Generated tables/APIs                     | Build reproducibility         |
| External code generator       | Tool-specific                                 | Large repetitive code                     | Debug/source mapping          |
| Compiler attributes           | Compiler or C23 subset depending on attribute | Diagnostics, ABI, optimization hints      | Portability                   |
| Linker scripts/symbol control | Toolchain-specific                            | Embedded/ABI layout                       | Not C language semantics      |

Generated-code approach:

```c id="p4_codegen_example"
/* generated: int_vec.h */
struct int_vec {
    int *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

int int_vec_push(struct int_vec *v, int value);
void int_vec_destroy(struct int_vec *v);
```

Instead of a `void *` vector, generated type-specific code can preserve type checking while reducing manual duplication. The cost is build-system complexity.

Compiler attributes should be labeled carefully. For example, `GCC` and `Clang` support many useful attributes for diagnostics and optimization, but they are not automatically portable ISO C. C23 standard attributes exist, but compiler support and exact use must be checked in the target toolchain.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters             | Better rule                                                   |
| --------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Treating compiler extension as C                    | Portability break          | Label extension and guard by compiler                         |
| Generating code without reviewing output            | Bugs become harder to see  | Check generated code into review or make generation auditable |
| Macro metaprogramming where codegen is clearer      | Debugging becomes painful  | Use external generation for large repetitive APIs             |
| Build-time configuration changes semantics silently | Hard-to-reproduce behavior | Record configuration and test variants                        |
| Assuming C23 feature support from standard alone    | Toolchains lag             | Check compiler support matrix in project policy               |

### Expression-Level vs Statement-Level Abstraction — when compactness harms correctness

C allows complex expressions with assignments, increments, calls, casts, and conditional operators. But compact expression-level abstraction can hide sequencing, conversion, and error-handling issues.

| Task                 | Expression-level form | Statement-level form             | Prefer                      |
| -------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Simple arithmetic    | `x = a + b;`          | same                             | Expression                  |
| Fallible operation   | `return f(g(h()));`   | staged checks                    | Statement                   |
| Resource acquisition | nested call           | explicit acquisition and cleanup | Statement                   |
| Conditional value    | `cond ? a : b`        | `if`/`else`                      | Expression if simple        |
| Complex mutation     | `*p++ = *q++`         | separated operations             | Statement if safety matters |
| Validation           | compound condition    | named predicate/helper           | Depends on clarity          |

Good compact expression:

```c id="p4_expr_good"
int max = a > b ? a : b;
```

Overcompressed expression:

```c id="p4_expr_bad"
return write_data(out, transform(parse(in), flags), get_len(in));
```

If any component can fail, allocate, mutate, or depend on evaluation order, this is poor C style.

Better staged form:

```c id="p4_expr_better"
struct parsed parsed;
struct transformed transformed;

if (parse(in, &parsed) != 0) {
    return -1;
}

if (transform(&parsed, flags, &transformed) != 0) {
    parsed_destroy(&parsed);
    return -1;
}

int rc = write_data(out, &transformed);
transformed_destroy(&transformed);
parsed_destroy(&parsed);
return rc;
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                    | Why it matters                         | Better rule                                 |
| ------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Multiple side effects in one expression    | Sequencing may be unclear or undefined | Split into statements                       |
| Hiding fallible calls inside arguments     | Return codes lost                      | Check each fallible call                    |
| Conditional operator with complex branches | Readability suffers                    | Use `if`/`else`                             |
| Relying on argument evaluation order       | Not generally guaranteed               | Compute arguments first                     |
| Clever pointer increments                  | Bounds/lifetime reasoning harder       | Prefer clear loops in safety-sensitive code |

### API Surface Minimalism — what not to expose

C APIs should expose the smallest surface that still allows correct and efficient use. Every exported function, macro, struct field, enum, and symbol is a compatibility burden.

| Candidate exposure     | Expose when                                   | Hide when                                   |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Struct fields          | Plain data and layout is intentionally public | Invariants, ownership, ABI evolution matter |
| Helper functions       | Useful to clients and stable                  | Internal implementation detail              |
| Macros                 | Need compile-time behavior                    | Function can express behavior               |
| Error codes            | Caller can act on them                        | Only internal diagnostics                   |
| Function pointer table | Users provide implementations                 | Only one implementation exists              |
| Constants              | Part of documented limits                     | Internal tuning value                       |
| Type definitions       | Public API uses them                          | Implementation-only detail                  |

Example: avoid exposing capacity if not part of contract.

```c id="p4_api_minimal"
size_t buffer_size(const Buffer *buf);
int buffer_append(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
```

Exposing `buffer_capacity` may be useful for performance-aware clients, but it also exposes more representation policy. The choice should be deliberate.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                              | Why it matters                  | Better rule                       |
| ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Exporting internal helpers           | Harder to change implementation | Use file-scope `static`           |
| Public macros for internal constants | Clients depend on tuning values | Keep internal constants private   |
| Overexposing struct layout           | ABI and invariant freeze        | Use opaque types                  |
| Adding API before real need          | Long-term maintenance burden    | Keep minimal, extend deliberately |
| Removing public symbol casually      | Breaks users                    | Treat public symbols as contracts |

### Part 4 Task-Pattern Reference — abstraction and composition decisions

| Task                              | Construct or pattern                   | When to use                                   | Tradeoff                              | Common pitfall                  |
| --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Simple computation                | Plain function                         | Stateless, infallible or simple failure       | Clear and testable                    | Overengineering with object API |
| Mutating object                   | `int f(T *obj, ...)`                   | Object state changes                          | Explicit pointer contract             | Null/lifetime ambiguity         |
| Reading object                    | `T f(const Obj *obj)` or status+output | No mutation through access path               | Const-correctness                     | Assuming thread safety          |
| Returning allocated object        | `int create(T **out)`                  | Allocation may fail                           | Clear status and ownership            | Undefined output on failure     |
| Managing lifecycle                | `init/deinit`, `create/destroy`        | Resource-owning type                          | Explicit cleanup                      | Inconsistent pair semantics     |
| Hidden representation             | Opaque struct                          | Invariant/API evolution                       | Boilerplate                           | Opaque trivial data             |
| Runtime behavior variation        | Function pointer/callback              | Real substitutability                         | Indirect calls and lifetime contracts | Wrong `userdata` lifetime       |
| Closed behavior variation         | `enum` plus `switch`                   | Finite variants                               | Simple and visible                    | Invalid enum values             |
| Generic operation                 | `void *` plus metadata/callbacks       | Type-erased container/API                     | Flexible but unsafe                   | Wrong cast/destructor           |
| Type-specific reusable expression | `static inline`                        | Small operation over one type                 | Type checked                          | Assuming guaranteed inlining    |
| Compile-time syntax abstraction   | Macro                                  | Array count, conditional compilation, codegen | Powerful but textual                  | Side-effect arguments           |
| Repeated invariant                | Helper function                        | Validation, growth, cleanup                   | Centralizes correctness               | Too-small vague wrappers        |
| Multiple resources                | `goto cleanup`                         | Several fallible acquisitions                 | Auditable cleanup                     | Arbitrary `goto`                |
| Plugin-like interface             | Ops struct                             | Open set of implementations                   | ABI/lifetime complexity               | No versioning                   |
| Table-like commands               | Dispatch table                         | Data-driven behavior                          | Extensible table                      | Null/mismatched function slots  |

### Abstraction Mechanism Decision Table — from lowest to highest ceremony

| Mechanism             | Ceremony    | Safety                  | Flexibility                | Best use                            | Avoid when                          |
| --------------------- | ----------- | ----------------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Direct expression     | Very low    | Depends on expression   | Low                        | Simple local computation            | Fallible or side-effect-heavy logic |
| Plain function        | Low         | Good                    | Medium                     | Most reusable behavior              | Needs compile-time syntax trick     |
| `static inline`       | Low-medium  | Good                    | Medium                     | Header helpers                      | Public ABI symbol needed            |
| Macro                 | Medium      | Low to medium           | High syntactic flexibility | Conditional/code generation         | Ordinary typed behavior             |
| Struct plus functions | Medium      | Good if disciplined     | Medium                     | Stateful abstraction                | Plain passive data                  |
| Opaque type           | Medium-high | High for representation | Medium                     | Invariant/resource/API boundary     | Trivial local struct                |
| Function pointer      | Medium-high | Medium                  | High runtime flexibility   | Callbacks/dispatch                  | Fixed direct behavior               |
| Ops table             | High        | Medium                  | High                       | Plugins/backends                    | Small closed variants               |
| `void *` generic API  | High        | Low-medium              | High                       | Generic containers/callbacks        | Type-specific code is clearer       |
| Code generation       | High        | Good after generation   | High                       | Many repeated type-specialized APIs | Small number of types               |

### Common Pitfalls and Anti-Patterns — Part 4 index

| Item                                  | Classification                 | Why it is harmful                     | Better pattern                            |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Arbitrary `goto`                      | Anti-pattern                   | Destroys local control-flow reasoning | Restrict to cleanup/error paths           |
| No cleanup path after acquisition     | Common pitfall                 | Leaks resources on failure            | Central cleanup label                     |
| Ignored callback lifetime             | Common pitfall                 | Dangling `userdata`                   | Document synchronous vs retained callback |
| Function pointer cast                 | Dangerous pattern              | Incompatible calls may be UB          | Exact typedef and signature               |
| Macro for ordinary function           | Context-dependent anti-pattern | Loses type checking                   | `static inline` or function               |
| Over-generic `void *` API             | Context-dependent anti-pattern | Type assumptions invisible            | Type-specific API or explicit metadata    |
| Public invariant-bearing struct       | Common design failure          | Clients break invariants              | Opaque type                               |
| Vtable for fixed two-case logic       | Over-abstraction               | Indirection without benefit           | `switch` or direct call                   |
| Copy-pasted resource cleanup          | Under-abstraction              | Inconsistent release logic            | Destroy helper                            |
| Returning internal mutable pointer    | Common pitfall                 | Invariant bypass and stale pointer    | Const view or accessor with lifetime rule |
| Callback under lock without contract  | Concurrency failure            | Reentrancy/deadlock risk              | Avoid or document lock behavior           |
| Hidden allocation in helper           | API design pitfall             | Caller misses failure/cleanup         | Name allocation and return status         |
| Expression-level fallible composition | Readability/safety failure     | Failure paths hidden                  | Staged statements                         |
| Scattered platform `#ifdef`           | Portability anti-pattern       | Untested fragments                    | Platform adapter layer                    |
| Changing public struct layout         | Compatibility failure          | ABI/source break                      | Opaque or versioned struct                |

### What Experienced C Programmers Notice — behavioral review cues

| Code feature               | Expert review question                                                   |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Function taking `T *`      | Is this borrowed, mutated, stored, or owned?                             |
| Function returning pointer | Who owns it, and how long is it valid?                                   |
| Callback registration      | Is callback synchronous or retained? Which thread calls it?              |
| `void *userdata`           | What exact type and lifetime are expected?                               |
| Macro call                 | Are arguments evaluated once? Is precedence safe?                        |
| `switch` over enum         | Is the value validated? Is exhaustiveness maintained?                    |
| `goto cleanup`             | Are resources initialized to safe values and destroyed in correct order? |
| Public struct              | Is layout intentionally part of the API/ABI?                             |
| Function pointer table     | Are all slots initialized? Is the ops table versioned?                   |
| Helper function            | Does it preserve an invariant or merely obscure code?                    |
| Direct field mutation      | Could this violate an invariant?                                         |
| Nested fallible calls      | Where are errors checked and resources cleaned?                          |
| Platform branch            | Is this ISO C, POSIX, compiler-specific, OS-specific, or ABI-specific?   |

### What to Memorize vs What to Look Up — Part 4 artifacts

| Memorize                                                      | Reason                                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| C always passes arguments by value                            | Prevents pointer-parameter misconceptions   |
| Use `T *` to mutate object, `T **` to replace caller pointer  | Core API design rule                        |
| Function pointers require exact compatible signatures         | Prevents UB and ABI issues                  |
| `void *userdata` is unchecked captured context                | Essential callback safety rule              |
| `goto cleanup` is acceptable for disciplined resource cleanup | C lacks native RAII                         |
| Public structs expose representation and often ABI            | Prevents premature layout commitment        |
| Opaque structs enforce field hiding                           | Key C abstraction pattern                   |
| Macros are token substitution before type checking            | Prevents function-like macro misconceptions |
| `static inline` is often preferable to function-like macros   | Keeps type checking and single evaluation   |
| Validate external enum/state before switching                 | C enums are not closed variants             |
| Direct helper functions are the default abstraction           | Avoids unnecessary generic systems          |
| Abstract repeated invariants, not just repeated text          | Prevents correctness drift                  |

| Look up when needed                                           | Why                                                       |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Exact `inline` linkage rules for target standard/compiler     | Historically subtle                                       |
| Compiler-specific attributes for API diagnostics              | Nonportable but useful                                    |
| ABI rules for function-pointer tables across shared libraries | Platform-specific                                         |
| Callback conventions in a library                             | Contract-specific                                         |
| Macro portability for variadic macros and empty `__VA_ARGS__` | Standard/compiler differences                             |
| Linker symbol visibility controls                             | Toolchain-specific                                        |
| Atomic and memory-order rules for callback/thread interaction | Covered more deeply in PART 7                             |
| `container_of` and intrusive data structure legality          | Requires layout, `offsetof`, extension, and aliasing care |

### Part 4 Closing Mental Model — explicit behavior, explicit contracts, explicit cleanup

C behavior abstraction is built from simple mechanisms. That simplicity is powerful because functions, branches, loops, and calls remain visible. It is dangerous because the language does not supply automatic resource unwinding, checked closures, native interfaces, method dispatch, generics, exceptions, or ownership tracking.

The professional model is:

| Behavioral question              | C-specific interpretation                                                                                     |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| What does this function require? | Check pointer validity, length, ownership, state, and nullability contracts.                                  |
| What can this function mutate?   | Look at pointer parameters, globals, callbacks, and hidden internal state.                                    |
| What can fail?                   | Return values, output parameters, allocation, I/O, callbacks, and conversions.                                |
| What must be cleaned up?         | Every acquired resource needs a visible release path.                                                         |
| What is abstracted?              | Determine whether abstraction protects an invariant or merely hides code.                                     |
| What is dynamic?                 | Function pointers, callbacks, and `void *` are unchecked behavioral boundaries.                               |
| What is public?                  | Public headers, structs, macros, symbols, and callbacks become compatibility contracts.                       |
| What is nonportable?             | Macros, attributes, callback ABI, platform branches, and function-pointer assumptions may cross out of ISO C. |

Part 4’s central rule is: **C abstraction should make invariants, ownership, failure, and behavior easier to audit**. If an abstraction hides those things, it is probably the wrong abstraction.
## PART 5 — Modules, Errors, Effects, Resources, and Boundaries by Task Pattern

### Scope of Part 5 — organizing C programs around boundaries

Part 5 treats C as a language for managing **program boundaries**: file boundaries, header boundaries, symbol boundaries, error boundaries, resource boundaries, trust boundaries, unsafe-operation boundaries, and compatibility boundaries. This is where C begins to look less like a small syntax language and more like a systems engineering environment. 

C has no native package system, no standard module system in the common modern sense, no exceptions, no native RAII, no ownership checker, and no standard high-level effect system. It instead uses translation units, headers, linkage, storage duration, return conventions, explicit cleanup, and disciplined APIs.

| Boundary task                | Primary C mechanisms                                        | What C provides                              | What remains programmer discipline              |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Declare module boundary      | `.h` headers, `.c` source files                             | Shared declarations and separate compilation | Header hygiene, minimal exposure                |
| Hide implementation          | File-scope `static`, incomplete types                       | Internal linkage and representation hiding   | Naming, build discipline                        |
| Share declarations           | Header files                                                | Textual inclusion                            | Avoid duplicate definitions and macro pollution |
| Organize large programs      | Translation units, object files, libraries                  | Separate compilation                         | Dependency management and ABI awareness         |
| Handle recoverable errors    | Return codes, sentinels, output params, `errno`             | Explicit failure values                      | Consistent checking and propagation             |
| Handle unrecoverable defects | `assert`, abort-like termination, controlled fatal errors   | Debug-time or process-level failure          | Distinguish bugs from external failures         |
| Manage resources             | `init/destroy`, `open/close`, `malloc/free`, cleanup labels | Manual release patterns                      | Ownership and lifetime correctness              |
| Express effects              | Function naming, pointer params, globals, I/O APIs          | No formal effect type system                 | Documentation and review                        |
| Define trust boundaries      | Validation functions, parsing layers                        | Ordinary functions and checks                | Security discipline                             |
| Isolate unsafe behavior      | Narrow helper modules                                       | Encapsulation by convention                  | Auditing and tool support                       |
| Preserve compatibility       | Opaque types, versioned structs, stable symbols             | Some source-level separation                 | ABI/versioning policy                           |

The central Part 5 rule is: **C module and boundary design is explicit engineering, not language-enforced architecture**.

### Headers and Source Files — declarations, definitions, and translation-unit boundaries

C program organization begins with the distinction between **declaration** and **definition**. A declaration introduces a name and type. A definition provides storage or function body. Headers usually contain declarations; source files usually contain definitions.

| Item                             | Usually belongs in header? | Usually belongs in `.c` file? | Reason                                                    |
| -------------------------------- | -------------------------: | ----------------------------: | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Public function declaration      |                        Yes |            Definition in `.c` | Clients need type; implementation should be compiled once |
| Public type declaration          |                        Yes |  Full definition maybe hidden | Interface needs names                                     |
| Public macro                     |                  Sometimes |                            No | Macro must be visible before use                          |
| Public `static inline` helper    |                  Sometimes |                            No | Each translation unit gets internal helper                |
| Non-`static` function definition |                         No |                           Yes | Avoid multiple definitions                                |
| File-scope private helper        |                         No |            Yes, with `static` | Hide implementation                                       |
| Global object definition         |                     Rarely |                           Yes | Avoid multiple definitions                                |
| `extern` object declaration      |                  Sometimes |        Definition in one `.c` | Shared object, if unavoidable                             |
| Opaque struct full definition    |                         No |                           Yes | Preserve representation hiding                            |
| Internal constants               |                         No |         Yes or private header | Avoid API pollution                                       |

Header example:

```c id="p5_header_buffer"
#ifndef BUFFER_H
#define BUFFER_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct buffer Buffer;

int buffer_create(Buffer **out, size_t capacity);
void buffer_destroy(Buffer *buf);

size_t buffer_size(const Buffer *buf);
int buffer_append(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);

#endif
```

Source file example:

```c id="p5_source_buffer"
#include "buffer.h"

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

static int buffer_reserve(Buffer *buf, size_t min_cap)
{
    /* private implementation helper */
    return 0;
}
```

The header is the public contract. The source file is the implementation unit. After preprocessing, each `.c` file plus included headers becomes a **translation unit**.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A header is like a module that gets compiled once.”                                                       |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A non-`static` function or global object defined in a header causes multiple-definition linker errors.     |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `#include` textually inserts the header into every translation unit that includes it.                      |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Put declarations in headers; put ordinary external definitions in exactly one `.c` file.                   |
| Boundary where the rule changes | `static inline` functions, macros, type definitions, and some constants are intentionally header-resident. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                         | Why it matters                                                           | Better rule                                      |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Defining ordinary functions in headers          | Multiple definitions or unintended code duplication                      | Use declarations in headers, definitions in `.c` |
| Defining global objects in headers              | Each translation unit may define its own object or conflict at link time | Use `extern` declaration plus one definition     |
| Including unnecessary headers in public headers | Increases build dependencies and namespace pollution                     | Use forward declarations where sufficient        |
| Forgetting include guards                       | Repeated inclusion can cause redefinition errors                         | Use include guards or controlled `#pragma once`  |
| Hiding required declarations in `.c` only       | Clients compile with missing prototypes                                  | Declare public API in header                     |

### Header Hygiene — minimal exposure, include guards, dependency control, and macro containment

Header quality determines build scalability and API clarity. Since headers are textually included, every name, macro, type, and included dependency in a public header leaks into client translation units.

| Header-hygiene task             | Pattern                                                 | Why it matters                            |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Prevent multiple inclusion      | Include guard                                           | Avoid redefinition                        |
| Minimize dependencies           | Forward declare incomplete structs                      | Reduce rebuilds and pollution             |
| Include what is required        | Include standard headers for public types               | Header should compile when included alone |
| Avoid private details           | Hide struct fields and helper macros                    | Preserve implementation freedom           |
| Prefix public macros            | `LIB_FOO_FLAG`                                          | Avoid macro collisions                    |
| Avoid using directives          | Not applicable in C, but avoid namespace-like pollution | C global namespace is fragile             |
| Test headers independently      | Compile a file that only includes the header            | Ensures self-contained interface          |
| Separate public/private headers | `buffer.h` vs `buffer_internal.h`                       | Avoid leaking internals                   |

Self-contained header rule:

```c id="p5_self_contained_header"
#include "buffer.h"

int main(void)
{
    return 0;
}
```

This should compile. If `buffer.h` uses `size_t`, it should include `<stddef.h>` itself rather than requiring clients to include it first.

Forward declaration:

```c id="p5_forward_decl"
typedef struct database Database;

int database_open(Database **out, const char *path);
void database_close(Database *db);
```

This avoids exposing the full `struct database` definition.

Include dependency example:

```c id="p5_header_stddef"
#ifndef DATABASE_H
#define DATABASE_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct database Database;

int database_read(Database *db, void *dst, size_t cap, size_t *out_n);

#endif
```

Because `size_t` appears in the public signature, the header must include `<stddef.h>`.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters                                | Better rule                                                           |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Header depends on previous include order              | Fragile clients                               | Every public header should be self-contained                          |
| Public header includes large private dependency       | Slower builds and tighter coupling            | Use incomplete types where possible                                   |
| Macro names too generic                               | Macro namespace is global after preprocessing | Prefix project macros                                                 |
| Private helper declarations in public header          | Clients depend on internals                   | Move to `.c` or private header                                        |
| Public header exposes platform-specific type casually | Portability leaks into API                    | Wrap platform detail behind opaque type or clearly label platform API |

### Translation Units, Object Files, and Linking — from source organization to program image

A C implementation typically compiles each translation unit into an object file and then links object files and libraries. The ISO C language defines translation behavior at the language level, but object-file formats, symbol tables, dynamic linking, and ABI rules are implementation/platform concerns.

| Layer               | C-level concept             | Toolchain-level artifact           | Why it matters                              |
| ------------------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Source file         | `.c`, `.h`                  | Text files                         | Human-maintained code                       |
| Preprocessed source | Translation unit            | Compiler input after preprocessing | Macros/includes resolved                    |
| Compilation         | Function/object definitions | Object file `.o`/`.obj`            | Contains machine code and symbols           |
| Static library      | Collection of objects       | `.a`, `.lib`                       | Link-time inclusion                         |
| Shared library      | Dynamic binary              | `.so`, `.dylib`, `.dll`            | Runtime linking and ABI                     |
| Link step           | Resolve external symbols    | Executable/library                 | Missing or duplicate symbols appear         |
| Loader/runtime      | Platform behavior           | Process image                      | Dynamic linking, relocation, initialization |

Example build flow:

```bash
cc -c buffer.c -o buffer.o
cc -c main.c -o main.o
cc buffer.o main.o -o app
```

If `main.c` calls `buffer_create`, the compiler needs a declaration to type-check the call. The linker needs exactly one compatible external definition to resolve the symbol.

Declaration in header:

```c id="p5_decl_header"
int buffer_create(Buffer **out, size_t capacity);
```

Definition in source:

```c id="p5_def_source"
int buffer_create(Buffer **out, size_t capacity)
{
    /* implementation */
}
```

The compiler checks source-level type compatibility within each translation unit. The linker resolves symbol names and binary references. ABI mismatches can survive source compilation and fail at runtime or produce corrupted behavior if declarations and definitions disagree across separately compiled components.

Interdisciplinary Lens: compiler/linker/ABI
What it clarifies: why C separates declarations from definitions and why headers exist.
Language feature involved: translation units, external declarations, object files, symbol resolution.
Practical consequence: a C programmer must reason about both source-level type declarations and binary-level linkage.
Limit of the lens: linker and ABI behavior are platform/toolchain-specific; ISO C does not define ELF, Mach-O, PE, or shared-library loading.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                    | Why it matters                                        | Better rule                                               |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Declaration in one file differs from definition in another | Undefined or ABI-corrupt behavior may result          | Put shared declarations in one header included by both    |
| Forgetting to link required object/library                 | Undefined reference at link time                      | Build system should model dependencies                    |
| Relying on link order accidentally                         | Some linkers are order-sensitive for static libraries | Understand target linker or use build system abstractions |
| Duplicate external definitions                             | Linker errors or ODR-like confusion in C terms        | One external definition per object/function               |
| Assuming source compatibility equals ABI compatibility     | Existing binaries may break                           | Treat ABI as separate contract                            |

### Linkage, Visibility, and Storage Duration — `static`, `extern`, globals, and internal APIs

C uses **linkage** to determine whether declarations of the same name refer to the same entity across scopes or translation units. Linkage is separate from storage duration and scope.

| Concept              | Question answered                                                           | Example                              |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Scope                | Where can this name be written?                                             | Block scope vs file scope            |
| Linkage              | Can this name refer to the same entity from another scope/translation unit? | External vs internal linkage         |
| Storage duration     | How long does the object’s storage exist?                                   | Automatic, static, allocated, thread |
| Visibility in binary | Is symbol exported from object/shared library?                              | Toolchain/ABI concern                |

`static` has different meanings depending on context.

| Form                         | Meaning                                       | Example                     | Professional use                      |
| ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| File-scope `static` function | Internal linkage                              | `static int helper(void);`  | Private helper                        |
| File-scope `static` object   | Internal linkage, static storage duration     | `static int counter;`       | Private global state                  |
| Block-scope `static` object  | Static storage duration, block scope          | `static int next_id;`       | Persistent local state                |
| Array parameter `static`     | Minimum argument contract in parameter syntax | `void f(int a[static 10]);` | Advanced contract hint; use carefully |
| `static inline`              | Internal linkage inline helper                | `static inline int f(...)`  | Header helper                         |

External declaration:

```c id="p5_extern_decl"
/* config.h */
extern int global_log_level;
```

Single definition:

```c id="p5_extern_def"
/* config.c */
int global_log_level = 0;
```

Private helper:

```c id="p5_static_helper"
static int parse_digit(unsigned char ch)
{
    return ch >= '0' && ch <= '9' ? ch - '0' : -1;
}
```

A file-scope helper should usually be `static` unless it is part of the intended external interface. This limits accidental symbol exposure and allows compiler optimization within the translation unit.

Global-state caution:

```c id="p5_global_state"
static int initialized;

int library_init(void)
{
    if (initialized) {
        return 0;
    }

    initialized = 1;
    return 0;
}
```

This may be fine in a single-threaded context but is not automatically thread-safe. Static storage duration is not a synchronization mechanism.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “`static` means the same thing everywhere.”                                                                                      |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A `static` local persists across calls, while a `static` file-scope function is merely hidden from other translation units.      |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `static` participates in different declaration contexts: linkage at file scope, storage duration at block scope.                 |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Always interpret `static` from its syntactic location.                                                                           |
| Boundary where the rule changes | `static` in array parameter declarations is a separate contract-like feature and should not be confused with storage or linkage. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                            | Why it matters                                     | Better rule                                       |
| -------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Forgetting `static` on private functions           | Accidental exported symbols                        | Mark file-local helpers `static`                  |
| Putting `extern` definition in header              | Multiple definitions                               | Header has `extern`; one `.c` has definition      |
| Using global mutable state casually                | Reentrancy and thread-safety problems              | Prefer explicit context objects                   |
| Confusing scope with linkage                       | Hidden name may still refer to persistent object   | Analyze scope/linkage/storage separately          |
| Assuming binary symbol visibility equals C linkage | Shared-library export rules are toolchain-specific | Manage visibility with platform tools when needed |

### Internal and Public Headers — scaling beyond one file

Large C projects often distinguish public headers, private internal headers, and source files. This separation prevents implementation details from becoming user contracts.

| File category           | Audience                          | Contents                                                 | Should avoid                                |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Public header           | Library users                     | Stable API declarations, public types, documented macros | Private fields, unstable helpers            |
| Private/internal header | Project implementation files      | Shared internal structs, helper declarations             | Installation/export to external users       |
| Source file             | One translation unit              | Definitions, private helpers, local state                | Public declarations not mirrored in headers |
| Generated header        | Build-generated declarations/data | Tables, version info, generated APIs                     | Untracked non-reproducible output           |
| Platform adapter header | Internal portability layer        | Unified wrappers for OS/compiler differences             | Scattered platform macros in public API     |

Example layout:

```text
include/
    mylib/buffer.h
src/
    buffer.c
    buffer_internal.h
    platform_posix.c
    platform_win32.c
tests/
    buffer_test.c
```

Public header:

```c id="p5_public_header"
typedef struct mylib_buffer MylibBuffer;

int mylib_buffer_create(MylibBuffer **out, size_t capacity);
void mylib_buffer_destroy(MylibBuffer *buf);
```

Internal header:

```c id="p5_internal_header"
struct mylib_buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

int mylib_buffer_reserve(MylibBuffer *buf, size_t min_cap);
```

The internal header is shared only among implementation files that need representation access. It is not part of the installed public API.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                            | Why it matters                       | Better rule                                              |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Installing internal headers accidentally           | Users depend on implementation       | Separate include paths for public/private headers        |
| Public headers include internal headers            | Internals leak into API              | Public headers should expose only stable contracts       |
| Internal helper names conflict with public names   | Symbol/name confusion                | Use prefixes and `static` where possible                 |
| Platform-specific details leak into public headers | Portability burden shifts to clients | Hide behind opaque types or adapter functions            |
| Tests include private headers by default           | Tests may overfit implementation     | Prefer public API tests; use internal tests deliberately |

### Error-Handling Design — recoverable failure, programmer bugs, and process-level failure

C does not have exceptions. Error handling is a design convention built on return values, output parameters, global/library error indicators, and control flow. The first decision is whether a failure is **recoverable external failure**, **programmer bug**, or **fatal unrecoverable condition**.

| Failure kind                       | Example                                        | C mechanism                                           | Professional response                |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Recoverable external failure       | File missing, parse error, allocation failure  | Status return, error code, output parameter           | Return error to caller               |
| Expected absence                   | Lookup not found                               | Null pointer, sentinel, status                        | Represent absence clearly            |
| Programmer bug                     | Violated internal precondition                 | `assert`, diagnostic abort in debug                   | Fix code; do not treat as user error |
| Security violation/malformed input | Invalid packet length                          | Validation failure return                             | Reject and preserve invariants       |
| Fatal process condition            | Corrupted invariant, unrecoverable environment | Controlled fatal logging/abort                        | Stop before worse corruption         |
| Library boundary error             | Invalid public API argument                    | Defined error return or documented contract violation | Choose strict documented policy      |

Error mechanism comparison:

| Mechanism            | When to use                           | Strength                     | Failure mode                      |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| `int` status         | Simple success/failure                | Conventional                 | Low detail                        |
| Error enum           | Known error family                    | Readable and typed-ish       | Not closed against invalid values |
| Negative error codes | System-style APIs                     | Compact                      | Convention-specific               |
| Null return          | Optional object or allocation failure | Simple                       | Low diagnostic detail             |
| Status plus output   | Fallible value production             | Separates result from error  | Output state must be defined      |
| `errno`              | APIs that specify it                  | Standard/POSIX compatibility | Stale or misused value            |
| Error object         | Rich diagnostics                      | Detailed context             | Lifecycle complexity              |
| `assert`             | Internal invariant                    | Cheap debug detection        | Disabled in release builds        |

Example: status plus output.

```c id="p5_error_status_output"
enum parse_status {
    PARSE_STATUS_OK = 0,
    PARSE_STATUS_EMPTY,
    PARSE_STATUS_RANGE,
    PARSE_STATUS_SYNTAX
};

enum parse_status parse_port(const char *s, unsigned short *out);
```

This avoids using every possible `unsigned short` value as both result and error channel.

Example: defining output state.

```c id="p5_error_output_state"
enum parse_status parse_port(const char *s, unsigned short *out)
{
    if (out == NULL) {
        return PARSE_STATUS_SYNTAX;
    }

    *out = 0;

    if (s == NULL || *s == '\0') {
        return PARSE_STATUS_EMPTY;
    }

    /* parse and range-check */

    return PARSE_STATUS_OK;
}
```

Setting `*out = 0` at the start defines a neutral failure state. Another valid convention is “leave `*out` untouched on failure,” but it must be documented and implemented consistently.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                  |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Use `assert` to check bad inputs.”                                                                                                          |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Release builds compiled with `NDEBUG` omit assertions and accept invalid external data.                                                      |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `assert` is for programmer invariants, not recoverable validation of untrusted input.                                                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use runtime checks for external input and public API misuse if the API promises graceful failure; use `assert` for internal impossibilities. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some low-level internal APIs deliberately define invalid arguments as contract violations; public-facing APIs should be explicit about this. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                   | Better rule                                   |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Ignoring return values                    | Failure silently propagates      | Check every fallible call                     |
| Mixed success conventions                 | Callers misread API              | Use consistent conventions within a module    |
| Using `errno` without API guarantee       | `errno` may be stale             | Read `errno` only after documented failure    |
| Undefined output on failure               | Caller may read invalid data     | Define output state                           |
| Treating allocation failure as impossible | Robust systems may need recovery | Decide and document allocation-failure policy |

### Error Propagation Patterns — local handling, wrapping, cleanup, and normalization

A C function that calls fallible functions must choose whether to handle, translate, propagate, or collapse errors. Since there are no exceptions, this is explicit code.

| Propagation pattern         | Use when                              | Shape                               |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Direct return               | Callee and caller use same convention | `return f();`                       |
| Normalize to module status  | Hide dependency-specific errors       | `return rc == 0 ? MOD_OK : MOD_IO;` |
| Preserve detailed code      | Caller can act on specifics           | `return err;`                       |
| Store diagnostic object     | Need context                          | `set_error(err, "...")`             |
| Cleanup then return         | Resources acquired before failure     | `goto cleanup; return rc;`          |
| Collapse to boolean         | Caller only needs success/failure     | `return f() == 0;`                  |
| Fatal on impossible failure | Internal invariant broken             | log and abort/assert                |

Example: cleanup and propagate.

```c id="p5_error_propagate_cleanup"
int write_records(const char *path, const struct record *records, size_t n)
{
    FILE *fp = NULL;
    int rc = -1;

    fp = fopen(path, "wb");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (record_write(fp, &records[i]) != 0) {
            goto cleanup;
        }
    }

    rc = 0;

cleanup:
    if (fp != NULL) {
        if (fclose(fp) != 0 && rc == 0) {
            rc = -1;
        }
    }

    return rc;
}
```

This example illustrates a subtle point: cleanup itself can fail. `fclose` can report failure for buffered output streams. A robust function must define whether cleanup failure matters and how it interacts with earlier errors.

Error normalization:

```c id="p5_error_normalize"
enum db_status {
    DB_OK = 0,
    DB_ERR_IO,
    DB_ERR_FORMAT,
    DB_ERR_NOMEM
};

static enum db_status db_status_from_errno(int e)
{
    switch (e) {
    case ENOMEM:
        return DB_ERR_NOMEM;
    default:
        return DB_ERR_IO;
    }
}
```

This example uses `ENOMEM`, which is available from `<errno.h>` on hosted systems, but mapping details depend on the APIs used.

Propagation decision table:

| Question                                          | If yes                          | If no                       |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Can caller recover differently by error kind?     | Preserve or classify error      | Collapse to generic failure |
| Is dependency error too low-level for public API? | Normalize                       | Propagate directly          |
| Did this function acquire resources?              | Cleanup before return           | Direct return may be fine   |
| Can cleanup fail?                                 | Define precedence of errors     | Simple cleanup              |
| Is failure an internal invariant violation?       | Assert/fatal may be appropriate | Return recoverable error    |
| Is additional context needed?                     | Error object/logging            | Status code sufficient      |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                           | Better rule                                                   |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Overwriting original error during cleanup            | Loses root cause                         | Preserve first error unless cleanup failure is more important |
| Ignoring cleanup failure always                      | Buffered I/O errors can surface at close | Define cleanup-error policy                                   |
| Returning dependency-specific errors from stable API | Couples API to implementation            | Normalize at boundary                                         |
| Collapsing all errors too early                      | Caller cannot recover intelligently      | Preserve detail where useful                                  |
| Logging and returning at every layer                 | Duplicate noisy diagnostics              | Decide which layer reports                                    |

### `errno` and Library Error Conventions — standard behavior, POSIX habits, and misuse

`errno` is often misunderstood. It is not a universal error channel for all C functions. It is meaningful only when an API specifies that it is set or used in a particular way.

| Rule                                                                 | Explanation                                |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `errno` is not cleared automatically before every call               | It may contain an old value                |
| Check `errno` only after a function signals failure                  | Otherwise it may be irrelevant             |
| Not all ISO C functions use `errno`                                  | Read the specific API contract             |
| POSIX uses `errno` extensively                                       | POSIX is not ISO C                         |
| Some functions return error number directly instead of using `errno` | Common in thread APIs                      |
| Save `errno` promptly if another call may change it                  | Error reporting functions can modify state |

Correct pattern for APIs that signal failure and set `errno`:

```c id="p5_errno_pattern"
#include <errno.h>
#include <stdio.h>

FILE *fp = fopen(path, "rb");
if (fp == NULL) {
    int saved_errno = errno;
    report_open_error(path, saved_errno);
    return -1;
}
```

Parsing functions such as `strtol` require a more careful pattern because a valid result may equal a boundary value. The API contract must be followed precisely.

Generic misuse:

```c id="p5_errno_misuse"
some_function();
if (errno != 0) {
    /* wrong unless some_function's contract says this is meaningful */
}
```

Better:

```c id="p5_errno_better"
if (some_function() != 0) {
    /* only now consult the documented error channel */
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                                         | Better rule                                               |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Checking `errno` without failure signal                 | Stale error                                            | Check function return first                               |
| Assuming all functions set `errno`                      | False for many APIs                                    | Read contract                                             |
| Calling another function before saving `errno`          | `errno` may change                                     | Save immediately                                          |
| Exposing raw `errno` as stable library API              | Couples API to OS/libc details                         | Normalize if API should be portable                       |
| Treating `errno` as thread-unsafe global simplistically | Modern implementations often make it thread-local-like | Still use according to API contract, not as general state |

### Recoverable vs Unrecoverable Errors — contract violation, external failure, and fatal state

C programs should not treat every failure alike. Some failures are expected environmental conditions; some are invalid inputs; some are internal bugs; some indicate corrupted state.

| Situation                          | Example                          | Recommended handling                                  |
| ---------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| External recoverable failure       | File not found                   | Return error                                          |
| Malformed untrusted input          | Invalid packet length            | Reject input and return validation error              |
| Allocation failure                 | `malloc` returns `NULL`          | Return error or documented fatal policy               |
| Public API null argument           | `buffer_append(NULL, ...)`       | Either return error or document contract violation    |
| Internal impossible state          | `len > cap` in private invariant | Assert, diagnostic, or fail closed                    |
| Security-critical invariant broken | Bounds inconsistency             | Stop operation; avoid continuing with corrupted state |
| Nonrecoverable process condition   | Required subsystem unavailable   | Controlled fatal error may be appropriate             |

Design choice table:

| API audience                              | Invalid argument policy                                  |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Public library used by unknown callers    | Prefer defined error return for common invalid arguments |
| Internal helper with strict preconditions | May use `assert` and document precondition               |
| Security boundary                         | Validate and reject; do not rely only on assertions      |
| Performance-critical internal path        | Preconditions may be unchecked after validation layer    |
| Embedded/freestanding code                | Policy may be project-specific; document clearly         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                         | Why it matters                      | Better rule                                        |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Recovering from corrupted invariants blindly                    | May worsen memory corruption        | Fail closed or abort in controlled way             |
| Aborting on ordinary user input error                           | Poor robustness                     | Return validation error                            |
| Returning error for impossible internal bug without diagnostics | Bug may be hidden                   | Assert/log internal contract violation             |
| Inconsistent public API invalid-argument behavior               | Users cannot write reliable callers | Define module-wide policy                          |
| Treating all allocation failure the same                        | Context matters                     | Decide per subsystem: recover, propagate, or fatal |

### Effects in C — mutation, I/O, allocation, global state, concurrency, and observability

C has no formal effect system. A function’s type does not say whether it performs I/O, allocates memory, mutates global state, locks a mutex, writes through a pointer, changes `errno`, calls callbacks, or starts threads.

| Effect kind               | C expression mechanism              | How to signal in API                     | Risk if hidden                   |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Mutates pointed-to object | `T *` parameter                     | Non-`const` pointer and verb name        | Unexpected state change          |
| Allocates memory          | `malloc`, create/dup functions      | `create`, `alloc`, `dup`, output pointer | Leak/failure ignored             |
| Frees/takes ownership     | `free`, destroy/take functions      | `destroy`, `free`, `take`                | Use-after-free                   |
| Performs I/O              | `FILE *`, file descriptors, OS APIs | `read`, `write`, `open`, `save` names    | Blocking/failure surprises       |
| Uses global state         | file-scope/static/global object     | Documentation/naming                     | Non-reentrant behavior           |
| Changes error state       | `errno`, module error object        | API contract                             | Stale or overwritten diagnostics |
| Calls user code           | callback/function pointer           | callback contract                        | Reentrancy/lifetime issues       |
