| Synchronizes              | locks, atomics                      | thread-safety documentation              | deadlock/data race               |
| Nonlocal jump             | `longjmp`                           | explicit contract                        | skipped cleanup                  |

Effect visibility examples:

```c id="p5_effect_names"
int buffer_append(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
int file_read_all(const char *path, unsigned char **out, size_t *out_len);
void logger_set_level(int level);
```

The names imply mutation, I/O/allocation, and global state respectively. C code benefits from names that reveal effects because the type system will not.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                        | Why it matters                              | Better rule                                     |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Function name hides allocation                                 | Caller may not check failure or free result | Use `create`, `alloc`, `dup`, `read_all` naming |
| Read-only-looking function mutates global cache                | Surprising thread/reentrancy behavior       | Document or avoid hidden mutation               |
| Callback invoked unexpectedly                                  | Reentrancy and control-flow surprise        | State callback timing                           |
| Output parameter used for both input and output without naming | Aliasing confusion                          | Name and document in/out behavior               |
| Function changes `errno` but does not use it as contract       | Debug confusion                             | Do not expose accidental error-state changes    |

### Resource Management Foundations — memory, files, locks, handles, and cleanup symmetry

A resource is anything that must be explicitly released: heap memory, file streams, file descriptors, sockets, mutexes, temporary files, mapped memory, parser contexts, handles, or library objects. C does not release arbitrary resources automatically when leaving scope.

| Resource        | Acquire                           | Release                     | Standard layer                              |
| --------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Heap memory     | `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`     | `free`                      | ISO C                                       |
| File stream     | `fopen`                           | `fclose`                    | ISO C hosted library                        |
| Temporary file  | `tmpfile`                         | `fclose`                    | ISO C hosted library                        |
| File descriptor | `open`                            | `close`                     | POSIX, not ISO C                            |
| Socket          | `socket`                          | `close`/platform equivalent | POSIX/platform                              |
| Mutex           | `pthread_mutex_init`/platform API | destroy/unlock APIs         | POSIX/platform or C threads where available |
| Memory mapping  | `mmap`                            | `munmap`                    | POSIX                                       |
| Library object  | `*_create`                        | `*_destroy`                 | Library convention                          |
| Thread          | thread creation API               | join/detach/close handle    | C/POSIX/platform depending API              |

Cleanup symmetry matters. If an API creates a resource, it should provide the corresponding release function or state the release mechanism.

| Pair pattern         | Meaning                                                  |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| `malloc` / `free`    | ISO C heap allocation                                    |
| `fopen` / `fclose`   | ISO C stream lifecycle                                   |
| `init` / `deinit`    | Caller owns storage; function manages internal resources |
| `create` / `destroy` | API often allocates object; destroy releases it          |
| `open` / `close`     | External resource/session                                |
| `lock` / `unlock`    | Synchronization region                                   |
| `retain` / `release` | Reference-counted convention, if implemented             |

Cleanup label pattern:

```c id="p5_resource_cleanup"
int use_resources(const char *path)
{
    FILE *fp = NULL;
    unsigned char *buf = NULL;
    int rc = -1;

    fp = fopen(path, "rb");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    buf = malloc(4096);
    if (buf == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    rc = process_stream(fp, buf, 4096);

cleanup:
    free(buf);

    if (fp != NULL) {
        if (fclose(fp) != 0 && rc == 0) {
            rc = -1;
        }
    }

    return rc;
}
```

Destroy in reverse acquisition order when resources depend on each other.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “The OS will clean resources when the program exits, so cleanup is optional.”                                                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A long-running process leaks file descriptors and eventually cannot open files.                                                                             |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Some process-exit cleanup exists at the OS level, but C program correctness includes bounded resource use during execution.                                 |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Release every resource at the correct lifetime boundary, not merely at process exit.                                                                        |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Short-lived command-line tools may rely on process cleanup for some memory in fatal-exit paths, but this should be a conscious policy, not ordinary design. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                           | Why it matters                              | Better rule                                     |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Cleanup only on success           | Failure path leaks                          | Cleanup must handle partial acquisition         |
| Freeing uninitialized pointer     | Undefined behavior if pointer indeterminate | Initialize resource variables to neutral values |
| Double cleanup                    | Double free/close may corrupt state         | Set pointer/handle to neutral value when useful |
| Mismatched allocator/deallocator  | Heap corruption or undefined behavior       | Release with matching API family                |
| Ignoring close failure for output | Buffered write errors may appear at close   | Define close-failure policy                     |

### `init/deinit` vs `create/destroy` — two lifecycle families

C APIs commonly choose between caller-provided storage and API-allocated storage. These are different lifecycle models and should not be mixed carelessly.

| Lifecycle style  | Shape                                                    | Storage owner        | Resource owner              | Best use                       |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| `init/deinit`    | `int obj_init(Obj *obj); void obj_deinit(Obj *obj);`     | Caller               | Object after init           | Stack/static/embedded-friendly |
| `create/destroy` | `int obj_create(Obj **out); void obj_destroy(Obj *obj);` | API allocates object | Caller owns returned object | Opaque heap objects            |
| `open/close`     | `int obj_open(Obj *, path); void obj_close(Obj *);`      | Varies               | External resource session   | Files, devices, connections    |
| `reset`          | `void obj_reset(Obj *);`                                 | Existing object      | Keeps allocation maybe      | Reuse object                   |
| `release`        | `T *obj_release_data(Obj *);`                            | Transfer out         | Caller receives resource    | Ownership transfer             |
| `take`           | `int obj_take_data(Obj *, T *data);`                     | Transfer in          | Object receives resource    | Avoid copy                     |

`init/deinit` example:

```c id="p5_init_deinit"
struct buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};

int buffer_init(struct buffer *buf, size_t capacity)
{
    if (buf == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    buf->data = NULL;
    buf->len = 0;
    buf->cap = 0;

    if (capacity == 0) {
        return 0;
    }

    buf->data = malloc(capacity);
    if (buf->data == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    buf->cap = capacity;
    return 0;
}

void buffer_deinit(struct buffer *buf)
{
    if (buf == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    free(buf->data);
    buf->data = NULL;
    buf->len = 0;
    buf->cap = 0;
}
```

`create/destroy` example:

```c id="p5_create_destroy"
int buffer_create(struct buffer **out, size_t capacity)
{
    struct buffer *buf;

    if (out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = NULL;

    buf = malloc(sizeof *buf);
    if (buf == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (buffer_init(buf, capacity) != 0) {
        free(buf);
        return -1;
    }

    *out = buf;
    return 0;
}

void buffer_destroy(struct buffer *buf)
{
    if (buf == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    buffer_deinit(buf);
    free(buf);
}
```

Decision table:

| Requirement                              | Prefer                                               |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Caller wants stack allocation            | `init/deinit`                                        |
| Type is opaque and size hidden           | `create/destroy`                                     |
| Allocation must use custom arena         | `init` with caller storage or allocator-aware create |
| Object may be embedded in another struct | `init/deinit`                                        |
| Public ABI should hide layout            | `create/destroy`                                     |
| Embedded/freestanding environment        | Often `init/deinit`                                  |
| Simple library usability                 | Often `create/destroy`                               |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                     | Why it matters                | Better rule                         |
| ----------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Calling `destroy` on stack object when it also frees object | Invalid free                  | Distinguish `deinit` from `destroy` |
| Forgetting to deinit embedded object                        | Internal resource leak        | Parent destroy must deinit members  |
| `init` leaves object partially initialized on failure       | Later cleanup unsafe          | Define failure state carefully      |
| `destroy` does not tolerate null inconsistently             | Caller cleanup becomes harder | Decide whether destroy accepts null |
| Mixing allocation families                                  | Wrong free function           | Pair create/destroy within same API |

### Part 5 Interim Reference — modules, errors, and resources covered so far

| Boundary task              | Preferred C pattern                        | Why                                    | Main failure mode                 |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Public interface           | Self-contained `.h`                        | Shared declarations                    | Include-order dependency          |
| Private implementation     | `.c` plus file-scope `static`              | Internal linkage                       | Accidental symbol export          |
| Representation hiding      | Incomplete struct in public header         | Encapsulation                          | Leaking private fields            |
| Shared global declaration  | `extern` in header, one definition in `.c` | Avoid duplicate definitions            | Defining object in header         |
| Header reuse               | Include guards                             | Prevent repeated inclusion             | Redefinition                      |
| Large-project internals    | Private headers                            | Share implementation-only declarations | Installing private headers        |
| Recoverable failure        | Status return/output param                 | Explicit error path                    | Ignored return                    |
| Rich error category        | Error enum/object                          | Better recovery/debugging              | Overcomplicated lifecycle         |
| External input error       | Validation and return                      | Security boundary                      | `assert` instead of runtime check |
| Internal invariant failure | `assert` or controlled fatal               | Detect programmer bug                  | Continuing corrupted state        |
| Resource acquisition       | Initialize variables, acquire stepwise     | Safe partial cleanup                   | Uninitialized cleanup             |
| Multiple resources         | `goto cleanup`                             | Central release path                   | Arbitrary jumps                   |
| Caller storage lifecycle   | `init/deinit`                              | Stack/embedded friendly                | Calling wrong cleanup             |
| API-allocated lifecycle    | `create/destroy`                           | Opaque heap object                     | Ownership ambiguity               |
| I/O cleanup                | Check close where relevant                 | Buffered errors may surface late       | Ignored `fclose` failure          |
| ABI-sensitive boundary     | Opaque/versioned API                       | Evolution                              | Public layout freeze              |

### Resource Cleanup Patterns Beyond Memory — files, locks, descriptors, temporary state, and partial failure

Memory is only one resource class. Professional C code must treat every acquired capability as requiring a release protocol. This includes ISO C streams, POSIX file descriptors, sockets, locks, memory mappings, temporary files, library handles, and partially initialized aggregates. The exact APIs belong to different layers: `malloc`/`free` and `FILE *` streams are ISO C hosted-library facilities; `open`/`close`, sockets, `mmap`, and many lock APIs are POSIX or platform-specific, not ISO C. 

| Resource kind                      | Typical acquire                          | Typical release                | Layer                                  | Cleanup hazard                           |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Heap memory                        | `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`            | `free`                         | ISO C                                  | Double free, leak, use-after-free        |
| Stream                             | `fopen`, `tmpfile`                       | `fclose`                       | ISO C hosted                           | Buffered write error may appear at close |
| File descriptor                    | `open`, `dup`, `pipe`                    | `close`                        | POSIX                                  | Descriptor leak, close-on-exec issue     |
| Socket                             | `socket`, `accept`                       | `close` or platform equivalent | POSIX/platform                         | Half-close and platform differences      |
| Mutex                              | `pthread_mutex_init`, platform lock init | unlock/destroy functions       | POSIX/platform/C threads depending API | Deadlock, forgotten unlock               |
| Memory map                         | `mmap`                                   | `munmap`                       | POSIX                                  | Size/lifetime mismatch                   |
| Temporary allocation inside object | `*_init` substep                         | parent `*_deinit`              | Project convention                     | Partial initialization leak              |
| Callback registration              | register function/userdata               | unregister function            | Library convention                     | Dangling userdata                        |
| Reference-counted object           | retain/acquire                           | release                        | Library convention                     | Leak or premature free                   |
| Foreign handle                     | library-specific open/create             | library-specific close/destroy | Library/ABI                            | Wrong allocator or wrong release API     |

A resource-owning function should usually follow three rules: initialize resource variables to neutral values, acquire resources in a clear sequence, and clean up in reverse or dependency-safe order.

Example with multiple resource families:

```c id="p5_multi_resource_cleanup"
int copy_file(const char *src_path, const char *dst_path)
{
    FILE *src = NULL;
    FILE *dst = NULL;
    unsigned char *buf = NULL;
    int rc = -1;

    src = fopen(src_path, "rb");
    if (src == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    dst = fopen(dst_path, "wb");
    if (dst == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    buf = malloc(8192);
    if (buf == NULL) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    for (;;) {
        size_t n = fread(buf, 1, 8192, src);

        if (n > 0 && fwrite(buf, 1, n, dst) != n) {
            goto cleanup;
        }

        if (n < 8192) {
            if (ferror(src)) {
                goto cleanup;
            }
            break;
        }
    }

    rc = 0;

cleanup:
    free(buf);

    if (dst != NULL) {
        if (fclose(dst) != 0 && rc == 0) {
            rc = -1;
        }
    }

    if (src != NULL) {
        if (fclose(src) != 0 && rc == 0) {
            rc = -1;
        }
    }

    return rc;
}
```

This example is intentionally explicit. It shows that resource cleanup is not just `free`; stream close can fail, and failure precedence must be defined.

| Cleanup pattern                 | Guarantee                            | Cost                               | Best use                                 |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Single cleanup label            | All exits pass one cleanup block     | Some `goto` ceremony               | Multiple resources                       |
| Early return before acquisition | Simple invalid-input handling        | Must stop after resources acquired | Guard clauses                            |
| Nested cleanup                  | Releases immediately after local use | Indentation and duplication        | Short resource scopes                    |
| Parent destroy function         | Centralizes aggregate cleanup        | Requires valid partial states      | Structs with owned fields                |
| Reverse-order cleanup           | Releases dependencies safely         | Must track acquisition order       | Resources depending on earlier resources |
| Null-tolerant destroy           | Simplifies partial cleanup           | Must define neutral state          | Heap objects and optional members        |
| Idempotent cleanup              | Repeated cleanup safe                | Harder to guarantee                | Complex state machines, rarely necessary |
| Defer-like compiler extension   | Less boilerplate                     | Non-ISO portability issue          | Project-specific GCC/Clang codebases     |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                                           |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Cleanup code is only needed for the success path; failure returns immediately.”                                                                                      |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | An allocation succeeds, the next acquisition fails, and the first resource leaks.                                                                                     |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C does not automatically unwind acquired resources when a function returns.                                                                                           |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Every resource acquisition should have a visible cleanup owner before the next fallible operation.                                                                    |
| Boundary where the rule changes | In fatal-exit paths for short-lived programs, a project may deliberately rely on process cleanup, but this must not leak into library or long-running process design. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                             | Why it matters                              | Better rule                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Cleanup code assumes full initialization                            | Partial failure can clean invalid resources | Initialize fields/resources to neutral states |
| Cleanup order ignores dependencies                                  | Later resource may depend on earlier one    | Release in reverse dependency order           |
| `realloc` result assigned directly to original pointer              | Failure loses original allocation           | Assign to temporary first                     |
| Lock acquired before fallible operation and not released on failure | Deadlock                                    | Cleanup labels should release locks too       |
| Mixing ISO C and POSIX cleanup assumptions                          | Portability confusion                       | Label resource API layer clearly              |

### Ownership, Lifetime, and Borrowing Documentation — making invisible contracts visible

C pointer types do not express ownership, borrowing, nullability, retention, or lifetime. Boundary design must make those contracts explicit. A function signature is insufficient unless it is paired with stable naming and documentation.

| Contract dimension | Example question                                          | How to express in C                                 |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Ownership in       | Does callee take responsibility for freeing this pointer? | Function name `take`, documentation                 |
| Ownership out      | Must caller free returned pointer?                        | `create`, `dup`, `alloc`, destroy/free pair         |
| Borrowed lifetime  | How long must pointer remain valid?                       | Comment: “valid only for call duration”             |
| Retained pointer   | Does callee store pointer after return?                   | Name `set`, `register`, documentation               |
| Nullability        | Is `NULL` accepted?                                       | Runtime check or documented precondition            |
| Mutability         | May callee modify pointed-to object?                      | `const` where read-only; non-`const` where mutation |
| Array extent       | How many elements may callee access?                      | Pointer plus `size_t` length                        |
| Output state       | What happens to `*out` on failure?                        | Set neutral value or document untouched             |
| Thread access      | Can object be used concurrently?                          | Thread-safety section in API comment                |
| Cleanup duty       | Which function releases the object?                       | Lifecycle pair naming                               |

Example of explicit API contract:

```c id="p5_contract_comment"
/*
 * buffer_set_data:
 * - buf must not be NULL.
 * - data may be NULL only when n == 0.
 * - The function copies n bytes from data; it does not retain data.
 * - On success, returns 0 and replaces the buffer contents.
 * - On failure, returns -1 and leaves buf unchanged.
 */
int buffer_set_data(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
```

Contrast with a dangerous undocumented signature:

```c id="p5_ambiguous_contract"
int buffer_set_data(Buffer *buf, unsigned char *data, size_t n);
```

This signature does not say whether `data` is borrowed, copied, retained, or taken. It also fails to say whether the buffer may mutate the source, whether `data` may be `NULL`, or what happens on failure.

Ownership naming table:

| Name         | Usually implies                             | Example                                  |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `create`     | Callee allocates; caller receives ownership | `image_create(&img)`                     |
| `destroy`    | Releases object and its owned internals     | `image_destroy(img)`                     |
| `init`       | Initializes caller-provided storage         | `image_init(&img)`                       |
| `deinit`     | Releases internals but not caller storage   | `image_deinit(&img)`                     |
| `dup`        | Creates owned duplicate                     | `string_dup(s)`                          |
| `borrow`     | Returns non-owning view                     | `map_borrow_value(m, key)`               |
| `take`       | Callee takes ownership from caller          | `buffer_take_data(buf, data, n)`         |
| `release`    | Object gives ownership to caller            | `buffer_release_data(buf, &data, &n)`    |
| `view`       | Returns temporary non-owning view           | `buffer_view(buf)`                       |
| `register`   | May retain callback/userdata                | `loop_register_handler(loop, cb, ctx)`   |
| `unregister` | Ends retained relationship                  | `loop_unregister_handler(loop, cb, ctx)` |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                         |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A pointer parameter clearly means the callee borrows the object.”                                                  |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | The callee stores the pointer and uses it after the caller’s stack object is gone.                                  |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A pointer type says nothing about retention.                                                                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Every API that stores a pointer beyond the call must say so explicitly.                                             |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Pure internal helpers may rely on project-wide conventions, but public APIs should not rely on unstated convention. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                       | Better rule                            |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- |
| Ambiguous ownership transfer                           | Causes double free or leak           | Use take/release/create/destroy naming |
| Returning borrowed internal data without validity rule | Caller may store stale pointer       | State invalidation events              |
| Inconsistent null policy                               | Callers cannot know whether to check | Define nullability per parameter       |
| Mutating through pointer not suggested by name         | Surprising side effects              | Use verbs that indicate mutation       |
| Storing caller buffer silently                         | Lifetime bug                         | State retention explicitly             |

### Secure String and Buffer Boundaries — length, capacity, termination, and truncation

C’s string model is a major boundary-risk area. A C string is a null-terminated character sequence in accessible storage. A buffer is storage with a size. A byte array may contain zero bytes. These concepts must not be conflated.

| Data shape             | Representation               | Required contract                              | Common mistake                   |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| C string               | `const char *s`              | Accessible bytes ending in `'\0'`              | Passing nonterminated buffer     |
| Mutable string buffer  | `char *dst, size_t cap`      | Capacity includes space for terminator         | Off-by-one overflow              |
| Byte buffer            | `unsigned char *p, size_t n` | Exactly `n` bytes accessible                   | Using string functions           |
| Length-bounded text    | `const char *p, size_t n`    | Text extent is `n`; not necessarily terminated | Calling `strlen`                 |
| Output buffer          | `char *out, size_t out_cap`  | Function writes at most capacity               | Not defining truncation behavior |
| Owned string result    | `char **out`                 | Caller cleanup defined                         | Leak or wrong allocator          |
| Interned/static string | `const char *`               | Valid for program/module lifetime              | Caller attempts to free          |

Safer output-buffer API shape:

```c id="p5_string_api"
int format_user_label(char *out, size_t out_cap,
                      const char *name, unsigned id);
```

This shape makes capacity explicit. The contract must define whether truncation is allowed, how failure is reported, and whether `out` is null-terminated on failure.

Example using `snprintf` carefully:

```c id="p5_snprintf_pattern"
#include <stdio.h>

int format_user_label(char *out, size_t out_cap,
                      const char *name, unsigned id)
{
    int n;

    if (out == NULL || out_cap == 0 || name == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    n = snprintf(out, out_cap, "%s:%u", name, id);
    if (n < 0) {
        out[0] = '\0';
        return -1;
    }

    if ((size_t)n >= out_cap) {
        out[0] = '\0';
        return -1; /* truncation treated as failure */
    }

    return 0;
}
```

The key design choice is whether truncation is a failure or acceptable result. Silent truncation is often a security or correctness problem.

Dangerous pattern:

```c id="p5_string_bad"
strcpy(dst, src);
```

This is only safe if `dst` is known to have enough space for all of `src` including the terminator. In public or boundary-facing code, this assumption should usually be replaced by an explicit capacity-aware design.

String/buffer decision table:

| Task                                  | Prefer                                                   | Avoid                                   |
| ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Copy unknown string into fixed buffer | `snprintf`, checked length then `memcpy` plus terminator | `strcpy`, unchecked `strcat`            |
| Work with binary data                 | `memcpy`, `memcmp`, explicit length                      | `strlen`, `strcmp`                      |
| Accept external text                  | Pointer plus max length or bounded parsing               | Unbounded `strlen` on untrusted storage |
| Return allocated string               | `int f(char **out)` with ownership contract              | Returning pointer to local array        |
| Compare strings                       | `strcmp`/bounded comparison as appropriate               | `==` pointer comparison for content     |
| Build paths/commands                  | Dedicated checked builder                                | Chained `strcat`                        |
| Preserve encoding correctness         | Validate encoding or treat as bytes                      | Assuming every `char *` is UTF-8        |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A destination `char *` is enough; the function can copy a string into it.”                                   |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | The callee writes past the end because it does not know capacity.                                             |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A pointer does not carry storage size.                                                                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Every output buffer API should take an explicit capacity and define truncation behavior.                      |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Fixed-size array pointers can encode a size in the type, but ordinary APIs usually use pointer plus capacity. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                            | Why it matters                                  | Better rule                                                            |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming `strncpy` always creates a valid C string | It may not null-terminate if source is too long | Prefer explicit checked copy or `snprintf` pattern                     |
| Silent truncation                                  | Can cause security or identity bugs             | Treat truncation as error unless explicitly acceptable                 |
| Forgetting terminator space                        | Off-by-one write                                | Allocate/capacity-check for length plus one                            |
| Using string APIs for binary data                  | Zero bytes truncate logical data                | Use memory APIs                                                        |
| Returning stack string                             | Dangling pointer                                | Use caller buffer, static constant, or allocated result with ownership |

### Trust Boundaries — external input, parsed representation, and fail-closed design

A trust boundary is any point where data crosses from a less trusted domain into internal program invariants. In C, trust-boundary mistakes easily become memory safety defects because invalid lengths, offsets, tags, and pointers can drive unchecked memory operations.

| Boundary          | Examples                  | C-specific risk                            | Defensive pattern                    |
| ----------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
| Command line      | `argv`                    | Missing arg, encoding, numeric parse error | Check `argc`, parse explicitly       |
| Environment       | `getenv`                  | Missing value, untrusted text              | Treat as optional external input     |
| File format       | bytes from disk           | Truncated, malformed, hostile lengths      | Parse field by field                 |
| Network input     | packet bytes              | Malicious sizes and states                 | Validate before allocation/access    |
| IPC/shared memory | external process data     | race, layout mismatch                      | Version/size checks, synchronization |
| Plugin/FFI        | external binary code      | ABI mismatch, callback misuse              | Narrow ABI contracts                 |
| User callback     | function pointer/userdata | reentrancy, lifetime                       | Callback contract                    |
| OS resource       | file descriptor/path      | TOCTOU, permission, blocking               | Check API returns, avoid assumptions |
| Hardware/MMIO     | registers/memory maps     | volatile, alignment, platform rules        | Platform-specific isolation          |

Parsing boundary pattern:

```c id="p5_parse_boundary"
int parse_message(const unsigned char *buf, size_t len,
                  struct message *out)
{
    uint16_t type;
    uint16_t payload_len;

    if (buf == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    message_init_empty(out);

    if (len < 4) {
        return -1;
    }

    type = read_be16(&buf[0]);
    payload_len = read_be16(&buf[2]);

    if (payload_len > len - 4) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (!message_type_is_valid(type)) {
        return -1;
    }

    return message_set_payload(out, type, &buf[4], payload_len);
}
```

This validates size before access and before allocation. It converts raw bytes into an internal representation only after checking invariants.

Fail-closed principle:

| Design question                  | Safer answer                                  |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Unknown enum value?              | Reject or map to explicit error state         |
| Length too large?                | Reject before allocation                      |
| Truncated data?                  | Reject                                        |
| Unsupported version?             | Reject or use documented compatibility path   |
| Invalid pointer from public API? | Return error or documented contract violation |
| Integer overflow risk?           | Reject before arithmetic                      |
| Unexpected callback result?      | Stop operation and clean up                   |

Interdisciplinary Lens: security engineering
What it clarifies: external data is adversarial until validated; invalid lengths and states become memory-corruption primitives in C.
Language feature involved: arrays, pointers, integer arithmetic, allocation, tagged unions, enums.
Practical consequence: validate before converting, allocating, indexing, copying, or dispatching.
Limit of the lens: security framing identifies risk, but exact correctness still requires ISO C semantics, OS API contracts, and project-specific threat models.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                           |
| -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Casting external bytes to structs      | Alignment, endian, padding, effective type, bounds risks | Parse explicitly                      |
| Allocating before checking maximum     | Resource exhaustion                                      | Apply configured limits first         |
| Integer overflow in offset calculation | Bounds check bypass                                      | Check before addition/multiplication  |
| Continuing after validation failure    | Internal invariant contamination                         | Return failure immediately            |
| Trusting callback/plugin behavior      | External code may violate assumptions                    | Treat callbacks as boundary crossings |

### Isolating Unsafe, Dynamic, or Unchecked Behavior — narrow waists and audit zones

C code often needs unchecked operations: casts, raw memory access, `void *`, unions, platform syscalls, compiler extensions, atomics, inline assembly, or ABI-level interfaces. The professional goal is not to pretend these do not exist, but to isolate them behind narrow, documented boundaries.

| Unsafe/dynamic mechanism      | Why needed                                 | Isolation strategy                               |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Pointer cast                  | FFI, generic data, representation access   | Validate before cast; wrap in helper             |
| `void *`                      | Generic callback/container                 | Pair with size/type/destructor/callback contract |
| Union punning/variant storage | Efficient alternatives                     | Use explicit tag and accessors                   |
| Raw memory copy               | Serialization, buffers, object copying     | Use only on valid storage; define overlap        |
| Compiler extension            | Diagnostics, performance, platform support | Guard by compiler feature macros                 |
| POSIX syscall                 | OS integration                             | Platform adapter layer                           |
| Inline assembly               | Hardware-specific operation                | Tiny architecture-specific module                |
| Atomics                       | Lock-free/shared scalar                    | Encapsulate memory-order protocol                |
| Signal handler state          | Async signal context                       | Use permitted operations only                    |
| ABI struct/function table     | Plugin/FFI                                 | Versioned, documented ABI                        |

Bad boundary:

```c id="p5_bad_unsafe_scatter"
/* Casts and raw reads scattered throughout parser */
const struct header *h = (const struct header *)buf;
process_type(h->type);
```

Better boundary:

```c id="p5_good_unsafe_boundary"
int header_parse(const unsigned char *buf, size_t len, struct header *out)
{
    if (buf == NULL || out == NULL || len < 4) {
        return -1;
    }

    out->type = read_be16(&buf[0]);
    out->length = read_be16(&buf[2]);

    return header_is_valid(out) ? 0 : -1;
}
```

The rest of the program sees a validated `struct header`, not raw bytes.

Audit-zone table:

| Boundary type              | Keep inside                                                | Expose outside                            |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Parser boundary            | Byte arithmetic, endian conversion, length checks          | Validated internal struct                 |
| FFI boundary               | ABI casts, foreign handles, calling convention assumptions | Typed wrapper functions                   |
| Generic container boundary | `void *`, element size, destructor callback                | Type-specific wrapper when possible       |
| Platform boundary          | POSIX/Windows APIs, errno mapping                          | Portable project API                      |
| Atomic boundary            | Memory orders and fences                                   | Simple thread-safe operation              |
| Macro boundary             | Token tricks                                               | Typed functions or generated explicit API |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Unsafe C operations are fine as long as the programmer is careful at each call site.”                                                          |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | One of many repeated casts forgets a length or alignment check.                                                                                 |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Human review does not scale when unchecked operations are scattered.                                                                            |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Put unchecked operations behind small helpers with explicit preconditions and tests.                                                            |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Performance-critical inner loops may contain low-level operations, but they need even stronger local documentation and validation before entry. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters             | Better rule                              |
| -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Scattered casts                              | Hard to audit              | Centralize conversion                    |
| Public `void *` APIs without wrappers        | Type errors spread         | Provide typed wrappers                   |
| Compiler extension in public portable header | Portability break          | Guard and isolate extensions             |
| Atomics exposed as raw fields                | Callers break protocol     | Provide operations, not raw shared state |
| Inline assembly mixed with business logic    | Nonportable and untestable | Isolate per architecture                 |

### Compatibility Boundaries — source compatibility, binary compatibility, ABI, and versioning

C compatibility has several layers. Source compatibility means code can be recompiled. Binary compatibility means existing compiled objects continue to work. ABI compatibility depends on platform calling conventions, object layout, symbol names, alignment, enum representation, and library loading behavior. ISO C does not define most ABI details.

| Compatibility kind     | Question                                        | Example break                                  |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Source compatibility   | Can client source still compile?                | Removed function declaration                   |
| Semantic compatibility | Does behavior remain compatible?                | Function now treats truncation as success      |
| Binary compatibility   | Can old object code still link/run?             | Changed struct layout or function signature    |
| ABI compatibility      | Are calling/layout conventions unchanged?       | Changed parameter type or enum size assumption |
| API compatibility      | Is public contract stable?                      | Changed ownership rule                         |
| Build compatibility    | Does project still build with same flags/tools? | New compiler extension required                |
| Platform compatibility | Does target OS/compiler still work?             | POSIX API introduced into ISO-only library     |

ABI-sensitive changes:

| Change                         | Source impact               | Binary impact                             |
| ------------------------------ | --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Add new function               | Usually compatible          | Usually compatible                        |
| Remove function                | Breaks                      | Breaks                                    |
| Change function parameter type | Breaks or warns             | Breaks ABI                                |
| Change return type             | Breaks                      | Breaks ABI                                |
| Add field to public struct     | May require source changes  | Breaks layout for old binaries            |
| Reorder struct fields          | May compile                 | Breaks semantics/ABI                      |
| Change enum values             | May compile                 | Breaks logic and possibly ABI assumptions |
| Change macro expansion         | Recompile required          | Source semantics may change               |
| Hide public struct later       | Breaks clients using fields | Breaks source design                      |
| Change ownership rule          | May compile                 | Semantic break                            |

Versioned options struct:

```c id="p5_versioned_options"
struct image_options {
    size_t struct_size;
    unsigned flags;
    unsigned max_width;
    unsigned max_height;
};

int image_decode_file(const char *path,
                      const struct image_options *options,
                      Image **out);
```

The callee can check `options->struct_size` to support older callers compiled with smaller versions of the struct.

Opaque type for ABI stability:

```c id="p5_opaque_abi"
typedef struct image Image;

int image_width(const Image *img);
int image_height(const Image *img);
void image_destroy(Image *img);
```

Clients do not depend on `struct image` layout.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                      | Why it matters                     | Better rule                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Treating public structs as easy to change                    | ABI/source break                   | Use opaque types if evolution matters    |
| Changing function signatures instead of adding new functions | Breaks callers                     | Add versioned/new function               |
| Exporting too many symbols                                   | Larger compatibility burden        | Keep internal symbols `static` or hidden |
| Assuming ABI is ISO C                                        | ABI is platform/toolchain-specific | Document target ABI                      |
| Changing ownership semantics silently                        | Compiles but breaks memory safety  | Treat ownership as API contract          |

### Platform, POSIX, Compiler Extension, and ISO C Boundaries — labeling the layer

C programmers often work across layers. The same source file may contain ISO C, POSIX calls, compiler intrinsics, OS-specific headers, and hardware assumptions. Correct documentation and module organization must label the layer.

| Feature                                   | Layer                            | Portability consequence                           |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| `malloc`, `free`, `fopen`, `fclose`       | ISO C hosted library             | Portable across hosted conforming implementations |
| `_Atomic`, `<stdatomic.h>`                | ISO C11+                         | Standard, but support varies in old toolchains    |
| `pthread_create`                          | POSIX                            | Not ISO C; not native Windows API                 |
| `open`, `read`, `write`, file descriptors | POSIX                            | Not ISO C streams                                 |
| `mmap`                                    | POSIX                            | Platform-specific memory mapping                  |
| `__attribute__((...))`                    | GCC/Clang extension              | Nonportable unless guarded                        |
| `#pragma once`                            | Common compiler extension        | Not ISO C                                         |
| `dlopen`                                  | POSIX-ish dynamic loading family | Platform-specific                                 |
| Windows `HANDLE`                          | Windows API                      | Platform-specific                                 |
| Inline assembly                           | Compiler/architecture-specific   | Highly nonportable                                |
| x86 cache assumption                      | Hardware                         | Not language guarantee                            |
| ELF symbol visibility                     | ABI/toolchain                    | Not ISO C                                         |

Platform adapter example:

```c id="p5_platform_adapter_header"
/* platform_file.h */
#ifndef PLATFORM_FILE_H
#define PLATFORM_FILE_H

typedef struct platform_file PlatformFile;

int platform_file_open_read(PlatformFile **out, const char *path);
int platform_file_read(PlatformFile *file, void *dst, size_t cap, size_t *out_n);
void platform_file_close(PlatformFile *file);

#endif
```

The implementation may use POSIX on Unix-like systems and another API elsewhere. The rest of the program depends on the project-level abstraction rather than scattered platform calls.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                  | Why it matters                      | Better rule                        |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Calling POSIX “C”                                        | Misstates portability               | Say POSIX when using POSIX         |
| Compiler extensions in portable headers                  | Breaks other compilers              | Guard or isolate                   |
| OS-specific types in public cross-platform API           | Leaks platform dependency           | Use opaque wrappers                |
| Hardware assumptions in ordinary code                    | Nonportable and optimizer-sensitive | Isolate architecture-specific code |
| Mixing `FILE *` and file descriptor assumptions casually | Different buffering/lifetime models | Keep API family consistent         |

### Boundary-Oriented Module Design — arranging files around risk

A robust C module often has a narrow public interface, an internal invariant-bearing implementation, and explicit adapters for unsafe or platform-specific edges.

| Module layer                 | Purpose                              | Typical files          | Contents                                         |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Public API                   | Stable client contract               | `include/lib/foo.h`    | Opaque types, public functions, public constants |
| Core implementation          | Maintains invariants                 | `src/foo.c`            | Struct definitions, internal helpers             |
| Internal shared declarations | Implementation-only coordination     | `src/foo_internal.h`   | Private structs/helpers                          |
| Parser/validator boundary    | Converts raw input to internal state | `src/foo_parse.c`      | Length checks, endian conversion                 |
| Platform adapter             | Hides OS differences                 | `src/platform_posix.c` | POSIX calls, errno mapping                       |
| Allocation/resource layer    | Centralizes memory/resource policy   | `src/alloc.c`          | wrappers, failure policy                         |
| Tests/fuzz harnesses         | Exercise boundaries                  | `tests/`, `fuzz/`      | Unit tests, fuzz entry points                    |

Design table:

| Risk                       | Module boundary response                                    |
| -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| External malformed bytes   | Dedicated parser that validates before constructing objects |
| Platform-specific API      | Adapter file with project-level interface                   |
| ABI stability              | Opaque public types                                         |
| Complex resource lifecycle | Central init/destroy functions                              |
| Generic `void *` callbacks | Typed wrapper layer                                         |
| Unsafe casts               | Local helper with preconditions                             |
| Macro complexity           | Private header or generated code                            |
| Global mutable state       | Explicit context object                                     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                        | Why it matters                      | Better rule                      |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| One huge `.c` file                             | Boundaries invisible                | Split by responsibility and risk |
| Header-per-function fragmentation              | Too much surface area               | Organize around cohesive modules |
| Platform `#ifdef`s in core logic               | Hard to test all variants           | Use platform adapter             |
| Parser and business logic interleaved          | Invalid input can contaminate state | Parse/validate first             |
| Private invariants exposed to tests as default | Tests overfit implementation        | Test public behavior primarily   |

### Sanitizers, Static Analysis, and Boundary Verification — tools support but do not replace reasoning

Although detailed tooling belongs to PART 9, boundary design should anticipate verification. Sanitizers, compiler warnings, static analysis, fuzzing, and assertions are especially valuable around C boundaries.

| Tool/check type              | Finds or helps find                                     | Boundary relevance                     |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Compiler warnings            | Type mismatches, format errors, suspicious control flow | Catch API misuse early                 |
| Address sanitizer            | Out-of-bounds, use-after-free, double free              | Memory/resource boundaries             |
| Undefined behavior sanitizer | Some UB classes                                         | Arithmetic, shifts, invalid operations |
| Thread sanitizer             | Data races                                              | Concurrency boundaries                 |
| Memory sanitizer             | Uninitialized reads                                     | Initialization boundaries              |
| Static analyzer              | Leaks, null dereferences, paths                         | Error/cleanup paths                    |
| Fuzzer                       | Input-driven crashes and sanitizer findings             | Parser/trust boundaries                |
| Assertions                   | Internal invariant violations                           | Module correctness                     |
| Unit tests                   | Contract behavior                                       | Public API boundaries                  |

Tool-literate boundary design:

| Boundary                     | Useful verification                      |
| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Parser                       | Fuzz with sanitizers                     |
| Allocation/cleanup           | Leak checks, ASan                        |
| Format-string APIs           | Compiler format warnings                 |
| Public API invalid arguments | Unit tests                               |
| Concurrency                  | TSan plus design review                  |
| Integer arithmetic           | UBSan, explicit checked arithmetic tests |
| Platform adapter             | Per-platform CI                          |
| ABI boundary                 | ABI tests/symbol checks where needed     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                     | Why it matters                            | Better rule                          |
| ------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Treating sanitizer-clean as proof           | Tools cover executions, not all behaviors | Combine tests with reasoning         |
| Running fuzzers without sanitizers          | Bugs may not become visible               | Fuzz under sanitizers                |
| Using assertions instead of tests           | Assertions check reached paths only       | Test boundary cases                  |
| Ignoring warnings in boundary code          | Warnings often indicate real defects      | Use strict warning policy            |
| Static analyzer suppressions without review | Hides real bugs                           | Suppress narrowly with justification |

### Boundary Task Decision Table — module, error, resource, and trust-boundary reference

| Boundary task              | Construct/API/pattern                 | Professional use                   | Pitfall                      |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Declare public API         | Self-contained header                 | Stable declarations                | Include-order dependence     |
| Hide implementation        | Opaque struct                         | Preserve invariants/layout freedom | Missing lifecycle API        |
| Hide helper symbols        | File-scope `static`                   | Internal linkage                   | Accidental external symbol   |
| Share global object        | `extern` declaration + one definition | Rare shared state                  | Header definition            |
| Organize private internals | Private header                        | Multi-file implementation          | Accidentally installed       |
| Handle simple failure      | `int` status                          | Conventional success/failure       | Ignored return               |
| Handle rich failure        | Error enum/object                     | Caller can distinguish causes      | Overcomplicated API          |
| Return fallible value      | Status plus output                    | Avoid sentinel collision           | Undefined output on failure  |
| Allocate object            | `create/destroy`                      | Opaque heap object                 | Ownership ambiguity          |
| Initialize caller storage  | `init/deinit`                         | Stack/embedded object              | Wrong cleanup function       |
| Release partial resources  | `goto cleanup`                        | Multiple fallible acquisitions     | Arbitrary jumps              |
| Manage strings             | pointer plus capacity/length          | Bounds-aware API                   | Silent truncation            |
| Parse external bytes       | Field-wise parser                     | Trust boundary                     | Raw struct cast              |
| Isolate POSIX/OS calls     | Platform adapter                      | Portability                        | Scattered `#ifdef`s          |
| Isolate casts/unsafe code  | Narrow helper                         | Auditable unchecked operation      | Scattered unsafe assumptions |
| Maintain ABI               | Opaque/versioned structs              | Shared libraries/plugins           | Public layout freeze         |
| Use callbacks safely       | Contract for timing/lifetime/thread   | Event/traversal APIs               | Dangling userdata            |
| Verify boundary            | Sanitizers, fuzzing, static analysis  | Defect detection                   | Treating tools as proof      |

### Resource Pattern Decision Table — guarantees, costs, and appropriate use

| Resource pattern       | Guarantee                                           | Cost                              | Best use                        | Common failure               |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| `malloc/free` directly | Explicit allocation and release                     | Manual cleanup                    | Simple local allocations        | Leak on early return         |
| `init/deinit`          | Caller storage reused and explicit internal cleanup | More call discipline              | Embedded structs, stack objects | Partial init mishandled      |
| `create/destroy`       | Allocation and initialization paired                | Heap dependency                   | Opaque objects                  | Caller forgets destroy       |
| `open/close`           | External session lifecycle                          | OS/API errors                     | Files, devices, connections     | Close failure ignored        |
| `retain/release`       | Shared ownership by convention                      | Reference-count correctness       | Shared library objects          | Cycle/leak/premature release |
| Cleanup label          | Centralized cleanup                                 | `goto` discipline                 | Multiple resources              | Jump into invalid scope      |
| Parent destructor      | Releases nested resources                           | Must maintain valid state         | Aggregates with owned members   | Missing member cleanup       |
| Transfer function      | Avoids copy and clarifies ownership change          | Contract complexity               | Large buffers, handles          | Caller uses after transfer   |
| Borrowed view          | Avoids copying                                      | Lifetime/invalidation constraints | Internal data access            | Stale pointer                |

### Error Mechanism Decision Table — when each failure channel fits

| Error mechanism           | Use when                                   | Avoid when                               | Key contract                            |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| `0` success, `-1` failure | Caller only needs success/failure          | Multiple recovery actions needed         | Whether `errno` or output is meaningful |
| Error enum                | Known failure categories                   | Need OS-specific raw errors              | Enum value meanings stable              |
| Null pointer return       | Lookup/optional object/allocation          | Need detailed diagnostics                | Whether null means absence or failure   |
| Sentinel value            | Valid domain has reserved impossible value | Domain may later include sentinel        | Sentinel formally reserved              |
| Status plus output        | Result domain has no spare sentinel        | Very small infallible computation        | Output state on failure                 |
| `errno`                   | API specifically documents it              | Custom portable library status           | Save/check only after failure           |
| Error object              | Need rich message/context                  | Allocation failure path must stay simple | Ownership/lifetime of error object      |
| `assert`                  | Internal programmer invariant              | External input validation                | Disabled builds must remain safe        |
| Fatal abort               | Continuing is unsafe                       | Recoverable user/environment error       | Diagnostic and cleanup policy           |

### Boundary Failure Modes — common defects by category

| Failure mode              | Boundary category             | Typical cause                              | Review cue                                                 |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| Multiple definition       | Header/module                 | Definition placed in header                | Does this header define storage or external function body? |
| Undefined reference       | Link boundary                 | Object/library missing                     | Is dependency linked?                                      |
| ABI mismatch              | Binary boundary               | Different declaration/definition or layout | Is one header shared by both sides?                        |
| Symbol collision          | Linkage boundary              | Unprefixed external names                  | Are private helpers `static`?                              |
| Memory leak               | Resource boundary             | Early return after acquisition             | Is there a cleanup path?                                   |
| Double free               | Ownership boundary            | Unclear ownership transfer                 | Who owns after this call?                                  |
| Use-after-free            | Lifetime boundary             | Borrowed pointer retained too long         | Does pointer outlive use?                                  |
| Buffer overflow           | Trust/string boundary         | Missing capacity or length check           | Where is size validated?                                   |
| Silent truncation         | String boundary               | Ignored `snprintf` result                  | Is truncation success or failure?                          |
| Stale `errno`             | Error boundary                | Checked without failure signal             | Did API document `errno`?                                  |
| Deadlock                  | Resource/concurrency boundary | Missing unlock or callback under lock      | Is lock released on all paths?                             |
| Invalid enum state        | Trust boundary                | External integer cast to enum              | Was value validated?                                       |
| Platform lock-in          | Portability boundary          | POSIX/compiler extension leaks             | Is layer labeled and isolated?                             |
| Macro pollution           | Header boundary               | Generic macro names                        | Are macros prefixed and minimal?                           |
| Untested conditional code | Build boundary                | `#ifdef` variants not built                | Does CI cover variants?                                    |

### Common Pitfalls and Anti-Patterns — Part 5 index

| Item                                             | Classification               | Why it is harmful                        | Better pattern                            |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Defining globals in headers                      | Common pitfall               | Multiple definitions or duplicated state | `extern` declaration plus one definition  |
| Public invariant-bearing struct                  | Design anti-pattern          | Clients can violate invariants           | Opaque type                               |
| Scattered `#ifdef` platform code                 | Portability anti-pattern     | Untested fragmented logic                | Platform adapter                          |
| Returning error but leaving output indeterminate | Common pitfall               | Caller may read garbage                  | Define output state                       |
| `assert` for user input                          | Dangerous misuse             | Disabled in release builds               | Runtime validation                        |
| Ignored return values                            | Common pitfall               | Failure silently propagates              | Check fallible calls                      |
| Direct `realloc` assignment                      | Common pitfall               | Original pointer lost on failure         | Use temporary                             |
| Silent string truncation                         | Security/correctness pitfall | Wrong identity/path/command              | Treat truncation as error unless explicit |
| Raw struct parse from external bytes             | Dangerous pattern            | Alignment/endian/padding/UB risk         | Explicit parser                           |
| Hidden ownership transfer                        | API design failure           | Double free/use-after-free               | Naming and documentation                  |
| Public compiler extension in portable API        | Portability failure          | Non-target compilers break               | Guard or isolate                          |
| Callback retaining stack userdata                | Lifetime bug                 | Dangling pointer                         | Define synchronous vs retained            |
| Cleanup duplicated by copy-paste                 | Under-abstraction            | One path drifts                          | Destroy helper/cleanup label              |
| Fatal exit for recoverable library error         | API anti-pattern             | Caller loses control                     | Return error                              |
| Continuing after corrupted invariant             | Safety failure               | Further memory corruption                | Fail closed                               |

### What Experienced C Programmers Notice — boundary review cues

| Code feature                      | Review question                                                                  |
| --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Public header                     | Is it self-contained and minimal?                                                |
| Header includes many dependencies | Are they required for public types, or can forward declarations reduce exposure? |
| File-scope function               | Should it be `static`?                                                           |
| Global object                     | Why is explicit context not used?                                                |
| `extern` declaration              | Where is the single definition?                                                  |
| Public struct                     | Is layout intentionally part of API/ABI?                                         |
| Opaque handle                     | Is lifecycle clear?                                                              |
| Function returning pointer        | Is it owned, borrowed, static, or internal view?                                 |
| Function taking pointer           | Is it nullable, borrowed, retained, mutated, or taken?                           |
| Function taking buffer            | Where is length/capacity?                                                        |
| `goto cleanup`                    | Are all resources initialized and released correctly?                            |
| `errno` use                       | Was failure detected before reading `errno`?                                     |
| `assert`                          | Is this an internal invariant, not untrusted input?                              |
| `#ifdef`                          | Is this platform layer isolated?                                                 |
| Cast near boundary                | What validation makes it legal?                                                  |
| Callback registration             | How long must `userdata` live?                                                   |
| Close/destroy call                | Can it fail, and is failure policy defined?                                      |

### What to Memorize vs What to Look Up — Part 5 artifacts

| Memorize                                                                     | Reason                                                      |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Headers are textual inclusion, not compiled modules                          | Prevents declaration/definition mistakes                    |
| Declarations belong in headers; ordinary definitions belong in one `.c` file | Prevents multiple-definition and missing-prototype problems |
| `static` at file scope means internal linkage                                | Hides private symbols                                       |
| `extern` in header requires exactly one definition elsewhere                 | Correct shared-object pattern                               |
| Scope, linkage, storage duration, and binary visibility are distinct         | Prevents global/static confusion                            |
| Public structs expose representation                                         | ABI and invariant consequences                              |
| C has no native RAII                                                         | Cleanup must be explicit                                    |
| `init/deinit` and `create/destroy` are different lifecycle families          | Prevents invalid free/cleanup                               |
| Every pointer API needs ownership/lifetime/nullability contract              | Types do not encode these                                   |
| `errno` is meaningful only when an API says so                               | Prevents stale-error bugs                                   |
| `assert` is not external validation                                          | Release builds may disable it                               |
| Trust-boundary data must be validated before use                             | Prevents memory-corruption paths                            |
| POSIX/compiler/OS/hardware behavior is not ISO C                             | Maintains portability clarity                               |

| Look up when needed                             | Why                                                      |
| ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Exact ISO C rules for external/internal linkage | Subtle cases with tentative definitions and declarations |
| Compiler/linker symbol visibility options       | Toolchain-specific                                       |
