| Human-readable config               | Simple line format or established library | Ad hoc fragile parser                       |
| Complex structured data             | Established JSON/XML/etc. library         | Large handwritten parser without tests      |
| High-performance binary interchange | Schema-based format/library               | Unversioned raw memory layout               |
| Internal cache file                 | Versioned explicit format                 | Assuming current struct layout is permanent |
| Security boundary                   | Parser with length checks and fuzzing     | Trusting lengths/tags from input            |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                          | Why it matters                         | Better rule                             |
| -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Serializing structs directly     | Layout and padding are not portable    | Serialize fields explicitly             |
| Forgetting endian                | Multi-byte integers vary by host       | Choose format endian                    |
| Trusting serialized length       | Malicious or corrupt input can overrun | Validate before allocation/access       |
| No version field                 | Format cannot evolve                   | Include version/magic where appropriate |
| Using text parser without bounds | Buffer overflow or truncation          | Parse with explicit lengths             |

### Logging and Observability — no standard logging framework

ISO C provides output streams and formatting but no logging framework, log levels, structured logging, tracing, metrics, or observability API. Serious programs usually build or adopt a logging layer.

| Task                    | ISO C tool                     | Ecosystem/project pattern    | Caveat                    |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| Print diagnostic        | `fprintf(stderr, ...)`         | Basic error output           | Format-string safety      |
| Include source location | `__FILE__`, `__LINE__`, macros | Logging macro wrapper        | Macro layer               |
| Log levels              | None                           | Project logger               | Global state/threading    |
| Structured logs         | None                           | JSON/log library             | Allocation/failure policy |
| Metrics/tracing         | None                           | Platform/library integration | Runtime overhead          |
| Error messages          | `strerror`, `perror`           | Error abstraction            | `errno` rules             |

Simple logging macro:

```c id="p6_log_macro"
#include <stdio.h>

#define LOG_ERROR(fmt, ...) \
    fprintf(stderr, "error:%s:%d: " fmt "\n", __FILE__, __LINE__, __VA_ARGS__)
```

This style is common, but variadic macro details and empty `__VA_ARGS__` behavior depend on standard/compiler support. A production logging layer should define portability policy.

Non-macro function:

```c id="p6_log_function"
void log_error_message(const char *message)
{
    if (message != NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "error: %s\n", message);
    }
}
```

A function is easier to type-check but cannot automatically capture call-site file and line without macro assistance.

`perror` example:

```c id="p6_perror"
FILE *fp = fopen(path, "rb");
if (fp == NULL) {
    perror("fopen");
    return -1;
}
```

`perror` reports based on `errno`. It is appropriate only immediately after an API failure where `errno` is meaningful.

Logging design table:

| Requirement              | Pattern                                          |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Minimal CLI diagnostic   | `fprintf(stderr, ...)`                           |
| Include file/line        | Macro wrapper                                    |
| Library code             | Usually do not print directly unless API says so |
| Application code         | Central logger acceptable                        |
| Error propagation        | Return error code; log at boundary               |
| Security-sensitive logs  | Avoid leaking secrets and untrusted raw data     |
| Concurrent logs          | Locking or atomic write strategy                 |
| Embedded/no-stdio target | Platform logging hook                            |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                         | Why it matters                    | Better rule                       |
| ----------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Library logs every error and also returns error | Duplicate noisy diagnostics       | Decide reporting layer            |
| Logging untrusted strings as format             | Format-string vulnerability       | Use fixed format: `"%s"`          |
| Logging after `errno` changed                   | Wrong diagnostic                  | Save `errno` immediately          |
| Logging secrets                                 | Security leak                     | Redact sensitive data             |
| Logging from signal handler                     | Most I/O is not async-signal-safe | Use restricted signal-safe design |

### Assertions, Diagnostics, and Defensive Checks — `<assert.h>` and project policy

`<assert.h>` provides `assert`, a diagnostic macro for programmer errors. It may be disabled by defining `NDEBUG`. It is not a replacement for runtime validation of external data.

| Mechanism          | Use for                                                 | Not for                                  |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `assert(expr)`     | Internal invariant, impossible state during development | User input, file data, network data      |
| Runtime `if` check | External input and recoverable errors                   | Proving internal logic during tests only |
| Static assertion   | Compile-time constants/layout assumptions               | Runtime validation                       |
| Compiler warnings  | Suspicious source patterns                              | Semantic proof                           |
| Sanitizers         | Dynamic detection of memory/UB/race issues              | Exhaustive correctness proof             |

Example:

```c id="p6_assert_invariant"
#include <assert.h>

void buffer_check_invariant(const struct buffer *buf)
{
    assert(buf != NULL);
    assert(buf->len <= buf->cap);
    assert(buf->data != NULL || buf->cap == 0);
}
```

External validation must remain runtime logic:

```c id="p6_runtime_validation"
if (payload_len > remaining) {
    return -1;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                  | Better rule                              |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `assert(user_input_is_valid)`                 | Can disappear in release builds | Use runtime validation                   |
| Assertions with side effects                  | Behavior changes under `NDEBUG` | Keep assert expressions side-effect-free |
| No assertions for complex internal invariants | Bugs found later and harder     | Assert module invariants in debug builds |
| Treating assert failure as recoverable        | It signals a programmer bug     | Fix code or validate earlier             |
| Overasserting obvious syntax facts            | Noise                           | Assert meaningful invariants             |

### Nonlocal Control Flow — `setjmp`, `longjmp`, and why they are specialized

`<setjmp.h>` provides `setjmp` and `longjmp`, a low-level nonlocal jump facility. It is sometimes used for error escape in old C libraries, interpreters, parsers, or embedded runtimes, but it is dangerous because it bypasses normal local cleanup.

| Mechanism             | Meaning                        | Use                    | Caveat                     |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------- | -------------------------- |
| `setjmp(env)`         | Save calling environment       | Establish jump point   | Return behavior is unusual |
| `longjmp(env, value)` | Jump back to saved environment | Escape deep call stack | Skips ordinary cleanup     |
| `jmp_buf`             | Saved context object           | Storage for jump state | Lifetime and scope matter  |

Conceptual example:

```c id="p6_setjmp_shape"
#include <setjmp.h>

static jmp_buf env;

void fail_now(void)
{
    longjmp(env, 1);
}

int run(void)
{
    if (setjmp(env) != 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    do_work_that_may_longjmp();
    return 0;
}
```

This is not exception handling with destructors. Resources acquired between `setjmp` and `longjmp` are not automatically released. In portable C, this can easily produce leaks and invalid states.

Decision table:

| Situation                      | Use `setjmp`/`longjmp`?                     |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Ordinary recoverable error     | Usually no; use return codes                |
| Deep interpreter/parser escape | Possibly, with strict cleanup design        |
| Signal recovery                | Highly specialized; signal rules apply      |
| Resource-heavy code            | Usually avoid                               |
| Public API                     | Avoid unless API explicitly built around it |
| Embedded/fatal escape          | Project-specific                            |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                        | Why it matters                                   | Better rule                                          |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Skipped cleanup                                | Leaks resources                                  | Avoid or centralize cleanup around jump boundaries   |
| Jumping to dead stack frame                    | Invalid control flow                             | `jmp_buf` must remain valid                          |
| Local variable value surprises after `longjmp` | Standard has restrictions around modified locals | Keep jump regions simple                             |
| Using it as ordinary exception system          | C lacks automatic destructors                    | Prefer return-code architecture                      |
| Mixing with locks                              | Deadlocks if unlock skipped                      | Do not longjmp across lock ownership unless designed |

### Signals — minimal ISO C facility, severe safety restrictions

`<signal.h>` provides a minimal signal facility. Real-world signal handling is often POSIX-specific. ISO C signal handlers are extremely restricted; ordinary library calls are not generally safe from signal handlers.

| Task                    | ISO C facility          | Caveat                                                    |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Register signal handler | `signal`                | Semantics limited and implementation-dependent in details |
| Raise signal            | `raise`                 | Controlled signal delivery                                |
| Communicate simple flag | `volatile sig_atomic_t` | Narrow use case                                           |
| Complex signal handling | Not portable ISO C      | POSIX-specific rules and async-signal-safe functions      |

Minimal flag pattern:

```c id="p6_signal_flag"
#include <signal.h>

static volatile sig_atomic_t interrupted = 0;

static void handle_signal(int sig)
{
    (void)sig;
    interrupted = 1;
}
```

The handler should do almost nothing. It should not call `printf`, `malloc`, or most library functions.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                    | Why it matters               | Better rule                          |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Calling non-safe functions in signal handler               | Undefined or unsafe behavior | Set a flag and return                |
| Treating `volatile sig_atomic_t` as thread synchronization | Different problem            | Use atomics/locks for threads        |
| Complex cleanup inside handler                             | Reentrancy hazards           | Defer cleanup to normal control flow |
| Assuming POSIX signal rules are ISO C                      | Different standards          | Label POSIX-specific behavior        |

### Atomics and Threads — ISO C facilities and ecosystem reality

C11 introduced atomics in `<stdatomic.h>` and threads in `<threads.h>`. In practice, atomics are important, while C threads support varies across toolchains and platforms. Many C projects use POSIX threads, Windows threads, or higher-level libraries.

| Task                     | ISO C facility       | Common ecosystem alternative      | Caveat                          |
| ------------------------ | -------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Atomic scalar operations | `<stdatomic.h>`      | Compiler/platform atomics         | Requires memory-order reasoning |
| Thread creation          | `<threads.h>`        | POSIX `pthread`, Windows threads  | Support/portability varies      |
| Mutex                    | `mtx_t` in C threads | `pthread_mutex_t`, platform locks | API layer differs               |
| Condition variable       | C threads            | POSIX/platform                    | Correct protocol required       |
| Thread-local storage     | `_Thread_local`      | compiler/platform TLS             | Initialization/lifetime caveats |
| Data-race detection      | None in ISO C        | ThreadSanitizer                   | Tooling, not language guarantee |

Atomic counter example:

```c id="p6_atomic_counter"
#include <stdatomic.h>

static atomic_uint counter = 0;

unsigned next_counter(void)
{
    return atomic_fetch_add(&counter, 1) + 1;
}
```

This is fine for a simple counter. It does not make a multi-field data structure safe.

Mutex-protected API shape:

```c id="p6_mutex_shape"
/* Lock type omitted because it depends on chosen threading API. */
struct shared_queue {
    void *lock;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
    void **items;
};
```

For compound invariants such as `len <= cap` and `items` consistency, a lock or carefully designed atomic protocol is required. One atomic field is not enough.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                | Better rule                                                           |
| -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using `volatile` for threads                 | Not synchronization           | Use atomics or locks                                                  |
| Making only one field atomic                 | Compound invariant still racy | Synchronize the whole invariant                                       |
| Choosing memory order casually               | Incorrect visibility/order    | Use simple sequential consistency until expertise justifies otherwise |
| Assuming C threads are universally available | Toolchain support varies      | Check project target                                                  |
| Calling callbacks while holding locks        | Deadlock/reentrancy           | Define callback-lock policy                                           |

### Networking — not ISO C, use POSIX/platform/library layer

ISO C has no socket API, DNS API, HTTP client, TLS, event loop, or async I/O. Networking is necessarily POSIX, platform-specific, or library-based.

| Task                    | ISO C support        | Common option                               |
| ----------------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| TCP/UDP sockets         | None                 | POSIX sockets, Winsock                      |
| DNS resolution          | None                 | `getaddrinfo` on POSIX/platform             |
| TLS                     | None                 | OpenSSL, mbedTLS, wolfSSL, platform TLS     |
| HTTP client             | None                 | libcurl, platform/library                   |
| Event loop              | None                 | libevent, libuv, epoll/kqueue/IOCP wrappers |
| Binary protocol parsing | Byte operations only | Custom parser with validation               |
| Serialization           | None                 | protocol libraries                          |

Networking boundary strategy:

| Requirement            | Pattern                                    |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Cross-platform sockets | Platform adapter or library                |
| TLS correctness        | Mature TLS library                         |
| HTTP                   | libcurl or equivalent                      |
| Custom protocol        | Explicit parser, fuzz tests                |
| Nonblocking I/O        | Event library or platform-specific layer   |
| Security boundary      | Validate lengths, states, timeouts, errors |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters                                   | Better rule                     |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------- |
| Treating sockets as C standard library   | Not ISO C                                        | Label POSIX/platform dependency |
| Writing TLS manually                     | Security-critical complexity                     | Use mature TLS library          |
| Assuming one `send` writes everything    | Partial writes                                   | Loop until complete or error    |
| Assuming one `recv` reads a full message | Stream boundaries differ from message boundaries | Frame protocol explicitly       |
| Trusting network length fields           | Memory corruption risk                           | Validate before allocation/copy |

### Subprocesses and OS Interaction — outside ISO C except minimal `system`

ISO C provides `system`, which passes a command string to the host environment. It is limited and often unsafe for untrusted input. Process management, pipes, environment control, working directories, permissions, and signals are POSIX/platform concerns.

| Task                 | ISO C support | POSIX/platform alternative                |
| -------------------- | ------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Run shell command    | `system`      | `fork/exec`, `posix_spawn`, platform APIs |
| Capture output       | None          | pipes + process APIs, popen on POSIX      |
| Environment variable | `getenv`      | POSIX `setenv`, platform APIs             |
| Current directory    | None          | POSIX/platform                            |
| File permissions     | None          | POSIX/platform                            |
| Process ID           | None          | POSIX/platform                            |
| Subprocess timeout   | None          | Platform/event logic                      |

`system` caution:

```c id="p6_system_caution"
int rc = system("ls");
```

Never build a `system` command by concatenating untrusted input.

Environment variable:

```c id="p6_getenv"
#include <stdlib.h>

const char *home = getenv("HOME");
if (home != NULL) {
    /* Treat as borrowed pointer to environment-managed storage. */
}
```

The returned pointer must not be freed and may be invalidated by environment changes.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                 | Better rule                                      |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Passing untrusted data to `system`            | Command injection              | Avoid shell; use exec-style APIs where available |
| Assuming `HOME` exists                        | Environment is optional        | Handle missing values                            |
| Modifying `getenv` result                     | Undefined/unsupported behavior | Treat as read-only borrowed data                 |
| Assuming POSIX process model on all platforms | Windows differs                | Use adapter/library                              |
| Ignoring subprocess exit conventions          | Shell/platform-specific        | Define target behavior                           |

### Command-Line Interfaces — `argc`, `argv`, parsing, and ecosystem choices

ISO C gives `main(int argc, char **argv)` but no standard option parser. Command-line parsing is therefore manual, POSIX (`getopt`), platform-specific, or library-based.

| CLI task             | ISO C support          | Common option                          |
| -------------------- | ---------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Access arguments     | `argc`, `argv`         | Standard                               |
| Parse integers       | `strtol` family        | Standard                               |
| Parse options        | None                   | POSIX `getopt`, custom parser, library |
| Print usage          | `fprintf(stderr, ...)` | Standard                               |
| Environment config   | `getenv`               | Standard for reading                   |
| Unicode command line | Not portable ISO C     | Platform-specific                      |

Manual minimal parsing:

```c id="p6_cli_manual"
int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
    int port;

    if (argc != 3) {
        fprintf(stderr, "usage: app --port PORT\n");
        return 2;
    }

    if (strcmp(argv[1], "--port") != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "unknown option: %s\n", argv[1]);
        return 2;
    }

    if (parse_int_range(argv[2], 1, 65535, &port) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "invalid port\n");
        return 2;
    }

    return run_server(port) == 0 ? 0 : 1;
}
```

CLI parsing decision table:

| Need                    | Prefer                                              |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Tiny tool               | Manual parsing                                      |
| POSIX-style options     | `getopt` if POSIX is acceptable                     |
| Cross-platform rich CLI | Third-party parser or project parser                |
| Numeric option          | `strtol` with range checks                          |
| File path argument      | Treat as external platform string                   |
| Exit status             | Document `0` success and nonzero failure categories |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                     | Why it matters                           | Better rule                       |
| ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Accessing `argv[1]` without checking `argc` | Out-of-bounds access                     | Check `argc` first                |
| Using `atoi` for options                    | No reliable error detection              | Use `strtol`                      |
| Assuming argument encoding                  | Platform/environment dependent           | Define policy                     |
| Returning arbitrary exit values             | Host interpretation may vary             | Use small documented status codes |
| Parsing options after side effects          | Invalid input may partially mutate state | Parse/validate first              |

### Configuration — files, environment, defaults, and validation

ISO C has no standard configuration framework. Configuration typically combines defaults, command-line arguments, environment variables, and config files parsed by project code or libraries.

| Config source           | Strength                 | Risk                                          |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------- |
| Compile-time macro      | Simple build-time switch | Rebuild required; configuration fragmentation |
| Default constants       | Predictable              | Hard-coded policy                             |
| Command-line args       | Explicit per invocation  | Parsing burden                                |
| Environment variables   | Easy deployment override | Global, untyped, untrusted strings            |
| Config file             | Rich persistent config   | Parser/security burden                        |
| Generated config header | Build-system integration | Reproducibility and include hygiene           |

Config loading pipeline:

| Stage                        | Purpose                          |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Establish defaults           | Valid baseline                   |
| Read environment/CLI/file    | External untrusted strings       |
| Parse and validate           | Convert to typed internal config |
| Check cross-field invariants | e.g., `min <= max`               |
| Freeze or pass config object | Avoid global mutation            |
| Report errors clearly        | Make invalid config actionable   |

Example config struct:

```c id="p6_config_struct"
struct app_config {
    unsigned port;
    size_t max_clients;
    int verbose;
};
```

Config parse should produce this internal typed representation only after validation.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                | Why it matters                     | Better rule                             |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Using config values before validation  | Invalid state contaminates program | Validate first                          |
| Environment values treated as trusted  | External input                     | Parse like user input                   |
| Scattered global config reads          | Hard to test and reason            | Load once into config object            |
| Compile-time macros for runtime policy | Build variants multiply            | Prefer runtime config where appropriate |
| No bounds on config values             | Resource exhaustion                | Apply limits                            |

### Testing — ISO C has no built-in framework

C has `assert`, but no standard unit-test framework. Testing is ecosystem/project-defined. Common options include custom test harnesses, CMocka, Unity, Check, Criterion, GoogleTest for C-compatible tests in C++ contexts, or project-specific minimal frameworks.

| Testing task              | ISO C support | Ecosystem/project option                             |
| ------------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Simple internal assertion | `assert`      | Debug-only                                           |
| Unit tests                | None          | Custom harness or framework                          |
| Property/fuzz testing     | None          | libFuzzer, AFL++, honggfuzz                          |
| Memory error detection    | None          | ASan/MSan/Valgrind                                   |
| Race detection            | None          | TSan                                                 |
| Coverage                  | None          | gcov/llvm-cov                                        |
| Mocking                   | None          | Link-time substitution, function pointers, framework |

Minimal test harness shape:

```c id="p6_test_harness"
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

#define CHECK(expr) do {                                      \
    if (!(expr)) {                                            \
        fprintf(stderr, "check failed: %s:%d: %s\n",          \
                __FILE__, __LINE__, #expr);                   \
        exit(1);                                              \
    }                                                         \
} while (0)

static void test_parse_port(void)
{
    unsigned short port = 0;

    CHECK(parse_port("80", &port) == PARSE_STATUS_OK);
    CHECK(port == 80);
    CHECK(parse_port("999999", &port) == PARSE_STATUS_RANGE);
}

int main(void)
{
    test_parse_port();
    return 0;
}
```

Testing decision table:

| Code type          | Important tests                                 |
| ------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Parser             | Valid, invalid, boundary, fuzz tests            |
| Allocating API     | Success, failure injection if possible, cleanup |
| String/buffer API  | Capacity edges, truncation, nulls               |
| Numeric conversion | Min/max, overflow, junk input                   |
| Data structure     | Invariants after operations                     |
| Platform adapter   | Per-platform integration tests                  |
| Concurrency        | Stress tests plus TSan, not tests alone         |
| ABI/public API     | Header self-compile, symbol/version checks      |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                             | Why it matters                        | Better rule                             |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Treating `assert` as test framework | Assertions vanish under `NDEBUG`      | Use test harness                        |
| No failure-path tests               | C bugs often live in cleanup paths    | Test allocation/I/O/parse failures      |
| No sanitizer runs                   | Memory bugs may pass functional tests | Run tests under sanitizers              |
| Testing only public happy path      | Boundaries unverified                 | Test invalid inputs and edge capacities |
| Flaky concurrency tests as proof    | Races may not reproduce               | Use TSan/design review                  |

### Debugging and Profiling Libraries/Tools — not standard library, but core C practice

Debugging and profiling are not ISO C library features, but they are central to professional C use. Tool choices are implementation/platform-specific.

| Task               | Common tools                                           | What they reveal           |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------- |
| Source debugging   | `gdb`, `lldb`, Visual Studio debugger                  | Stack, variables, memory   |
| Memory errors      | AddressSanitizer, Valgrind                             | OOB, use-after-free, leaks |
| Undefined behavior | UBSan                                                  | Some UB classes            |
| Race detection     | ThreadSanitizer                                        | Data races                 |
| CPU profiling      | `perf`, Instruments, VTune, gprof, callgrind           | Hot functions/call paths   |
| Heap profiling     | heaptrack, massif, sanitizer tools                     | Allocation hotspots        |
| Coverage           | gcov, llvm-cov                                         | Tested code regions        |
| Static analysis    | clang-tidy, scan-build, cppcheck, commercial analyzers | Path-sensitive issues      |

Debugging build flags are ecosystem-specific, but common development builds include debug info and warnings. Production flags differ.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                       | Better rule                                    |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Debugging optimized build without debug info | Variables/control flow confusing     | Use debug build first                          |
| Ignoring compiler warnings                   | Warnings often predict real bugs     | Treat warnings seriously                       |
| Profiling before correctness                 | Optimizes broken behavior            | Validate first                                 |
| Micro-optimizing without profile             | Wasted complexity                    | Measure hotspots                               |
| Running only one tool                        | Each tool sees different bug classes | Combine warnings, sanitizers, tests, profilers |

### Dependency and Package Workflows — no universal standard C package manager

ISO C has no package manager. C dependency management is convention- and toolchain-driven. Projects use system packages, vendored source, Git submodules, CMake FetchContent, Meson wraps, pkg-config, vcpkg, Conan, buildroot/Yocto, or platform package managers.

| Dependency task                  | Common tool/pattern                  | Caveat                                          |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Discover installed library flags | `pkg-config`                         | Unix-like ecosystem; package must provide `.pc` |
| Cross-platform build config      | CMake, Meson                         | Tool-specific language                          |
| Traditional builds               | Make/autotools                       | Mature but variable complexity                  |
| Package/dependency manager       | Conan, vcpkg, system packages        | Ecosystem fragmentation                         |
| Vendor source                    | Add third-party code to tree         | Update/security burden                          |
| Embedded builds                  | buildroot, Yocto, custom             | Cross-compilation complexity                    |
| ABI versioning                   | shared-library version scripts/tools | Platform-specific                               |
| Header-only dependency           | Include source/header                | Macro/config pollution risk                     |

`pkg-config` usage conceptually:

```bash id="p6_pkg_config"
cc main.c $(pkg-config --cflags --libs libcurl) -o app
```

This is not ISO C. It is build ecosystem practice.

Dependency decision table:

| Requirement                    | Prefer                                      |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| System Unix library            | `pkg-config` if available                   |
| Cross-platform project         | CMake or Meson with clear dependency policy |
| Reproducible builds            | Lock versions or vendor carefully           |
| Security-sensitive dependency  | Track CVEs and update process               |
| ABI-stable external library    | Use documented ABI/version constraints      |
| Embedded/cross-compiled target | Toolchain file/sysroot-aware build          |
| Small single-file library      | Vendor with license and update plan         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters                    | Better rule                         |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Assuming dependency manager exists everywhere         | C ecosystem fragmented            | Define project dependency policy    |
| Ignoring ABI version                                  | Runtime link failures or crashes  | Match headers and libraries         |
| Mixing debug/release or runtime libraries incorrectly | ABI/runtime mismatch              | Keep toolchain consistent           |
| Vendoring without update plan                         | Security and bug debt             | Track upstream                      |
| Build scripts hide compiler flags                     | Debugging portability issues hard | Make flags visible and reproducible |

### Build-System Interaction with Library Use — headers, link flags, and feature tests

Library use in C is inseparable from build configuration. Including a header is not enough; the program must also link the corresponding object or library, and feature availability may depend on macros or standards modes.

| Task                    | Source-level action                | Build-level action                  |
| ----------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Use standard header     | `#include <stdio.h>`               | Usually no extra library            |
| Use math library        | `#include <math.h>`                | May need `-lm` on Unix-like systems |
| Use POSIX threads       | `#include <pthread.h>`             | Often `-pthread`                    |
| Use third-party library | Include library header             | Link library flags                  |
| Use optional feature    | Feature-test macro or config check | Build-system detection              |
| Use generated config    | Include `config.h`                 | Generate before compile             |
| Use C standard version  | Write C11/C17/C23 code             | Compiler `-std=` flag               |

Feature detection example:

```c id="p6_config_header_example"
/* generated config.h */
#define HAVE_POSIX_MMAP 1
```

Then:

```c id="p6_config_feature_use"
#include "config.h"

#if HAVE_POSIX_MMAP
/* POSIX mmap implementation */
#else
/* fallback */
#endif
```

Feature-test logic should be centralized. Scattered compiler/OS checks make portability fragile.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                  | Better rule                        |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Header found but library not linked                  | Link failure                    | Model link dependencies            |
| Compile succeeds with wrong library version          | Runtime/ABI failure             | Check version compatibility        |
| Feature macro assumed instead of tested              | Build breaks on other platforms | Use build-system feature detection |
| `#ifdef` spread across codebase                      | Hard to test variants           | Centralize configuration           |
| Using C23 feature without standard flag/tool support | Compile failure                 | Set and document standard mode     |

### Documentation Tools — comments, headers, and generated API docs

ISO C has comments, but no standard docstring or documentation generator. Documentation is produced by convention and tools such as Doxygen, Sphinx+Breathe, gtk-doc, or project-specific systems.

| Documentation task     | Common C practice                |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Public API contract    | Comments in public header        |
| Ownership/lifetime     | Explicit prose near declaration  |
| Error behavior         | Return convention documented     |
| Thread-safety          | Function/type-level note         |
| Versioning/deprecation | Macro attributes or doc comments |
| Internal invariant     | Comments near struct/helper      |
| Generated docs         | Doxygen-style comments           |

Example public API comment:

```c id="p6_api_doc"
/*
 * Reads the entire file at path.
 *
 * On success:
 * - returns 0
 * - stores an allocated buffer in *out_data
 * - stores the byte length in *out_len
 * - caller must free(*out_data)
 *
 * On failure:
 * - returns -1
 * - stores NULL in *out_data
 * - stores 0 in *out_len
 */
int read_file_alloc(const char *path,
                    unsigned char **out_data,
                    size_t *out_len);
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                | Better rule                             |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Header docs omit ownership                       | Memory bugs                   | Document allocation/free responsibility |
| Docs describe implementation instead of contract | Clients depend on internals   | Document observable behavior            |
| No failure-state documentation                   | Caller misuse                 | Define outputs on failure               |
| Doxygen comments treated as enforcement          | Tools do not check semantics  | Pair with tests/static analysis         |
| Internal invariants undocumented                 | Maintainers break assumptions | Document near representation            |

### Core Ecosystem Library Areas — practical map beyond ISO C

Many serious tasks require non-ISO libraries. The correct choice depends on portability, security, licensing, ABI stability, performance, and project constraints.

| Task                       | Common C ecosystem options                         | Decision rule                           | Caveat                          |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Compression                | zlib, zstd, liblzma                                | Choose format/performance/support       | ABI and versioning              |
| TLS/crypto                 | OpenSSL, mbedTLS, wolfSSL, libsodium               | Prefer mature audited libraries         | API misuse risk                 |
| HTTP/network               | libcurl, libevent, libuv                           | Use established library                 | Event model and dependency cost |
| Regex                      | POSIX regex, PCRE2                                 | Choose syntax/performance/Unicode needs | Not ISO C                       |
| JSON                       | cJSON, jansson, yyjson                             | Choose ownership/performance model      | Parser security                 |
| XML                        | libxml2                                            | Mature XML support                      | Complexity                      |
| Database                   | SQLite, libpq, MySQL client libraries              | Use official/mature client              | Linking and threading modes     |
| CLI parsing                | getopt, argp, third-party                          | Platform needs                          | POSIX vs cross-platform         |
| Testing                    | Unity, CMocka, Check, Criterion                    | Project style/toolchain                 | Dependency overhead             |
| Logging                    | zlog, syslog on POSIX, custom                      | Deployment environment                  | Thread-safety                   |
| Cross-platform portability | APR, GLib, libuv, SDL-like layers depending domain | Avoid writing every adapter             | Dependency weight               |
| Embedded support           | vendor HALs, CMSIS, RTOS APIs                      | Target-specific                         | Not hosted ISO C                |

Library evaluation table:

| Criterion         | Question                                |
| ----------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Standard layer    | ISO C, POSIX, platform, or third-party? |
| License           | Compatible with project?                |
| ABI stability     | Can binaries rely on it?                |
| Security history  | Maintained and patched?                 |
| Ownership model   | Clear create/destroy rules?             |
| Thread model      | Thread-safe? Requires init?             |
| Error model       | Return codes, errno, error objects?     |
| Allocation policy | Custom allocator support?               |
| Build integration | CMake/pkg-config/system packages?       |
| Portability       | Target compilers/platforms supported?   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters                      | Better rule                                            |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Choosing tiny unmaintained parser for hostile input | Security risk                       | Prefer maintained libraries for complex formats        |
| Ignoring ownership conventions of library           | Leaks or invalid frees              | Read lifecycle docs                                    |
| Mixing library versions                             | ABI/runtime failures                | Match headers and linked binary                        |
| Assuming thread safety                              | Data races                          | Check library threading contract                       |
| Wrapping library poorly                             | Leaks dependency details everywhere | Create narrow adapter if portability/evolution matters |

### Task × Library Reference — practical lookup table

| Task                 | ISO C tool                            | POSIX/platform/ecosystem alternative | Canonical use       | Caveat                      |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Allocate memory      | `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `free` | custom allocator, arena              | Dynamic storage     | Manual lifecycle            |
| Copy bytes           | `memcpy`, `memmove`                   | optimized/platform routines          | Buffers and arrays  | Overlap/object validity     |
| Work with strings    | `strlen`, `strcmp`, `snprintf`        | safer project string library         | C strings           | Null termination            |
| Parse integers       | `strtol`, `strtoul`                   | custom parser                        | CLI/config          | Check `end`, range, `errno` |
| Print formatted text | `printf`, `fprintf`, `snprintf`       | logging library                      | Diagnostics/output  | Format mismatch             |
| Read file stream     | `fopen`, `fread`, `fgets`             | POSIX `open/read`, platform APIs     | Portable stream I/O | Buffering/error checks      |
| Directory traversal  | none                                  | POSIX `opendir/readdir`, platform    | Filesystem tools    | Non-ISO                     |
| Regex                | none                                  | POSIX regex, PCRE2                   | Pattern matching    | Library choice              |
| JSON/XML             | none                                  | cJSON/jansson/libxml2/etc.           | Data interchange    | Parser security             |
| Networking           | none                                  | sockets, libcurl, libuv              | Network I/O         | Non-ISO                     |
| Threads              | C threads where supported             | pthreads, Windows threads            | Concurrency         | API portability             |
| Atomics              | `<stdatomic.h>`                       | compiler/platform atomics            | Shared scalars      | Memory order                |
| Testing              | none                                  | Unity/CMocka/custom                  | Unit tests          | No standard framework       |
| Fuzzing              | none                                  | libFuzzer/AFL++                      | Parser hardening    | Toolchain                   |
| Debugging            | none                                  | gdb/lldb/sanitizers                  | Defect diagnosis    | Build flags                 |
| Build dependency     | none                                  | CMake/Meson/pkg-config               | Project integration | Ecosystem fragmentation     |

### Standard Library Pitfall Index — high-frequency mistakes

| Pitfall                                 | Library area             | Why it matters              | Review cue                        |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| `malloc(sizeof p)` when object intended | Allocation               | Under-allocation            | Should it be `sizeof *p`?         |
| Direct `p = realloc(p, n)`              | Allocation               | Leak on failure             | Is there a temporary?             |
| `memcpy` with overlap                   | Memory                   | Undefined behavior          | Could ranges overlap?             |
| `memcmp` for struct equality            | Memory/layout            | Padding bytes               | Are fields compared semantically? |
| `strlen` on untrusted buffer            | Strings                  | OOB read                    | Is terminator guaranteed?         |
| `strncpy` assumed safe                  | Strings                  | May not terminate           | Is termination guaranteed?        |
| `%d` for `size_t`                       | Formatted I/O            | Variadic mismatch           | Should be `%zu`                   |
| Untrusted format string                 | Formatted I/O            | Format-string vulnerability | Is format literal fixed?          |
| `%s` without width in `scanf`           | Input                    | Buffer overflow             | Is width specified?               |
| `atoi`                                  | Parsing                  | No error reporting          | Use `strtol`                      |
| `while (!feof(fp))`                     | File I/O                 | Wrong EOF logic             | Is loop driven by read result?    |
| Ignored `fclose` on output              | File I/O                 | Lost write error            | Is close checked?                 |
| Passing negative `char` to `ctype`      | Character classification | Undefined behavior          | Cast to `unsigned char`           |
| `assert` for input validation           | Diagnostics              | Disabled in release         | Runtime check needed              |
| `longjmp` across resources              | Nonlocal control         | Cleanup skipped             | Are resources owned across jump?  |
| `volatile` for threads                  | Concurrency              | Not synchronization         | Use atomics/locks                 |
| Treating POSIX as ISO C                 | Portability              | Nonportable API             | Is layer labeled?                 |

### Part 6 Cost Model — standard-library and ecosystem costs

| Operation or pattern     | Usual cost                    | Hidden cost                                 | How to detect it                  | When it matters              | When not to optimize prematurely |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| `malloc/free` per object | Heap operation                | Fragmentation, locks, failure paths         | Heap profiler, allocation tracing | Hot paths, embedded, servers | Startup/setup allocations        |
| `realloc` growth         | May copy entire allocation    | Invalidates old pointers                    | Profiling, code review            | Large dynamic buffers        | Small bounded buffers            |
| `strlen`                 | Linear scan                   | Repeated scans become quadratic             | Profiling                         | Large strings/loops          | Small strings                    |
| `strcat` chains          | Repeated scans                | Quadratic building                          | Profiling/review                  | Dynamic string construction  | Tiny fixed messages              |
| `memcpy` large buffers   | Linear memory bandwidth       | Cache effects                               | Profiler/perf counters            | Large data processing        | Small structs/buffers            |
| `printf` formatting      | Parsing format and conversion | Locale, locks, allocation in implementation | Profiling                         | Logging hot paths            | Error messages                   |
| `fread/fwrite`           | Buffered I/O                  | Syscalls, buffering, short I/O              | I/O profiling                     | Large files                  | Small config files               |
| `qsort`                  | Generic sort with callbacks   | Indirect comparator calls, no inlining      | Profiling                         | Large arrays                 | Small arrays                     |
| Regex library            | Pattern matching              | Compilation/captures/Unicode cost           | Benchmark                         | High-volume matching         | Rare config validation           |
| JSON/XML parser          | Allocation and validation     | DOM memory, error paths                     | Heap profiler                     | Large documents              | Small trusted config             |
| TLS/HTTP library         | Network/crypto overhead       | Handshake, cert validation, buffers         | Tracing/profiling                 | High-throughput services     | Simple CLI fetch                 |
| Atomics                  | CPU synchronization           | Cache-line contention                       | Perf/TSan/design review           | Shared hot counters          | Rare flag checks                 |
| Logging                  | Formatting and I/O            | Locks, disk latency                         | Profiling                         | Hot loops                    | Low-rate diagnostics             |
| Sanitizers               | Runtime instrumentation       | Slower execution, memory overhead           | Build config                      | Testing                      | Production unless intended       |

### What to Memorize vs What to Look Up — Part 6 artifacts

| Memorize                                                               | Reason                              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| ISO C library is small; many tasks are POSIX/platform/library tasks    | Prevents false portability claims   |
| `malloc` gives uninitialized storage; `calloc` zero-initializes        | Core allocation behavior            |
| Use temporary for `realloc`                                            | Prevents leaks                      |
| `memcpy` requires non-overlap; `memmove` permits overlap               | Core memory rule                    |
| C strings require null termination                                     | Prevents unbounded reads            |
| Use `%zu` for `size_t`                                                 | High-frequency variadic correctness |
| Use `<inttypes.h>` macros for fixed-width integer formatting           | Portability                         |
| Use `strtol` family instead of `atoi`                                  | Parse error handling                |
| Drive file loops by read result, not `feof`                            | Correct stream I/O                  |
| Cast `char` to `unsigned char` before `ctype` functions                | Avoids UB                           |
| `assert` may disappear under `NDEBUG`                                  | Not input validation                |
| `volatile` is not thread synchronization                               | Concurrency correctness             |
| Networking, subprocess control, directories, regex, JSON are not ISO C | Library/platform decision           |

| Look up when needed                                 | Reason                             |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Exact C23 library changes and compiler support      | Toolchains vary                    |
| `printf` format for less common types               | Variadic correctness               |
| `strtod`/floating error behavior                    | Locale and range details           |
| `fseek`/large-file portability                      | Platform-specific                  |
| C threads support on target                         | Implementation variability         |
| POSIX API contracts                                 | Not ISO C                          |
| Third-party library ownership/error/threading model | Library-specific                   |
| Secure temporary-file APIs                          | Platform-specific                  |
| Build/link flags for dependencies                   | Toolchain-specific                 |
| Unicode/text handling libraries                     | C standard library is insufficient |

### Part 6 Closing Mental Model — the standard library is a low-level toolkit, not a safety framework

The C standard library provides essential primitives: allocation, byte operations, strings, streams, formatting, parsing, math, time, diagnostics, atomics, and a few generic algorithms. It does not provide a modern batteries-included application framework. It does not automatically track lengths, ownership, encodings, paths, resources, errors, threads, or dependencies.

A professional C library-use decision should answer:

| Question                                                              | Why it matters                     |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Is this ISO C, POSIX, compiler-specific, OS-specific, or third-party? | Determines portability             |
| Does this API require explicit size or capacity?                      | Prevents buffer errors             |
| What is the ownership result?                                         | Prevents leaks and invalid frees   |
| How is failure reported?                                              | Prevents ignored errors            |
| Is the input trusted and terminated/bounded?                          | Prevents out-of-bounds reads       |
| Are format strings and variadic arguments matched?                    | Prevents undefined behavior        |
| Does this function use global state such as locale or `errno`?        | Affects reentrancy and diagnostics |
| Can cleanup fail?                                                     | Important for I/O                  |
| Does the chosen library have a clear ABI and update policy?           | Production maintenance             |
| Does the task exceed ISO C’s library scope?                           | Requires ecosystem/platform design |

The practical rule is: **use the ISO C library as a precise primitive layer, not as a complete safety layer**. For complex tasks such as networking, serialization, cryptography, path handling, regular expressions, testing, and dependency management, choose libraries and tools deliberately, isolate their contracts, and label the portability layer clearly.
## PART 7 — Semantics, Runtime, Memory, Concurrency, and Implementation Model

### Scope of Part 7 — from source text to executable behavior

Part 7 explains how C programs get their meaning and how that meaning is implemented by compilers, linkers, runtimes, operating systems, ABIs, and hardware. Earlier parts focused on source syntax, data modeling, abstraction, boundaries, and library use. This part explains why those mechanisms behave the way they do. 

C must be understood through several distinct layers:

| Layer                   | What it defines                                     | Example                                             | Why it matters                                             |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| ISO C abstract machine  | Language-level meaning of valid C programs          | object lifetime, expressions, UB, sequence rules    | Determines whether C source has defined behavior           |
| Implementation          | Compiler and standard-library choices               | `GCC`, `Clang`, libc behavior                       | Determines implementation-defined behavior and extensions  |
| Translation environment | Preprocessor, compiler, assembler, linker           | object files, archives, shared libraries            | Determines program construction                            |
| ABI                     | Binary calling/layout contract                      | argument registers, struct passing, stack alignment | Determines library and FFI compatibility                   |
| Operating system        | Process, files, virtual memory, threads, signals    | POSIX, Windows, embedded RTOS                       | Determines external resource behavior                      |
| Hardware                | Instruction set, memory hierarchy, cache, alignment | x86-64, ARM, RISC-V                                 | Determines performance and some implementation constraints |
| Tooling                 | Warnings, sanitizers, debugger, profiler            | ASan, UBSan, `gdb`, `perf`                          | Helps detect violations, but does not define C semantics   |

The most important rule is: **C is not defined by the behavior of one compiled executable on one machine**. A C program’s source-level validity comes from the C standard and the implementation’s documented choices. Observed behavior is evidence, not a specification.

### Syntax vs Semantics — what source form does and does not guarantee

C syntax says what forms are grammatically valid. Semantics says what those forms mean. A syntactically valid C program can still have undefined behavior, unspecified behavior, implementation-defined behavior, locale-dependent behavior, or platform-specific behavior.

| Category                        | Meaning                                                                | Example kind                                                  | Programmer obligation       |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Defined behavior                | Standard specifies the behavior                                        | `1 + 2` for `int` without overflow                            | Normal reasoning applies    |
| Undefined behavior              | Standard imposes no requirements                                       | out-of-bounds access, signed overflow                         | Must avoid                  |
| Unspecified behavior            | Standard allows multiple possibilities without requiring documentation | order of evaluation of function arguments                     | Do not depend on one choice |
| Implementation-defined behavior | Implementation must document its choice                                | signed integer representation historically, `char` signedness | Check target implementation |
| Locale-dependent behavior       | Depends on current locale                                              | character classification, formatting                          | Define locale policy        |
| Extension behavior              | Compiler/library provides extra feature                                | `__attribute__`, statement expressions                        | Guard and document          |
| POSIX/platform behavior         | Outside ISO C                                                          | `open`, `pthread_create`, `mmap`                              | Label platform dependency   |
| ABI behavior                    | Binary convention                                                      | struct return convention                                      | Check target ABI            |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “If a program compiles and prints the expected result, the program is valid C.”                                                    |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | The same code breaks under optimization, another compiler, or another CPU.                                                         |
| Correct semantic explanation    | The program may have depended on undefined, unspecified, implementation-defined, or platform-specific behavior.                    |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Validate source-level assumptions against the C standard, compiler documentation, ABI, and platform API contracts.                 |
| Boundary where the rule changes | In deliberately platform-specific code, relying on documented implementation or ABI behavior is acceptable if isolated and tested. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters                                         | Better rule                        |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- |
| Treating compiler output as the language definition | Optimization may exploit invalid assumptions           | Reason from source semantics first |
| Assuming all nonportable behavior is UB             | Some behavior is implementation-defined or unspecified | Classify the behavior accurately   |
| Assuming all surprising behavior is compiler bug    | Invalid C often licenses surprising optimization       | Check UB and aliasing first        |
| Treating POSIX as ISO C                             | Portability confusion                                  | Label standard layer               |
| Depending on evaluation order not guaranteed by C   | Program may vary across compilers                      | Sequence side effects explicitly   |

### The C Abstract Machine — observable behavior, optimization, and the as-if rule

The C standard defines an **abstract machine**. A conforming implementation may transform the program in any way that preserves the observable behavior of a valid program. This is commonly called the **as-if rule**: the implementation can do anything “as if” the specified observable behavior is unchanged.

Observable behavior includes, broadly, volatile accesses and interactions with files at program termination, among other standard-defined effects. It does not mean every intermediate variable assignment or memory layout intuition must appear literally in generated machine code.

| Concept                   | Meaning                                                      | Practical consequence                                         |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Abstract machine          | Standard-defined model of program execution                  | C source is not direct hardware script                        |
| As-if rule                | Compiler may transform code if observable behavior preserved | Dead stores, inlining, constant folding, loop transformations |
| Observable behavior       | Effects the standard treats as externally visible            | Not every source step is preserved                            |
| Valid program assumption  | Optimizer may assume UB does not occur                       | Invalid source can produce surprising executable behavior     |
| Volatile access           | Special observable access category                           | Useful for MMIO/signal-like cases, not thread synchronization |
| Sequence and side effects | Rules for when modifications become visible                  | Complex expressions can be dangerous                          |

Example:

```c id="p7_as_if"
int f(int x)
{
    int y = x * 2;
    return y + 1;
}
```

The compiler does not need to create a real variable `y`. It may compute the result directly, inline the function, or fold calls with constant arguments.

Optimization becomes dangerous to intuition when UB is present:

```c id="p7_ub_opt"
int greater_after_increment(int x)
{
    return x + 1 > x;
}
```

For signed `int`, if `x == INT_MAX`, `x + 1` overflows, which is undefined behavior. The compiler may optimize this function as if it always returns true for all valid executions, because valid executions cannot include signed overflow.

Interdisciplinary Lens: compiler theory
What it clarifies: optimizers reason over the language’s valid-program semantics, not over every possible machine execution of invalid source.
Language feature involved: signed overflow, bounds checks, aliasing, dead-code elimination.
Practical consequence: UB can remove the assumptions that debugging by observation relies on.
Limit of the lens: compiler theory explains legal transformations, but exact emitted code remains implementation-, target-, and flag-dependent.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                  | Why it matters                                        | Better rule                                                               |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Expecting every source variable to exist in machine code | Optimizer may remove or transform it                  | Use debug builds for debugging source state                               |
| Thinking UB means “will crash here”                      | Compiler may optimize around impossible invalid cases | Treat UB as no semantics                                                  |
| Assuming `volatile` fixes all optimization surprises     | `volatile` has narrow semantics                       | Use the right synchronization/resource mechanism                          |
| Reading generated assembly as proof of portable behavior | Assembly is target/compiler-specific                  | Use assembly for performance/ABI inspection, not standard semantics proof |

### Translation Phases — preprocessing, compilation, assembly, linking

C execution begins long before runtime. Source passes through translation phases, usually implemented as preprocessing, compilation, assembly, and linking.

| Stage         | Input                       | Output                               | Main concerns                                |
| ------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| Preprocessing | `.c` plus headers           | Preprocessed translation unit        | `#include`, macros, conditional compilation  |
| Compilation   | Preprocessed C              | Assembly or intermediate/object code | Type checking, optimization, code generation |
| Assembly      | Assembly                    | Object file                          | Machine instructions, relocations            |
| Linking       | Object files/libraries      | Executable/shared library            | Symbol resolution, relocation, library order |
| Loading       | Executable/shared libraries | Running process                      | Dynamic linking, initialization, OS loader   |
| Execution     | Process image               | Program behavior                     | Runtime state, resources, I/O                |

The source file is not the compilation unit after preprocessing. The translation unit includes text from headers and macro expansions.

Example diagnostic workflow:

```bash id="p7_preprocess_command"
cc -E main.c -o main.i
cc -S main.c -o main.s
cc -c main.c -o main.o
cc main.o util.o -o app
```

These commands are toolchain conventions, not ISO C syntax, but they reflect the professional compilation model.

| Problem                             | Likely stage                                                      |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Macro expands unexpectedly          | Preprocessing                                                     |
| Type mismatch                       | Compilation                                                       |
| Undefined reference                 | Linking                                                           |
| Duplicate symbol                    | Linking                                                           |
| Missing shared library at execution | Loading/runtime                                                   |
| ABI mismatch                        | Linking or runtime                                                |
| Segmentation fault                  | Runtime, often caused by source UB/resource bug                   |
| Different behavior under `-O2`      | Optimization interacting with UB, unspecified behavior, or timing |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                          | Why it matters                                             | Better rule                                  |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Debugging macro-heavy code without seeing expansion              | Actual compiled tokens differ from source appearance       | Inspect preprocessed output                  |
| Assuming include order is irrelevant                             | Macros/declarations depend on inclusion                    | Make headers self-contained                  |
| Treating linker errors as syntax errors                          | They involve definitions and symbols                       | Check object files and libraries             |
| Compiling files separately with inconsistent macros              | Different translation units may see different APIs/layouts | Centralize config headers                    |
| Mixing headers from one library version with binary from another | ABI mismatch                                               | Keep headers and linked library synchronized |

### Translation Units and Separate Compilation — declarations, definitions, and consistency

Each translation unit is compiled separately. The compiler usually does not see the whole program at once unless link-time optimization is used. Headers exist to keep declarations consistent across translation units.

| C mechanism                    | Runtime/toolchain effect                            | Professional consequence                              |
| ------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Function declaration in header | Compiler type-checks calls in each translation unit | Include the same header in caller and definition file |
| Function definition in `.c`    | Emits code/symbol                                   | Exactly one external definition                       |
| `static` helper                | Internal linkage                                    | Private to translation unit                           |
| `extern` object declaration    | Refers to external object                           | One definition elsewhere                              |
| Macro config                   | Affects source before compilation                   | Must be consistent where layout/API depends on it     |
| Inline helper in header        | Each translation unit may get definition            | Linkage rules matter                                  |
| LTO                            | Compiler may optimize across units                  | Still must obey source semantics                      |

Correct pattern:

```c id="p7_header_decl"
/* mathutil.h */
#ifndef MATHUTIL_H
#define MATHUTIL_H

int add_checked(int a, int b, int *out);

#endif
```

```c id="p7_source_def"
/* mathutil.c */
#include "mathutil.h"

int add_checked(int a, int b, int *out)
{
    /* implementation */
    return 0;
}
```

Including the public header in the defining `.c` file ensures the compiler checks that the definition matches the declaration.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “If two `.c` files declare the same function manually, the linker will ensure the signatures match.”          |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Caller compiled with one declaration, callee defined with another, producing ABI-corrupt calls.               |
| Correct semantic explanation    | The linker resolves symbols, not full C type contracts in the source-language sense.                          |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Put shared declarations in a header and include it both where the function is called and where it is defined. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some toolchains/LTO/debug info can detect more mismatches, but portable C practice does not rely on that.     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                    | Why it matters                                      | Better rule                                                                            |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Manual duplicate declarations                              | Drift between caller and callee                     | Use one header                                                                         |
| Different macros change struct layout per translation unit | ABI/object interpretation mismatch                  | Centralize configuration                                                               |
| Private helper missing `static`                            | Symbol exported unintentionally                     | Mark internal functions `static`                                                       |
| Public inline rules misunderstood                          | Linkage errors or duplicated code                   | Use `static inline` for header helpers unless exact external-inline rules are intended |
| Assuming LTO fixes bad program organization                | LTO optimizes; it does not replace clean interfaces | Keep declarations consistent                                                           |

### ABI and Calling Conventions — where C meets binary reality

The **Application Binary Interface** determines how compiled code calls functions, passes arguments, returns values, aligns the stack, lays out certain data, names symbols, and links libraries. ISO C does not specify a platform ABI.
