| ABI concern                 | Example                                     | Why it matters             |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Calling convention          | Which registers/stack slots carry arguments | Function calls and FFI     |
| Return convention           | How structs or large values are returned    | ABI compatibility          |
| Stack alignment             | Required before calls                       | Crashes or performance     |
| Struct layout               | Field offsets, padding, alignment           | Binary interfaces          |
| Enum representation         | Size/sign choice in ABI                     | Public ABI risk            |
| Variadic calling convention | How extra args are passed                   | `printf`-style correctness |
| Symbol naming               | External symbol representation              | Linker/FFI                 |
| Shared library versioning   | Dynamic linking contract                    | Deployment                 |
| Object-file format          | ELF, Mach-O, PE/COFF                        | Toolchain-specific         |

Example: source-compatible but ABI-breaking change.

```c id="p7_abi_struct_v1"
struct options {
    int flags;
    int timeout_ms;
};
```

Changing to:

```c id="p7_abi_struct_v2"
struct options {
    int flags;
    long timeout_ms;
    int retries;
};
```

may compile for clients that rebuild, but existing binaries expecting the old layout can break. Public struct layout is therefore an ABI commitment.

Function signature ABI break:

```c id="p7_abi_func_v1"
int process(int flags);
```

Changing to:

```c id="p7_abi_func_v2"
int process(long flags);
```

can change calling convention details depending on platform. Even if source call sites look similar, binary compatibility is broken.

Interdisciplinary Lens: ABI analysis
What it clarifies: source-level declarations become binary contracts at library and FFI boundaries.
Language feature involved: function declarations, public structs, enums, variadic functions, shared libraries.
Practical consequence: public C APIs should prefer opaque handles, versioned structs, and stable function signatures.
Limit of the lens: ABI rules are platform-specific; there is no single universal “C ABI.”

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                           | Better rule                                    |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Passing public structs by value across ABI             | Calling/return convention can be fragile | Prefer pointers to documented structs          |
| Exposing enums in ABI without policy                   | Representation may vary                  | Use fixed-width integer fields where necessary |
| Changing public struct layout                          | Breaks binary clients                    | Use opaque structs or versioned size fields    |
| Calling variadic function with wrong type              | ABI reads wrong argument representation  | Match format/contracts exactly                 |
| Assuming C ABI is identical across compilers/platforms | It is not                                | Check target ABI documentation                 |

### Execution Model — program startup, `main`, static initialization, termination

In a hosted environment, a C program begins execution through implementation/runtime startup code that eventually calls `main`. Before `main`, static storage duration objects are initialized. After `main` returns or `exit` is called, termination handlers and library cleanup may run.

| Phase                 | What happens                            | Layer                     |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| Program loading       | OS loader maps executable/libraries     | OS/ABI                    |
| Runtime startup       | C runtime prepares environment          | Implementation            |
| Static initialization | Objects with static storage initialized | ISO C plus implementation |
| `main` called         | Program entry point                     | ISO C hosted              |
| Normal execution      | Program code runs                       | C/OS/library              |
| `exit` processing     | Registered handlers, stream flushing    | ISO C library             |
| Process termination   | OS reclaims process resources           | OS                        |

Static initialization:

```c id="p7_static_init"
static int counter;
static int limit = 100;
```

`counter` is zero-initialized; `limit` is initialized to `100`. This happens before program execution reaches `main`.

Automatic local initialization is different:

```c id="p7_auto_uninit"
void f(void)
{
    int x;      /* indeterminate */
    int y = 0;  /* initialized */
}
```

Termination:

```c id="p7_atexit"
#include <stdlib.h>

static void cleanup_global_state(void)
{
    /* cleanup */
}

int main(void)
{
    if (atexit(cleanup_global_state) != 0) {
        return EXIT_FAILURE;
    }

    return EXIT_SUCCESS;
}
```

`atexit` can register normal termination handlers. This is not a substitute for local resource management; it is mostly for process-level cleanup.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                                                  | Why it matters                              | Better rule                                   |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming all local variables start as zero                                               | Automatic locals do not                     | Initialize before use                         |
| Relying on global initialization order across translation units for complex dependencies | C has limited initialization model          | Avoid complex global initialization           |
| Treating `atexit` as general cleanup                                                     | Long-running resources need earlier release | Clean resources at ownership boundary         |
| Calling `exit` from library code                                                         | Prevents caller control                     | Return errors unless fatal policy is explicit |
| Assuming OS cleanup justifies leaks                                                      | Bad for libraries and long-running programs | Manage resources deliberately                 |

### Expression Evaluation and Sequencing — order, side effects, and undefined behavior

C evaluation order is precise in some places and unspecified in many others. Correct programs must not depend on unspecified operand or argument order.

| Construct        | Evaluation/sequence property                                                                        | Practical rule                                 |                                                    |                             |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| `&&`             | Left operand evaluated first; right only if needed                                                  | Safe short-circuit guard                       |                                                    |                             |
| `                |                                                                                                     | `                                              | Left operand evaluated first; right only if needed | Safe short-circuit fallback |
| Comma operator   | Left evaluated, then right                                                                          | Rare explicit sequencing                       |                                                    |                             |
| Conditional `?:` | Condition evaluated first; selected branch evaluated                                                | Useful for simple value selection              |                                                    |                             |
| Function call    | Function designator and arguments evaluated before call, but argument order not generally specified | Do not rely on argument order                  |                                                    |                             |
| Assignment       | Side effect timing governed by sequencing rules                                                     | Avoid multiple unsequenced access/modification |                                                    |                             |
| `++`/`--`        | Side effect plus value computation                                                                  | Avoid complex combinations                     |                                                    |                             |
| Full expression  | Side effects complete by end of full expression                                                     | Separate statements clarify                    |                                                    |                             |

Dangerous expression:

```c id="p7_eval_bad"
int i = 0;
a[i] = i++;
```

This kind of expression can involve unsequenced or unclear access/modification depending on exact form. Professional C avoids such constructs.

Safer:

```c id="p7_eval_good"
int i = 0;
a[i] = i;
i++;
```

Function argument order trap:

```c id="p7_arg_order_bad"
printf("%d %d\n", i++, i++);
```

The order in which the two `i++` arguments are evaluated is not specified. Avoid this.

Short-circuit guard:

```c id="p7_short_circuit"
if (p != NULL && p->len > 0) {
    use(p);
}
```

Here, `p != NULL` is evaluated first. If false, `p->len` is not evaluated.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                          |   |                                                                               |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | - | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “C evaluates expressions left to right.”                                             |   |                                                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Different compilers print different values for `printf("%d %d\n", i++, i++);`.       |   |                                                                               |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C guarantees sequencing in some operators but not general argument evaluation order. |   |                                                                               |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Put side effects in separate statements unless sequencing is explicitly guaranteed.  |   |                                                                               |
| Boundary where the rule changes | `&&`, `                                                                              |   | `, comma operator, and the first operand of `?:` provide specific sequencing. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                             | Why it matters                         | Better rule                                   |
| ----------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Multiple `i++` in one expression    | Unspecified or undefined behavior risk | Use separate statements                       |
| Relying on function argument order  | Not generally specified                | Compute arguments first                       |
| Macro argument side effects         | Macro may duplicate expression         | Avoid side-effect macro args                  |
| Hiding assignments in conditions    | Readability and bug risk               | Use explicit statements unless idiom is clear |
| Overusing clever pointer increments | Bounds/lifetime review harder          | Prefer clear loops                            |

### Call Strategy — pass by value, pointers, arrays, and return values

C uses **pass by value**. Every argument expression initializes a parameter object or parameter value according to the function type. Pointers are values too. Passing a pointer allows the callee to access the pointed-to object, but the pointer itself is still copied.

| Parameter form    | What is passed                           | Callee can mutate caller object? | Callee can replace caller variable? |
| ----------------- | ---------------------------------------- | -------------------------------: | ----------------------------------: |
| `int x`           | integer value                            |                               No |                                  No |
| `struct S s`      | struct value                             |                               No |                                  No |
| `int *p`          | pointer value                            |                    Yes, if valid |                                  No |
| `int **pp`        | pointer to caller’s pointer              |                              Yes |                                 Yes |
| `const T *p`      | pointer value with read-only access path |                  Not through `p` |                                  No |
| `T a[]` parameter | adjusted to `T *`                        |                    Yes, if valid |                    No length passed |
| `T (*a)[N]`       | pointer to array of `N`                  |       Yes, with fixed inner size |                                  No |

Example:

```c id="p7_pass_value"
void set_zero(int x)
{
    x = 0;
}

void set_pointed_zero(int *p)
{
    if (p != NULL) {
        *p = 0;
    }
}
```

Returning structs by value:

```c id="p7_return_struct"
struct point {
    int x;
    int y;
};

struct point point_make(int x, int y)
{
    struct point p = { .x = x, .y = y };
    return p;
}
```

Returning small structs by value is often fine. Returning large or resource-owning structs requires copy/ownership policy.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                       | Better rule                                         |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Saying C has pass-by-reference                    | Obscures pointer copy semantics      | Say “pass pointer by value”                         |
| Expecting parameter reassignment to affect caller | It affects only local parameter      | Use pointer-to-pointer or return new value          |
| Passing arrays expecting length preservation      | Array parameter adjusts to pointer   | Pass length explicitly                              |
| Returning pointer to local object                 | Lifetime ended                       | Return by value or allocate with ownership contract |
| Returning large structs blindly                   | ABI/copy cost and ownership concerns | Use output parameter or pointer when appropriate    |

### Storage Duration and Lifetime — automatic, static, allocated, and thread storage

C object lifetime depends on storage duration and allocation. Lifetime is separate from scope and pointer reachability.

| Storage duration | How created                                     | Lifetime                           | Example                  |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| Automatic        | Block-scope object without `static`, parameters | Entry to block/function until exit | `int x;` inside function |
| Static           | File-scope objects, block-scope `static`        | Entire program execution           | `static int count;`      |
| Thread           | `_Thread_local` objects                         | Lifetime of thread                 | `_Thread_local int e;`   |
| Allocated        | `malloc` family                                 | From allocation until `free`       | `p = malloc(n);`         |

Automatic lifetime example:

```c id="p7_auto_lifetime"
int *bad_pointer(void)
{
    int x = 42;
    return &x; /* invalid after return */
}
```

Allocated lifetime example:

```c id="p7_alloc_lifetime"
int *make_int(int value)
{
    int *p = malloc(sizeof *p);
    if (p != NULL) {
        *p = value;
    }
    return p;
}
```

The caller owns the returned allocation if the API says so.

Static local:

```c id="p7_static_local"
int next_id(void)
{
    static int id = 0;
    return ++id;
}
```

The object `id` persists across calls. This introduces hidden state and is not automatically thread-safe.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A pointer keeps an object alive.”                                                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A pointer to a local variable is used after the function returns.                                                |
| Correct semantic explanation    | C has no tracing lifetime system; pointer reachability does not extend automatic object lifetime.                |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Know the storage duration of every object whose address escapes.                                                 |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Dynamically allocated storage persists until `free`, but ownership and cleanup then become explicit obligations. |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters                                           | Better rule                             |
| --------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Returning address of local variable                 | Dangling pointer                                         | Return by value or allocate             |
| Storing pointer to stack object in callback context | Use-after-return                                         | Ensure context outlives callback        |
| Assuming static local is reentrant/thread-safe      | Shared state                                             | Use explicit context or synchronization |
| Freeing pointer then using it                       | Lifetime ended                                           | Do not access after `free`              |
| Confusing scope with lifetime                       | Object may outlive name or name may hide lifetime issues | Analyze separately                      |

### Stack, Heap, Static Storage, and Virtual Memory — implementation model vs ISO C model

C standards talk about storage duration, not “stack” and “heap” as formal universal requirements. In practice, implementations commonly use stack-like automatic storage, heap allocation, and static data segments, but these are implementation strategies.

| Informal region      | C concept                   | Typical implementation      | Caveat                                     |
| -------------------- | --------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Stack                | Automatic storage duration  | Call stack frames           | Not specified as “stack” by ISO C          |
| Heap                 | Allocated storage duration  | Allocator-managed memory    | Allocator behavior implementation-specific |
| Static data          | Static storage duration     | data/bss segments           | Initialization before `main`               |
| Thread-local storage | Thread storage duration     | TLS segment/runtime support | Implementation/platform-specific           |
| Memory-mapped region | Not ISO C allocation        | OS mapping                  | POSIX/platform                             |
| Registers            | Optimization/storage choice | CPU registers               | `register` keyword mostly obsolete         |

Architecture/OS model:

| Mechanism          | Practical effect                                                   |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Virtual memory     | Invalid accesses may fault, but C UB may also optimize away checks |
| Guard pages        | Stack overflow may fault if detected                               |
| Allocator metadata | Buffer overflows may corrupt heap state                            |
| Cache locality     | Contiguous arrays often outperform linked structures               |
| Alignment          | Misaligned access may be slow or invalid                           |
| Page size          | Affects `mmap`, allocation, I/O behavior                           |
| ASLR               | Addresses vary between runs                                        |

Interdisciplinary Lens: operating systems and architecture
What it clarifies: why automatic storage is fast but bounded, why heap allocation can fail, why cache locality matters, and why invalid memory access may crash.
Language feature involved: storage duration, arrays, allocation, pointers, alignment.
Practical consequence: use stack/heap/static storage deliberately; do not infer ISO C validity from whether hardware traps.
Limit of the lens: OS and hardware behavior explain common implementations, but ISO C semantics still define source validity.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                           | Why it matters                   | Better rule                                 |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Allocating huge arrays as automatic locals                        | Stack exhaustion                 | Use heap or caller-managed storage if large |
| Assuming heap allocation always succeeds                          | Allocation failure possible      | Check allocation results                    |
| Treating segmentation fault as guaranteed invalid-access behavior | UB need not crash                | Avoid UB, do not rely on crash              |
| Optimizing for cache without measuring                            | Premature complexity             | Profile                                     |
| Assuming all targets tolerate misalignment                        | Some fault or slow significantly | Respect alignment                           |

### Object Representation, Effective Type, and Aliasing — why bytes are not arbitrary objects

C permits inspection of object representation through character types, especially `unsigned char`, but it does not permit arbitrary access to storage through incompatible typed pointers. Effective type and aliasing rules allow compilers to optimize based on assumptions about which typed accesses can refer to the same object.

| Concept                     | Meaning                                                                            | Practical consequence                       |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Object representation       | Bytes that represent an object value                                               | Can inspect with `unsigned char`            |
| Effective type              | Type associated with an object for access rules                                    | Controls valid typed access                 |
| Strict aliasing             | Compiler may assume incompatible pointer types do not alias except permitted cases | Violations can miscompile                   |
| Character-type access       | Character types may inspect object representation                                  | Useful for serialization/debugging          |
| Alignment                   | Address must satisfy type requirement                                              | Pointer cast alone does not align           |
| Lifetime                    | Object must exist before typed access                                              | Raw storage is not automatically every type |
| Trap/invalid representation | Some bit patterns may not be valid values for some types                           | Reading can be invalid                      |

Safe byte inspection:

```c id="p7_byte_inspect"
void dump_object_bytes(const void *obj, size_t n)
{
    const unsigned char *p = obj;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        print_byte(p[i]);
    }
}
```

Risky reinterpretation:

```c id="p7_alias_bad"
float f = 1.0f;
int *ip = (int *)&f;
int bits = *ip; /* strict aliasing/alignment/effective-type problem */
```

Safer representation copy:

```c id="p7_memcpy_bits"
#include <string.h>
#include <stdint.h>

uint32_t float_bits(float f)
{
    uint32_t bits;
    memcpy(&bits, &f, sizeof bits);
    return bits;
}
```

This still assumes `sizeof bits == sizeof f`; robust code should check or use a static assertion where appropriate.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                              |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Casting a pointer changes the object’s type.”                                                                           |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Type-punning through an incompatible pointer works at `-O0` and fails at `-O2`.                                          |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A pointer cast changes the expression type, not necessarily the effective type or valid aliasing relation of the object. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use `memcpy` or documented compiler extensions for representation reinterpretation; avoid incompatible pointer aliasing. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some compilers support union punning or aliasing extensions, but those are implementation-specific and must be labeled.  |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                                | Better rule                             |
| ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Type-punning through incompatible pointer | UB/optimization issue                         | Use `memcpy` or sanctioned method       |
| Assuming `void *` erases effective type   | It erases static pointer type only            | Restore correct type before access      |
| Casting byte buffer to struct pointer     | Alignment/effective type/endian/padding risks | Parse or `memcpy` into proper object    |
| Ignoring padding bytes                    | Raw comparison/serialization bugs             | Compare/serialize fields                |
| Violating `restrict`                      | UB via broken no-alias promise                | Use only when aliasing contract is true |

### Alignment and Padding — layout constraints from the machine world

Alignment means that objects of a type may need addresses that are multiples of a particular value. Padding is unused storage inserted to satisfy alignment or layout requirements.

| Layout concept   | Example consequence                                                  |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Member alignment | Struct may contain padding between fields                            |
| Tail padding     | Struct size may exceed sum of fields                                 |
| Array alignment  | Each array element must be properly aligned                          |
| Over-alignment   | Special types may require stronger alignment                         |
| Packed layout    | Compiler extension may reduce padding but create misaligned accesses |
| ABI layout       | Public layout tied to platform ABI                                   |
| Serialization    | Raw struct bytes are not portable format                             |

Example:

```c id="p7_padding_example"
struct example {
    char c;
    int i;
};
```

This often has padding after `c`. Reordering fields can reduce size, but public layout and readability must be considered.

```c id="p7_layout_reorder"
struct better_layout {
    int i;
    char c;
};
```

This may be smaller, but field reordering changes ABI and source semantics if layout is public.

Alignment-sensitive allocation:

```c id="p7_align_alloc"
#include <stdlib.h>

/* aligned_alloc has size/alignment constraints; check target standard/library support. */
```

Do not overuse specialized alignment APIs unless there is a real need.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                            | Why it matters                                  | Better rule                     |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Assuming packed structs are safer for wire formats | Misalignment and nonportable extension behavior | Serialize explicitly            |
| Comparing structs with padding using `memcmp`      | Padding bytes may differ                        | Compare fields                  |
| Sending raw struct over network                    | Endian/padding/ABI issues                       | Define protocol layout          |
| Ignoring alignment after pointer cast              | Misaligned access may fault                     | Use `memcpy` or aligned storage |
| Reordering public struct fields for size           | ABI/source break                                | Treat public layout as contract |

### Arrays, Pointers, and Provenance — bounds, one-past, and valid arithmetic

Pointer arithmetic is defined relative to array objects. A pointer one past the last element may be formed for comparison/arithmetic, but not dereferenced.

| Pointer operation                           | Defined when                                        | Common failure                       |
| ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `&a[i]`                                     | `i` within array bounds                             | Out-of-bounds                        |
| `a + i`                                     | Points within array or one past                     | Going beyond one-past                |
| `p - q`                                     | Same array object or one-past relation              | Subtract unrelated pointers          |
| `p < q`                                     | Same array object                                   | Compare unrelated object addresses   |
| Dereference `*p`                            | Points to valid object/function of appropriate type | Null, dangling, one-past             |
| Convert array to pointer                    | Most expression contexts                            | Losing length                        |
| Pointer to object treated as length-1 array | Some arithmetic one-past allowed                    | Assuming adjacent objects form array |

Example:

```c id="p7_one_past"
int a[3] = {1, 2, 3};

int *begin = &a[0];
int *end = &a[3]; /* one past */

for (int *p = begin; p != end; p++) {
    use(*p);
}
```

This is valid. But `*end` is invalid.

Unrelated pointer comparison:

```c id="p7_unrelated_ptr_compare"
int x;
int y;

/* Relational comparison such as &x < &y is not generally meaningful in ISO C. */
```

Pointer provenance is a complex area in modern C semantics discussions. For professional practice, the safe rule is: **derive pointers from the object or array they access; do not manufacture object access by integer arithmetic unless the implementation and context explicitly support it**.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                                  | Why it matters                          | Better rule                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Dereferencing one-past pointer                                           | Undefined behavior                      | Use one-past only as sentinel                 |
| Pointer arithmetic across separate objects                               | Undefined behavior                      | Keep arithmetic within arrays                 |
| Assuming pointer integer round-trip preserves all access rights portably | Subtle implementation/provenance issues | Avoid except narrow documented low-level uses |
| Treating adjacent struct fields as array elements                        | Not an array                            | Access by member names                        |
| Losing array length at function boundary                                 | Bounds lost                             | Pass length                                   |

### Undefined Behavior — why it exists and why it is not a runtime error

Undefined behavior is one of C’s defining semantic features. It exists partly to support efficient implementation across diverse hardware, avoid runtime checks, and let compilers optimize under assumptions that valid programs do not perform invalid operations.

| UB example                                                      | Why it matters                                |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Out-of-bounds access                                            | Compiler may assume indexes are valid         |
| Use-after-free                                                  | Object lifetime ended                         |
| Null pointer dereference                                        | No valid object                               |
| Signed integer overflow                                         | Optimizer may assume it never happens         |
| Uninitialized read                                              | Value may be indeterminate                    |
| Strict aliasing violation                                       | Optimizer may assume non-alias                |
| Misaligned access                                               | Target may not support it                     |
| Data race                                                       | C memory model gives no defined behavior      |
| Modifying string literal                                        | Literal storage not modifiable                |
| Invalid `printf` argument type                                  | Variadic call interprets wrong representation |
| Falling off non-`void` function without return where value used | Missing return value                          |
| Violating `restrict` contract                                   | Optimizer assumes no alias                    |

UB is not the same as an exception, trap, signal, or diagnostic. A program with UB may appear to work, crash, corrupt data, leak secrets, or be optimized into unexpected behavior.

Example: signed overflow.

```c id="p7_signed_overflow"
#include <limits.h>

int f(int x)
{
    return x + 1 > x;
}
```

For all defined executions, this may be optimized as true. `x == INT_MAX` would overflow, and that execution is not defined.

Example: bounds.

```c id="p7_oob"
int a[4] = {0};
a[4] = 1; /* UB: one past is not an element */
```

Example: use-after-free.

```c id="p7_uaf"
int *p = malloc(sizeof *p);
free(p);
*p = 1; /* UB */
```

Interdisciplinary Lens: safety engineering and compiler theory
What it clarifies: UB is both a correctness problem and an optimization contract.
Language feature involved: memory access, integer arithmetic, aliasing, lifetime, data races.
Practical consequence: sanitizers and warnings help detect UB, but the core defense is writing code whose invariants exclude UB.
Limit of the lens: not every invalid-looking or surprising behavior is UB; distinguish unspecified and implementation-defined behavior.

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                                               |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Undefined behavior means the runtime will do some unpredictable but local thing.”                                                                        |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A bounds check disappears or code far away behaves differently under optimization.                                                                        |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Once UB is possible on a path, the standard imposes no semantic requirements for that execution, and optimizers may reason as if UB paths are impossible. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Treat UB as a source-level invalidity, not a runtime event to catch.                                                                                      |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some implementations define behavior for specific nonstandard operations as extensions, but that must be documented and isolated.                         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                               | Why it matters                  | Better rule                                      |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Testing only under `-O0`              | UB may appear harmless          | Test optimized builds with sanitizers            |
| Treating sanitizer-clean as proof     | Sanitizers cover executed paths | Combine tools with reasoning                     |
| Relying on wraparound signed overflow | UB                              | Use unsigned intentionally or checked arithmetic |
| Ignoring uninitialized reads          | Can be UB and security leak     | Initialize objects                               |
| Assuming use-after-free will crash    | It may corrupt silently         | Enforce ownership/lifetime                       |

### Implementation-Defined and Unspecified Behavior — less severe than UB, still important

Not all nonportable behavior is undefined. Correct classification matters.

| Behavior class         | Implementation obligation                               | Example                            | How to handle                          |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Undefined              | No requirements                                         | Out-of-bounds access               | Avoid entirely                         |
| Implementation-defined | Implementation documents choice                         | `char` signedness                  | Check documentation, constrain targets |
| Unspecified            | One of several possibilities; no documentation required | Function argument evaluation order | Do not depend on choice                |
| Locale-specific        | Depends on locale                                       | `isalpha`, formatting              | Define locale policy                   |
| Extension              | Implementation-defined extra feature                    | `__attribute__`                    | Guard and isolate                      |

Examples:

```c id="p7_impl_defined_char"
char c = (char)255;
/* Whether plain char is signed is implementation-defined. */
```

Unspecified argument order:

```c id="p7_unspecified_args"
g(f1(), f2()); /* order of f1 and f2 evaluation not generally specified */
```

Implementation-defined choices are not “bad” if the project explicitly targets that implementation. The problem is accidental dependence.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                   | Why it matters                    | Better rule                                       |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Calling implementation-defined behavior UB                | Imprecise reasoning               | Classify accurately                               |
| Depending on unspecified order                            | Nonportable behavior              | Sequence explicitly                               |
| Forgetting to document implementation assumptions         | Portability breaks later          | Record target data model and compiler assumptions |
| Assuming implementation-defined means arbitrary every run | Usually stable per implementation | Check docs                                        |
| Assuming extension behavior is portable                   | It is not ISO C                   | Guard with feature checks                         |

### Sanitizers and Warnings as Semantic Aids — powerful but incomplete

Tools do not define C semantics, but they help detect violations. Sanitizers instrument executions; warnings detect suspicious source patterns; static analyzers reason about paths.

| Tool category              | Helps detect                                            | Limitation                                    |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Compiler warnings          | Suspicious declarations, conversions, format mismatches | Not complete proof                            |
| AddressSanitizer           | OOB, use-after-free, double free                        | Runtime path coverage                         |
| UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer | Some UB classes                                         | Does not catch all UB                         |
| MemorySanitizer            | Uninitialized reads                                     | Requires compatible build/environment         |
| ThreadSanitizer            | Data races                                              | Runtime coverage; false positives/limitations |
| Static analyzers           | Leaks, null, path bugs                                  | Modeling limits                               |
| Fuzzers                    | Input-triggered failures                                | Harness and coverage dependent                |
| Debuggers                  | Runtime state                                           | Debugging optimized UB can mislead            |

Tool-oriented rule:

| Situation              | Useful tool                               |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Parser or file format  | Fuzzer + ASan/UBSan                       |
| Pointer-heavy code     | ASan + static analysis                    |
| Integer-heavy boundary | UBSan + explicit tests                    |
| Threaded code          | TSan + design review                      |
| Format-heavy code      | Compiler format warnings                  |
| Cleanup paths          | Static analyzer + failure injection tests |
| Performance suspicion  | Profiler, not intuition                   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                  | Why it matters                       | Better rule                         |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Running sanitizers only on debug happy-path tests        | Many bugs remain unexecuted          | Combine with fuzzing and edge tests |
| Treating warnings as style only                          | Warnings often indicate real defects | Use strict warning policy           |
| Disabling sanitizer reports without root cause           | Masks bugs                           | Suppress narrowly and document      |
| Expecting sanitizer to detect all strict-aliasing issues | Not complete                         | Avoid questionable aliasing         |
| Profiling sanitizer builds for performance               | Instrumentation distorts cost        | Profile release-like builds         |
### Numeric Semantics — integers, promotions, conversions, overflow, and shifts

C numeric behavior is not “ordinary mathematics.” Integer operations are governed by type rank, signedness, promotions, usual arithmetic conversions, representation rules, and overflow rules. These details matter because numeric mistakes often become memory-size mistakes, bounds-check bypasses, and undefined behavior.

| Topic                        | C semantic issue                                                       | Practical consequence                            |
| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Integer promotions           | Narrow integer types often promote to `int` or `unsigned int`          | Expression type may surprise                     |
| Usual arithmetic conversions | Mixed signed/unsigned operands convert before operation                | Negative values can become large unsigned values |
| Signed overflow              | Undefined behavior                                                     | Optimizer may assume it never happens            |
| Unsigned overflow            | Defined modulo arithmetic                                              | Often still a bug in size calculations           |
| Integer division             | Truncates toward zero for signed integers in modern C                  | Remainder/sign behavior must be understood       |
| Left shift                   | Can be UB for signed or invalid shift counts                           | Use unsigned for bit operations                  |
| Right shift                  | Signed negative right shift is implementation-defined in many contexts | Avoid relying on arithmetic shift portably       |
| Shift count                  | Negative or too-large shift count is UB                                | Validate count                                   |
| Narrowing conversion         | Value may change or be implementation-defined                          | Range-check before converting                    |
| Boolean context              | Zero false, nonzero true                                               | Not the same as semantic validity                |

Integer promotion example:

```c id="p7_int_promotion"
unsigned char a = 250;
unsigned char b = 10;

int sum = a + b; /* promoted before addition */
```

Signed/unsigned trap:

```c id="p7_signed_unsigned"
int i = -1;
size_t n = 10;

if (i < n) {
    /* May not behave as mathematical -1 < 10 because i converts to size_t. */
}
```

Better:

```c id="p7_signed_unsigned_safe"
if (i >= 0 && (size_t)i < n) {
    use_index((size_t)i);
}
```

Signed overflow trap:

```c id="p7_signed_overflow_2"
int add_one(int x)
{
    return x + 1; /* UB if x == INT_MAX */
}
```

Checked addition shape:

```c id="p7_checked_add"
#include <limits.h>

int int_add_checked(int a, int b, int *out)
{
    if (out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if ((b > 0 && a > INT_MAX - b) ||
        (b < 0 && a < INT_MIN - b)) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = a + b;
    return 0;
}
```

Unsigned arithmetic can be intentional for bit patterns:

```c id="p7_unsigned_flags"
unsigned flags = 0;
flags |= 1u << 3;
```

But unsigned arithmetic can hide bugs in size calculations:

```c id="p7_unsigned_underflow"
size_t remaining = len - used; /* wrong if used > len */
```

Better:

```c id="p7_unsigned_underflow_safe"
if (used > len) {
    return -1;
}

size_t remaining = len - used;
```

Shift safety:

```c id="p7_shift_safe"
int set_bit(unsigned *out, unsigned bit)
{
    if (out == NULL || bit >= sizeof *out * CHAR_BIT) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out |= 1u << bit;
    return 0;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters                     | Better rule                                         |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming signed overflow wraps           | It is UB                           | Check before operation or use unsigned deliberately |
| Using unsigned as general “safe integer” | Wraparound can hide invalid values | Use unsigned for sizes/bit patterns with checks     |
| Mixed signed/unsigned comparison         | Converts before comparison         | Check sign before casting                           |
| Shifting by width or more                | UB                                 | Validate shift count                                |
| Left-shifting signed values              | Can be UB or nonportable           | Use unsigned for bit operations                     |
| Narrowing from `size_t` to `int`         | Value may not fit                  | Range-check first                                   |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Unsigned arithmetic is safer because it cannot be negative.”                                                                 |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `len - used` becomes a huge value when `used > len`, passing a later capacity check.                                          |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Unsigned arithmetic is modulo arithmetic; it prevents negative representation, not invalid arithmetic.                        |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Check ordering before unsigned subtraction and check multiplication before allocation.                                        |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Cryptographic, hash, checksum, and bitset code may intentionally rely on unsigned wraparound and should document that intent. |

### Floating-Point Semantics — approximation, environment, NaN, infinities, and reproducibility

C floating-point behavior is influenced by the C standard, implementation choices, floating-point hardware, optimization flags, and sometimes the floating-point environment. Most modern hosted systems use IEEE-754-like behavior, but portable C should not assume every detail unless the implementation documents it.

| Topic                | Issue                                                                 | Practical consequence                |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Representation       | Binary floating approximates many decimal values                      | Decimal equality expectations fail   |
| Rounding             | Operations round to representable values                              | Accumulated error                    |
| NaN                  | Not-a-number values compare unequal, including to themselves          | Equality logic can break             |
| Infinity             | Overflow or division may produce infinities on IEEE-like systems      | Must classify results where relevant |
| Signed zero          | `+0.0` and `-0.0` may differ in some operations                       | Numerical edge cases                 |
| Floating environment | Exceptions/rounding modes may be observable if used                   | Optimizations may interact           |
| Excess precision     | Intermediate precision may vary historically/implementation-wise      | Reproducibility concerns             |
| Fast-math flags      | May violate strict IEEE assumptions                                   | Performance vs correctness tradeoff  |
| `errno`/math errors  | Math functions have specified error behavior but details require care | Check API contract                   |

Example:

```c id="p7_float_decimal"
double x = 0.1 + 0.2;

/* Do not assume x == 0.3 in general numerical code. */
```

NaN behavior:

```c id="p7_nan"
#include <math.h>

int is_self_equal(double x)
{
    return x == x; /* false if x is NaN */
}
```

Classification:

```c id="p7_float_classify"
#include <math.h>

int safe_use_double(double x)
{
    if (!isfinite(x)) {
        return -1;
    }

    use_finite_double(x);
    return 0;
}
```

Floating comparison must be domain-specific:

```c id="p7_float_tolerance"
#include <math.h>

int nearly_equal_abs(double a, double b, double tol)
{
    return fabs(a - b) <= tol;
}
```

This absolute-tolerance helper is not universally correct. For values with large magnitude, relative tolerance may be needed; for values near zero, absolute tolerance may be needed. Numerical analysis determines the correct rule.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                                       | Better rule                                               |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Expecting decimal exactness                            | Binary floating approximation                        | Use domain-aware tolerance or decimal library if required |
| Comparing NaN with `==`                                | NaN compares unequal to everything                   | Use `isnan`                                               |
| Ignoring infinities                                    | Arithmetic may produce nonfinite values              | Use `isfinite` where needed                               |
| Using fast-math flags casually                         | May change NaN/signed-zero/associativity assumptions | Use only with numerical policy                            |
| Assuming reproducible results across compilers/targets | Floating semantics and optimizations vary            | Define reproducibility requirements                       |
| Treating `DBL_EPSILON` as universal tolerance          | Scale-dependent                                      | Choose tolerance from problem domain                      |

### Variadic Functions — ABI-sensitive calls with weak type checking

C variadic functions, such as `printf`, accept a variable number of arguments. Their fixed parameters are type-checked normally, but the variadic part is interpreted according to a separate contract. This makes them ABI-sensitive and error-prone.

| Feature                     | Meaning                                            | Risk                                            |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Ellipsis                    | `int f(const char *fmt, ...);`                     | Extra args not checked like ordinary parameters |
| Default argument promotions | `float` becomes `double`; small integers promote   | Format/va_arg type must match promoted type     |
| `va_list`                   | Traversal state for variadic arguments             | Must use correct `va_start`, `va_arg`, `va_end` |
| Format string contract      | Specifies expected argument types                  | Mismatch can be UB                              |
| No runtime count            | Callee needs sentinel, format, or explicit count   | Reading too many args is UB                     |
| ABI dependence              | Calling convention handles variadic args specially | FFI and function pointer mismatch risk          |

Variadic wrapper example:

```c id="p7_variadic_wrapper"
#include <stdarg.h>
#include <stdio.h>

void log_message(const char *level, const char *fmt, ...)
{
    va_list ap;

    fprintf(stderr, "[%s] ", level);

    va_start(ap, fmt);
    vfprintf(stderr, fmt, ap);
    va_end(ap);

    fputc('\n', stderr);
}
```

`va_arg` type must match the actual promoted argument type:

```c id="p7_va_arg_shape"
int sum_ints(size_t count, ...)
{
    va_list ap;
    int total = 0;

    va_start(ap, count);

    for (size_t i = 0; i < count; i++) {
        total += va_arg(ap, int);
    }

    va_end(ap);
    return total;
}
```

Calling:

```c id="p7_va_arg_call"
int total = sum_ints(3, 10, 20, 30);
```

This works because the function expects `int` arguments. Passing a different type and reading it as `int` is invalid.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters                   | Better rule                                |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Wrong `printf` format                                  | Variadic mismatch                | Use exact specifiers and compiler warnings |
| Reading wrong type with `va_arg`                       | Undefined behavior               | Match promoted type                        |
| Forgetting `va_end`                                    | Violates variadic protocol       | Always pair with `va_start`                |
| No count/sentinel/format                               | Callee cannot know how many args | Provide explicit protocol                  |
| Passing variadic function through incompatible pointer | ABI/type mismatch                | Use exact declarations                     |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                          |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Variadic arguments are just a flexible array of values.”                                                                            |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | `printf("%d", (size_t)n)` prints garbage or corrupts argument reading.                                                               |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Variadic functions interpret raw call arguments according to the format/protocol and ABI; mismatched types are not safely converted. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Prefer fixed-parameter functions unless variadic behavior is truly justified; use compiler diagnostics for format APIs.              |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Carefully designed logging/format APIs may require variadic wrappers, but they should be narrow and tested.                          |

### Dynamic Allocation Semantics — storage, object lifetime, `realloc`, and allocator boundaries

Allocation functions provide storage. Correct use requires size calculation, initialization, ownership, and matching release.

| Function        | Semantic role                     | Important rule                           |
| --------------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `malloc`        | Allocate uninitialized storage    | Contents indeterminate                   |
| `calloc`        | Allocate zero-initialized storage | Still can fail                           |
| `realloc`       | Resize allocated storage          | On failure original remains valid        |
| `free`          | End allocated storage lifetime    | Pointer must be valid allocation or null |
| `aligned_alloc` | Allocate with specified alignment | Size/alignment constraints apply         |

`malloc` does not call constructors because C has no constructors. The program must initialize the object representation to a valid value before reading.

```c id="p7_malloc_init"
struct node *node = malloc(sizeof *node);
if (node == NULL) {
    return -1;
}

node->next = NULL;
node->value = 0;
```

`free` ends lifetime:

```c id="p7_free_lifetime"
free(node);
/* node still holds a pointer value, but dereferencing it is invalid. */
node = NULL;
```

Setting to `NULL` is useful for the local variable, but it does not update other aliases.

```c id="p7_free_alias"
int *p = malloc(sizeof *p);
int *q = p;

free(p);
p = NULL;

/* q is still a dangling pointer. */
```

`realloc` invalidation:

```c id="p7_realloc_invalidate"
unsigned char *old = buf->data;
unsigned char *tmp = realloc(buf->data, new_cap);

if (tmp == NULL) {
    return -1;
}

buf->data = tmp;
/* old and any interior pointers into old allocation must be considered invalid. */
```

Even if `realloc` returns the same address, code should treat old interior pointers as invalidated by policy unless carefully proven.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                   | Why it matters        | Better rule                         |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Reading uninitialized `malloc` storage                    | Indeterminate value   | Initialize before read              |
| Direct `realloc` assignment                               | Leak on failure       | Use temporary                       |
| Using pointer after `free`                                | Lifetime ended        | Do not access through aliases       |
| Assuming `free(p); p = NULL;` fixes all dangling pointers | Other aliases remain  | Manage ownership centrally          |
| Forgetting allocation-size overflow                       | Under-allocation      | Check before multiplication         |
| Mixing allocation APIs                                    | Heap corruption or UB | Free with matching allocator family |

### Dynamic Libraries, FFI, and Plugin Boundaries — C as binary interface

C is commonly used as a foreign-function interface because C ABIs are widely supported. But this is an ABI fact, not purely an ISO C fact. FFI requires precise agreement on calling convention, type layout, ownership, error handling, threading, and symbol visibility.

| Boundary issue     | Question                                                     |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Calling convention | Does caller call callee with same ABI?                       |
| Type layout        | Are struct sizes, alignment, and field offsets identical?    |
| Integer widths     | Are fixed-width types used where necessary?                  |
| String encoding    | Is text UTF-8, locale encoding, platform encoding, or bytes? |
| Ownership          | Which side allocates and frees? With which allocator?        |
| Error reporting    | Return code, error object, `errno`, callback?                |
| Threading          | Can calls occur concurrently?                                |
| Versioning         | How does caller detect ABI version?                          |
| Symbol visibility  | Which functions are exported?                                |
| Lifetime           | How long do returned pointers remain valid?                  |

FFI-safe API shape:

```c id="p7_ffi_api"
typedef struct image Image;

int image_load_file(const char *path, Image **out);
void image_destroy(Image *img);

int image_width(const Image *img, int *out_width);
int image_height(const Image *img, int *out_height);
```

This avoids passing structs by value and hides layout. It uses explicit destruction and status returns.

Risky FFI API shape:

```c id="p7_ffi_bad"
struct image {
    int width;
    int height;
    unsigned char *pixels;
};

struct image image_load_file(const char *path);
```

This exposes layout, ownership, allocation family, return convention, and pointer semantics.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                   | Better rule                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Passing ownership across runtimes without matching free | Heap mismatch                    | Provide library-owned destroy function   |
| Exposing public structs unnecessarily                   | ABI freeze                       | Use opaque handles                       |
| Using `long` in cross-platform ABI                      | Width differs across data models | Use fixed-width types where ABI needs it |
| Returning borrowed pointer without lifetime rule        | Use-after-free                   | Document validity duration               |
| Assuming `errno` crosses FFI cleanly                    | Runtime/library-specific         | Prefer explicit error return/object      |
| Calling callbacks after foreign object freed            | Lifetime bug                     | Registration/unregistration protocol     |

### Program Environment — command line, environment, locale, files, and host assumptions

Hosted C programs run in a host environment. The standard gives some access to command-line arguments, environment through `getenv`, files through streams, locale, and program termination. Many details are host-defined.

| Environment feature       | C/POSIX/platform status      | Caveat                                                |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `argc`, `argv`            | Hosted C entry convention    | Encoding and shell parsing platform-specific          |
| `getenv`                  | ISO C                        | Returned pointer is borrowed; environment is external |
| Current working directory | Not ISO C                    | POSIX/platform                                        |
| File paths                | `fopen` accepts path strings | Path syntax/encoding platform-specific                |
| Locale                    | ISO C global locale          | Affects classification/formatting/parsing             |
| Exit status               | `return` from `main`, `exit` | Host interprets status                                |
| Signals                   | ISO C minimal; POSIX richer  | Handler restrictions                                  |
| Threads                   | C11/POSIX/platform           | API and support vary                                  |
| Timezone                  | Host environment             | ISO C time functions are limited                      |

Command-line rule:

```c id="p7_argc_rule"
int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
    if (argc < 2) {
        return 2;
    }

    return run_with_path(argv[1]);
}
```

`argv[1]` is valid only if `argc >= 2`.

Environment variable:

```c id="p7_getenv_rule"
const char *home = getenv("HOME");

if (home != NULL) {
    /* Borrowed pointer; do not free or modify. */
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                     | Why it matters              | Better rule                                                   |
| ----------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Assuming `argv` encoding                                    | Platform-specific           | Treat as host-environment text                                |
| Modifying `getenv` result                                   | Not allowed by API contract | Treat as read-only borrowed data                              |
| Assuming paths are slash-separated                          | Platform-specific           | Use platform/path abstraction if portable                     |
| Library changes global locale                               | Process-wide side effect    | Let application control locale                                |
| Assuming exit status meanings beyond simple success/failure | Host-specific               | Use `EXIT_SUCCESS`, `EXIT_FAILURE`, or documented small codes |

### Volatile — what it is, what it is not

`volatile` is one of the most frequently misunderstood C qualifiers. It tells the implementation that accesses through a volatile-qualified lvalue have special observable behavior and should not be optimized away in the same way as ordinary accesses. It does not provide atomicity, mutual exclusion, or inter-thread happens-before relationships.

| Use case                                       | `volatile` appropriate?                 | Better mechanism if not                    |
| ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Memory-mapped I/O register                     | Often yes in embedded/platform code     | Also need hardware barriers/platform rules |
| Variable modified by signal handler            | Narrowly, `volatile sig_atomic_t`       | Keep handler simple                        |
| Inter-thread flag                              | No                                      | `_Atomic` or lock                          |
| Prevent optimizer from removing benchmark code | Usually no                              | Use proper benchmarking barriers/tooling   |
| Shared data structure                          | No                                      | Mutex/atomic protocol                      |
| Polling hardware state                         | Often platform-specific volatile access | Platform memory-order rules                |

Signal flag example:

```c id="p7_volatile_signal"
#include <signal.h>

static volatile sig_atomic_t interrupted = 0;

static void on_signal(int sig)
{
    (void)sig;
    interrupted = 1;
}
```

Thread flag should use atomics:

```c id="p7_atomic_flag"
#include <stdatomic.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

static atomic_bool stop_requested = false;
```

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “`volatile` means shared variable, so it is safe between threads.”                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Two threads communicate through volatile variables but still observe inconsistent data or data races.            |
| Correct semantic explanation    | `volatile` constrains certain optimizations around accesses; it does not establish synchronization or atomicity. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use `volatile` for special observable accesses, not thread synchronization.                                      |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Embedded MMIO often requires volatile, but also requires platform-specific ordering and barrier knowledge.       |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                              | Better rule                                   |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Volatile counter increment between threads    | Increment is not atomic                     | Use atomic fetch/add or lock                  |
| Volatile struct shared between threads        | Data race remains                           | Synchronize access                            |
| Assuming volatile orders nonvolatile accesses | Not a general memory barrier                | Use atomics/fences/platform barriers          |
| Overusing volatile                            | Disables useful optimization without safety | Use only for real volatile objects            |
| Ignoring hardware memory model for MMIO       | Volatile may be insufficient                | Use platform-specific barriers where required |

### Atomics and the C Memory Model — data races, atomic objects, and memory order

C11 introduced a memory model and atomic operations. The central rule is that data races on non-atomic objects produce undefined behavior. Atomics allow safe concurrent access to individual atomic objects, but correct concurrent data structures still require protocol design.

| Concept                | Meaning                                                                      | Practical consequence                   |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Data race              | Conflicting unsynchronized accesses to non-atomic object, at least one write | Undefined behavior                      |
| Atomic object          | Object accessed with atomic operations                                       | Avoids data race on that object         |
| Memory order           | Ordering constraints on atomic operations                                    | Controls visibility and reordering      |
| Sequential consistency | Strong default order                                                         | Easiest to reason about, may cost more  |
| Acquire/release        | Synchronization handoff pattern                                              | Requires careful pairing                |
| Relaxed                | Atomicity without ordering                                                   | Good for counters, not publication      |
| Fence                  | Ordering operation separate from access                                      | Specialized                             |
| Lock                   | Higher-level synchronization                                                 | Often preferred for compound invariants |

Atomic counter:

```c id="p7_atomic_counter_2"
#include <stdatomic.h>

static atomic_uint counter = 0;

unsigned next_id(void)
{
    return atomic_fetch_add(&counter, 1) + 1;
}
```

This is safe for generating unique counter values modulo overflow policy. It does not protect any other object.

Publication pattern concept:

```c id="p7_atomic_publication"
#include <stdatomic.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

static int data;
static atomic_bool ready = false;

void producer(void)
{
    data = 42;
    atomic_store_explicit(&ready, true, memory_order_release);
}

int consumer(int *out)
{
    if (atomic_load_explicit(&ready, memory_order_acquire)) {
        *out = data;
        return 0;
    }

    return -1;
}
```

This illustrates acquire/release at a high level. Real programs should prefer simpler synchronization unless memory-order expertise is present.

Data race example:

```c id="p7_data_race_bad"
static int shared;

void thread_a(void)
{
    shared = 1;
}

void thread_b(void)
{
    int x = shared;
}
```

If these run concurrently without synchronization, there is a data race, and behavior is undefined.

Compound invariant problem:

```c id="p7_compound_atomic_problem"
struct queue {
    atomic_size_t len;
    size_t cap;
    void **items;
};
```

Making `len` atomic does not make `cap` and `items` safe or make the queue invariant safe. Compound data structures usually need a mutex or carefully designed lock-free algorithm.

Memory-order decision table:

| Need                                           | Common choice                                                        |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Simple shared counter with no ordering meaning | Relaxed may be sufficient                                            |
| Publish data then signal ready                 | Release store + acquire load                                         |
| Simple correctness over performance            | Sequential consistency                                               |
| Protect multi-field invariant                  | Mutex/lock                                                           |
| Wait-free/lock-free data structure             | Expert atomic design                                                 |
| Communication with signal handler              | Specialized rules; often `sig_atomic_t` or atomics depending context |
| Hardware/device ordering                       | Platform-specific barriers, not just C atomics                       |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                      | Better rule                           |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Non-atomic shared variable between threads              | Data race UB                        | Use atomic or lock                    |
| Atomic flag with non-atomic data but no acquire/release | Visibility bug                      | Use synchronization pattern           |
| Relaxed order for publication                           | Data may not be visible as intended | Use release/acquire or lock           |
| Assuming atomics make data structure safe               | Invariants span fields              | Synchronize invariant, not just field |
| Writing lock-free code casually                         | Extremely high correctness burden   | Prefer locks unless justified         |

### Threads — ISO C threads, POSIX, platform APIs, and reentrancy

C’s threading story is layered. ISO C defines a threads API in C11, but availability varies. POSIX threads are common on Unix-like systems. Windows has its own threading APIs. Thread safety is therefore a property of both C code and the chosen platform/library.

| Topic                         | C/POSIX/platform issue                                          | Practical consequence             |
| ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Thread creation               | C threads, POSIX, Windows differ                                | Abstract if portable              |
| Mutex/condition variables     | API-specific                                                    | Correct lock protocol required    |
| Thread-local storage          | `_Thread_local`, platform TLS                                   | Lifetime/initialization concerns  |
