| Reentrancy                    | Function can be safely called again before prior call completes | Avoid hidden static mutable state |
| Thread safety                 | Safe concurrent use                                             | Must be documented per API        |
| Cancellation                  | POSIX-specific complexity                                       | Cleanup hazards                   |
| Library thread initialization | Some libraries require setup                                    | Read library docs                 |
| Callback threads              | Callback may run on arbitrary thread                            | Document and synchronize          |

Reentrancy problem:

```c id="p7_reentrancy_bad"
char *format_id(int id)
{
    static char buf[32];

    snprintf(buf, sizeof buf, "%d", id);
    return buf;
}
```

This function is not reentrant and not thread-safe. A later call overwrites the same buffer.

Better caller-buffer API:

```c id="p7_reentrancy_good"
int format_id(char *buf, size_t cap, int id)
{
    int n;

    if (buf == NULL || cap == 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    n = snprintf(buf, cap, "%d", id);
    return n >= 0 && (size_t)n < cap ? 0 : -1;
}
```

Thread-safety documentation table:

| API statement                       | Meaning                                                 |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Not thread-safe                     | Concurrent use without external synchronization invalid |
| Reentrant                           | No hidden shared mutable state for independent calls    |
| Thread-safe for distinct objects    | Different instances can be used concurrently            |
| Thread-safe for same object         | Internal synchronization or atomics protect object      |
| Caller must hold lock               | External synchronization required                       |
| Callback may occur on worker thread | Caller must synchronize callback state                  |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                        | Why it matters            | Better rule                                |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Static local buffer return                     | Not reentrant/thread-safe | Caller-provided buffer or allocated result |
| Hidden global state                            | Race/reentrancy issues    | Use explicit context objects               |
| Lock held during callback                      | Deadlock/reentrancy risk  | Avoid or document                          |
| Destroying object while another thread uses it | Use-after-free            | Define lifetime synchronization            |
| Assuming library is thread-safe                | Library-specific          | Check documentation                        |

### Signals, Async Contexts, and Nonlocal Control — restricted execution contexts

Signals and nonlocal jumps are specialized runtime mechanisms. They can break normal control-flow assumptions and cleanup paths.

| Mechanism                             | Risk                                                  | Professional rule                  |
| ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Signal handler                        | Can interrupt ordinary code; very few operations safe | Set simple flag; handle later      |
| `volatile sig_atomic_t`               | Narrow communication with signal handler              | Not general thread synchronization |
| `longjmp`                             | Skips stack frames and cleanup                        | Avoid across resource ownership    |
| Async cancellation                    | Platform-specific cleanup hazards                     | Avoid or contain                   |
| Callback from foreign/runtime context | Unknown locks/thread state                            | Define callback contract           |

Signal flag:

```c id="p7_signal_flag_2"
static volatile sig_atomic_t got_signal = 0;

static void handle_signal(int sig)
{
    (void)sig;
    got_signal = 1;
}
```

Normal loop:

```c id="p7_signal_loop"
while (!got_signal) {
    do_unit_of_work();
}
```

This is only a minimal pattern. Real signal behavior in POSIX programs has additional rules outside ISO C.

`longjmp` cleanup hazard:

```c id="p7_longjmp_leak"
void inner(void)
{
    char *p = malloc(100);
    if (p == NULL) {
        longjmp(env, 1);
    }

    longjmp(env, 1); /* p leaked */
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters              | Better rule                                     |
| --------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Calling `printf` in signal handler                  | Not generally safe          | Set flag                                        |
| `longjmp` across locked region                      | Deadlock                    | Do not jump across ownership of locks/resources |
| Using `volatile sig_atomic_t` for threads           | Wrong synchronization model | Use atomics/locks                               |
| Complex cleanup in async context                    | Reentrancy hazards          | Defer to normal flow                            |
| Assuming POSIX signal semantics in ISO C discussion | Different layer             | Label POSIX behavior                            |

### Freestanding vs Hosted C — environment assumptions

C distinguishes hosted and freestanding implementations. Hosted implementations provide full standard library support and call `main` normally. Freestanding implementations, common in kernels, embedded firmware, bootloaders, and some runtime code, have fewer required library facilities and different startup assumptions.

| Environment               | Characteristics                                    | Examples                                |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Hosted                    | Full standard library, `main`, OS-like environment | Desktop/server programs                 |
| Freestanding              | Minimal library requirements, custom entry/startup | Embedded firmware, kernels, bootloaders |
| Bare metal                | Direct hardware control                            | MCUs                                    |
| Kernel                    | No normal libc assumptions                         | OS kernels                              |
| Runtime/library internals | May run before full environment                    | libc, language runtimes                 |

Hosted assumption examples:

| Assumption         | Hosted?                   | Freestanding?              |
| ------------------ | ------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| `main` as entry    | Yes                       | Not necessarily            |
| `FILE *` I/O       | Yes in hosted library     | Usually absent or custom   |
| `malloc` available | Common but still may fail | May be absent/custom       |
| OS process cleanup | Host/OS                   | Not applicable             |
| `printf` output    | Usually                   | May require custom backend |
| Signals            | Hosted library feature    | Often absent/custom        |
| Threads            | Implementation/platform   | Often custom/absent        |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                   | Better rule                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Assuming libc in embedded code                          | May not exist or be partial      | Check environment             |
| Using heap before allocator initialized                 | Runtime failure                  | Define startup phases         |
| Using floating math in firmware without support         | Code size/runtime issues         | Check toolchain/runtime       |
| Assuming `printf` is cheap                              | Large code size and dependencies | Use minimal logging if needed |
| Treating freestanding code like hosted application code | Wrong environment model          | Document target environment   |

### Performance Semantics and Cost Model — what C exposes

C exposes performance-relevant details more directly than many languages. But source-level intuition is not enough; compilers and hardware transform execution.

| Cost area             | C-level source cue       | Implementation reality                           |
| --------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Function call         | `f(x)`                   | May inline or call                               |
| Pointer indirection   | `*p`                     | Cache miss or register access depending locality |
| Array traversal       | `a[i]` loop              | Often cache-friendly                             |
| Linked list traversal | `node = node->next`      | Pointer chasing, poor locality                   |
| Struct copy           | `s = t` or pass by value | May be registers, memory copy, ABI-dependent     |
| Heap allocation       | `malloc/free`            | Allocator overhead, locks, fragmentation         |
| Branch                | `if`, `switch`           | Branch prediction, jump tables                   |
| Function pointer      | `fp()`                   | Indirect call, harder to inline                  |
| Volatile access       | `volatile` object        | Optimization constrained                         |
| Atomic operation      | atomic API               | CPU ordering/cache cost                          |
| I/O call              | `fread`, `write`         | Buffering/syscall/device cost                    |
| `memcpy`              | raw copy                 | Often optimized heavily                          |
| `printf`              | formatting               | Expensive relative to simple computation         |

Cost decision table:

| Goal                    | First strategy                                                     |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Faster loops            | Use clear contiguous data; profile                                 |
| Fewer allocations       | Batch, reuse buffers, use arenas where justified                   |
| Better cache locality   | Prefer arrays/struct-of-arrays when measured                       |
| Less call overhead      | Let compiler inline; consider `static inline` for hot tiny helpers |
| Avoid generic overhead  | Use type-specific code in hot paths                                |
| Reduce I/O cost         | Buffer operations; avoid per-byte I/O                              |
| Avoid atomic contention | Reduce shared mutable state                                        |
| ABI-stable performance  | Avoid exposing layout too early; use opaque internals              |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters                         | Better rule                                   |
| --------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Micro-optimizing before profiling                   | Adds complexity without benefit        | Measure first                                 |
| Assuming C source maps literally to instructions    | Optimizer transforms code              | Inspect optimized assembly only when needed   |
| Using linked lists for speed by habit               | Cache locality often poor              | Prefer contiguous storage unless nodes needed |
| Overusing `volatile` to force performance behavior  | Hurts optimization without correctness | Use profiling and correct barriers            |
| Ignoring algorithmic complexity because C is “fast” | Bad algorithms stay bad                | Choose appropriate data structures            |

### Security Semantics — memory corruption, integer bugs, and trust boundaries

C’s lack of automatic memory safety makes security engineering inseparable from semantics. Many exploit classes correspond directly to semantic violations or unchecked boundary conditions.

| Vulnerability class               | C root cause                                  | Defense                                   |
| --------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Stack buffer overflow             | Out-of-bounds write to automatic object       | Bounds checks, safe API shape, sanitizers |
| Heap overflow                     | Allocation size miscalculation or bounds bug  | Checked arithmetic, length validation     |
| Use-after-free                    | Lifetime ended but pointer reused             | Ownership discipline, ASan                |
| Double free                       | Ownership confusion                           | Clear lifecycle, neutral states           |
| Integer overflow                  | Size/range computation invalid                | Checked arithmetic                        |
| Format-string bug                 | Untrusted format string                       | Fixed format strings                      |
| Uninitialized memory disclosure   | Padding/uninitialized read                    | Initialize, avoid raw struct exposure     |
| TOCTOU                            | OS state changes between check/use            | Platform-specific secure patterns         |
| Data race                         | Unsynchronized shared state                   | Locks/atomics                             |
| Invalid function pointer/callback | Type/lifetime mismatch                        | Exact signatures and registration policy  |
| Injection through shell/API       | Passing untrusted text to command interpreter | Avoid shell or escape with strong API     |

Security boundary pattern:

| Step                               | Required action                      |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Receive external data              | Treat as untrusted bytes/text        |
| Check length before access         | Avoid OOB                            |
| Check arithmetic before allocation | Avoid under-allocation               |
| Validate tags/enums                | Avoid invalid dispatch               |
| Convert to internal representation | Establish invariants                 |
| Operate on internal state          | No repeated raw parsing              |
| Serialize output explicitly        | Avoid leaking padding/internal state |
| Clean up on every path             | Avoid leaks and stale state          |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                             | Why it matters              | Better rule         |
| ----------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------- |
| Bounds check after pointer access   | Too late                    | Check before access |
| Allocate before max-size validation | Resource exhaustion         | Apply limits first  |
| Raw struct output                   | Padding disclosure          | Serialize fields    |
| Trusting null-terminated input      | OOB read                    | Use bounded input   |
| Logging untrusted string as format  | Format-string vulnerability | Use `"%s"`          |

### Runtime Interaction with the OS — processes, virtual memory, files, and resources

Many behaviors that programmers associate with C are actually OS behaviors. Segmentation faults, virtual address spaces, file descriptors, process IDs, memory maps, and threads are outside ISO C.

| Observed behavior                    | Layer                                              |
| ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Segmentation fault on invalid access | OS/hardware reaction to some invalid memory access |
| Heap allocation pages                | Allocator + OS virtual memory                      |
| File descriptor exhaustion           | OS resource limit                                  |
| Address randomization                | OS loader/security feature                         |
| Shared library loading               | OS/ABI/toolchain                                   |
| Signal delivery                      | OS/POSIX/platform plus C signal abstraction        |
| Thread scheduling                    | OS/runtime                                         |
| Stack overflow detection             | OS/hardware/runtime                                |
| Memory overcommit                    | OS policy                                          |
| Page faults                          | Hardware/OS virtual memory                         |

Important distinction:

| Source-level issue  | Possible runtime symptom                        |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Out-of-bounds write | May appear to work, corrupt data, or fault      |
| Use-after-free      | May appear to work until allocator reuses block |
| Stack overflow      | May fault or corrupt                            |
| Data race           | May appear nondeterministic                     |
| ABI mismatch        | May corrupt arguments or crash                  |
| Missing library     | Loader error                                    |
| File resource leak  | Later `open` fails                              |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                   | Why it matters                          | Better rule                                 |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Thinking no crash means no bug                            | UB may remain latent                    | Use sanitizers and reasoning                |
| Depending on ASLR/address values                          | Nonportable and unstable                | Do not use addresses as persistent identity |
| Assuming OS cleans resources soon enough                  | Long-running programs exhaust resources | Close/free at ownership boundary            |
| Treating file descriptors and `FILE *` as interchangeable | Different buffering/lifetime            | Use correct API family                      |
| Assuming virtual memory exists in all C targets           | Embedded/freestanding differs           | Know target environment                     |

### Semantic Classification Reference — how to classify a surprising behavior

| Question                                                       | If yes | Classification direction                   |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| Does the C standard impose no requirements?                    | Yes    | Undefined behavior                         |
| Does the implementation document a choice?                     | Yes    | Implementation-defined                     |
| Does the standard allow several choices without documentation? | Yes    | Unspecified                                |
| Does behavior depend on current locale?                        | Yes    | Locale-dependent                           |
| Does behavior come from compiler extension?                    | Yes    | Extension behavior                         |
| Does behavior come from POSIX/OS API?                          | Yes    | Platform/POSIX behavior                    |
| Does behavior concern binary layout/calling?                   | Yes    | ABI behavior                               |
| Does behavior depend on hardware cache/instruction details?    | Yes    | Hardware/architecture behavior             |
| Is it merely inefficient but defined?                          | Yes    | Performance issue, not semantic invalidity |

Examples:

| Behavior                           | Classification                |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Signed `int` overflow              | Undefined behavior            |
| Out-of-bounds array access         | Undefined behavior            |
| Function argument evaluation order | Unspecified behavior          |
| Plain `char` signedness            | Implementation-defined        |
| Text formatting under locale       | Locale-dependent              |
| `pthread_create` behavior          | POSIX/platform behavior       |
| `__attribute__((packed))`          | Compiler extension            |
| Struct passing in registers        | ABI behavior                  |
| Cache miss from linked list        | Hardware/performance behavior |
| `malloc` returning `NULL`          | Defined library behavior      |

### Part 7 Semantic Failure Index — what went wrong and where to look

| Symptom                                        | Possible semantic cause                            | First investigation                         |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Works at `-O0`, fails at `-O2`                 | UB, strict aliasing, uninitialized read, data race | Sanitizers, warnings, alias/lifetime review |
| Linker undefined reference                     | Missing definition/library                         | Build/link dependencies                     |
| Duplicate symbol                               | Header definition or missing `static`              | Declarations/definitions/linkage            |
| Random value in local variable                 | Uninitialized automatic object                     | Initialization path                         |
| Buffer length becomes huge                     | Unsigned underflow                                 | Arithmetic checks                           |
| Crash after `realloc`                          | Old pointer/interior pointer used                  | Realloc invalidation                        |
| Wrong `printf` output                          | Format mismatch                                    | Format specifier and argument type          |
| Parser accepts malformed input                 | Missing validation or overflow                     | Trust-boundary checks                       |
| Threaded code intermittently fails             | Data race or lifetime issue                        | TSan, synchronization design                |
| Struct comparison unreliable                   | Padding bytes                                      | Field-wise comparison                       |
| File write appears successful but data missing | Ignored `fclose`/flush error                       | I/O error handling                          |
| FFI call corrupts stack                        | ABI/signature mismatch                             | FFI declarations/calling convention         |
| Callback uses invalid context                  | Retained stack `userdata`                          | Callback lifetime contract                  |
| Hardware register access optimized strangely   | Missing/incorrect volatile/barrier                 | Platform MMIO rules                         |

### Part 7 Common Pitfalls and Anti-Patterns

| Item                                   | Classification           | Why harmful                       | Better pattern                               |
| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Reasoning from assembly first          | Semantic anti-pattern    | Assembly is implementation output | Reason from C rules, inspect assembly later  |
| Signed overflow as wraparound          | UB                       | Optimizer may assume impossible   | Checked arithmetic or unsigned intentionally |
| `while (!feof(fp))` mental model       | Runtime/library pitfall  | EOF observed after read attempt   | Drive by read result                         |
| Type-punning through pointer casts     | Aliasing UB              | Miscompilation                    | `memcpy` or documented extension             |
| `volatile` for threads                 | Concurrency anti-pattern | Data race remains                 | `_Atomic` or locks                           |
| Scattered platform assumptions         | Portability failure      | Hard to audit                     | Platform adapter                             |
| Raw struct serialization               | Representation bug       | Padding/endian/layout             | Explicit serialization                       |
| Longjmp across resources               | Cleanup failure          | Leaks/locks held                  | Return-code cleanup or strict jump boundary  |
| Public ABI using casual `long`/structs | Compatibility bug        | Data model/platform variation     | Fixed-width/opaque/versioned API             |
| Ignoring unspecified evaluation order  | Nonportable behavior     | Different results                 | Sequence explicitly                          |
| Assuming sanitizer-clean means correct | Tooling overtrust        | Coverage incomplete               | Combine reasoning, tests, tools              |

### What to Memorize vs What to Look Up — Part 7 artifacts

| Memorize                                                           | Reason                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- |
| C source targets an abstract machine, not literal hardware         | Prevents portable-assembly misconception |
| UB means no semantics, not guaranteed crash                        | Central to C correctness                 |
| Signed overflow is UB; unsigned wraps modulo                       | Core arithmetic distinction              |
| Function argument evaluation order is not generally specified      | Avoids side-effect bugs                  |
| Object lifetime is separate from pointer values                    | Prevents dangling access                 |
| Pointer arithmetic is within arrays and one-past only              | Prevents bounds/provenance bugs          |
| Pointer casts do not change object type or guarantee alignment     | Prevents aliasing bugs                   |
| `memcpy` is the usual safe representation-copy tool                | Avoids type-punning UB                   |
| `volatile` is not synchronization                                  | Prevents concurrency bugs                |
| Non-atomic data races are UB                                       | Core C memory model                      |
| ABI is not ISO C                                                   | Prevents FFI/library mistakes            |
| Headers must keep declarations consistent across translation units | Prevents ABI/call mismatch               |
| OS/hardware symptoms do not define C validity                      | Prevents “it didn’t crash” reasoning     |

| Look up when needed                                        | Why                                   |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Exact standard wording for effective type and aliasing     | Subtle and technical                  |
| Target ABI calling convention                              | Platform-specific                     |
| Compiler documentation for implementation-defined behavior | Required for portability assumptions  |
| Atomic memory-order rules                                  | Difficult and error-prone             |
| Floating-point environment behavior                        | Implementation/optimization-sensitive |
| Signal-safety rules                                        | ISO vs POSIX differ                   |
| Freestanding target library support                        | Environment-specific                  |
| Linker/shared-library visibility                           | Toolchain-specific                    |
| C23 feature support and changes                            | Compiler adoption varies              |
| Sanitizer coverage and flags                               | Tool-specific                         |

### Part 7 Closing Mental Model — defined C is a contract among source, compiler, ABI, OS, and hardware

Part 7’s central idea is that C behavior exists at multiple layers. The C standard defines the abstract-machine semantics of valid programs. The implementation chooses documented details and extensions. The ABI defines binary compatibility. The operating system provides processes, files, virtual memory, threads, and signals. The hardware supplies instructions, caches, alignment constraints, and memory-order realities.

A professional C programmer asks:

| Question                                                                 | Why it matters                                                 |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Is this behavior defined by ISO C?                                       | Determines portable source semantics                           |
| Is this implementation-defined or unspecified?                           | Determines whether target documentation or avoidance is needed |
| Is this compiler extension, POSIX, OS, ABI, or hardware behavior?        | Determines portability layer                                   |
| Could UB exist on this path?                                             | Invalidates reasoning and optimization assumptions             |
| What object exists here, with what lifetime and effective type?          | Determines valid access                                        |
| What are the arithmetic ranges and conversions?                          | Prevents overflow and bounds bugs                              |
| Are side effects sequenced?                                              | Prevents evaluation-order bugs                                 |
| Are resources released on every path?                                    | Prevents runtime leaks/deadlocks                               |
| Are shared objects synchronized?                                         | Prevents data-race UB                                          |
| Does the public binary interface depend on layout or calling convention? | Prevents ABI breakage                                          |

The compact rule is: **C gives high control only to programs that stay inside precisely understood contracts**. Outside those contracts, the machine may still do something, but C no longer promises what.
## PART 8 — Idioms, Patterns, Data Structures, and Systems Design in C

### Scope of Part 8 — from mechanisms to idiomatic C design

Part 8 treats C as a practical design language. Earlier parts covered syntax, data modeling, functions, modules, resources, library use, and runtime semantics. This part asks how those mechanisms combine into idiomatic C programs: how to structure data structures, design ownership patterns, build parsers, implement buffers, write callbacks, use tables, organize initialization, and avoid both over-engineering and unsafe minimalism.

C idioms are not decorative style. In C, idioms are often **safety protocols**. A pattern such as `goto cleanup`, `data/len/cap`, `init/deinit`, `create/destroy`, `pointer + length`, or `enum tag + union payload` exists because the language does not enforce those properties automatically.

| Design task          | Idiomatic C pattern                    | Problem solved                | Main risk                        |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Dynamic sequence     | `data`, `len`, `cap` struct            | Growable contiguous storage   | Overflow, `realloc` invalidation |
| Resource lifecycle   | `init/deinit`, `create/destroy`        | Explicit cleanup              | Partial initialization bugs      |
| Error cleanup        | `goto cleanup`                         | Centralized release path      | Arbitrary jump misuse            |
| Optional output      | Status return plus output parameter    | Avoid sentinel collision      | Undefined output on failure      |
| Encapsulation        | Opaque struct plus functions           | Hide invariants/layout        | Lifecycle boilerplate            |
| Generic behavior     | Function pointer plus `void *userdata` | Callback/polymorphic behavior | Lifetime/type erasure            |
| Variant data         | Tagged union                           | Manual sum type               | Tag/payload mismatch             |
| Command dispatch     | Table of names and function pointers   | Data-driven behavior          | Invalid/missing function slots   |
| Parsing              | Cursor/state-machine pattern           | Controlled input consumption  | Bounds and rollback errors       |
| Intrusive structures | Embedded node fields                   | Allocation/control efficiency | Layout/type convention risk      |
| Platform boundary    | Adapter layer                          | Isolate nonportable APIs      | Leaky abstraction                |
| Testable design      | Explicit context objects               | Avoid hidden globals          | More plumbing                    |

The Part 8 rule is: **idiomatic C design makes the invisible contract visible enough to audit**.

### Idiom Taxonomy — syntax idioms, data idioms, resource idioms, and boundary idioms

C idioms can be grouped by what they protect.

| Idiom category      | Examples                                                                        | Protects against                                               |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Syntax idioms       | `sizeof *p`, one declaration per pointer, braces around control bodies          | Misread declarations, allocation-size bugs, control-flow edits |
| Data idioms         | pointer plus length, `len <= cap`, tagged union, wrapper struct                 | Bounds bugs, invalid variants, domain mixups                   |
| Resource idioms     | `init/deinit`, `create/destroy`, `goto cleanup`, null-tolerant destroy          | Leaks, double frees, partial-init bugs                         |
| Error idioms        | `0` success, status enum, output parameter, preserve first error                | Ignored failures, ambiguous results                            |
| API idioms          | opaque types, prefix names, `const` views, options struct                       | ABI breakage, invariant leaks, namespace collisions            |
| Generic idioms      | `void *userdata`, function pointer typedefs, `_Generic`, macro-generated code   | Reusable behavior with controlled type erasure                 |
| Parser idioms       | cursor, token stream, state machine, length-bounded input                       | OOB reads, malformed input contamination                       |
| Concurrency idioms  | explicit context, lock around invariant, atomic scalar, no callbacks under lock | Data races, deadlocks                                          |
| Portability idioms  | platform adapter, feature-detection header, no scattered `#ifdef`               | Nonportable code sprawl                                        |
| Verification idioms | invariant assertions, fuzz harnesses, sanitizer builds                          | Latent boundary bugs                                           |

### Naming Idioms — making ownership, mutability, and effects visible

C names carry semantic load because the type system cannot encode many contracts. Naming should indicate whether a function creates, borrows, mutates, destroys, parses, validates, or transfers ownership.

| Name pattern | Conventional meaning                        | Example                            | Contract implication                                  |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `*_init`     | Initialize caller-provided object           | `buffer_init(&buf)`                | Caller owns storage; must deinit on success           |
| `*_deinit`   | Release internals of caller-provided object | `buffer_deinit(&buf)`              | Does not free object storage itself                   |
| `*_create`   | Allocate and initialize object              | `buffer_create(&buf)`              | Caller receives owned object                          |
| `*_destroy`  | Release object and probably its storage     | `buffer_destroy(buf)`              | Usually accepts object from `create`                  |
| `*_clear`    | Reset contents but keep object usable       | `buffer_clear(buf)`                | Usually preserves allocation                          |
| `*_reset`    | Return object to initial state              | `parser_reset(p)`                  | Must define retained resources                        |
| `*_reserve`  | Ensure capacity                             | `buffer_reserve(buf, n)`           | May allocate; object unchanged on failure if promised |
| `*_append`   | Add to existing data                        | `buffer_append(buf, data, n)`      | Mutates object; may allocate                          |
| `*_borrow`   | Return non-owning reference                 | `map_borrow_value(m, k)`           | Caller must not free                                  |
| `*_take`     | Callee assumes ownership                    | `buffer_take_data(buf, data, n)`   | Caller must stop using transferred object             |
| `*_release`  | Caller receives ownership                   | `buffer_release_data(buf, &p, &n)` | Object gives up resource                              |
| `*_view`     | Return temporary non-owning view            | `buffer_view(buf)`                 | Validity/invalidation rule required                   |
| `*_from_*`   | Convert/parse/validate                      | `mode_from_int(raw, &mode)`        | May fail                                              |
| `*_is_*`     | Predicate                                   | `buffer_is_empty(buf)`             | Should avoid mutation                                 |
| `*_try_*`    | Fallible operation                          | `queue_try_pop(q, &item)`          | Failure is normal                                     |
| `*_unsafe_*` | Preconditions not checked                   | `buffer_unsafe_data(buf)`          | Requires local discipline                             |

Poor name:

```c id="p8_bad_name"
int buffer_set(Buffer *buf, unsigned char *data, size_t n);
```

This does not say whether `data` is copied, borrowed, retained, or taken.

Clearer alternatives:

```c id="p8_clear_names"
int buffer_copy_data(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n);
int buffer_take_data(Buffer *buf, unsigned char *data, size_t n);
const unsigned char *buffer_borrow_data(const Buffer *buf);
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                             | Better rule                               |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------- |
| Vague verbs like `handle`, `process`, `do`, `set` | Hide effects and ownership                 | Use verb-object names with ownership cue  |
| `get` returns owned allocation                    | Caller may not free                        | Use `create`, `dup`, or `copy`            |
| `destroy` only partially clears object            | Caller expects full release                | Match naming to lifecycle                 |
| `init` allocates object storage itself            | Confuses `init/deinit` vs `create/destroy` | Keep lifecycle families distinct          |
| Predicate mutates state                           | Surprising side effect                     | Keep `is/has/can` functions observational |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                    |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Documentation can explain ownership; names do not matter much.”                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Callers free a pointer after passing it to a function that actually took ownership.            |
| Correct semantic explanation    | In C, ownership is not visible in the type. Names are part of the practical contract.          |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use names that encode lifecycle and transfer semantics before readers open the implementation. |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Tiny file-local helpers may rely on nearby context, but public APIs need explicit naming.      |

### The `data/len/cap` Idiom — dynamic arrays and growable buffers

The most common C replacement for a vector is a struct containing a pointer, logical length, and capacity.

```c id="p8_vec_struct"
struct byte_vec {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Core invariant:

```c id="p8_vec_invariant"
/*
 * Valid byte_vec invariant:
 * - len <= cap
 * - data points to cap bytes when cap > 0
 * - data is NULL when cap == 0, unless project permits malloc(0)-style non-null
 */
```

Initialization:

```c id="p8_vec_init"
void byte_vec_init(struct byte_vec *v)
{
    if (v == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    v->data = NULL;
    v->len = 0;
    v->cap = 0;
}
```

Destruction:

```c id="p8_vec_deinit"
void byte_vec_deinit(struct byte_vec *v)
{
    if (v == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    free(v->data);
    v->data = NULL;
    v->len = 0;
    v->cap = 0;
}
```

Reserve operation:

```c id="p8_vec_reserve"
int byte_vec_reserve(struct byte_vec *v, size_t min_cap)
{
    unsigned char *new_data;
    size_t new_cap;

    if (v == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (v->cap >= min_cap) {
        return 0;
    }

    new_cap = v->cap == 0 ? 16 : v->cap;

    while (new_cap < min_cap) {
        if (new_cap > SIZE_MAX / 2) {
            return -1;
        }

        new_cap *= 2;
    }

    new_data = realloc(v->data, new_cap);
    if (new_data == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    v->data = new_data;
    v->cap = new_cap;
    return 0;
}
```

Append operation:

```c id="p8_vec_append"
int byte_vec_append(struct byte_vec *v,
                    const unsigned char *src,
                    size_t n)
{
    if (v == NULL || (src == NULL && n != 0)) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (n > SIZE_MAX - v->len) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (byte_vec_reserve(v, v->len + n) != 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    memcpy(v->data + v->len, src, n);
    v->len += n;
    return 0;
}
```

This idiom is compact, efficient, and common, but it requires disciplined invariants.

| Operation           | Required invariant or check               |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Read `data[i]`      | `i < len`                                 |
| Write append region | `len + n <= cap`                          |
| Grow capacity       | Check multiplication/addition overflow    |
| `realloc`           | Use temporary pointer                     |
| Return `data` view  | State invalidation after mutation/growth  |
| Copy vector         | Define deep copy or prohibit shallow copy |
| Destroy vector      | Free owned `data` once                    |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                         | Better rule                      |
| ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Updating `len` before successful copy/allocation | Object enters invalid state on failure | Mutate state after success       |
| Direct `realloc` assignment                      | Original allocation lost on failure    | Use temporary                    |
| Forgetting `len + n` overflow check              | Capacity check bypass                  | Check before addition            |
| Returning `data` then growing vector             | Caller view becomes stale              | Document invalidation            |
| Shallow-copying vector struct                    | Two vectors share allocation           | Provide `clone` or prohibit copy |

### String Builder Idiom — dynamic C string with explicit invariant

A string builder is a specialized dynamic buffer whose data remains null-terminated.

```c id="p8_string_builder"
struct string_builder {
    char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Typical invariant:

```c id="p8_string_builder_invariant"
/*
 * Valid string_builder invariant:
 * - len < cap when data is non-NULL
 * - data[len] == '\0'
 * - cap includes space for the terminator
 * - len counts bytes before the terminator
 */
```

Initialization with empty string:

```c id="p8_sb_init"
int sb_init(struct string_builder *sb)
{
    if (sb == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    sb->data = malloc(1);
    if (sb->data == NULL) {
        sb->len = 0;
        sb->cap = 0;
        return -1;
    }

    sb->data[0] = '\0';
    sb->len = 0;
    sb->cap = 1;
    return 0;
}
```

Append bytes from a valid C string:

```c id="p8_sb_append_cstr"
int sb_append_cstr(struct string_builder *sb, const char *s)
{
    size_t n;

    if (sb == NULL || s == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    n = strlen(s);

    if (n > SIZE_MAX - sb->len - 1) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (sb_reserve(sb, sb->len + n + 1) != 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    memcpy(sb->data + sb->len, s, n + 1);
    sb->len += n;
    return 0;
}
```

The `n + 1` copy includes the null terminator.

| Design choice                                | Meaning                       |
| -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| `len` excludes terminator                    | Common and convenient         |
| `cap` includes terminator space              | Prevents off-by-one ambiguity |
| `data` always non-null after successful init | Simplifies callers            |
| Append function treats truncation as error   | Avoids silent data loss       |
| Builder owns `data`                          | Requires `sb_deinit`          |
| `sb_cstr` returns borrowed view              | Invalid after mutation/deinit |

Accessor:

```c id="p8_sb_cstr"
const char *sb_cstr(const struct string_builder *sb)
{
    return sb == NULL || sb->data == NULL ? "" : sb->data;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                                  | Why it matters                        | Better rule                                  |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Capacity excludes terminator in some functions and includes it in others | Off-by-one bugs                       | Define one invariant                         |
| Calling `strlen` repeatedly in append loop                               | Quadratic behavior                    | Track `len`                                  |
| Returning mutable `char *` freely                                        | Caller may break terminator invariant | Return `const char *` view                   |
| Appending from own buffer without overlap handling                       | `memcpy` overlap risk                 | Detect overlap or use `memmove` if supported |
| Treating bytes as Unicode characters                                     | `len` is byte count                   | Define encoding separately                   |

### Slice/View Idiom — pointer plus length as one object

Passing pointer and length as separate parameters is common. For APIs with many sequence values, a span-like struct reduces mismatches.

```c id="p8_slice"
struct byte_view {
    const unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
};

struct byte_span {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
};
```

Read-only view:

```c id="p8_view_checksum"
unsigned byte_view_checksum(struct byte_view view)
{
    unsigned sum = 0;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < view.len; i++) {
        sum += view.data[i];
    }

    return sum;
}
```

This still requires `view.data` to be valid for `view.len` bytes. The struct carries length; it does not prove lifetime.

View construction:

```c id="p8_view_from_array"
#define BYTE_VIEW_FROM_ARRAY(a) \
    ((struct byte_view){ (const unsigned char *)(a), sizeof(a) })
```

This macro should be used carefully: it treats the entire object representation of `a` as bytes if `a` is an array of bytes or intended byte storage. For typed arrays, a type-specific view macro is clearer.

Typed view:

```c id="p8_int_view"
struct int_view {
    const int *data;
    size_t len;
};
```

| View type            | Mutability            | Owns data?  | Typical use         |
| -------------------- | --------------------- | ----------- | ------------------- |
| `struct byte_view`   | Read-only bytes       | No          | Parser input        |
| `struct byte_span`   | Mutable bytes         | No          | Output buffer       |
| `struct int_view`    | Read-only `int` array | No          | Algorithm input     |
| `struct string_view` | Read-only text bytes  | No          | Length-bounded text |
| `struct buffer`      | Mutable owned storage | Usually yes | Dynamic buffer      |

String view:

```c id="p8_string_view"
struct string_view {
    const char *data;
    size_t len;
};
```

Important: a `string_view` is not necessarily null-terminated. Do not pass it to `strlen`, `strcmp`, or `%s` unless a terminator is guaranteed or copied.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters              | Better rule                      |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Treating view as owner                                | Double free or invalid free | Views never free data            |
| Treating string view as C string                      | Missing terminator          | Use length-aware operations      |
| Returning view to local array                         | Dangling pointer            | Referent must outlive view       |
| Mutating through read-only view by cast               | Breaks contract             | Use mutable span                 |
| Separating pointer and length after constructing view | Reintroduces mismatch risk  | Pass view as a unit where useful |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “A view struct makes buffer handling safe.”                                                                            |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A view points to a local array that has gone out of scope.                                                             |
| Correct semantic explanation    | A view carries extent, not ownership or lifetime.                                                                      |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Treat views as borrowed pointer-length pairs with explicit lifetime.                                                   |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Some libraries implement reference-counted or owning slice types, but that is an additional convention beyond plain C. |

### Opaque Object Idiom — public handle, private representation

Opaque objects are idiomatic for modules with invariants or resources.

Public header:

```c id="p8_opaque_header"
#ifndef RING_BUFFER_H
#define RING_BUFFER_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct ring_buffer RingBuffer;

int ring_buffer_create(RingBuffer **out, size_t capacity);
void ring_buffer_destroy(RingBuffer *rb);

int ring_buffer_push(RingBuffer *rb, unsigned char value);
int ring_buffer_pop(RingBuffer *rb, unsigned char *out);
size_t ring_buffer_size(const RingBuffer *rb);

#endif
```

Private implementation:

```c id="p8_opaque_impl"
struct ring_buffer {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t cap;
    size_t head;
    size_t len;
};
```

Invariant:

```c id="p8_ring_invariant"
/*
 * Valid ring_buffer invariant:
 * - len <= cap
 * - head < cap when cap > 0
 * - data points to cap bytes
 * - logical element i lives at data[(head + i) % cap]
 */
```

Opaque type advantages:

| Advantage                       | Why it matters         |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------- |
| Clients cannot mutate fields    | Invariant protection   |
| Struct layout can change        | ABI/source flexibility |
| Resource cleanup centralized    | Fewer leaks            |
| Function names define behavior  | Easier review          |
| Internal helpers remain private | Smaller public surface |

Opaque type costs:

| Cost                                         | Consequence                                                  |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| More functions                               | Boilerplate                                                  |
| Heap allocation common                       | Allocation failure and cleanup                               |
| Less direct stack allocation                 | May be unsuitable for embedded unless alternative API exists |
| Accessor overhead                            | Usually optimized or acceptable, but must be considered      |
| Debugging requires implementation visibility | Less transparent to users                                    |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters           | Better rule                                 |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Opaque object without clear destroy function | Leak risk                | Pair create/destroy                         |
| Returning mutable internal fields            | Opaqueness defeated      | Return const views or controlled operations |
| Exposing private header to clients           | Representation leaks     | Separate public/private include paths       |
| Opaque trivial structs                       | Unnecessary ceremony     | Use public value structs for plain data     |
| Hidden global singleton behind opaque API    | Reentrancy/thread issues | Prefer explicit context object              |

### Context Object Idiom — explicit state instead of globals

A context object carries state that might otherwise become global. This improves testability, reentrancy, and concurrent use.

Bad global style:

```c id="p8_global_bad"
static int log_level;

void logger_set_level(int level)
{
    log_level = level;
}

void logger_write(const char *msg)
{
    if (log_level > 0) {
        fputs(msg, stderr);
    }
}
```

Context style:

```c id="p8_context_logger"
struct logger {
    FILE *stream;
    int level;
};

void logger_init(struct logger *log, FILE *stream, int level)
{
    log->stream = stream;
    log->level = level;
}

int logger_write(struct logger *log, int level, const char *msg)
{
    if (log == NULL || msg == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (level <= log->level) {
        return fputs(msg, log->stream) >= 0 ? 0 : -1;
    }

    return 0;
}
```

Context object benefits:

| Benefit               | Explanation                                 |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Reentrancy            | Multiple independent instances              |
| Testability           | Tests can pass fake streams or state        |
| Thread design         | Each thread can own context or lock context |
| Explicit dependencies | Functions reveal what state they use        |
| Config isolation      | No scattered global reads                   |
| Easier cleanup        | Context owns resources visibly              |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters    | Better rule                                       |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Context stores borrowed pointer without lifetime rule | Dangling pointer  | Document borrow/retain                            |
| Context becomes giant global-in-disguise              | Low cohesion      | Split contexts by responsibility                  |
| Passing context everywhere unnecessarily              | Noise             | Use context where state/config/resources are real |
| Hidden fallback to global context                     | Surprise coupling | Make default context explicit                     |
| Context copied by value with owning resources         | Shallow-copy bug  | Define copy policy                                |

### Error Object Idiom — rich diagnostics without exceptions

For simple code, status codes are enough. For complex libraries, an error object can carry category, code, message, location, or nested cause. This must be designed carefully because error reporting itself must not become a source of allocation failure or leaks.

Simple error struct:

```c id="p8_error_struct"
struct error {
    int code;
    char message[128];
};
```

Function shape:

```c id="p8_error_api"
int parse_config(const char *path,
                 struct config *out,
                 struct error *err);
```

Setting error:

```c id="p8_error_set"
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdarg.h>

static void error_set(struct error *err, int code, const char *fmt, ...)
{
    va_list ap;

    if (err == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    err->code = code;

    va_start(ap, fmt);
    vsnprintf(err->message, sizeof err->message, fmt, ap);
    va_end(ap);

    err->message[sizeof err->message - 1] = '\0';
}
```

This avoids dynamic allocation inside error handling. It may truncate messages; the project must decide whether that is acceptable.

Error strategy table:

| Need                         | Pattern                                 |
| ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Simple internal helper       | `int` status                            |
| Public known error category  | Error enum                              |
| Detailed human diagnostic    | Caller-provided error object            |
| Preserve OS error            | Save `errno` into error object promptly |
| Allocation failure reporting | Avoid allocation in error path          |
| Nested parse errors          | Error object with line/column/context   |
| Library should not print     | Return error object/status to caller    |
| Application boundary         | Log/report error once                   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                   | Why it matters           | Better rule                                |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| Error object allocates while reporting allocation failure | Recursive failure        | Use fixed buffer or caller storage         |
| Function logs and also fills error                        | Duplicate reporting      | Separate reporting from propagation        |
| Error object lifetime unclear                             | Dangling message pointer | Use owned/fixed storage or clear ownership |
| Overwriting first error during cleanup                    | Root cause lost          | Preserve first meaningful error            |
| Returning raw internal errors in public API               | Coupling                 | Normalize at boundary                      |

### Tagged Union Idiom — manual variants with active-member discipline

Tagged unions are C’s standard manual pattern for representing variants.

```c id="p8_tagged_union"
enum token_kind {
    TOKEN_INT,
    TOKEN_IDENT,
    TOKEN_END,
    TOKEN_ERROR
};

struct token {
    enum token_kind kind;
    union {
        long int_value;
        struct string_view ident;
        int error_code;
    } as;
};
```

Constructor-style helpers:

```c id="p8_token_helpers"
struct token token_make_int(long value)
{
    struct token t;
    t.kind = TOKEN_INT;
    t.as.int_value = value;
    return t;
}

struct token token_make_error(int code)
{
    struct token t;
    t.kind = TOKEN_ERROR;
    t.as.error_code = code;
    return t;
}
```

Access helper:

```c id="p8_token_access"
int token_get_int(const struct token *t, long *out)
{
    if (t == NULL || out == NULL || t->kind != TOKEN_INT) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = t->as.int_value;
    return 0;
}
```

If union variants contain owned resources, the type needs destroy and copy policy.

```c id="p8_tagged_destroy"
void value_destroy(struct value *v)
{
    if (v == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    switch (v->kind) {
    case VALUE_STRING:
        free(v->as.string);
        break;

    case VALUE_INT:
    case VALUE_NULL:
        break;
    }

    v->kind = VALUE_NULL;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                 | Why it matters          | Better rule                |
| --------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Direct union field access everywhere    | Tag checks inconsistent | Use constructors/accessors |
| Forgetting to update tag                | Invalid variant state   | Centralize mutation        |
| Owned payload without destructor        | Leak                    | Define destroy             |
| Copying variant with owned field        | Shallow copy            | Define clone               |
| Assuming enum tag impossible to corrupt | C enum not closed       | Validate at boundaries     |

### Table-Driven Dispatch Idiom — data-oriented behavior selection

Instead of long chains of `if`/`else`, C code often uses tables of names, opcodes, states, or function pointers.

Command table:

```c id="p8_command_table"
struct command {
    const char *name;
    int (*run)(int argc, char **argv);
};

static int cmd_help(int argc, char **argv);
static int cmd_version(int argc, char **argv);

static const struct command commands[] = {
    { "help", cmd_help },
    { "version", cmd_version }
};
```

Lookup:

```c id="p8_command_lookup"
static const struct command *find_command(const char *name)
{
    for (size_t i = 0; i < sizeof commands / sizeof commands[0]; i++) {
        if (strcmp(commands[i].name, name) == 0) {
            return &commands[i];
        }
    }

    return NULL;
}
```

Dispatch:

```c id="p8_command_dispatch"
int run_command(const char *name, int argc, char **argv)
{
    const struct command *cmd = find_command(name);

    if (cmd == NULL || cmd->run == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    return cmd->run(argc, argv);
}
```

Table-driven design is useful when behavior is naturally data-like.

| Good table use          | Poor table use                                            |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Command name → function | Two trivial hardcoded branches                            |
| Opcode → handler        | Logic with complex stateful preconditions hidden in table |
| Character class table   | Locale/encoding-sensitive parsing without policy          |
| State transition table  | Irregular semantic conditions better expressed in code    |
| Format descriptor table | Public ABI without versioning                             |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                              | Why it matters              | Better rule                            |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Missing sentinel or count            | Iteration over table unsafe | Use explicit count or array-size macro |
| Null function pointer slot           | Invalid call                | Validate or make table complete        |
| Table not sorted but binary searched | Lookup fails                | Match search strategy to invariant     |
| Public table layout exposed          | ABI freeze                  | Hide table or version it               |
| Callback signatures inconsistent     | UB risk                     | Use exact typedef                      |

### State Machine Idiom — explicit transitions and controlled mutation

State machines appear in parsers, protocols, embedded controllers, and event processing. C represents them with `enum` state labels, a context struct, and transition functions.

```c id="p8_state_machine_struct"
enum conn_state {
    CONN_CLOSED,
    CONN_CONNECTING,
    CONN_OPEN,
    CONN_ERROR
};

struct connection {
    enum conn_state state;
    int error_code;
};
```

Transition function:

```c id="p8_state_transition"
int connection_on_opened(struct connection *c)
{
    if (c == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    switch (c->state) {
    case CONN_CONNECTING:
        c->state = CONN_OPEN;
        return 0;

    case CONN_CLOSED:
    case CONN_OPEN:
    case CONN_ERROR:
    default:
        c->state = CONN_ERROR;
        c->error_code = -1;
        return -1;
    }
}
```

State-machine rules:

| Rule                                                     | Why                                  |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Mutate state in transition functions                     | Centralizes invariants               |
| Validate external events before transition               | Prevents invalid state contamination |
| Have explicit error/closed terminal states               | Clarifies lifecycle                  |
| Avoid state plus many independent flags unless necessary | Prevents invalid combinations        |
| Document allowed transitions                             | Makes review possible                |
| Test invalid transitions                                 | Security and robustness              |

Transition table example:

```c id="p8_transition_table"
struct transition {
    enum conn_state from;
    int event;
    enum conn_state to;
};
```

Transition tables are best when the machine is regular. If actions are complex, switch-based or function-per-state design may be clearer.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters          | Better rule             |
| --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------- |
| State changed in many random functions        | Hard to audit           | Centralize transitions  |
| No default invalid-state handling             | Invalid enum can escape | Validate or fail closed |
| State flags represent impossible combinations | Logic explosion         | Use enum/tagged union   |
| Side effects before transition validation     | Partial mutation        | Validate first          |
| Table hides nontrivial action semantics       | Hard to debug           | Use explicit functions  |

### Parser Cursor Idiom — bounded input consumption

A parser often maintains a cursor over a bounded buffer. This avoids unbounded string operations and makes input consumption explicit.

```c id="p8_parser_cursor"
struct cursor {
    const unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t pos;
};
```

Invariant:

```c id="p8_cursor_invariant"
/*
 * Valid cursor invariant:
 * - pos <= len
 * - data points to len bytes when len > 0
 */
```

Peek:

```c id="p8_cursor_peek"
int cursor_peek(const struct cursor *c, unsigned char *out)
{
    if (c == NULL || out == NULL || c->pos >= c->len) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = c->data[c->pos];
    return 0;
}
```

Consume:

```c id="p8_cursor_take"
int cursor_take(struct cursor *c, unsigned char *out)
{
    if (cursor_peek(c, out) != 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    c->pos++;
    return 0;
}
```

Consume fixed count:

```c id="p8_cursor_take_bytes"
int cursor_take_bytes(struct cursor *c,
                      const unsigned char **out,
                      size_t n)
{
    if (c == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (n > c->len - c->pos) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = c->data + c->pos;
    c->pos += n;
    return 0;
}
```

The expression `c->len - c->pos` is safe only because the invariant says `pos <= len`.

Parser cursor benefits:

| Benefit                         | Explanation                           |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Explicit bounds                 | No accidental unbounded reads         |
| Controlled consumption          | Backtracking/rollback can be designed |
| Easy fuzzing                    | Parser accepts byte span              |
| No reliance on null termination | Works for binary and bounded text     |
| Centralized overflow checks     | Offset logic in helpers               |

Rollback mark:

```c id="p8_cursor_mark"
size_t mark = c->pos;

if (parse_optional_part(c) != 0) {
    c->pos = mark;
}
```

Rollback should be used carefully; side effects outside cursor movement must also be rolled back or delayed.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters                        | Better rule                          |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Advancing cursor before validating full field | Partial-consumption bug               | Validate availability first          |
| Using `strlen` inside bounded parser          | OOB read risk                         | Use cursor length                    |
| Rollback cursor but not output state          | Inconsistent parse result             | Delay writes or use temporary output |
| Integer overflow in `pos + n`                 | Bounds bypass                         | Check with subtraction form          |
| Parsing and committing side effects together  | Invalid input partially mutates state | Parse into temporary, then commit    |

### Ownership Transfer Idiom — `take`, `release`, and move-like behavior

C has no move semantics, but ownership transfer can be modeled explicitly.

Owned buffer:

```c id="p8_owned_buffer"
struct owned_bytes {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
};
```

Take ownership:

```c id="p8_take_data"
int owned_bytes_take(struct owned_bytes *b,
                     unsigned char *data,
                     size_t len)
{
    if (b == NULL || (data == NULL && len != 0)) {
        return -1;
    }

    free(b->data);
    b->data = data;
    b->len = len;
    return 0;
}
```

After successful call, the caller must not free `data`.

Release ownership:

```c id="p8_release_data"
int owned_bytes_release(struct owned_bytes *b,
                        unsigned char **out_data,
                        size_t *out_len)
{
    if (b == NULL || out_data == NULL || out_len == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out_data = b->data;
    *out_len = b->len;

    b->data = NULL;
    b->len = 0;
    return 0;
}
```

After release, the caller owns the returned pointer and must free it with the correct allocator.

Move-like transfer between structs:

```c id="p8_move_like"
void owned_bytes_move(struct owned_bytes *dst,
                      struct owned_bytes *src)
{
    if (dst == NULL || src == NULL || dst == src) {
        return;
    }

    free(dst->data);

    dst->data = src->data;
    dst->len = src->len;

    src->data = NULL;
    src->len = 0;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters     | Better rule                         |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Caller uses pointer after `take`              | Use-after-transfer | Treat ownership as gone             |
| Release function forgets to neutralize source | Double free        | Set source to empty                 |
| Move overwrites destination without cleanup   | Leak               | Destroy destination first           |
| Move self-assignment not handled              | Can free own data  | Check `dst == src`                  |
| Transfer across allocator families            | Wrong free         | Keep allocator identity with object |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                          |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Assigning one owning struct to another transfers ownership.”        |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Both structs later free the same pointer.                            |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Assignment copies pointer values; it does not neutralize the source. |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use explicit move/take/release functions for ownership transfer.     |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Plain value structs with no owning resources can be assigned freely. |

### Copy and Clone Idioms — shallow, deep, and prohibited copies

C assignment performs member-wise copy. Whether that is correct depends on the object.

| Object kind                       | Assignment safe?          | Required policy                    |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Plain scalar struct               | Usually yes               | Ordinary assignment                |
| Struct with borrowed pointers     | Maybe                     | Document shared borrow             |
| Struct with owned pointer         | Usually no                | Deep clone or move-only convention |
| Struct with file/socket handle    | Usually no                | Duplicate handle or prohibit copy  |
| Struct with mutex/atomic protocol | Usually no                | Noncopyable by convention          |
| Tagged union with owned variant   | No unless handled         | Tag-aware clone/destroy            |
| Opaque object                     | Not assignable by clients | Provide clone if needed            |

Deep clone:

```c id="p8_clone"
int owned_bytes_clone(struct owned_bytes *dst,
                      const struct owned_bytes *src)
{
    unsigned char *copy = NULL;

    if (dst == NULL || src == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (src->len != 0) {
        copy = malloc(src->len);
        if (copy == NULL) {
            return -1;
        }

        memcpy(copy, src->data, src->len);
    }

    free(dst->data);
    dst->data = copy;
    dst->len = src->len;
    return 0;
}
```

Prohibit copying by convention:

```c id="p8_nocopy_comment"
struct file_owner {
    FILE *fp;
};

/*
 * file_owner is noncopyable by convention.
 * Do not assign or memcpy file_owner objects.
 * Use file_owner_open and file_owner_close only.
 */
```

C cannot directly mark a struct noncopyable in ISO C. Opaque types help because clients cannot copy representation by value if they only hold pointers.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                          | Better rule                             |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| `memcpy` owning object                               | Duplicates ownership unsafely           | Use clone function                      |
| Assignment of struct with handle                     | Double close or shared handle confusion | Define duplicate/transfer/prohibit      |
| Clone mutates destination before allocation succeeds | Loses old valid state                   | Allocate first, then commit             |
| Copy omits hidden invariant                          | Invalid object                          | Use module-level clone                  |
| Public struct with noncopyable convention            | Clients may still copy                  | Prefer opaque type if copying dangerous |

### Intrusive Data Structure Idiom — embedding links inside objects

Intrusive structures store linkage fields inside the contained object rather than allocating wrapper nodes.

```c id="p8_intrusive_node"
struct list_node {
    struct list_node *next;
};

struct task {
    struct list_node link;
    int priority;
    const char *name;
};
```

Benefits:

| Benefit                                | Explanation                                           |
| -------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Fewer allocations                      | Node lives inside object                              |
| Stable identity                        | Object and node share lifetime                        |
| Efficient removal in low-level systems | Common in kernels/embedded                            |
| Flexible membership                    | Object can contain multiple links for different lists |

Costs and hazards:

| Hazard                                                          | Explanation                                          |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Container recovery is low-level                                 | Requires offset/layout convention                    |
| Object lifetime must outlive list membership                    | Dangling list nodes otherwise                        |
| Same node cannot be in two lists simultaneously unless designed | Link field overwritten                               |
| Type safety is weak                                             | Link pointer alone does not identify containing type |
| Public layout coupling                                          | Embedding exposes representation                     |

A singly linked intrusive list can operate on `struct list_node *`, but recovering `struct task *` from `link` requires an offset-based pattern. Standard `offsetof` helps compute field offsets, while common `container_of` macros may rely on compiler extensions or careful implementation assumptions. This should be treated as advanced low-level C and isolated.

Safer alternative for many applications:

```c id="p8_nonintrusive_node"
struct task_node {
    struct task *task;
    struct task_node *next;
};
```

This adds allocation/storage overhead but has clearer ownership boundaries.

Decision table:

| Requirement                         | Prefer                                               |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Kernel/embedded low-level structure | Intrusive list                                       |
