| Ordinary application list           | Nonintrusive node or dynamic array                   |
| Frequent traversal                  | Dynamic array if possible                            |
| Stable node addresses               | Intrusive or allocated nodes                         |
| Multiple collections per object     | Multiple embedded link fields with strict convention |
| Type-safe API clarity               | Nonintrusive typed wrapper                           |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                | Why it matters             | Better rule                   |
| -------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Object freed while still linked        | Dangling list              | Remove before destroy         |
| Reusing same link for two lists        | Corrupts list              | One link field per membership |
| Blind `container_of` use               | Type/layout assumptions    | Encapsulate and test          |
| Intrusive list for small simple arrays | Poor locality/complexity   | Prefer contiguous storage     |
| Public intrusive layout without need   | ABI and invariant exposure | Hide inside module            |

### Callback and Visitor Idioms — controlled user-supplied behavior

Callbacks parameterize behavior. A visitor pattern applies a callback to elements.

Typed visitor:

```c id="p8_typed_visitor"
typedef int (*int_visit_fn)(int *value, void *userdata);

int int_vec_visit(struct int_vec *v,
                  int_visit_fn visit,
                  void *userdata)
{
    if (v == NULL || visit == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    for (size_t i = 0; i < v->len; i++) {
        int rc = visit(&v->data[i], userdata);
        if (rc != 0) {
            return rc;
        }
    }

    return 0;
}
```

Visitor contract must define whether callback may mutate the vector, append elements, remove elements, or store element pointers.

Read-only visitor:

```c id="p8_readonly_visitor"
typedef int (*int_read_fn)(int value, void *userdata);

int int_view_visit(struct int_view view,
                   int_read_fn visit,
                   void *userdata)
{
    if (visit == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    for (size_t i = 0; i < view.len; i++) {
        int rc = visit(view.data[i], userdata);
        if (rc != 0) {
            return rc;
        }
    }

    return 0;
}
```

Callback contract table:

| Contract dimension                  | Must be specified              |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Synchronous or retained             | Determines `userdata` lifetime |
| May callback mutate collection?     | Determines traversal validity  |
| May callback store element pointer? | Determines lifetime rule       |
| May callback call back into API?    | Reentrancy                     |
| What return value stops traversal?  | Error propagation              |
| Which thread invokes callback?      | Synchronization                |
| Is `userdata` allowed to be `NULL`? | Context convention             |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                   | Why it matters                    | Better rule                                 |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Callback appends while visiting dynamic array             | `realloc` invalidates pointers    | Forbid or design mutation protocol          |
| Retained callback with stack userdata                     | Dangling pointer                  | Document synchronous vs retained            |
| Callback error ignored                                    | Operation continues after failure | Define return convention                    |
| Callback under lock                                       | Reentrancy/deadlock               | Avoid or document                           |
| Generic `void *` item passed when typed callback possible | Loses type checking               | Prefer typed callbacks inside typed modules |

### Function Pointer Table Idiom — manual interfaces and backends

A function pointer table represents a set of operations. It is C’s manual version of an interface or vtable.

```c id="p8_ops_table"
struct allocator_ops {
    void *(*alloc)(void *ctx, size_t size);
    void *(*realloc)(void *ctx, void *ptr, size_t size);
    void (*free)(void *ctx, void *ptr);
};

struct allocator {
    void *ctx;
    const struct allocator_ops *ops;
};
```

Wrapper:

```c id="p8_allocator_wrapper"
void *allocator_alloc(struct allocator *a, size_t size)
{
    if (a == NULL || a->ops == NULL || a->ops->alloc == NULL) {
        return NULL;
    }

    return a->ops->alloc(a->ctx, size);
}
```

This idiom is appropriate when multiple implementations are genuinely needed: system allocator, arena allocator, test allocator, quota allocator, tracing allocator.

Function table contract:

| Contract      | Question                               |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Ownership     | Who owns `ctx`?                        |
| Lifetime      | Must `ops` outlive object?             |
| Completeness  | Are all function pointers required?    |
| Thread-safety | Can operations be called concurrently? |
| Error policy  | How do functions report failure?       |
| Versioning    | Can table grow in future?              |
| Nullability   | Are optional slots allowed?            |
| ABI           | Is table layout public/stable?         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters        | Better rule                             |
| ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Ops table for one implementation                 | Over-abstraction      | Use direct calls                        |
| Missing version field in public ops table        | Future extension hard | Add size/version for ABI-sensitive APIs |
| `ctx` lifetime unclear                           | Dangling pointer      | Define owner                            |
| Null slot called                                 | Invalid call          | Validate or require full table          |
| Function signatures almost match but not exactly | UB risk               | Use typedefs                            |

### Arena and Pool Allocation Idioms — controlled lifetime allocation

Arena allocation groups many allocations under one lifetime: allocate many objects, then release all at once. It can reduce fragmentation and cleanup complexity when object lifetimes are naturally grouped.

Simple arena shape:

```c id="p8_arena_struct"
struct arena {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Allocate from arena:

```c id="p8_arena_alloc_simple"
void *arena_alloc(struct arena *a, size_t size)
{
    void *p;

    if (a == NULL) {
        return NULL;
    }

    if (size > a->cap - a->len) {
        return NULL;
    }

    p = a->data + a->len;
    a->len += size;
    return p;
}
```

This simplified version omits alignment. A correct general-purpose arena must align returned pointers appropriately.

Arena reset:

```c id="p8_arena_reset"
void arena_reset(struct arena *a)
{
    if (a != NULL) {
        a->len = 0;
    }
}
```

Arena benefits:

| Benefit                      | Explanation                |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Fast allocation              | Bump pointer               |
| Simple cleanup               | Reset/free whole arena     |
| Good locality                | Contiguous storage         |
| Fewer per-object frees       | Simpler error paths        |
| Useful for parsers/compilers | Many same-lifetime objects |

Arena risks:

| Risk                          | Explanation                         |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| No individual free            | Unsuitable for arbitrary lifetimes  |
| Alignment required            | Raw bump pointer must align         |
| Reset invalidates all objects | Borrowed pointers become dangling   |
| Large peak memory             | Objects live until arena reset      |
| Hidden ownership              | Objects look normal but arena-owned |

Decision table:

| Use arena when                                       | Avoid arena when                                          |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Many objects share same lifetime                     | Objects have independent lifetimes                        |
| Parser/compiler request scope                        | Long-lived mutable graph with deletions                   |
| Cleanup all-at-once is acceptable                    | Individual destruction required                           |
| Performance profiling shows allocator overhead       | Memory usage must shrink incrementally                    |
| Object destructors are trivial or separately handled | Objects own external resources needing individual cleanup |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                             | Why it matters                   | Better rule                                 |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Ignoring alignment                                                  | Misaligned access UB/performance | Align every allocation                      |
| Storing arena object after reset                                    | Dangling pointer                 | Reset only when all borrows ended           |
| Putting resources needing destructors in arena without cleanup list | Leaks external resources         | Use cleanup callbacks or separate ownership |
| Arena grows without bounds                                          | Memory exhaustion                | Apply limits                                |
| Using arena to avoid ownership thinking                             | Lifetime bugs still exist        | Document arena lifetime                     |

### Handle Table Idiom — stable IDs instead of raw pointers

A handle table maps small integer handles to internal objects. This is common in C APIs that want to avoid exposing pointers or need validation.

```c id="p8_handle_table"
struct handle_entry {
    int in_use;
    struct object obj;
};

struct object_table {
    struct handle_entry *entries;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Handle lookup:

```c id="p8_handle_lookup"
int object_table_get(struct object_table *t,
                     unsigned handle,
                     struct object **out)
{
    if (t == NULL || out == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = NULL;

    if (handle >= t->cap) {
        return -1;
    }

    if (!t->entries[handle].in_use) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = &t->entries[handle].obj;
    return 0;
}
```

Handle benefits:

| Benefit                            | Explanation                          |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Hides pointers                     | Public API need not expose addresses |
| Validates stale/invalid references | Table can reject missing handles     |
| Enables generation counters        | Detect use-after-close more reliably |
| ABI-friendly                       | Handles can be fixed-width integers  |
| Centralizes lifetime               | Table owns objects                   |

Generation handle idea:

```c id="p8_generation_handle"
struct handle {
    unsigned index;
    unsigned generation;
};
```

The table entry also stores a generation. When an entry is reused, generation increments, so stale handles can be rejected.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                     | Better rule                                          |
| -------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Reusing handles without generation           | Stale handle may access new object | Use generation counters if stale handles matter      |
| Returning internal pointer from handle table | Bypasses validation later          | Keep operations handle-based or define view lifetime |
| No capacity/range check                      | OOB access                         | Validate handle before indexing                      |
| Threaded handle table without lock           | Data race                          | Synchronize table                                    |
| Public handle type too small                 | Exhaustion/wrap                    | Choose width deliberately                            |

### Flag and Bitmask Idiom — compact sets of options

Bitmasks are common for options and flags. Use unsigned types and clear naming.

```c id="p8_flags"
enum open_flags {
    OPEN_READ  = 1u << 0,
    OPEN_WRITE = 1u << 1,
    OPEN_TRUNC = 1u << 2
};
```

Function:

```c id="p8_flags_func"
int file_open_ex(const char *path, unsigned flags);
```

Check:

```c id="p8_flags_check"
if ((flags & OPEN_WRITE) != 0) {
    enable_write();
}
```

Validate flags:

```c id="p8_flags_validate"
#define OPEN_ALL_FLAGS (OPEN_READ | OPEN_WRITE | OPEN_TRUNC)

int open_flags_valid(unsigned flags)
{
    return (flags & ~OPEN_ALL_FLAGS) == 0;
}
```

Flags vs enum states:

| Use enum state when                                 | Use flags when                        |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Values are mutually exclusive                       | Options can combine                   |
| Exactly one mode should be active                   | Zero or more properties may be active |
| Switch dispatch is natural                          | Bitwise tests are natural             |
| Invalid combinations should be impossible by design | Some combinations may need validation |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                    | Why it matters                        | Better rule                                                     |
| ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Using signed type for bit shifts           | Shift UB/nonportable                  | Use unsigned constants                                          |
| Forgetting parentheses in flag tests       | Precedence bugs                       | Write `(flags & FLAG) != 0`                                     |
| Not validating unknown flags in public API | Future/invalid bits accepted silently | Reject unknown bits unless forward-compat policy says otherwise |
| Treating flags as enum state               | Invalid combinations                  | Separate mode and flags                                         |
| Running out of bits                        | ABI/API limitation                    | Choose width and extension policy                               |

### Options Struct Idiom — extensible configuration without parameter explosion

When a function has many optional parameters, an options struct is clearer and more evolvable than a long parameter list.

```c id="p8_options_struct"
struct parser_options {
    size_t struct_size;
    unsigned flags;
    size_t max_depth;
    size_t max_tokens;
};
```

Call:

```c id="p8_options_call"
struct parser_options opts = {
    .struct_size = sizeof opts,
    .flags = 0,
    .max_depth = 64,
    .max_tokens = 100000
};

parser_create_with_options(&parser, &opts);
```

Function:

```c id="p8_options_func"
int parser_create_with_options(Parser **out,
                               const struct parser_options *opts)
{
    struct parser_options effective = {
        .struct_size = sizeof effective,
        .flags = 0,
        .max_depth = 64,
        .max_tokens = 100000
    };

    if (opts != NULL) {
        if (opts->struct_size < offsetof(struct parser_options, max_depth) + sizeof opts->max_depth) {
            return -1;
        }

        effective = *opts;
    }

    return parser_create_configured(out, &effective);
}
```

This example illustrates the pattern, but versioned struct handling must be done carefully. `struct_size` checks should match actual backward-compatibility policy.

Options struct benefits:

| Benefit                      | Explanation                                           |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Avoids long parameter list   | Easier call sites                                     |
| Supports defaults            | `NULL` or zero-initialized options                    |
| Evolvable                    | Add fields with `struct_size` policy                  |
| Self-documenting             | Field names at call site with designated initializers |
| Groups related configuration | One object can be validated                           |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters       | Better rule                                          |
| ------------------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Options struct without initialization rule        | Uninitialized fields | Provide defaults and require zero-init or size field |
| Changing public options layout without versioning | ABI/source break     | Use `struct_size` for public ABI                     |
| Flags plus fields with inconsistent combinations  | Invalid config       | Validate cross-field invariants                      |
| Copying unknown future fields blindly             | ABI issue            | Only read fields covered by size                     |
| Too many options for simple function              | Overdesign           | Use direct parameters when stable and few            |

### PART 8 Interim Reference — idioms covered so far

| Task                    | Idiom                        | Use when                                | Main review question                     |
| ----------------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Growable byte storage   | `data/len/cap`               | Dynamic buffer                          | Is `len <= cap` always preserved?        |
| Growable C string       | string builder               | Repeated string construction            | Is terminator invariant maintained?      |
| Borrowed sequence       | view/span struct             | Pointer and length should stay together | Does referent outlive view?              |
| Hide representation     | opaque object                | Invariants/resources/API evolution      | Is lifecycle clear?                      |
| Avoid globals           | context object               | Multiple instances/tests/threads        | What does context own or borrow?         |
| Rich error reporting    | caller-provided error object | Caller needs diagnostics                | Can error reporting fail?                |
| Variant value           | tagged union                 | Closed alternatives with payloads       | Is tag checked before payload access?    |
| Command dispatch        | table of function pointers   | Data-like operation set                 | Are table entries valid and counted?     |
| Explicit transitions    | state machine                | Protocol/parser/controller              | Are invalid transitions handled?         |
| Bounded parsing         | cursor                       | External byte/text input                | Are bounds checked before consumption?   |
| Ownership transfer      | `take/release/move`          | Avoid copy or transfer resource         | Is source neutralized?                   |
| Deep copy               | `clone`                      | Owning object copy required             | Does failure leave destination valid?    |
| Embedded links          | intrusive structure          | Low-level efficient containers          | Is lifetime/list membership controlled?  |
| User behavior           | callback visitor             | Traversal/custom operation              | Is callback lifetime/reentrancy defined? |
| Backend interface       | ops table                    | Real interchangeable implementations    | Is `ctx` ownership defined?              |
| Group lifetime          | arena                        | Many same-lifetime objects              | Is alignment and reset lifetime safe?    |
| Stable public reference | handle table                 | Hide pointers/validate handles          | Are stale handles detected?              |
| Option set              | bitmask flags                | Combinable boolean options              | Are unknown bits validated?              |
| Extensible config       | options struct               | Many/future parameters                  | Is initialization/version policy clear?  |
### Ring Buffer Idiom — fixed-capacity queue without shifting

A ring buffer represents a FIFO queue in fixed contiguous storage. It avoids moving elements on every push/pop by wrapping indices modulo capacity.

```c id="p8_ring_struct"
struct ring_u8 {
    unsigned char *data;
    size_t cap;
    size_t head;
    size_t len;
};
```

Invariant:

```c id="p8_ring_invariant_2"
/*
 * Valid ring_u8 invariant:
 * - len <= cap
 * - head < cap when cap > 0
 * - data points to cap bytes when cap > 0
 * - logical item i is at data[(head + i) % cap]
 */
```

Initialization:

```c id="p8_ring_init"
int ring_u8_init(struct ring_u8 *r, size_t cap)
{
    if (r == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    r->data = NULL;
    r->cap = 0;
    r->head = 0;
    r->len = 0;

    if (cap == 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    r->data = malloc(cap);
    if (r->data == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    r->cap = cap;
    return 0;
}
```

Push:

```c id="p8_ring_push"
int ring_u8_push(struct ring_u8 *r, unsigned char value)
{
    size_t tail;

    if (r == NULL || r->len == r->cap) {
        return -1;
    }

    tail = (r->head + r->len) % r->cap;
    r->data[tail] = value;
    r->len++;
    return 0;
}
```

Pop:

```c id="p8_ring_pop"
int ring_u8_pop(struct ring_u8 *r, unsigned char *out)
{
    if (r == NULL || out == NULL || r->len == 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    *out = r->data[r->head];
    r->head = (r->head + 1) % r->cap;
    r->len--;
    return 0;
}
```

Destroy:

```c id="p8_ring_deinit"
void ring_u8_deinit(struct ring_u8 *r)
{
    if (r == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    free(r->data);
    r->data = NULL;
    r->cap = 0;
    r->head = 0;
    r->len = 0;
}
```

Ring buffer design choices:

| Design choice         | Meaning                            | Tradeoff                                              |
| --------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Track `len`           | Full and empty are distinguishable | Stores extra field                                    |
| Leave one slot empty  | Full if next tail equals head      | Wastes one slot but simpler in some lock-free designs |
| Power-of-two capacity | Use bitmask instead of `%`         | Requires capacity constraint                          |
| Fixed capacity        | No allocation after init           | Push can fail when full                               |
| Growable ring         | More flexible                      | Complex reallocation and order preservation           |
| Byte-specific ring    | Simple and type-safe               | Needs variants for other types                        |
| `void *` ring         | Generic                            | Ownership and type erasure risks                      |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                 | Why it matters                                                        | Better rule                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Confusing full and empty when only head/tail are stored | Queue state ambiguous                                                 | Track `len` or reserve one slot  |
| Modulo by zero capacity                                 | Undefined behavior                                                    | Reject `cap == 0`                |
| Forgetting wraparound                                   | Out-of-bounds access                                                  | Use helper for index calculation |
| Returning internal contiguous view blindly              | Ring may wrap into two segments                                       | Provide two-slice view if needed |
| Assuming single-thread safety                           | Concurrent push/pop needs synchronization or special lock-free design | Use lock or atomic protocol      |

### Linked List Idioms — when node-based structures are justified

Linked lists are simple to write but often overused. They are useful when stable node addresses, frequent insertion/removal with known nodes, or intrusive membership matters. They are often poor for dense traversal because of allocation overhead and poor cache locality.

Singly linked node:

```c id="p8_slist_node"
struct int_node {
    int value;
    struct int_node *next;
};
```

List owner:

```c id="p8_slist"
struct int_list {
    struct int_node *head;
};
```

Push front:

```c id="p8_slist_push_front"
int int_list_push_front(struct int_list *list, int value)
{
    struct int_node *node;

    if (list == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    node = malloc(sizeof *node);
    if (node == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    node->value = value;
    node->next = list->head;
    list->head = node;
    return 0;
}
```

Destroy:

```c id="p8_slist_destroy"
void int_list_clear(struct int_list *list)
{
    struct int_node *cur;

    if (list == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    cur = list->head;
    while (cur != NULL) {
        struct int_node *next = cur->next;
        free(cur);
        cur = next;
    }

    list->head = NULL;
}
```

Pointer-to-link removal:

```c id="p8_slist_remove"
size_t int_list_remove_value(struct int_list *list, int value)
{
    struct int_node **link;
    size_t removed = 0;

    if (list == NULL) {
        return 0;
    }

    link = &list->head;

    while (*link != NULL) {
        struct int_node *cur = *link;

        if (cur->value == value) {
            *link = cur->next;
            free(cur);
            removed++;
        } else {
            link = &cur->next;
        }
    }

    return removed;
}
```

The `struct int_node **link` idiom is useful because it treats `head` and `next` uniformly as “the pointer that points to the current node.”

Linked list decision table:

| Requirement                                     | Linked list appropriate?        |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Frequent traversal of many items                | Usually no; prefer array/vector |
| Frequent insert/delete at known node            | Often yes                       |
| Stable node addresses                           | Often yes                       |
| Unknown length but append-heavy                 | Dynamic array often better      |
| Need O(1) concatenation/splicing                | Doubly/intrusive list may fit   |
| Tiny list with simple logic                     | Maybe acceptable                |
| Memory-constrained contiguous storage available | Array/pool often better         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                               | Why it matters            | Better rule                                   |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Reading `cur->next` after `free(cur)`                 | Use-after-free            | Save next before free                         |
| No list owner cleanup                                 | Node leaks                | Provide clear/destroy                         |
| Using linked list for cache-sensitive data            | Poor locality             | Prefer contiguous arrays                      |
| Losing head when removing first node                  | List corruption           | Use pointer-to-link or explicit head handling |
| Sharing nodes across lists without ownership protocol | Double free or corruption | Define ownership and membership               |

### Hash Table Idiom — buckets, keys, ownership, and resizing

C has no standard hash table. A hash table must define key representation, value ownership, collision handling, load factor, resizing, and destruction.

Separate chaining shape:

```c id="p8_hash_entry"
struct map_entry {
    char *key;
    int value;
    struct map_entry *next;
};

struct string_int_map {
    struct map_entry **buckets;
    size_t bucket_count;
    size_t size;
};
```

Hash function shape:

```c id="p8_hash_fn"
static size_t hash_string(const char *s)
{
    size_t h = 1469598103934665603ull;

    while (*s != '\0') {
        h ^= (unsigned char)*s;
        h *= 1099511628211ull;
        s++;
    }

    return h;
}
```

This example uses constants associated with FNV-1a-style hashing. For security-sensitive hash tables exposed to adversarial keys, hash choice and collision-resistance policy matter.

Lookup:

```c id="p8_hash_lookup"
int map_get(const struct string_int_map *m,
            const char *key,
            int *out)
{
    size_t index;
    struct map_entry *e;

    if (m == NULL || key == NULL || out == NULL || m->bucket_count == 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    index = hash_string(key) % m->bucket_count;

    for (e = m->buckets[index]; e != NULL; e = e->next) {
        if (strcmp(e->key, key) == 0) {
            *out = e->value;
            return 0;
        }
    }

    return -1;
}
```

Hash table design choices:

| Design choice      | Options                                          | Consequence                                     |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Collision strategy | chaining, open addressing                        | Memory layout and deletion complexity           |
| Key ownership      | copy keys, borrow keys, intern keys              | Lifetime and cleanup                            |
| Value ownership    | inline value, pointer value, destructor callback | Cleanup policy                                  |
| Hash function      | fast non-cryptographic, keyed hash               | Performance vs adversarial collision resistance |
| Resize policy      | load factor threshold                            | Performance stability                           |
| Equality           | string, byte view, custom comparator             | Must match hash                                 |
| Iteration order    | unspecified, insertion order, sorted separately  | API expectations                                |
| Failure handling   | insertion can fail                               | Allocation and resize errors                    |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters       | Better rule                                 |
| --------------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Borrowed keys from temporary storage          | Dangling keys        | Copy keys unless borrow lifetime is clear   |
| Hash and equality inconsistent                | Lookup fails         | Equal keys must hash the same               |
| No resize                                     | Performance degrades | Track load factor                           |
| Resize mutates map before allocation succeeds | Map corruption       | Allocate new table first, then commit       |
| No destructor for owned keys/values           | Leaks                | Define cleanup callbacks or fixed ownership |

### Tree Idioms — binary search trees, balanced trees, and when not to write one

Trees are useful for ordered data, interval queries, prefix structures, ASTs, and hierarchical representation. A plain binary search tree is easy but can degenerate. Balanced trees are complex and often better delegated to a tested library unless project needs justify implementation.

Binary tree node:

```c id="p8_tree_node"
struct int_tree_node {
    int key;
    struct int_tree_node *left;
    struct int_tree_node *right;
};
```

Lookup:

```c id="p8_tree_lookup"
struct int_tree_node *int_tree_find(struct int_tree_node *root, int key)
{
    while (root != NULL) {
        if (key < root->key) {
            root = root->left;
        } else if (key > root->key) {
            root = root->right;
        } else {
            return root;
        }
    }

    return NULL;
}
```

Tree design table:

| Task                         | Tree pattern                |
| ---------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Ordered map/set              | Balanced tree               |
| Syntax representation        | AST with tagged nodes       |
| Prefix matching              | Trie                        |
| Range/interval query         | Interval tree               |
| Hierarchical config          | Tree of nodes               |
| Priority access              | Heap often better than tree |
| Fast lookup without ordering | Hash table often better     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                     | Why it matters             | Better rule                                |
| ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Unbalanced BST for adversarial/sorted input | Degenerates to linked list | Use balanced tree or randomized strategy   |
| Recursive traversal on huge/deep tree       | Stack overflow             | Use iterative traversal or depth limits    |
| No ownership rule for child nodes           | Leaks/double frees         | Parent owns children or document otherwise |
| Complex balanced tree written casually      | Subtle bugs                | Use tested implementation unless necessary |
| Comparing keys inconsistently               | Tree invariant breaks      | Central comparator                         |

### Heap/Priority Queue Idiom — array-backed tree

A binary heap is usually stored in an array, giving good locality and simple index arithmetic.

```c id="p8_heap_struct"
struct int_heap {
    int *data;
    size_t len;
    size_t cap;
};
```

Index formulas:

| Relation      | Formula                    |
| ------------- | -------------------------- |
| Parent of `i` | `(i - 1) / 2` when `i > 0` |
| Left child    | `2 * i + 1`                |
| Right child   | `2 * i + 2`                |

Push up:

```c id="p8_heap_sift_up"
static void heap_sift_up(struct int_heap *h, size_t i)
{
    while (i > 0) {
        size_t parent = (i - 1) / 2;

        if (h->data[parent] <= h->data[i]) {
            break;
        }

        int tmp = h->data[parent];
        h->data[parent] = h->data[i];
        h->data[i] = tmp;

        i = parent;
    }
}
```

Heap design choices:

| Choice                         | Consequence                                       |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Min-heap vs max-heap           | Comparator direction                              |
| Inline values vs pointers      | Ownership and copying                             |
| Fixed capacity vs growable     | Failure and allocation model                      |
| Custom comparator              | Function pointer overhead or macro specialization |
| Stable priority for equal keys | Need sequence number                              |
| Decrease-key support           | Requires index handles or additional map          |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                          | Why it matters                 | Better rule                   |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------ | ----------------------------- |
| Child index overflow                             | Large heaps                    | Check or use bounds carefully |
| Comparator not strict/consistent                 | Heap invariant unstable        | Central comparator            |
| Returning pointer to internal array then pushing | `realloc` invalidates pointer  | Document invalidation         |
| Forgetting capacity growth failure               | Push can fail                  | Return status                 |
| Assuming heap iteration is sorted                | Heap array is not fully sorted | Pop repeatedly or sort        |

### AST and Node-Graph Idioms — representing structured language data

Abstract syntax trees and node graphs are common in compilers, interpreters, config parsers, and analyzers. C designs usually use tagged structs, pointers to child nodes, and arena allocation or explicit destructors.

Expression node:

```c id="p8_ast_node"
enum expr_kind {
    EXPR_INT,
    EXPR_ADD,
    EXPR_NAME
};

struct expr {
    enum expr_kind kind;
    union {
        long int_value;

        struct {
            struct expr *left;
            struct expr *right;
        } add;

        struct string_view name;
    } as;
};
```

Constructor:

```c id="p8_ast_constructor"
struct expr *expr_new_add(struct expr *left, struct expr *right)
{
    struct expr *e = malloc(sizeof *e);

    if (e == NULL) {
        return NULL;
    }

    e->kind = EXPR_ADD;
    e->as.add.left = left;
    e->as.add.right = right;
    return e;
}
```

Destructor:

```c id="p8_ast_destroy"
void expr_destroy(struct expr *e)
{
    if (e == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    switch (e->kind) {
    case EXPR_ADD:
        expr_destroy(e->as.add.left);
        expr_destroy(e->as.add.right);
        break;

    case EXPR_INT:
    case EXPR_NAME:
        break;
    }

    free(e);
}
```

This assumes tree ownership: each child has exactly one parent. If nodes are shared in a DAG, recursive destroy can double free. In that case, reference counting, arena lifetime, or graph-aware cleanup is required.

AST design table:

| Design issue             | Common solution                     |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Many nodes same lifetime | Arena allocation                    |
| Tree ownership           | Parent owns children                |
| Shared nodes             | Reference counting or arena         |
| Source locations         | Store line/column/span              |
| Node variants            | Tagged union                        |
| Visitor operations       | Switch or function table            |
| Deep recursion risk      | Iterative traversal or depth limits |
| Error recovery           | Error nodes or partial AST policy   |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters         | Better rule                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Recursive destroy on shared graph                 | Double free            | Define tree vs DAG ownership                  |
| Deep recursive traversal                          | Stack overflow         | Enforce depth or use iterative traversal      |
| Missing tag handling                              | Invalid variant access | Exhaustive switch policy                      |
| Name string view points to temporary lexer buffer | Dangling view          | Intern/copy names or keep source buffer alive |
| Arena reset while AST still used                  | Dangling pointers      | Tie AST lifetime to arena                     |

### Reference Counting Idiom — shared ownership by convention

Reference counting is a manual shared-ownership pattern. It can be useful, but it adds complexity and does not automatically solve cycles or thread safety.

```c id="p8_refcounted"
struct object {
    unsigned refcount;
    char *name;
};
```

Retain:

```c id="p8_retain"
struct object *object_retain(struct object *obj)
{
    if (obj != NULL) {
        obj->refcount++;
    }

    return obj;
}
```

Release:

```c id="p8_release"
void object_release(struct object *obj)
{
    if (obj == NULL) {
        return;
    }

    if (obj->refcount == 0) {
        abort(); /* internal bug */
    }

    obj->refcount--;

    if (obj->refcount == 0) {
        free(obj->name);
        free(obj);
    }
}
```

Thread-safe reference counting requires atomics or external locking, and even then object lifetime races are subtle.

Reference counting decision table:

| Use reference counting when           | Avoid when                          |
| ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Shared ownership is real              | Single owner suffices               |
| Object graph is mostly acyclic        | Cycles are common                   |
| Lifetime is hard to centralize        | Ownership can be lexical/contextual |
| API users need independent references | Borrowed references are enough      |
| Destruction timing matters            | Arena/free-all lifetime is suitable |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters              | Better rule                           |
| ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Cycles                                   | Refcount never reaches zero | Use weak references or explicit break |
| Non-atomic refcount across threads       | Data race                   | Use locking/atomics                   |
| Retain after object may already be freed | Use-after-free              | Need lifetime protocol                |
| Missing retain on stored reference       | Dangling pointer            | Retain when storing shared ownership  |
| Overusing refcount                       | Complexity                  | Prefer clear ownership where possible |

### Error-Handling Pattern Catalog — choosing the right failure shape

C error patterns should be consistent inside a module.

| Pattern                      | Example                  | Best use                  |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------- |
| `0` success, `-1` failure    | `return -1;`             | Simple internal APIs      |
| Error enum                   | `PARSE_ERR_RANGE`        | Public known error family |
| Status plus output           | `int parse(T *out)`      | Fallible value production |
| Nullable pointer             | `find` returns `NULL`    | Optional object lookup    |
| `create(T **out)`            | Allocate object          | Fallible ownership return |
| Caller-provided error object | `struct error *err`      | Rich diagnostics          |
| Preserve `errno`             | save `errno` immediately | OS/libc boundary          |
| Cleanup label                | `goto cleanup`           | Multiple resources        |
| Fatal/assert                 | `assert(false)`          | Internal impossible state |

Pattern combination:

```c id="p8_error_combo"
int config_load(const char *path,
                struct config *out,
                struct error *err)
{
    FILE *fp = NULL;
    int rc = -1;

    if (path == NULL || out == NULL) {
        error_set(err, CONFIG_ERR_ARG, "invalid argument");
        return -1;
    }

    fp = fopen(path, "rb");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        error_set(err, CONFIG_ERR_IO, "cannot open file");
        return -1;
    }

    if (config_parse_file(fp, out, err) != 0) {
        goto cleanup;
    }

    rc = 0;

cleanup:
    if (fclose(fp) != 0 && rc == 0) {
        error_set(err, CONFIG_ERR_IO, "cannot close file");
        rc = -1;
    }

    return rc;
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters         | Better rule                                |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Multiple error conventions in one module | Callers misuse results | Standardize                                |
| Output not initialized on failure        | Caller reads garbage   | Define failure output state                |
| Error object mandatory for simple API    | Boilerplate            | Allow `NULL` error sink if practical       |
| Logging in low-level library             | Duplicated diagnostics | Return errors; log at application boundary |
| Ignoring cleanup failure                 | Lost I/O errors        | Define cleanup failure precedence          |

### Initialization Pattern Catalog — zero, explicit init, and valid empty state

Initialization is central in C because uninitialized objects have indeterminate values. Patterns should make valid states easy to obtain and cleanup safe.

| Pattern                      | Example                         | Use                                     |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Zero-is-valid                | `struct vec v = {0};`           | Simple structs where zero means empty   |
| Explicit init                | `vec_init(&v);`                 | Nontrivial invariants                   |
| Designated initializer       | `.field = value`                | Public value structs/options            |
| Factory/create               | `obj_create(&obj)`              | Opaque/heap objects                     |
| Static initializer macro     | `VEC_INIT`                      | Constants or local init                 |
| Init with cleanup on failure | `init` sets neutral state first | Partial initialization                  |
| Lazy init                    | initialize on first use         | Global/cache-like state, thread caveats |

Zero-is-valid vector:

```c id="p8_zero_valid"
struct byte_vec v = {0};
```

This works only if the module defines `{0}` as a valid empty vector.

Static initializer macro:

```c id="p8_static_init_macro"
#define BYTE_VEC_INIT { NULL, 0, 0 }

struct byte_vec v = BYTE_VEC_INIT;
```

Explicit init is clearer when invariants are not obvious.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                       | Why it matters     | Better rule                                            |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Assuming zero is valid without module promise | Invalid state      | Document zero-valid types                              |
| `init` fails after partially writing fields   | Cleanup unsafe     | Establish neutral state first                          |
| Double init without deinit                    | Leak old resources | Define whether reinit allowed                          |
| Destroy uninitialized object                  | UB or invalid free | Initialize before destroy or use zero-valid convention |
| Static local lazy init in threaded code       | Race               | Use proper once mechanism                              |

### Cleanup Pattern Catalog — local cleanup, object cleanup, and failure paths

Cleanup idioms should make partial failure safe and reviewable.

| Cleanup pattern               | Shape                          | Use                                            |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Single `cleanup` label        | `goto cleanup;`                | Multiple resources                             |
| Multiple labels               | `goto fail_buf;` etc.          | Low-level code with strict reverse acquisition |
| Parent destructor             | `parent_deinit` cleans members | Aggregates                                     |
| Null-tolerant destroy         | `destroy(NULL)` allowed        | Easier cleanup                                 |
| Reset-to-empty after destroy  | fields set neutral             | Avoid accidental reuse                         |
| Scope-local immediate cleanup | release as soon as done        | Short resource lifetime                        |
| Cleanup stack/list            | register cleanup callbacks     | Arena/resource batch                           |
| Compiler cleanup attribute    | extension                      | Non-ISO projects only                          |

Multiple-label style:

```c id="p8_multi_label_cleanup"
int f(void)
{
    char *a = malloc(100);
    if (a == NULL) {
        return -1;
    }

    char *b = malloc(200);
    if (b == NULL) {
        goto fail_a;
    }

    char *c = malloc(300);
    if (c == NULL) {
        goto fail_b;
    }

    use(a, b, c);

    free(c);
    free(b);
    free(a);
    return 0;

fail_b:
    free(b);
fail_a:
    free(a);
    return -1;
}
```

Single-label style is usually easier when resources are initialized to neutral values. Multiple labels can be useful in low-level code but become error-prone if overused.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters           | Better rule                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| Jumping into scope with uninitialized variables        | Invalid control flow/use | Only jump forward to cleanup     |
| Cleanup label uses uninitialized resource              | UB                       | Initialize to neutral value      |
| Destroy order wrong                                    | Dependency bug           | Release reverse dependency order |
| Cleanup hides main logic                               | Readability              | Keep resource lifetimes narrow   |
| Overusing compiler cleanup extensions in portable code | Non-ISO                  | Isolate extension use            |

### Module Layout Idiom — public header, private header, implementation, tests

A common professional layout:

```text id="p8_module_layout"
include/
    project/foo.h
src/
    foo.c
    foo_internal.h
tests/
    foo_test.c
```

Public header:

```c id="p8_public_header_pattern"
#ifndef PROJECT_FOO_H
#define PROJECT_FOO_H

#include <stddef.h>

typedef struct project_foo ProjectFoo;

int project_foo_create(ProjectFoo **out);
void project_foo_destroy(ProjectFoo *foo);

#endif
```

Implementation:

```c id="p8_impl_pattern"
#include "project/foo.h"
#include "foo_internal.h"

struct project_foo {
    size_t count;
};
```

Test:

```c id="p8_test_public"
#include "project/foo.h"

static void test_create_destroy(void)
{
    ProjectFoo *foo = NULL;

    CHECK(project_foo_create(&foo) == 0);
    CHECK(foo != NULL);
    project_foo_destroy(foo);
}
```

Module design rules:

| Rule                                   | Why                                       |
| -------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Public header self-contained           | Clients can include it directly           |
| Implementation includes its own header | Checks declaration/definition consistency |
| Private helpers `static` unless shared | Avoid symbol pollution                    |
| Internal header not installed          | Prevent client dependency on internals    |
| Tests mostly public API                | Avoid overfitting implementation          |
| Internal tests explicit                | Useful for complex invariants             |
| Feature macros centralized             | Avoid inconsistent translation units      |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                        | Better rule                |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| `.c` file does not include its public header | Definition may drift from declaration | Always include own header  |
| Public header includes private header        | Internals leak                        | Separate public/private    |
| Generic names exported                       | Symbol collision                      | Prefix public names        |
| Test only internal fields                    | Refactor breaks tests unnecessarily   | Test behavior primarily    |
| Header requires include order                | Fragile API                           | Make header self-contained |

### Platform Adapter Idiom — isolating non-ISO behavior

When code needs POSIX, Windows, compiler extensions, or hardware APIs, isolate them behind a project-level interface.

Public/internal adapter header:

```c id="p8_platform_header"
#ifndef PLATFORM_TIME_H
#define PLATFORM_TIME_H

#include <stdint.h>

int platform_monotonic_ns(uint64_t *out_ns);

#endif
```

POSIX implementation:

```c id="p8_platform_posix"
int platform_monotonic_ns(uint64_t *out_ns)
{
    /* clock_gettime-based implementation, POSIX-specific */
}
```

Other implementation:

```c id="p8_platform_other"
int platform_monotonic_ns(uint64_t *out_ns)
{
    /* Windows or embedded implementation */
}
```

Adapter benefits:

| Benefit                       | Explanation                            |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Portability logic centralized | Fewer scattered `#ifdef`s              |
| Core code cleaner             | Uses project abstraction               |
| Testing easier                | Mock adapter possible                  |
| Layer labeled                 | ISO vs POSIX vs OS distinction visible |
| Future ports easier           | Add implementation file                |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters            | Better rule                            |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Platform `#ifdef`s in business logic              | Hard to test and maintain | Adapter layer                          |
| Adapter exposes native handles unnecessarily      | Leaks platform dependency | Use opaque wrapper                     |
| Adapter hides important semantics                 | Wrong assumptions         | Document blocking, errors, time units  |
| Different implementations have different behavior | Portability bug           | Define common contract                 |
| Feature detection by OS macro only                | Capability may vary       | Use build-system checks where possible |

### Defensive Programming Idioms — checks, assertions, and fail-closed behavior

Defensive C code distinguishes external validation from internal invariant checks.

| Check type                 | Mechanism                    | Use                         |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Public argument validation | `if` return error            | API robustness              |
| Internal invariant         | `assert`                     | Programmer bug detection    |
| Boundary validation        | length/range/tag checks      | Security                    |
| Compile-time invariant     | static assertion             | Layout/constant assumptions |
| Runtime fail-closed        | return error or abort safely | Corrupted state             |
| Sanitizer-assisted check   | debug/test builds            | Dynamic defect detection    |

Example:

```c id="p8_defensive_append"
int buffer_append(Buffer *buf, const unsigned char *data, size_t n)
{
    if (buf == NULL || (data == NULL && n != 0)) {
        return -1;
    }

    assert(buffer_invariant_holds(buf));

    if (n > SIZE_MAX - buffer_size(buf)) {
        return -1;
    }

    return buffer_append_checked(buf, data, n);
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                        | Why it matters      | Better rule                              |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Assertion for untrusted input                                  | Disabled in release | Runtime validation                       |
| Runtime check for impossible internal state with no diagnostic | Bugs hidden         | Assert/log internal corruption           |
| Continuing after invariant corruption                          | More severe damage  | Fail closed                              |
| Defensive checks everywhere without clear contract             | Noise               | Check at boundaries and invariant points |
| Removing checks for performance without proof                  | Security regression | Profile and preserve boundary checks     |

### Performance Idioms — clear first, then measure

Idiomatic C performance usually begins with correct data layout and ownership, not clever expressions.

| Performance goal                    | Idiom                                          | Caveat                     |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Sequential traversal                | Contiguous arrays                              | Need capacity management   |
| Avoid per-object allocation         | arena/pool                                     | Lifetime grouping          |
| Avoid repeated string scans         | string builder with `len`                      | Maintain terminator        |
| Avoid callback overhead in hot loop | type-specific function or macro-generated code | Measure first              |
| Improve branch dispatch             | table or switch                                | Depends on compiler/data   |
| Reduce copies                       | views/borrows                                  | Lifetime complexity        |
| Improve locality                    | struct layout or SoA                           | Public ABI and readability |
| Avoid lock contention               | sharding/thread-local state                    | Complexity                 |
| Avoid I/O overhead                  | buffering                                      | Flush/error handling       |
| Avoid format overhead               | structured binary/log buffering                | Debuggability              |

Example: avoid repeated `strlen` in append loop.

Bad:

```c id="p8_perf_bad_strlen"
for (size_t i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    strcat(dst, parts[i]);
}
```

Better:

```c id="p8_perf_sb"
for (size_t i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    if (sb_append_cstr(&sb, parts[i]) != 0) {
        return -1;
    }
}
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                       | Why it matters      | Better rule                                     |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Optimizing before invariants are correct                      | Fast wrong code     | Correctness first                               |
| Choosing linked list for “fast insert” but traversing heavily | Poor locality       | Measure and prefer arrays often                 |
| Exposing layout for speed prematurely                         | ABI lock-in         | Hide layout until performance requires exposure |
| Macro micro-optimization without profiling                    | Debuggability loss  | Use `static inline` or direct code              |
| Ignoring allocator cost                                       | Hot allocation path | Pool/arena after profiling                      |

### Testing Idioms — boundary tests, invariant tests, and fuzzable APIs

C APIs should be shaped to support tests. Parser functions that take `const unsigned char *data, size_t len` are easier to fuzz than functions that only read from global files. Context objects are easier to test than hidden globals.

Testable parser signature:

```c id="p8_fuzzable_parser"
int message_parse(const unsigned char *data,
                  size_t len,
                  struct message *out);
```

Fuzz harness shape:

```c id="p8_fuzz_harness"
int LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(const unsigned char *data, size_t size)
{
    struct message msg;

    if (message_parse(data, size, &msg) == 0) {
        message_destroy(&msg);
    }

    return 0;
}
```

This is a libFuzzer-specific entry point, not ISO C, but the API shape is broadly useful.

Testing pattern table:

| Code pattern          | Test strategy                                      |
| --------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `data/len/cap` buffer | Append, reserve, overflow edges, cleanup           |
| Parser cursor         | Truncated inputs, maximum lengths, fuzz            |
| Tagged union          | Each variant, invalid tag if boundary-facing       |
| Hash table            | Collision cases, resize, key ownership             |
| Arena                 | Alignment, reset invalidation policy               |
| Error object          | Failure messages and truncation                    |
| Platform adapter      | Fake or per-platform integration tests             |
| Callback API          | Null callback, retained userdata, callback failure |
| State machine         | Valid and invalid transitions                      |
| Resource cleanup      | Failure injection and sanitizer leak checks        |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                    | Better rule                |
| -------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Only happy-path tests                        | C bugs live in edge/failure paths | Test failure and cleanup   |
| Parser API tied to files only                | Hard to fuzz                      | Provide buffer parser core |
| Tests depend on private layout unnecessarily | Refactor friction                 | Test public behavior       |
| No sanitizer runs                            | Memory bugs pass tests            | Run tests under sanitizers |
| No maximum-size tests                        | Overflow/resource bugs            | Test bounds and limits     |

### Anti-Pattern Catalog — C designs that commonly fail

| Anti-pattern                                      | Why it fails                               | Better pattern                          |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------- |
| Global mutable singleton everywhere               | Non-reentrant, hard to test, thread-unsafe | Context object                          |
| Public struct with owning pointers and no policy  | Shallow-copy bugs                          | Opaque type or explicit copy/destroy    |
| `void *` generic API where only one type exists   | Loses type safety                          | Type-specific API                       |
| Macro DSL for ordinary logic                      | Hard to debug and reason                   | Functions/structs                       |
| Linked list by default                            | Poor locality/allocation overhead          | Dynamic array                           |
| Raw struct serialization                          | Padding/endian/ABI bugs                    | Explicit serialization                  |
| Error code ignored by convention                  | Silent failure                             | Consistent status checking              |
| `assert` for external input                       | Disabled in release                        | Runtime validation                      |
| Hidden allocation in read-only-looking function   | Ownership surprise                         | Naming and status return                |
| Callback retained without unregister              | Dangling context                           | Registration lifecycle                  |
| Arena used for objects with independent lifetimes | Dangling/peak memory issues                | Individual ownership                    |
| Reference counting used to avoid ownership design | Cycles and complexity                      | Clear ownership or arena                |
| Platform APIs scattered                           | Portability collapse                       | Adapter layer                           |
| Lock-free structure without expertise             | Data races/ABA/memory-order bugs           | Lock first; atomics only when justified |
| Overly clever expression code                     | Sequencing/maintenance bugs                | Statement-level clarity                 |

### Pattern Selection Matrix — choose the idiom by problem shape

| Problem shape                       | Prefer                  | Avoid                              |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Many elements, frequent traversal   | Dynamic array/vector    | Linked list                        |
| Unknown growth, append-heavy        | `data/len/cap`          | Realloc one byte/element at a time |
| Fixed-size FIFO                     | Ring buffer             | Shifting array on every pop        |
| External byte input                 | Parser cursor/view      | C string assumptions               |
| Internal variant value              | Tagged union            | Untagged union                     |
| Stateful library object             | Opaque handle           | Public mutable fields              |
| Need multiple independent instances | Context object          | Global singleton                   |
| Rich diagnostics                    | Error object            | Printing inside library            |
| Many same-lifetime allocations      | Arena                   | Individual frees everywhere        |
| Shared ownership truly required     | Refcount                | Borrowing with unclear lifetime    |
| Runtime backend variation           | Ops table               | Giant open-ended switch            |
| Closed finite behavior              | Enum + switch           | Vtable                             |
| Cross-platform OS feature           | Adapter layer           | Scattered `#ifdef`                 |
| Public many-option API              | Options struct          | Long parameter list                |
| Flags combine independently         | Bitmask                 | Enum state                         |
| Mutually exclusive state            | Enum                    | Bitmask flags                      |
| Need ABI evolution                  | Opaque/versioned struct | Public layout                      |

### Pattern Cost Model — what each idiom buys and costs

| Idiom             | Buys                            | Costs                   | Primary risk                  |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| `data/len/cap`    | Efficient growable sequence     | Manual allocation       | Overflow/realloc invalidation |
| String builder    | Efficient C string construction | Terminator invariant    | Off-by-one                    |
| View/span         | Keeps length with pointer       | Borrow lifetime         | Dangling view                 |
| Opaque object     | Invariant/ABI protection        | Boilerplate/heap        | Lifecycle ambiguity           |
| Context object    | Reentrancy/testability          | Parameter plumbing      | God-context                   |
| Error object      | Rich diagnostics                | API complexity          | Error-path allocation         |
| Tagged union      | Compact variants                | Manual tag discipline   | Wrong member access           |
| Dispatch table    | Data-driven behavior            | Indirect calls          | Bad table invariant           |
| State machine     | Explicit lifecycle              | Switch/table complexity | Invalid transitions           |
| Parser cursor     | Bounded parsing                 | More code               | Partial rollback bugs         |
| Move/take/release | Efficient transfer              | Contract complexity     | Use-after-transfer            |
| Clone             | Safe copy                       | Allocation/failure      | Partial mutation              |
| Intrusive list    | Fewer allocations               | Layout coupling         | Dangling membership           |
| Ops table         | Runtime polymorphism            | ABI/lifetime burden     | Wrong `ctx`                   |
| Arena             | Fast grouped allocation         | Coarse lifetime         | Reset dangling                |
| Handle table      | Validatable public references   | Table management        | Stale handle                  |
| Flags             | Compact options                 | Validation              | Invalid combinations          |
| Options struct    | Extensible config               | Init/version policy     | Uninitialized fields          |

### Common Review Questions — idiomatic C code audit

| Code feature               | Review question                                          |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Struct with pointer field  | Owned or borrowed? Copyable or not?                      |
| Function returning pointer | Owned, borrowed, static, or internal view?               |
| Function taking `void *`   | What type and lifetime are expected?                     |
| Dynamic array              | Are `len`, `cap`, and allocation size checked?           |
| String builder             | Is `data[len] == '\0'` always true?                      |
| View/span                  | Does referent outlive view?                              |
| Opaque type                | Is lifecycle complete and documented?                    |
| Tagged union               | Is tag checked before payload access?                    |
| Hash table                 | Who owns keys and values?                                |
| Callback                   | Synchronous or retained? May it reenter?                 |
| Cleanup path               | Are all resources initialized and released exactly once? |
| Error object               | Can error reporting itself fail?                         |
| Arena                      | What invalidates arena pointers?                         |
| Refcount                   | Are cycles possible? Is it thread-safe?                  |
| Platform call              | Is it isolated behind adapter?                           |
| Macro                      | Are arguments evaluated once? Is it type-safe enough?    |
| Public API                 | Is representation or ABI exposed unnecessarily?          |

### What to Memorize vs What to Look Up — Part 8 artifacts

| Memorize                                                       | Reason                             |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| `data/len/cap` is the core dynamic-buffer idiom                | Recurs throughout C systems        |
| `init/deinit` differs from `create/destroy`                    | Prevents invalid cleanup           |
| `goto cleanup` is a disciplined resource idiom                 | C has no RAII                      |
| Pointer-plus-length views do not own data                      | Prevents invalid free/dangling use |
| Opaque structs protect invariants and layout                   | Core abstraction pattern           |
| Tagged unions require manual tag discipline                    | Prevents invalid variant access    |
| Function pointer plus `userdata` is not a checked closure      | Callback safety                    |
| Context objects beat hidden globals for testability/reentrancy | Scalable design                    |
| Ownership transfer must be explicit with `take/release/move`   | Prevents double free               |
| Raw struct serialization is not a format                       | Portability/security               |
| Linked lists are not the default high-performance container    | Cache locality                     |
| Arena allocation trades individual free for grouped lifetime   | Lifetime clarity                   |
| Platform-specific code should be isolated                      | Portability                        |

| Look up when needed                                    | Why                           |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------- |
| Specific hash function security/performance properties | Domain-dependent              |
| Balanced tree algorithms                               | Easy to implement incorrectly |
| Lock-free queue/ring buffer algorithms                 | Memory-order subtlety         |
| `container_of` legality and compiler-extension details | Advanced low-level C          |
| ABI/versioning patterns for public ops tables          | Platform-specific             |
| Arena alignment formulas                               | Must be exact                 |
| Third-party container libraries                        | Project/ecosystem choice      |
| Fuzzer/test framework integration                      | Tool-specific                 |
| Secure parser design for complex formats               | Format-specific               |

### Part 8 Closing Mental Model — idioms are enforceable conventions around weak language guarantees

C idioms exist because C gives direct mechanisms but few automatic guards. A growable vector is not a built-in type; it is a pointer, length, capacity, and invariant. A class-like object is not a class; it is an opaque struct plus lifecycle functions. A variant is not a checked algebraic data type; it is an enum tag plus union payload. A callback is not a closure; it is a function pointer plus unchecked context. A module is not a package; it is a header, translation unit, linkage, naming, and build boundary.

The professional test for a C idiom is not whether it looks elegant. It is whether it makes the following easier to audit:

| Audit target  | Idiom must clarify                          |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Ownership     | Who frees what?                             |
| Lifetime      | How long is this pointer valid?             |
| Bounds        | What size is accessible?                    |
| Capacity      | How much can be written?                    |
| Invariant     | What must always be true?                   |
| Failure       | What happens on allocation/parse/I/O error? |
| Mutation      | What state changes?                         |
| Copying       | Is shallow copy valid?                      |
| Variant       | Which payload is active?                    |
| Threading     | Who synchronizes what?                      |
| Portability   | Which layer is this behavior from?          |
| Compatibility | What public contract is being created?      |

The compact rule is: **good C style is not minimal syntax; good C style is visible control over memory, resources, errors, and invariants.**
## PART 9 — Toolchains, Build Systems, Diagnostics, Testing, Debugging, Profiling, Portability, and Professional Workflow

### Scope of Part 9 — turning C source into reliable shipped software

Part 9 treats C as a professional engineering environment rather than only a language. C programs are shaped by compilers, linkers, build systems, warnings, sanitizers, static analyzers, debuggers, profilers, package workflows, CI, coding standards, portability policy, and release discipline.

C’s language core is small, but its professional ecosystem is large and fragmented. There is no universal C package manager, no single standard build system, no single runtime, no universal ABI, no guaranteed sanitizer availability, and no single portability target. A serious C project therefore needs explicit policy.

| Workflow task              | Common tool or practice                                | What it provides                       | What it does not guarantee            |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Compile source             | `cc`, `gcc`, `clang`, MSVC                             | Translation, diagnostics, optimization | Correctness of undefined programs     |
| Link program               | linker, compiler driver                                | Symbol resolution, binary construction | ABI compatibility by itself           |
| Build project              | Make, CMake, Meson, Ninja, Autotools                   | Dependency orchestration               | Good module design                    |
| Configure features         | config headers, feature tests                          | Platform adaptation                    | True portability without testing      |
| Diagnose source issues     | warnings, static analyzers                             | Early defect detection                 | Exhaustive proof                      |
| Detect runtime memory bugs | ASan, Valgrind-like tools                              | Dynamic memory-error detection         | Coverage of unexecuted paths          |
| Detect UB                  | UBSan, warnings, review                                | Some UB classes                        | All UB                                |
| Detect races               | TSan, review                                           | Data-race discovery                    | Complete concurrency proof            |
| Debug behavior             | `gdb`, `lldb`, IDE debugger                            | Runtime inspection                     | Valid source semantics                |
| Profile performance        | `perf`, Instruments, VTune, callgrind, profilers       | Hotspot evidence                       | Automatic optimization strategy       |
| Test behavior              | unit tests, integration tests, fuzzers                 | Regression and boundary checking       | Absence of bugs                       |
| Package dependencies       | system packages, `pkg-config`, vcpkg, Conan, vendoring | Dependency acquisition/link flags      | Universal reproducibility             |
| Release libraries          | versioning, symbol policy, ABI tests                   | Compatibility discipline               | ABI safety if layout changes casually |
| Enforce style              | formatters, linters, code review                       | Consistency and reviewability          | Semantic correctness                  |

The Part 9 rule is: **C correctness is a workflow property as much as a source-code property**. Because the language leaves many hazards unchecked, professional C relies on a deliberate toolchain and verification stack.

### Toolchain Mental Model — compiler driver, preprocessor, compiler, assembler, linker

A C “compiler command” often invokes several tools. The `cc`/`gcc`/`clang` command is commonly a **driver** that can preprocess, compile, assemble, and link.

| Stage         | Typical input             | Typical output            | Common command shape           | Failure examples                          |
| ------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------- |
