| Preprocessing | `.c` plus headers         | preprocessed source       | `cc -E file.c`                 | macro expansion surprise, missing include |
| Compilation   | preprocessed C            | assembly or object code   | `cc -S file.c`, `cc -c file.c` | type error, warning, optimization issue   |
| Assembly      | assembly                  | object file               | usually driven by compiler     | assembler syntax/target error             |
| Linking       | object files/libraries    | executable/shared library | `cc a.o b.o -o app`            | undefined reference, duplicate symbol     |
| Loading       | executable/shared objects | process image             | OS loader                      | missing shared library, ABI mismatch      |

Example commands:

```bash id="p9_toolchain_commands"
cc -E main.c -o main.i
cc -S main.c -o main.s
cc -c main.c -o main.o
cc main.o util.o -o app
```

The compiler driver should usually be used for linking C programs rather than invoking the linker directly, because the driver knows the standard runtime libraries, startup objects, and platform conventions.

| Problem                          | Likely layer           | First check                                |
| -------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `#ifdef` branch unexpected       | Preprocessor           | inspect preprocessed output                |
| Function call has wrong type     | Compilation            | header declarations                        |
| `undefined reference to foo`     | Linking                | missing object/library or wrong link order |
| `multiple definition of foo`     | Linking                | definition in header or duplicate object   |
| Program cannot find `.so`/`.dll` | Loading                | runtime library path/install layout        |
| Crash at FFI boundary            | ABI/runtime            | calling convention and type layout         |
| Works in debug, fails optimized  | Semantics/optimization | UB, aliasing, uninitialized data, race     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                          | Why it matters                     | Better rule                               |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Treating link errors as compile errors                           | Different failure layer            | Diagnose symbol definitions and libraries |
| Invoking linker directly without understanding runtime startup   | Missing startup/runtime objects    | Link through compiler driver              |
| Assuming include path and library path are the same              | Headers and binaries are distinct  | Configure both compile and link paths     |
| Mixing headers from one version with library binary from another | ABI/runtime mismatch               | Keep dependency versions synchronized     |
| Debugging macro code without preprocessing                       | Actual source differs after macros | Inspect `-E` output when needed           |

### Compiler Selection — GCC, Clang, MSVC, embedded compilers, and portability policy

C compilers share the language core but differ in diagnostics, extensions, defaults, standard support, ABI integration, sanitizer support, and target ecosystems.

| Compiler family                  | Common context                            | Strength                                   | Portability caveat                                       |
| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| GCC                              | Unix-like, embedded cross toolchains      | Mature optimization and platform support   | GCC extensions are not ISO C                             |
| Clang/LLVM                       | Unix-like, macOS, tooling-heavy workflows | Diagnostics, sanitizers, tooling           | Clang extensions/tooling differ from GCC in details      |
| MSVC                             | Windows ecosystem                         | Windows integration, MSBuild/Visual Studio | Historically different C standard support and extensions |
| TinyCC/PCC/other small compilers | Special contexts                          | Fast/simple                                | Limited diagnostics/optimization/features                |
| Vendor embedded compilers        | MCU/DSP/safety-critical targets           | Hardware integration                       | Nonstandard extensions and partial library support       |
| Cross compilers                  | Target differs from host                  | Platform builds                            | Sysroot/ABI/toolchain configuration burden               |

Compiler policy should answer:

| Policy question                    | Why it matters                        |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Which C standard mode is required? | C99, C11, C17, C23 features differ    |
| Which compilers are supported?     | Determines extensions and warnings    |
| Which warnings are mandatory?      | Keeps defect detection consistent     |
| Are compiler extensions allowed?   | Affects portability                   |
| Are sanitizers required in CI?     | Affects defect discovery              |
| Which targets are supported?       | Data model, ABI, library availability |
| Is freestanding supported?         | Standard library assumptions change   |
| Are cross builds supported?        | Build system must model host/target   |

Example policy statement:

```text id="p9_compiler_policy"
Supported language mode: C11 or later.
Supported compilers: GCC and Clang on Linux/macOS; MSVC support is optional.
Compiler extensions: allowed only inside platform/compatibility layers.
Warnings: project must build warning-clean under configured warning set.
Sanitizers: ASan/UBSan required for test builds where supported.
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters           | Better rule                           |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------- |
| Accidentally depending on one compiler extension     | Portability break        | Isolate and guard extensions          |
| Using compiler default standard mode                 | Defaults vary            | Pass explicit `-std=` or equivalent   |
| Treating all warning flags as portable               | Flags differ by compiler | Use compiler-specific flag sets       |
| Assuming embedded compiler supports hosted library   | Freestanding limitations | Check target runtime                  |
| Using new standard features without toolchain policy | Build failures           | Declare standard and compiler support |

### Warning Strategy — diagnostics as part of the contract

Compiler warnings are not cosmetic. Many warnings correspond to real C defect patterns: format mismatch, uninitialized variables, implicit conversions, missing prototypes, shadowing, strict aliasing, unreachable code, and suspicious control flow.

| Warning category            | Finds                                | Typical importance            |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------- |
| Format warnings             | `printf`/`scanf` type mismatches     | Very high                     |
| Missing prototypes          | Unchecked function declarations      | High                          |
| Conversion warnings         | Narrowing, signed/unsigned surprises | High but noisy if unmanaged   |
| Uninitialized use           | Possible indeterminate reads         | Very high                     |
| Shadowing                   | Name hiding                          | Medium/high depending project |
| Switch enum warnings        | Missing enum cases                   | High for state machines       |
| Unreachable/dead code       | Logic mistakes                       | Medium/high                   |
| Strict aliasing warnings    | Some aliasing hazards                | Medium; incomplete            |
| Pointer arithmetic warnings | Suspicious pointer use               | High in safety code           |
| Implicit fallthrough        | Missing `break`                      | High in `switch` logic        |

Example warning policy for GCC/Clang-like workflows:

```bash id="p9_warning_flags"
-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wformat=2 -Wshadow -Wconversion -Wsign-conversion -Wswitch-enum -Wstrict-prototypes -Wmissing-prototypes
```

This is illustrative, not universal. Some flags are too noisy for legacy codebases unless introduced gradually. MSVC and embedded compilers have different flag names and diagnostic models.

Warnings-as-errors policy:

```bash id="p9_werror"
-Werror
```

This can keep new warnings from entering code, but it can also break builds when compiler versions add diagnostics. A practical policy may use `-Werror` in CI for supported compiler versions and avoid it for unknown downstream compilers.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                         | Why it matters                    | Better rule                                          |
| ----------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Ignoring format warnings                        | Variadic UB risk                  | Treat as serious defects                             |
| Blanket disabling warnings                      | Hides real issues                 | Suppress narrowly with justification                 |
| Enabling all conversion warnings without policy | Noise leads to warning fatigue    | Introduce gradually and fix systematically           |
| `-Werror` for public library consumers          | New compiler warnings break users | Apply internally, not necessarily in installed flags |
| Assuming warnings prove correctness             | Warnings are incomplete           | Combine with tests, sanitizers, review               |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “Warnings are style preferences.”                                                                               |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | A `%d`/`size_t` mismatch works on one platform and fails on another.                                            |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Many warnings identify type, ABI, conversion, or control-flow hazards that C itself may not reject.             |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Treat warning-clean builds as a baseline quality gate, while remembering warnings are not proofs.               |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Legacy or platform headers may require targeted suppressions, but suppressions should be narrow and documented. |

### Optimization Builds — `-O0`, `-O1`, `-O2`, `-O3`, LTO, and debugability

Optimization changes the generated program while preserving the observable behavior of valid C programs. If behavior changes under optimization, suspect undefined behavior, unspecified-order dependence, data races, uninitialized reads, aliasing violations, or timing assumptions before blaming the optimizer.

| Build mode              | Typical purpose                     | Strength                                | Caveat                                                 |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `-O0`                   | Debugging, fast compile             | Variables easier to inspect             | Hides some optimization-sensitive UB                   |
| `-Og`                   | Debuggable optimization             | Better debugging than high optimization | Compiler-specific                                      |
| `-O1`                   | Light optimization                  | Some cleanup                            | Less common as release target                          |
| `-O2`                   | Common release optimization         | Strong general optimization             | Debugging harder                                       |
| `-O3`                   | More aggressive optimization        | May help compute-heavy code             | Can increase code size or not improve real performance |
| `-Os`/size optimization | Reduce binary size                  | Useful embedded/cache-sensitive code    | May reduce speed                                       |
| LTO                     | Cross-translation-unit optimization | Better inlining/dead-code removal       | Build/link complexity and ABI assumptions              |

Optimization is not a substitute for algorithmic design. A poor data structure remains poor at `-O3`.

Optimization review table:

| Symptom                        | Likely investigation                                                            |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Works at `-O0`, fails at `-O2` | UBSan/ASan/MSan/TSan, aliasing, uninitialized reads                             |
| Release faster but wrong       | UB or race                                                                      |
| Debugger cannot show variable  | Optimizer removed/reordered it                                                  |
| Binary too large after `-O3`   | Try `-O2`, `-Os`, inspect inlining                                              |
| LTO changes behavior           | Existing UB or ODR-like build/config mismatch                                   |
| Strict aliasing-sensitive bug  | Try sanitizers/review; do not rely only on `-fno-strict-aliasing` unless policy |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                         | Why it matters                     | Better rule                 |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Debugging only `-O0`                            | Optimization-sensitive bugs escape | Test optimized builds too   |
| Assuming `-O3` is always faster                 | Code size/cache effects            | Measure                     |
| Disabling optimization to “fix” UB              | Hides bug                          | Fix invalid source          |
| Profiling debug builds                          | Misleading costs                   | Profile release-like builds |
| Using optimization flags without recording them | Nonreproducible performance        | Version build flags         |

### Build Systems — Make, CMake, Meson, Ninja, Autotools, IDE projects

C has no standard build system. A project must choose one based on portability, dependency needs, target platforms, generated files, cross-compilation, developer familiarity, and release expectations.

| Build tool              | Common use                            | Strength                            | Caveat                                    |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Make                    | Simple Unix builds, low-level control | Ubiquitous, transparent             | Manual dependency handling can be fragile |
| Ninja                   | Fast low-level build executor         | Speed                               | Usually generated by CMake/Meson          |
| CMake                   | Cross-platform projects               | Large ecosystem and IDE integration | Language complexity, version differences  |
| Meson                   | Modern declarative builds             | Clear syntax, fast with Ninja       | Smaller ecosystem than CMake              |
| Autotools               | Portable Unix packages                | Mature feature detection            | Complex and dated workflow                |
| MSBuild/Visual Studio   | Windows-native projects               | IDE/platform integration            | Less portable                             |
| Bazel/Buck-like systems | Large monorepos                       | Hermetic/reproducible potential     | Integration overhead                      |
| Custom scripts          | Small projects                        | Simple initially                    | Often fails as project grows              |

Minimal Makefile pattern:

```makefile id="p9_makefile"
CC := cc
CFLAGS := -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -g
LDFLAGS :=

OBJS := main.o buffer.o parser.o

app: $(OBJS)
	$(CC) $(OBJS) $(LDFLAGS) -o app

%.o: %.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

clean:
	rm -f $(OBJS) app
```

This is simple but does not handle header dependencies unless extended.

CMake-style conceptual target model:

```cmake id="p9_cmake_shape"
add_library(mylib buffer.c parser.c)
target_include_directories(mylib PUBLIC include)
target_compile_features(mylib PUBLIC c_std_11)

add_executable(app main.c)
target_link_libraries(app PRIVATE mylib)
```

The target model helps attach include directories, compile definitions, and libraries to the target that needs them.

Meson-style conceptual target model:

```meson id="p9_meson_shape"
mylib = library('mylib',
  ['buffer.c', 'parser.c'],
  include_directories: include_directories('include'))

app = executable('app', 'main.c', link_with: mylib)
```

Build-system decision table:

| Project need                         | Likely fit                                                |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tiny one-platform program            | Make or simple compiler command                           |
| Cross-platform library               | CMake or Meson                                            |
| Traditional Unix source distribution | Autotools or Meson/CMake with install rules               |
| Windows-first GUI/IDE workflow       | Visual Studio/MSBuild or CMake                            |
| Embedded firmware                    | vendor build system, CMake, Make, or RTOS-specific system |
| Large monorepo                       | organization-specific build system                        |
| Package-manager integration          | CMake/Meson/pkg-config support useful                     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                              | Why it matters                           | Better rule                                  |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Build system hides compile flags                     | Hard to reproduce bugs                   | Make flags visible                           |
| Include directories global everywhere                | Header conflicts and accidental coupling | Use target-scoped includes                   |
| Link libraries globally                              | Hidden dependencies                      | Attach libraries to targets                  |
| Not tracking generated files                         | Race/stale builds                        | Model generation explicitly                  |
| No clean separation of host tools vs target binaries | Cross-build failure                      | Distinguish build machine and target machine |

### Header Dependency Tracking — avoiding stale builds

C headers are textually included. If a header changes, every translation unit depending on it may need recompilation. Build systems must track these dependencies.

| Problem                                | Cause                                              | Solution                                  |
| -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Source not rebuilt after header change | Build system did not know dependency               | Compiler-generated dependency files       |
| Too many rebuilds                      | Public headers include unnecessary private headers | Forward declarations and minimal includes |
| Circular includes                      | Headers depend on each other structurally          | Incomplete types and layering             |
| Include-order bugs                     | Header not self-contained                          | Compile header-alone test                 |
| Macro configuration mismatch           | Different files see different definitions          | Central config header                     |

GCC/Clang-style dependency generation concept:

```bash id="p9_depflags"
-MMD -MP
```

These flags are toolchain-specific but common in Make-based builds.

Header hygiene review:

| Header question                                         | Why                                |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Does it compile when included alone?                    | Self-contained contract            |
| Does it include only what public signatures require?    | Build speed and API clarity        |
| Does it expose private types/macros?                    | Avoid coupling                     |
| Does it use include guards?                             | Avoid redefinition                 |
| Does it depend on config macros?                        | Ensure consistent config inclusion |
| Does it include C++ compatibility wrappers if intended? | Interop policy                     |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                     | Why it matters           | Better rule                       |
| ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------- |
| Public header includes every dependency     | Slow builds, pollution   | Forward-declare where possible    |
| Private header installed as public          | API leakage              | Separate include paths            |
| Header uses `size_t` but omits `<stddef.h>` | Include-order dependency | Include required standard headers |
| Header defines non-`static` objects         | Multiple definitions     | Use declarations only             |
| Inconsistent feature macros before headers  | ABI/API mismatch         | Centralize configuration          |

### Configuration and Feature Detection — portable builds require evidence

Portable C projects often need to detect headers, functions, types, compiler features, library versions, endianness, atomics support, thread APIs, and platform behavior. Guessing from OS macros is sometimes necessary but often insufficient.

| Feature kind       | Detection method                   | Caveat                                          |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Header exists      | build-system compile check         | Cross-compilation needs care                    |
| Function exists    | compile/link check                 | Function may exist but have different semantics |
| Type size          | compile-time test or target config | Cannot run target binary in some cross builds   |
| Compiler attribute | feature macro or compile check     | Compiler-specific                               |
| Library version    | package metadata or header macro   | Header/library mismatch possible                |
| Endianness         | platform macro/config/test         | Runtime vs compile-time target                  |
| POSIX feature      | feature-test macros and checks     | API availability varies                         |
| C standard feature | compiler mode and feature check    | Standard support incomplete                     |

Generated config header pattern:

```c id="p9_config_h"
#ifndef PROJECT_CONFIG_H
#define PROJECT_CONFIG_H

#define PROJECT_HAVE_POSIX_MMAP 1
#define PROJECT_HAVE_STDATOMIC 1
#define PROJECT_WORDS_BIGENDIAN 0

#endif
```

Usage:

```c id="p9_config_use"
#include "project_config.h"

#if PROJECT_HAVE_POSIX_MMAP
/* mmap-based implementation */
#else
/* fallback implementation */
#endif
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                       | Better rule                               |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------- |
| Assuming Linux means every POSIX extension exists | Distribution/libc differences        | Feature-test actual capability            |
| Running feature tests during cross-compilation    | Built test may not run on build host | Use compile-only tests or toolchain files |
| Scattered `#ifdef __linux__`                      | Portability sprawl                   | Centralize platform decisions             |
| Config header included inconsistently             | Different translation units disagree | Include config first where needed         |
| Header/library version mismatch                   | Compile succeeds, runtime fails      | Verify both compile and link dependencies |

### Cross-Compilation — build, host, target, sysroot, and ABI discipline

Cross-compilation means building code on one machine to run on another. It requires strict separation of the machine doing the build and the machine that will run the output.

| Term           | Meaning                                                                                      |
| -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Build machine  | Where the build tools run                                                                    |
| Host machine   | Where the produced program will run                                                          |
| Target machine | For compilers, sometimes the machine generated code targets; terminology varies by toolchain |
| Sysroot        | Root filesystem-like directory for target headers/libraries                                  |
| Toolchain file | Build-system description of compiler, sysroot, flags                                         |
| Emulator       | Runs target binaries during tests/config checks                                              |
| ABI triple     | Architecture/vendor/OS/libc target identity                                                  |

Cross-build concerns:

| Concern                                                            | Why it matters                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Headers must match target libraries                                | ABI compatibility                   |
| Cannot run target binaries on build machine unless emulator exists | Configure tests must be cross-aware |
| `sizeof(long)` may differ                                          | Data model changes                  |
| Endianness may differ                                              | Serialization/tests                 |
| Alignment may differ                                               | Layout and pointer casts            |
| Standard library may be partial                                    | Embedded/freestanding               |
| Compiler builtins may differ                                       | Intrinsics and atomics              |
| Test execution needs target/emulator/device                        | CI complexity                       |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                     | Why it matters                | Better rule                                   |
| ----------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Using host headers with target compiler                     | ABI mismatch                  | Use sysroot                                   |
| Configure script runs target executable on host             | Fails or gives wrong result   | Cross-aware feature detection                 |
| Assuming same data model as developer machine               | Integer/pointer widths differ | Test target assumptions                       |
| Linking host library into target binary                     | Invalid binary                | Separate host/target dependency paths         |
| Building generator tool for target when it must run on host | Build failure                 | Distinguish build tools from target artifacts |

### Static Libraries, Shared Libraries, and Linking Policy

C libraries can be built and consumed as static archives or shared libraries. Each choice affects deployment, ABI compatibility, symbol visibility, startup, and updates.

| Library form      | Typical extension                | Strength                                 | Caveat                                                          |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Static library    | `.a`, `.lib`                     | Simple deployment, no runtime dependency | Larger binaries, link-order issues, license/update implications |
| Shared library    | `.so`, `.dylib`, `.dll`          | Shared updates, smaller executables      | ABI stability and runtime loading issues                        |
| Object library    | `.o`, build-system object target | Internal build reuse                     | Not usually public distribution                                 |
| Header-only style | macros/static inline             | Easy include                             | Code bloat/macro pollution                                      |
| Plugin module     | shared object loaded dynamically | Extensibility                            | ABI/versioning and loader errors                                |

Linking concerns:

| Concern                |                     Static |                               Shared |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------: | -----------------------------------: |
| Link order             |            Often important |        Less often but still possible |
| ABI stability          | Less for fully rebuilt app |                             Critical |
| Symbol visibility      |              Still matters |                       Very important |
| Runtime availability   |          bundled in binary |             loader must find library |
| Updates                |   requires relink/redeploy | can update library if ABI compatible |
| Duplicate dependencies |                   possible |                             possible |
| License implications   |           project-specific |                     project-specific |

Symbol visibility policy:

| Symbol category      | Recommended handling                   |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Public API functions | Export intentionally                   |
| Internal helpers     | `static` or hidden visibility          |
| Global data          | Avoid exporting unless necessary       |
| Plugin entry points  | Export stable names                    |
| Third-party symbols  | Avoid accidental re-export if possible |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                 | Better rule                                 |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Exporting every non-static function          | ABI surface grows accidentally | Hide internal symbols                       |
| Changing shared-library struct layout        | Breaks old binaries            | Use opaque/versioned structs                |
| Static link order wrong                      | Undefined references           | Understand target linker or build system    |
| Relying on runtime library path accidentally | Deployment failure             | Define install/runtime path policy          |
| Mixing debug/release library ABIs            | Runtime corruption             | Keep build variants compatible or separated |

### ABI Compatibility Checks — public C APIs are binary contracts

A C library’s ABI includes function signatures, calling conventions, struct layout, enum sizes, symbol names, global variables, alignment, and sometimes macro-generated expectations.

| ABI surface                 | Risk                                           |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Exported function signature | Changing parameters/return breaks callers      |
| Public struct layout        | Field changes break binary clients             |
| Public enum representation  | Compiler/ABI assumptions                       |
| Public global variables     | Layout and synchronization issues              |
| Inline functions/macros     | Source compatibility rather than binary symbol |
| Callback signatures         | Plugin ABI break                               |
| Ops tables                  | Layout/version break                           |
| Allocator ownership         | Cross-library free mismatch                    |
| Error-code values           | Semantic compatibility                         |
| Symbol versioning           | Platform-specific compatibility management     |

ABI-safe patterns:

| Pattern                                          | Why                               |
| ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------- |
| Opaque handles                                   | Hide layout                       |
| Versioned options structs                        | Add fields safely                 |
| Size/version fields in ops tables                | Detect ABI version                |
| Add new functions instead of changing signatures | Preserve old callers              |
| Avoid public global variables                    | Reduces ABI and threading hazards |
| Use fixed-width types where binary width matters | Avoid data-model surprises        |
| Provide destroy/free functions                   | Avoid allocator mismatch          |
| Symbol visibility control                        | Export only intended ABI          |

Example versioned ops table:

```c id="p9_versioned_ops"
struct plugin_ops {
    size_t struct_size;
    int (*init)(void *ctx);
    int (*run)(void *ctx);
    void (*destroy)(void *ctx);
};
```

The callee can inspect `struct_size` before reading later fields.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                       | Why it matters         | Better rule                                     |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Adding field to public struct used by old binaries            | ABI break              | Opaque or versioned struct                      |
| Changing callback signature                                   | Breaks plugins         | Add new versioned interface                     |
| Returning allocated memory freed by caller with wrong runtime | Heap mismatch          | Provide library-specific free                   |
| Exposing `long` in cross-platform ABI                         | Width varies           | Use fixed-width or documented ABI-specific type |
| Assuming source-compatible means ABI-compatible               | Old binaries may break | Test ABI separately                             |

### Dependency Discovery and Package Integration — headers, libraries, versions

C dependency integration must locate headers, libraries, compiler flags, link flags, and runtime dependencies. There is no universal solution.

| Mechanism              | Common context                  | Strength                   | Caveat                      |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------- | -------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| System package manager | Linux/BSD/macOS distributions   | Easy install               | Version/platform variation  |
| `pkg-config`           | Unix-like development libraries | Provides cflags/libs       | Not universal               |
| CMake package config   | CMake ecosystem                 | Target-based integration   | Package quality varies      |
| Meson dependency       | Meson ecosystem                 | Good feature detection     | Ecosystem-specific          |
| vcpkg                  | Cross-platform packages         | Windows/CMake friendly     | Tooling dependency          |
| Conan                  | C/C++ package manager           | Versioned dependency graph | Setup complexity            |
| Vendoring              | Include source in project       | Reproducibility/control    | Update/security burden      |
| Git submodule          | Source dependency               | Version pinning            | Workflow friction           |
| Manual paths           | Small local build               | Simple                     | Fragile and nonreproducible |

`pkg-config` concept:

```bash id="p9_pkg_config"
cc main.c $(pkg-config --cflags --libs libcurl) -o app
```

Target-based build systems model dependency usage more safely than global flags.

Dependency policy should state:

| Question                                      | Why                              |
| --------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Are dependencies vendored or system-provided? | Reproducibility vs maintenance   |
| What versions are supported?                  | ABI/API stability                |
| Are static and shared builds supported?       | Deployment differences           |
| Are dependencies optional?                    | Feature flags and fallback paths |
| How are security updates tracked?             | Vulnerability management         |
| Are licenses compatible?                      | Legal release requirement        |
| Is cross-compilation supported?               | Sysroot and package discovery    |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters         | Better rule                           |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Header found, wrong library linked                | ABI mismatch           | Use package metadata/targets          |
| Static and shared dependencies mixed accidentally | Link/runtime issues    | Define linkage policy                 |
| Vendored dependency never updated                 | Security debt          | Track upstream versions               |
| Relying on developer machine paths                | Nonreproducible builds | Use build-system dependency discovery |
| Optional dependency changes public ABI silently   | Compatibility issue    | Make feature macros/API explicit      |

### Formatting and Style Tools — consistency as review infrastructure

Formatting does not guarantee correctness, but consistent formatting reduces review noise and makes control flow, ownership, and declarations easier to audit.

| Tool/practice             | Purpose                  | Caveat                         |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------ |
| `clang-format`            | Automatic formatting     | Style file must be agreed      |
| `indent`/other formatters | Formatting               | Tool-specific behavior         |
| EditorConfig              | Basic editor consistency | Limited C semantics            |
| Naming conventions        | API readability          | Must match project             |
| Include ordering          | Dependency clarity       | Avoid mechanical churn         |
| Comment conventions       | Contract documentation   | Comments must stay accurate    |
| Pre-commit hooks          | Local quality gate       | Should not be too slow         |
| CI formatting check       | Enforce consistency      | Requires clear version pinning |

Style policy examples:

| Style area                | Typical decision                                                         |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Brace placement           | Project convention                                                       |
| Pointer `*` binding style | `int *p` vs `int* p`; be consistent                                      |
| Type names                | `_t` suffix may be reserved in POSIX contexts; choose project convention |
| Public prefix             | Required for exported names                                              |
| Macro names               | Uppercase project-prefixed                                               |
| Internal functions        | `static` and possibly module prefix                                      |
| Error return convention   | Uniform within module                                                    |
| Ownership verbs           | `create/destroy`, `init/deinit`, `take/release`                          |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                 | Why it matters       | Better rule                                      |
| --------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Style debates replacing semantic review | Wastes review effort | Automate formatting                              |
| Inconsistent pointer declaration style  | Misread declarations | One pointer per declaration or strong convention |
| Generic public names                    | Symbol collisions    | Prefix public API                                |
| Comments repeat code but omit contracts | Low value            | Document ownership, lifetime, failure            |
| Formatter version drift                 | CI churn             | Pin formatter version where possible             |

### Static Analysis — path reasoning beyond compiler warnings

Static analyzers inspect source without executing it. They can find null dereferences, leaks, double frees, use-after-free paths, uninitialized values, dead stores, API misuse, and some security issues.

| Analyzer type                    | Examples                                    | Strength                    | Caveat                          |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Compiler static analyzer         | Clang Static Analyzer, GCC analyzer         | Integrated path checks      | False positives/coverage limits |
| Standalone analyzers             | cppcheck, commercial tools                  | Specialized checks          | Configuration effort            |
| Lint-like tools                  | clang-tidy-like checks                      | Style/API patterns          | C/C++ focus varies              |
| Security analyzers               | CodeQL-like systems                         | Queryable patterns          | Requires rule quality           |
| Formal/static verification tools | Frama-C-like tools, abstract interpretation | Stronger reasoning possible | Annotation and expertise burden |

Static-analysis use cases:

| Code area            | Useful checks                           |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Error paths          | leaks, double cleanup                   |
| Pointer-heavy code   | null/dangling paths                     |
| Allocation logic     | ownership and leaks                     |
| Format strings       | mismatch and injection                  |
| Integer bounds       | overflow/truncation                     |
| Concurrency wrappers | lock/unlock paths                       |
| API use              | custom checkers for project conventions |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                             | Why it matters           | Better rule                            |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | -------------------------------------- |
| Ignoring analyzer due to some false positives       | Real bugs missed         | Triage and tune                        |
| Blanket suppressions                                | Hides defects            | Suppress narrowly                      |
| Running analyzer only once                          | Regressions enter later  | Integrate into CI                      |
| Treating analyzer as proof                          | It models approximations | Combine with tests/tools               |
| Not teaching analyzer project ownership conventions | Noisy results            | Use annotations/config where available |

### Sanitizers — dynamic detection of memory, UB, and race defects

Sanitizers instrument compiled programs to detect classes of bugs during execution. They are highly valuable for C but depend on compiler/runtime support and executed test coverage.

| Sanitizer                        | Finds                                                                  | Typical use                              |
| -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| AddressSanitizer                 | out-of-bounds, use-after-free, double free, some leaks                 | default test build for memory-heavy code |
| UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer       | signed overflow, invalid shifts, misalignment, some invalid operations | semantic bug detection                   |
| MemorySanitizer                  | uninitialized reads                                                    | advanced Linux/Clang-heavy workflows     |
| ThreadSanitizer                  | data races                                                             | threaded code                            |
| LeakSanitizer                    | leaks                                                                  | often part of ASan on some platforms     |
| Control-flow/safe-stack variants | specific hardening/debugging                                           | toolchain-specific                       |

Typical sanitizer build concept:

```bash id="p9_sanitizer_flags"
cc -g -O1 -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer \
   main.c buffer.c parser.c -o app_san
```

Toolchain support and exact flags vary.

Sanitizer strategy:

| Target                          | Recommended run                                         |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Unit tests                      | ASan + UBSan                                            |
| Parser/fuzzer                   | ASan + UBSan, sometimes MSan                            |
| Threaded tests                  | TSan separately                                         |
| Release candidate               | Sanitized test suite plus optimized non-sanitized suite |
| Embedded target                 | Host-side tests where possible; target tools vary       |
| Third-party library integration | Sanitized builds if dependencies compatible             |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                           | Why it matters                            | Better rule                         |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Running sanitizers only on trivial tests          | Low coverage                              | Combine with fuzzing and edge tests |
| Combining incompatible sanitizers blindly         | Some cannot be combined                   | Use separate jobs                   |
| Profiling sanitizer build                         | Instrumentation distorts performance      | Profile release-like build          |
| Ignoring sanitizer report due to “works normally” | Sanitizer likely found real UB/memory bug | Fix source                          |
| Shipping sanitizer runtime accidentally           | Deployment issue unless intentional       | Separate build configurations       |

Failure-driven explanation:

| Step                            | Explanation                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tempting but wrong mental model | “If ASan finds nothing, memory safety is proven.”                                                                                |
| Surprising behavior or bug      | Production crashes on input path never exercised under ASan.                                                                     |
| Correct semantic explanation    | Sanitizers detect bugs on executed paths under their instrumentation model.                                                      |
| Professional rule of thumb      | Use sanitizers as a high-value defect detector, not as a proof system.                                                           |
| Boundary where the rule changes | Exhaustive formal verification or complete bounded exploration can prove more, but ordinary sanitizer testing is not exhaustive. |

### Fuzzing — input-driven discovery of boundary bugs

Fuzzing generates many inputs to test parsers, decoders, protocol handlers, file readers, and other boundary-facing code. C benefits strongly from fuzzing because malformed inputs often expose bounds, integer, state-machine, and cleanup bugs.

| Fuzzing target        | Good API shape                                      | Why                                 |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Binary parser         | `parse(const unsigned char *data, size_t len, ...)` | Direct byte input                   |
| Text parser           | pointer plus length                                 | Avoids null-termination assumptions |
| File format reader    | buffer parser core plus file wrapper                | Fuzzer avoids filesystem overhead   |
| Compression/decoder   | byte-span input                                     | Boundary-heavy                      |
| State machine         | sequence-of-events input                            | Transition validation               |
| Serializer round-trip | encode/decode property                              | Invariant checking                  |
| Public API            | structured random calls                             | Lifecycle bugs                      |

libFuzzer-style harness shape:

```c id="p9_fuzz_harness"
int LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(const unsigned char *data, size_t size)
{
    struct message msg;

    if (message_parse(data, size, &msg) == 0) {
        message_destroy(&msg);
    }

    return 0;
}
```

This is tool-specific, but the API shape is broadly good: no globals, no required files, explicit byte buffer, cleanup on success.

Fuzzing strategy:

| Practice                              | Reason                           |
| ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Run with ASan/UBSan                   | Crashes become actionable        |
| Add seed corpus                       | Improves coverage                |
| Add dictionary for structured formats | Helps reach deeper parser states |
| Keep harness deterministic            | Reproducibility                  |
| Avoid excessive logging               | Fuzzing performance              |
| Check invariants after parse          | Finds semantic bugs              |
| Minimize crashing inputs              | Easier debugging                 |
| Run continuously for boundary code    | Regression detection             |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                | Why it matters                | Better rule                             |
| -------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Fuzzing only file wrapper              | Slow and noisy                | Fuzz parser core                        |
| Leaking on every fuzz iteration        | Fuzzer slows or reports noise | Cleanup every successful partial object |
| Treating timeout as irrelevant         | Algorithmic complexity bug    | Investigate pathological inputs         |
| Ignoring non-crash wrong acceptance    | Security logic bug            | Add semantic assertions                 |
| Harness uses undefined behavior itself | False findings                | Keep harness simple and checked         |

### Unit, Integration, System, and Regression Testing — layered validation

C testing should be layered because different failures appear at different scales.

| Test layer       | Scope                              | Finds                             |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Unit test        | One function/module                | Local logic and edge cases        |
| Invariant test   | Internal data structure properties | Representation bugs               |
| Integration test | Modules together                   | Boundary mismatch                 |
| System test      | Whole executable/library behavior  | Real workflow failures            |
| Regression test  | Specific past bug                  | Prevents recurrence               |
| Fuzz test        | Many generated inputs              | Boundary and parser bugs          |
| ABI/API test     | Public compatibility               | Release stability                 |
| Performance test | Throughput/latency/resource use    | Regressions                       |
| Stress test      | Long-running/high-load behavior    | Leaks, races, resource exhaustion |

Minimal test harness:

```c id="p9_test_harness"
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

#define CHECK(expr) do {                                              \
    if (!(expr)) {                                                    \
        fprintf(stderr, "%s:%d: check failed: %s\n",                  \
                __FILE__, __LINE__, #expr);                           \
        abort();                                                      \
    }                                                                 \
} while (0)

static void test_buffer_empty(void)
{
    struct byte_vec v = {0};

    CHECK(v.len == 0);
    CHECK(byte_vec_append(&v, (const unsigned char *)"a", 1) == 0);
    CHECK(v.len == 1);

    byte_vec_deinit(&v);
}

int main(void)
{
    test_buffer_empty();
    return 0;
}
```

Test design table:

| Code type      | Required edge tests                                          |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Dynamic array  | zero length, growth, overflow, realloc failure if injectable |
| String builder | terminator, truncation, large append, embedded zero policy   |
| Parser         | empty, truncated, malformed, maximum, random                 |
| Hash table     | collisions, resize, deletion, key ownership                  |
| File I/O       | missing file, short read/write, close failure if mockable    |
| Error API      | output state on failure                                      |
| Callback API   | null callback, callback failure, retained userdata           |
| Threaded code  | concurrent access, lifetime shutdown                         |
| FFI boundary   | ABI version mismatch, null handles                           |
| Public headers | compile standalone                                           |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                      | Why it matters                    | Better rule                      |
| -------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Tests only happy path                        | Failure paths rot                 | Test invalid and edge cases      |
| No cleanup in tests                          | Sanitizer noise and real leaks    | Destroy every object             |
| Tests depend on private fields unnecessarily | Refactor pain                     | Prefer public behavior tests     |
| Random tests without seed recording          | Nonreproducible failures          | Log seed/input                   |
| No tests under optimized build               | Optimization-sensitive UB escapes | Test optimized configuration too |

### Failure Injection — testing the paths C usually gets wrong

C programs often fail in allocation, I/O, and partial initialization paths. These paths are hard to cover without deliberate failure injection.

| Failure source            | Injection strategy                         |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `malloc` failure          | allocator wrapper with fail-after-N policy |
| `realloc` failure         | controlled allocator                       |
| File open failure         | invalid path/permission/mocked file layer  |
| Short read/write          | fake stream/platform adapter               |
| Callback failure          | callback returns error at chosen step      |
| Parser allocation failure | allocator hook                             |
| Close failure             | mock I/O layer                             |
| Thread race/shutdown      | stress and controlled synchronization      |
| Platform API failure      | adapter-level fake implementation          |

Allocator wrapper concept:

```c id="p9_fail_alloc"
struct test_allocator {
    size_t calls;
    size_t fail_at;
};

void *test_malloc(struct test_allocator *a, size_t size)
{
    a->calls++;

    if (a->fail_at != 0 && a->calls == a->fail_at) {
        return NULL;
    }

    return malloc(size);
}
```

To use this effectively, production code must be allocator-aware or at least testable through a narrow allocation layer.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                | Better rule                                      |
| ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Never testing allocation failure          | Cleanup paths unverified      | Add failure-injection tests for resource modules |
| Mock layer differs too much from real API | False confidence              | Keep adapter contract precise                    |
| Failure test ignores leak checking        | Cleanup bug missed            | Run under ASan/LSan                              |
| Injecting only first allocation failure   | Later partial states untested | Fail each allocation point                       |
| Treating close failure as impossible      | Output loss                   | Test or define policy                            |

### Debugging Workflow — from symptom to semantic cause

Debugging C should move from symptom to layer classification: source logic, UB, memory lifetime, data race, ABI mismatch, build mismatch, platform API failure, or performance issue.

| Symptom                    | Likely category                           | First tools                   |
| -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Crash with invalid address | OOB, null, use-after-free, stack overflow | ASan, debugger                |
| Random output              | uninitialized read, race, UB              | MSan/UBSan/TSan, warnings     |
| Works under debugger only  | race, uninitialized data, timing          | sanitizers, logging carefully |
| Fails only optimized       | UB/aliasing/unsequenced behavior          | UBSan, ASan, review           |
| Wrong function called      | function pointer/table corruption         | debugger, ASan                |
| Stack corruption           | buffer overflow, ABI mismatch             | ASan, ABI review              |
| Link failure               | missing object/library                    | build logs                    |
| Runtime loader failure     | shared library path/version               | `ldd`/platform equivalent     |
| Hang/deadlock              | lock/callback/thread issue                | debugger, TSan, thread dump   |
| Slow performance           | algorithm/data/layout issue               | profiler                      |

Debugging sequence:

| Step                | Action                                         |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Reproduce           | Get exact command/input/environment            |
| Minimize            | Reduce input/test case                         |
| Classify            | Compile, link, runtime, UB, resource, platform |
| Instrument          | Add sanitizers/warnings/debug info             |
| Inspect             | Use debugger/logs/core dump                    |
| Verify invariant    | Check data structure contracts                 |
| Patch               | Fix source cause, not only symptom             |
| Add regression test | Prevent recurrence                             |
| Run broader suite   | Ensure no adjacent breakage                    |

Common debugger practices:

```bash id="p9_debug_build"
cc -g -O0 -Wall -Wextra main.c buffer.c -o app_dbg
```

For optimized bugs:

```bash id="p9_debug_optimized"
cc -g -O2 -fsanitize=address,undefined main.c buffer.c -o app_san
```

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                                | Why it matters               | Better rule                          |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Debugging invalid C as if machine behavior were stable | UB misleads                  | Run sanitizers first                 |
| Adding prints that change timing                       | Race may disappear           | Use TSan or controlled logging       |
| Inspecting optimized variable values naively           | Optimizer may remove/reorder | Use debug build and source reasoning |
| Fixing crash location only                             | Root cause earlier           | Trace ownership/bounds history       |
| No minimized repro                                     | Hard to verify fix           | Reduce and add regression            |

### Core Dumps, Backtraces, and Postmortem Debugging

C programs often fail outside a live debugger. Core dumps, crash reports, and backtraces are essential for postmortem analysis.

| Artifact              | Use                                  |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Core dump             | Memory/register snapshot at crash    |
| Backtrace             | Call stack summary                   |
| Symbol file           | Maps addresses to functions/lines    |
| Build ID              | Identifies exact binary              |
| Logs                  | Context before crash                 |
| Minidump/crash report | Platform-specific compact crash data |
| Sanitizer report      | Instrumented failure location        |
| Reproducer input      | Best debugging artifact              |

Postmortem requirements:

| Requirement                   | Why                     |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| Debug symbols preserved       | Meaningful stack traces |
| Exact binary version known    | Reproducibility         |
| Source revision known         | Match code              |
| Optimization flags known      | Debug interpretation    |
| Dependency versions known     | ABI/runtime context     |
| Input/config captured if safe | Reproduce               |
| Sensitive data redacted       | Security/privacy        |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                  | Why it matters               | Better rule                     |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Stripped binary with no separate symbols | Useless backtrace            | Archive debug symbols           |
| No build ID/version                      | Cannot match crash to source | Embed/version artifacts         |
| Logs omit error context                  | Hard reproduction            | Log boundary failures clearly   |
| Crash dump contains secrets              | Security risk                | Define dump handling policy     |
| Backtrace treated as root cause          | Often only symptom           | Analyze memory/lifetime history |

### Profiling — measuring before optimizing

C performance work should be evidence-driven. Profilers show where time, allocation, cache misses, branches, locks, or I/O costs occur.

| Profiling type            | Finds                                 | Examples                             |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| CPU sampling              | Hot functions/stacks                  | `perf`, Instruments, VTune           |
| Instrumentation profiling | Exact call counts/times with overhead | gprof-like tools                     |
| Cache profiling           | locality/cache misses                 | cachegrind/perf counters             |
| Heap profiling            | allocation hot spots/leaks            | heaptrack, massif                    |
| Lock profiling            | contention                            | platform tools                       |
| I/O tracing               | system calls, latency                 | `strace`-like tools, platform traces |
| Benchmarking              | operation-level performance           | custom harness                       |
| Coverage-guided profiling | optimization feedback                 | PGO workflows                        |

Benchmark harness principles:

| Principle                           | Reason                     |
| ----------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Use release-like optimization       | Debug builds mislead       |
| Warm up where relevant              | Cache/runtime effects      |
| Avoid measuring I/O unless intended | Noise                      |
| Use sufficient iterations           | Reduce variance            |
| Prevent optimizer removing work     | Benchmark correctness      |
| Record compiler/flags/hardware      | Reproducibility            |
| Compare against baseline            | Detect regression          |
| Measure memory as well as time      | Allocation often dominates |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                           | Why it matters                  | Better rule                 |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Optimizing guessed hotspot        | Wasted complexity               | Profile first               |
| Profiling sanitizer build         | Instrumentation distorts timing | Use release-like builds     |
| Microbenchmark not representative | Wrong optimization target       | Benchmark real workload too |
| Ignoring allocation profile       | CPU profile incomplete          | Track heap behavior         |
| Treating one run as truth         | Noise                           | Repeat and report variance  |

### Coverage — knowing what tests execute

Coverage tools show which lines, branches, or functions executed during tests. Coverage does not prove correctness, but low coverage highlights untested code.

| Coverage kind         | Meaning                    | Caveat                             |
| --------------------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Line coverage         | Lines executed             | Does not cover branch alternatives |
| Branch coverage       | Branch outcomes tested     | Still not semantic proof           |
| Function coverage     | Functions called           | Coarse                             |
| Condition coverage    | Boolean subconditions      | More detailed                      |
| Fuzz coverage         | Input exploration guidance | Tool-specific                      |
| Differential coverage | New/changed lines covered  | Useful in CI                       |

Coverage strategy:

| Code area                 | Coverage expectation             |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Parser error paths        | High branch coverage desirable   |
| Cleanup paths             | Must be exercised                |
| Platform adapters         | Per-platform coverage            |
| Generic containers        | Edge-case coverage               |
| Safety checks             | Invalid input coverage           |
| Fatal internal assertions | Tested indirectly or debug tests |
| Dead code                 | Remove or justify                |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                 | Why it matters                  | Better rule                         |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Chasing 100% line coverage mechanically | Low-value tests                 | Focus on risk and branches          |
| Ignoring failure-path coverage          | C cleanup bugs missed           | Test failures deliberately          |
| Coverage only on debug config           | Release-specific code untested  | Cover relevant build variants       |
| Generated code skews metrics            | Misleading numbers              | Exclude or separately measure       |
| Coverage treated as correctness         | Executed wrong code still wrong | Combine with assertions and oracles |

### CI for C Projects — matrix, artifacts, and quality gates

Continuous integration is especially important for C because compiler, platform, data model, optimization, and sanitizer behavior differ.

| CI dimension    | Examples                                     |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Compiler matrix | GCC, Clang, MSVC if supported                |
| Standard mode   | C99/C11/C17/C23 policy                       |
| Optimization    | debug, release                               |
| Sanitizers      | ASan/UBSan, TSan separately                  |
| Platforms       | Linux, macOS, Windows, embedded emulator     |
| Architectures   | x86-64, ARM64, 32-bit if relevant            |
| Build options   | static/shared, optional dependencies         |
| Tests           | unit, integration, fuzz smoke                |
| Static analysis | analyzer job                                 |
| Formatting      | style check                                  |
| Packaging       | install/export test                          |
| ABI             | symbol/API compatibility check for libraries |

Example CI gate table:

| Gate                                    | Blocks merge?                                 |
| --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Build warning-clean on primary compiler | Yes                                           |
| Unit tests pass                         | Yes                                           |
| ASan/UBSan tests pass                   | Yes where supported                           |
| Formatting check                        | Usually yes                                   |
| Static analyzer critical findings       | Yes after triage                              |
| Fuzz smoke test                         | Yes for parsers                               |
| Coverage threshold                      | Project-dependent                             |
| Performance benchmark                   | Usually non-blocking unless regression severe |
| ABI compatibility                       | Yes for stable library releases               |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                               | Why it matters          | Better rule                               |
| ------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| CI only tests one compiler            | Portability bugs escape | Matrix supported compilers                |
| CI only debug build                   | Optimized UB escapes    | Test release/sanitized builds             |
| Sanitizer job allowed to fail forever | Tool loses value        | Fix or explicitly scope unsupported cases |
| No artifact retention                 | Hard postmortem         | Save logs, binaries, reports              |
| Optional dependencies untested        | Broken feature variants | Include feature matrix selectively        |

### Reproducible Builds — controlling inputs to control outputs

Reproducible builds aim to make artifacts traceable and, sometimes, byte-for-byte identical. C builds often depend on timestamps, paths, compiler versions, environment variables, dependency versions, and generated code.

| Reproducibility input   | Risk                           |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Compiler version        | Different codegen/warnings     |
| Build flags             | ABI/performance changes        |
| Header/library versions | ABI mismatch                   |
| Absolute paths          | Debug info differences         |
| Timestamps              | Binary differences             |
| Generated files         | Non-determinism                |
| Environment variables   | Hidden configuration           |
| Locale/timezone         | Sorting/formatting differences |
| Build parallelism       | Race in generation             |
| Dependency source       | Supply-chain drift             |

Reproducibility practices:

| Practice                                         | Purpose                    |
| ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------- |
| Pin toolchain versions where needed              | Stable output              |
| Record compiler and flags                        | Debug/release traceability |
| Lock dependency versions                         | Prevent drift              |
| Generate config deterministically                | Stable builds              |
| Avoid embedding build timestamps unless intended | Byte reproducibility       |
| Use clean builds in release                      | Avoid stale artifacts      |
| Archive source revision and patches              | Rebuildability             |
| Separate build and source directories            | Clean configuration        |
| Verify install/package in CI                     | Release confidence         |

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                    | Why it matters         | Better rule                                  |
| ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Release built from dirty tree              | Untraceable artifact   | Enforce clean source or record diff          |
| Generated code not checked or reproducible | Build drift            | Make generation deterministic                |
| Dependency fetched from moving branch      | Nonreproducible        | Pin commits/versions                         |
| Build flags undocumented                   | Cannot reproduce bugs  | Emit build configuration                     |
| Different local and CI builds              | Environment dependency | Container/toolchain policy where appropriate |

### Security Hardening Flags and Runtime Defenses

Compiler and linker hardening options can reduce exploitability, but they do not make unsafe C safe. They are defense-in-depth.

| Hardening area           | Examples                                           | Helps with                |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| Stack protection         | stack canaries                                     | Some stack overflows      |
| Fortified libc calls     | `_FORTIFY_SOURCE`-style mechanisms where supported | Some buffer misuse        |
| PIE/ASLR support         | position-independent executable                    | Address randomization     |
| RELRO/bind-now           | linker/loader hardening                            | GOT overwrite mitigation  |
| NX/DEP                   | non-executable memory                              | Code injection mitigation |
| Control-flow integrity   | compiler-supported CFI                             | Indirect call abuse       |
| Sanitizers in testing    | ASan/UBSan/TSan                                    | Defect discovery          |
| Warnings/static analysis | source issues                                      | Preventive detection      |
| Fuzzing                  | parser boundary bugs                               | Input robustness          |

Hardening policy must be platform-specific. Flags differ across GCC, Clang, MSVC, linkers, operating systems, and libc implementations.

Common Pitfalls:

| Pitfall                                   | Why it matters                    | Better rule                 |
| ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Treating hardening as bug fix             | Vulnerability may remain          | Fix root cause              |
| Applying flags without testing            | Runtime/link issues               | Test hardened builds        |
| Assuming all platforms support same flags | Portability break                 | Platform-specific flag sets |
| Ignoring performance/compat impact        | Some mitigations cost             | Measure where relevant      |
| Shipping without symbol/version policy    | Hardening incomplete at ABI layer | Control exports too         |

### Documentation and API Contracts — headers as user-facing documents

C public headers are often the primary API documentation. They should describe ownership, lifetime, errors, thread safety, ABI stability, and portability.

| Contract item                 | Should appear where                                   |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Ownership of returned pointer | Function comment                                      |
| Required cleanup function     | Function/type comment                                 |
| Output state on failure       | Function comment                                      |
| Nullability                   | Parameter comment or annotations if project uses them |
| Array length/capacity         | Parameter description                                 |
| Thread safety                 | Type/module comment                                   |
| Reentrancy                    | Function/module comment                               |
| Callback timing               | Callback registration docs                            |
| Error codes                   | Error enum docs                                       |
| ABI/versioning                | Public header/release notes                           |
| Platform dependency           | Header/module docs                                    |

Example public contract:

